clusters.title: Remove the dead CDP screencast plumbing in plugin-browser
clusters.oneLine: Delete the entire CDP screencast push cluster (dispatch cases, CDPSession type, sidecar emit wiring, browser-session onEvent channel) orphaned by the PR #205 polling revert.
clusters.rationale: Confirmed dead by repo-wide grep (callers only inside plugin-browser + dist artifacts); already journaled in TECH_DEBT as dormant debt. ~57 lines + ~9 cross-file symbols. Typecheck-gated, no test covers the removed path.

findings.title: Entire CDP screencast plumbing in plugin-browser is dead (no caller after polling revert)
findings.problem: The browser surface was reverted from a CDP Page.startScreencast push to screenshot polling (PR #205, see TECH_DEBT). The whole screencast cluster it needed is now unreachable: the `startScreencast`/`stopScreencast` JSON-RPC cases + `stopScreencast()` helper + `cdp`/`emit` SidecarState fields (dispatch.ts), the `CDPSession` interface and `newCDPSession` on PlaywrightHandle (types.ts), the `state.emit` wiring (sidecar.ts), and the unsolicited-event channel `onEvent`/`eventListeners`/`browserSidecarOnEvent` + the `reply.event && !reply.id` branch in handleLine (browser-session.ts). Nothing invokes any of it.
findings.fix: Delete the two screencast dispatch cases + stopScreencast() helper + cdp/emit SidecarState fields + the stopScreencast() call in teardown(); remove the CDPSession interface and newCDPSession from PlaywrightHandle in types.ts; drop the state.emit assignment in sidecar.ts main(); remove onEvent/eventListeners/browserSidecarOnEvent and the unsolicited-event branch in handleLine from browser-session.ts. If screencast is ever revived, re-add it gated behind a polling fallback (TECH_DEBT note).
findings.testStrategy: Run plugin-browser unit tests; the screencast path has no test, so removal only needs the existing goto/screenshot/eval suite green plus `pnpm build` typecheck (CDPSession type import drops).
findings.complexity: 262 lines -> ~205 lines in dispatch.ts; removes ~9 cross-file symbols
findings.retiresTechDebt: TECH_DEBT.md 'Browser surface reverted from CDP screencast' dormant-debt entry (lines 140-151)

findings.title: Dormant screencast event-push plumbing: onEvent/eventListeners/browserSidecarOnEvent has no consumer
findings.problem: The unsolicited-event channel (Sidecar.eventListeners Set, Sidecar.onEvent(), the exported browserSidecarOnEvent(), and the handleLine `reply.event && !reply.id` branch) was added to forward the sidecar's CDP `screencastFrame` push to the browser surface. PR #205 reverted the surface to polling (browser-surface.ts uses `frame` RPC polling, NOT screencast), so nothing subscribes. This is dead 'just in case' plumbing: it maintains a listener Set, an extra parse branch on every sidecar line, and a public API surface that no code calls.
findings.fix: Delete `eventListeners`, `onEvent()`, `browserSidecarOnEvent()`, the `reply.event && !reply.id` branch in handleLine, and the corresponding export in index.ts. Keep the sidecar-side `startScreencast`/`stopScreencast` (unit 69) only if a future push path is planned; otherwise remove that too in a coordinated change. Document the polling decision so the plumbing isn't re-added.
findings.testStrategy: Build + typecheck the package; existing browser-session.test.ts (sidecar protocol) and web-fetch tests must stay green. Grep CI guard that browserSidecarOnEvent has no importer.

findings.title: Dormant CDP screencast push path — startScreencast/stopScreencast/emit/cdp never invoked
findings.problem: The entire CDP `Page.startScreencast` push pipeline in dispatch.ts (the `startScreencast` + `stopScreencast` RPC cases, `state.emit`, `state.cdp`, the `screencastFrame` handler/ack loop) plus the `CDPSession` type and `newCDPSession` projection in types.ts are dead. PR #205 reverted the browser surface from CDP screencast to screenshot polling because screencast emits 0 frames for blank/static pages; browser-surface.ts now polls the `frame` method (its own comment, line 13, explains 'Why polling and not a CDP Page.startScreencast push'). This is ~40 lines of non-trivial, hard-to-reason-about CDP/event plumbing kept alive 'just in case', plus the dead consumer `browserSidecarOnEvent` in browser-session.ts.
findings.fix: Remove the `startScreencast`/`stopScreencast` cases, the `emit`/`cdp` fields on SidecarState, the `stopScreencast` helper, the `CDPSession`/`newCDPSession` types, and `state.emit` wiring in sidecar.ts plus `browserSidecarOnEvent`/`onEvent` in browser-session.ts — OR, if the surface is expected to re-adopt screencast, leave a single TODO and a test pinning the polling decision. teardown() should drop its now-unconditional `stopScreencast(state)` call.
findings.testStrategy: After removal, `pnpm build` + run dispatch.test.ts and browser-session.test.ts; assert browser-surface polling still emits frames via the `frame` method.

clusters.title: Delete one-reference / zero-consumer dead exports across the repo
clusters.oneLine: Remove ~13 proven-unused exports: REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS, runProcessBinary, deep-research constants, formatRequirementIssues, isOAuthProvider, printWarn, accentWash, useLatestDeepLink, schedulerLogPath, homeCwd, countNodes(core), SkillRouter/buildSkillIndexPrompt, PhaseMarker, testing matchers, killAndUnlinkRunner export, WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY re-export.
clusters.rationale: Each verified by repo-wide grep (only the definition, no static/dynamic importer; invariant #11 dynamic-dispatch check performed). Pure deletions, typecheck-gated. Several flagged @deprecated and hand-maintained for no effect.

findings.title: REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS is a deprecated export with no code consumer
findings.problem: `REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS` is marked @deprecated ('Use REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS — enforcement is now allow-by-default-deny'). Its doc claims it is 'Retained for UI affordance-gating: a renderer can still read this to gray out host-only controls', but no renderer or any other code actually imports it. The WS bus no longer consults it. It is pure dead weight that must be hand-maintained in lockstep with new host-only commands (the doc shows it being appended to for mobileGateway commands) for no behavioral effect.
findings.fix: Delete the `REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS` export (and its trailing maintenance burden). If a renderer ever needs affordance-gating, derive it from REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS instead.
findings.testStrategy: pnpm build typecheck across the monorepo (no importer to break); desktop-ipc-contract tests stay green.

findings.title: runProcessBinary is dead — its stated consumer (screenshot tool) migrated to runProcess + temp file
findings.problem: `runProcessBinary` is documented as 'Used by the screenshot tool to preserve binary PNG bytes', but the screenshot tool no longer calls it. screenshot.ts now captures via `runProcess('screencapture', ...)` writing to a temp file, then reads bytes back with `fs.readFile(outTmp)`. No code references runProcessBinary anywhere.
findings.fix: Delete `runProcessBinary` (~52 lines). Its sibling runProcess remains the live primitive.
findings.testStrategy: plugin-computer-control tools.test.ts stays green; build typecheck confirms no importer.

findings.title: Three deep-research constants (MIN_SUBAGENTS, PLANNING_/SYNTHESIS_MAX_ITERATIONS) are unreferenced
findings.problem: `MIN_SUBAGENTS`, `PLANNING_MAX_ITERATIONS`, and `SYNTHESIS_MAX_ITERATIONS` are exported constants with no reader anywhere in the package or repo — leftovers from a removed per-phase iteration-cap mechanism (the live cap is SUBAGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS; MAX_SUBAGENTS is the live fan-out cap).
findings.fix: Delete the three unused constant declarations.
findings.testStrategy: mode-deep-research tests + build typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: core requirements.ts exports formatRequirementIssues with no caller and no barrel re-export
findings.problem: `formatRequirementIssues(check)` is exported from core's requirements.ts but is neither re-exported from packages/core/src/index.ts (which only re-exports RequirementRegistry from this module) nor called anywhere in the repo.
findings.fix: Delete `formatRequirementIssues` (and any now-unused helpers it alone pulls in).
findings.testStrategy: core test suite + build typecheck; no importer to break.

findings.title: cli wizard isOAuthProvider helper is unused
findings.problem: `isOAuthProvider(def)` is exported from the auth wizard but called nowhere — no caller in the wizard flow or elsewhere in the cli/repo.
findings.fix: Delete `isOAuthProvider`.
findings.testStrategy: cli build typecheck + wizard tests; no importer.

findings.title: printWarn is an unused sibling of the live printError
findings.problem: `printWarn(message)` mirrors `printError` (yellow 'warn:' tag) but is never called. printError is used; printWarn is dead.
findings.fix: Delete `printWarn` (re-add if a warn path ever needs it).
findings.testStrategy: cli build typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: accentWash (translucent node-card wash) is unused; only accentHex is consumed
findings.problem: `accentWash(accent)` builds a color-mix wash for workflow node card backgrounds but no component imports it. The file's other export, accentHex, IS used (Palette/WorkflowCanvas/NodeInspector).
findings.fix: Delete `accentWash`.
findings.testStrategy: desktop build/typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: useLatestDeepLink hook is unused (DeepLinkBridge is the live consumer)
findings.problem: `useLatestDeepLink()` (a useSyncExternalStore hook returning the latest deep-link payload) has no caller. The deep-link feature is driven by `DeepLinkBridge` (imported in main.tsx) and the module-local `deepLinkStore`; nothing reads the 'latest' value via this hook.
findings.fix: Delete `useLatestDeepLink`. (Optionally drop the now-internal-only `export` on deepLinkStore too.)
findings.testStrategy: desktop build/typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: schedulerLogPath() is exported but never called
findings.problem: `schedulerLogPath()` returns the scheduler service's log path but has no consumer — neither the doctor checks, the schedule commands, nor anywhere else reads it.
findings.fix: Delete `schedulerLogPath`.
findings.testStrategy: cli build/typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: homeCwd() default-cwd helper in plugin-terminal is unused
findings.problem: `homeCwd()` ('Home dir helper for surfaces that want a sane default cwd') wraps os.homedir() but is called nowhere in the terminal plugin or repo.
findings.fix: Delete `homeCwd` (callers that need a default cwd already use os.homedir() directly or the spawn default).
findings.testStrategy: plugin-terminal build/typecheck; no importer.

findings.title: REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS is a deprecated security export with zero code consumers and is stale
findings.problem: The `@deprecated` `REMOTE_DISALLOWED_COMMANDS` set is documented as 'retained for UI affordance-gating: a renderer can still read this to gray out the host-only controls', but no renderer or any other module reads it. Worse, even for that stated purpose it is wrong: it lists only 9 commands while ~30 genuinely host-only commands (desks.create/remove/setActive, all settings.*, vault writes, prefs.update, app.updateCli/updateDashboard, onboarding.*, workflows.save/setEnabled/validateDraft, etc.) are absent — so any consumer trusting it would un-gray host-mutating controls it should hide. A dead, misleading set in a security-critical contract invites someone to revive it as a blocklist and reintroduce the exact privilege-escalation hole the allow-list (REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS) replaced.
findings.fix: Delete the export (and its JSDoc). If affordance-gating is desired, derive it once at the call site as `IpcCommandName[]` minus REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS rather than maintaining a hand-curated, drift-prone second list.
findings.testStrategy: Remove the export; `tsc` across the workspace stays green (no static importers); add a guard test asserting the renderer affordance helper derives host-only commands from REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS so the two can never drift.

findings.title: SkillRouter + buildSkillIndexPrompt are dead — runtime uses SDK helper + registry filter instead
findings.problem: The entire router.ts module (SkillRouter class with resolve/filterByTriggers/classify, and buildSkillIndexPrompt) has no production consumer. The actual runtime skill-index prompt is built by @moxxy/sdk's buildSystemPromptWithSkills (mode-helpers.ts) and trigger filtering at runtime is done by SkillRegistryImpl.filterByTriggers (registries/skills.ts). router.ts is a parallel, never-wired implementation kept alive only by router.test.ts.
findings.fix: Delete router.ts and its export line in skills/index.ts (and the re-export from core/index.ts) plus router.test.ts; OR, if a future classifier router is intended, fold its trigger-match logic into SkillRegistryImpl.filterByTriggers (already duplicated there) so there is one source of truth.
findings.testStrategy: After deletion, run pnpm build + typecheck across the monorepo to confirm no consumer; grep confirms only the test referenced it.

findings.title: Exported countNodes in core is dead; plugin-view re-implements it because it can't import core
findings.problem: core/src/view/parse.ts exports countNodes (re-exported from core/src/index.ts:47), but the only real consumer — plugin-view's present_view tool — re-implements an identical countNodes locally (packages/plugin-view/src/index.ts:34-37) rather than importing core's. It MUST, because plugin-view depends on @moxxy/sdk at runtime and only on @moxxy/core as a devDependency (package.json), so it cannot import the core symbol. The core export therefore has zero runtime importers (verified: grep for `countNodes` across packages/apps shows only the core export line, plugin-view's local copy, and parse-extended.test.ts which imports the core one purely for its own coverage). The function operates solely on ViewNode (an @moxxy/sdk type) and has no reason to live in core.
findings.fix: Move countNodes into @moxxy/sdk (e.g. alongside ViewNode in view-renderer.ts) as the single source of truth, have both core (if it still needs it) and plugin-view import it from the SDK, and drop the duplicate in plugin-view and the now-unused core export. Keeps invariant 1 (sdk zero-internal-deps; countNodes only touches the SDK's own ViewNode type).
findings.testStrategy: Keep parse-extended.test.ts's countNodes case but point it at the SDK export; add a plugin-view test asserting nodeCount uses the shared helper. Type-check ensures no remaining importer of the removed core export.

findings.title: PhaseMarker component is dead — no static or dynamic references anywhere in repo
findings.problem: PhaseMarker is exported and its JSDoc claims it is 'used by <ChatView> and <StatusLine>', but it is referenced by nothing. Dead plumbing that ships in the CLI bundle and misleads future readers with a false usage claim.
findings.fix: Delete PhaseMarker.tsx. If a phase-marker glyph row is desired in future, EventLine.tsx already renders the equivalent '◆ thinking' / file-edit rows.
findings.testStrategy: Build + typecheck the plugin-cli package after deletion; CI green confirms no importer existed.

findings.title: Vitest custom matchers (matchers.ts) are dead: zero importers of @moxxy/testing/matchers
findings.problem: matchers.ts registers three Vitest matchers (toContainEventOfType, toContainToolCall, toMatchEventSequence) via a top-level expect.extend side-effect. The matchers only take effect when the module is imported. package.json exposes them at the './matchers' subpath, but nothing in the repo imports them, so the side-effect never runs and the matchers (advertised in the package description) are never available to any test.
findings.fix: Either (a) wire it up: add `import '@moxxy/testing/matchers'` to a shared vitest setupFiles entry (tooling/vitest-preset) and add type augmentation, then add at least one consuming test; or (b) delete matchers.ts and its './matchers' export and drop the 'Vitest matchers' claim from the package description. Given the package's purpose, (a) is preferable but requires real adoption; otherwise remove the dormant plumbing.
findings.testStrategy: If wired up, add a test that uses each matcher against a real EventLogReader and asserts pass/fail messages. If removed, CI build/typecheck confirms no broken imports.

findings.title: killAndUnlinkRunner is `export`ed but never imported anywhere (only used internally)
findings.problem: killAndUnlinkRunner is declared `export async function` but is not re-exported from packages/runner/src/index.ts and is not imported by any other file in the repo. Its only caller is the internal maybeRecoverFromMismatch (line 949) in the same module. The `export` keyword is therefore dead — no external consumer can or does reach it.
findings.fix: Drop the `export` keyword (make it a module-private function), or, if it is meant to be public recovery API, re-export it from index.ts and document it. Given no consumer, prefer making it private.
findings.testStrategy: After removing `export`, `pnpm build` + typecheck must stay green; confirm no test imports it.

findings.title: Dead re-export of WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY from WorkflowCanvas
findings.problem: WorkflowCanvas.tsx imports WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY from @moxxy/workflows-builder solely to re-export it (`export { WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY };` at EOF). Its only consumer, WorkflowBuilder.tsx, imports the symbol directly from @moxxy/workflows-builder, not from WorkflowCanvas. The re-export has no importer.
findings.fix: Delete the `import`-for-reexport of WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY and the trailing `export { WORKFLOW_ERROR_KEY };`.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck + existing WorkflowsPanel.test suite still green after removal.

findings.title: useLatestDeepLink export is dead — zero consumers, dormant 'future routing' plumbing
findings.problem: useLatestDeepLink() (and the deepLinkStore.latest() read path it is the only caller of) has no consumers anywhere. The module doc itself states 'No routing is wired yet' and 'For future routing'. DeepLinkBridge still usefully drains/forwards links into the store, but the reactive read hook is dead until a routing feature actually subscribes.
findings.fix: Either delete useLatestDeepLink + DeepLinkStore.last/latest() until a consumer exists (store just needs push/subscribe for the live-event invariant), or wire the intended routing consumer. Keeping it as documented dormant plumbing is acceptable only if explicitly tracked in TECH_DEBT.
findings.testStrategy: After removal, `pnpm --filter @moxxy/desktop typecheck` stays green; if kept, add a test that pushes a link and asserts useLatestDeepLink re-renders with it.

findings.title: accentWash() is an unused export — no static or dynamic references
findings.problem: The exported helper accentWash(accent) is never consumed anywhere in the repo. It is a presentational helper with no registry/string-keyed/dynamic-dispatch path that could keep it live, so it is genuinely dead plumbing that adds maintenance surface.
findings.fix: Delete the accentWash function (lines 23-26). It depends only on accentHex + the existing --color-card-bg CSS var, so removal is self-contained. If a translucent node wash is wanted later, reintroduce it at the call site.
findings.testStrategy: After deletion, run `pnpm -C apps/desktop typecheck` and the existing WorkflowsPanel.test.tsx; a knip/ts-prune dead-export check would also confirm no remaining importer.

findings.title: Exported `schedulerLogPath()` has no consumers (static or dynamic)
findings.problem: schedulerLogPath() is an exported plain function (not registry-dispatched, not referenced by package.json#moxxy or any dynamic import). Repo-wide grep finds the only occurrence at its own definition site. It is dead export surface.
findings.fix: Remove schedulerLogPath(); callers needing the path can use serviceLogPath(SCHEDULER_SERVICE) directly (the body it already delegates to).
findings.testStrategy: Remove and run build/typecheck/test for @moxxy/cli; no references should break.

findings.title: printWarn export has zero callers anywhere in the repo
findings.problem: `printWarn` is exported alongside `printError` as the canonical stderr warning helper but is never imported. `printError` is used by ~10 command modules; `printWarn` by none. Dormant 'just in case' plumbing.
findings.fix: Either delete `printWarn`, or adopt it at the existing ad-hoc warn sites (commands that currently do `process.stderr.write(colors.yellow(...))`). Prefer adoption to keep the consistent `warn:` tag, otherwise remove.
findings.testStrategy: If removed, `pnpm --filter @moxxy/cli typecheck` stays green (no consumers). If adopted, snapshot the stderr `warn:` prefix in one command test.

findings.title: Exported isOAuthProvider() is dead — zero static or dynamic references repo-wide
findings.problem: isOAuthProvider(def) is exported but never imported or dispatched anywhere. The two real call sites (init.ts loginOAuth handler, login.ts loginStatus filter) inline `def.auth?.kind === 'oauth'` instead, so this helper is dormant duplicate plumbing.
findings.fix: Delete isOAuthProvider, or — if a shared predicate is wanted — keep it and refactor the inline checks in init.ts/login.ts to call it. Removal is safest given no current consumers.
findings.testStrategy: After deletion, `pnpm build` + typecheck across the cli package confirms no broken import; grep confirms no remaining references.

findings.title: Three exported constants are never referenced anywhere (dormant plumbing)
findings.problem: MIN_SUBAGENTS, PLANNING_MAX_ITERATIONS and SYNTHESIS_MAX_ITERATIONS are exported but never imported by any file. MIN_SUBAGENTS is documented as a 'minimum subagents for fan-out' floor that is never enforced (runPlanningPhase only checks queries.length > MAX_SUBAGENTS and queries.length === 0). The two *_MAX_ITERATIONS constants are dead because the planner/synthesis phases use runSingleShotTurn (a no-tools single round), which takes only { maxTokens } and has no iteration concept — so an iteration cap can never apply. They are leftover plumbing that never got wired.
findings.fix: Delete MIN_SUBAGENTS, PLANNING_MAX_ITERATIONS, SYNTHESIS_MAX_ITERATIONS. If a real minimum-fanout floor is wanted, enforce MIN_SUBAGENTS in runPlanningPhase (refuse/pad plans below it); otherwise drop it.
findings.testStrategy: After deletion, `pnpm build` + typecheck the package; a knip/ts-prune pass should report zero unused exports in constants.ts.

findings.title: homeCwd() is dead — exported from pty.ts but not in barrel and never called
findings.problem: homeCwd() is a helper 'for surfaces that want a sane default cwd' but no surface uses it. The surface and tool both derive cwd from ctx.cwd / process.cwd(), never from homeCwd(). It is also not re-exported from index.ts (only createTerminalProcess + TerminalProcess are), so it is not even reachable as public API.
findings.fix: Delete homeCwd() and the unused `import os from 'node:os'` it is the sole user of.
findings.testStrategy: Build/typecheck after removal; grep confirms zero references.

findings.title: Unused `mono` prop on shared settings `Row` primitive
findings.problem: `Row` accepts and threads a `mono?: boolean` prop into the title's className, but no caller ever passes it. Verified across the repo: the only `<Row ...>` callers from settings-primitives are ProvidersTab.tsx:104 and McpTab.tsx:45 (the other `<Row>` hits in Transcript/WorkspaceFiles are different, locally-defined Row components). Neither passes `mono`. Vault rendering uses className="mono" directly on its own card, not via this prop. Dead prop = noise in the shared API.
findings.fix: Remove the `mono` prop from `Row`'s signature and its className usage.
findings.testStrategy: tsc + existing settings render tests stay green after removal.

clusters.title: Replace SkillGallery inline search markup with the shared SearchBox primitive
clusters.oneLine: SkillGallery re-implements the settings SearchBox primitive byte-for-byte; import and delete ~28 lines of markup.
clusters.rationale: High-severity duplication already journaled in TECH_DEBT (2026-06-17). One-line replacement, guarantees visual parity with MCP/Vault/Providers tabs.

findings.title: SkillGallery hand-rolls a near-verbatim copy of the shared settings SearchBox primitive
findings.problem: SkillGallery.tsx L57-84 inlines a search row (flex container with the same padding/background/border/borderRadius, an Icon name='search', and an <input type='search'> with identical flex/border/outline/background/fontSize/color styles) that is byte-for-byte the SearchBox primitive exported from settings-primitives.tsx L13-52 and already used by the MCP/Vault/Providers tabs. Styling drift between the Skills search and the other tabs' search is a when-not-if.
findings.fix: Import `SearchBox` from '../settings-primitives' (already imports EmptyState from there) and replace the inline block with `<SearchBox value={query} onChange={setQuery} placeholder="Search skills…" />`. Delete the duplicate markup. Exactly the fix described in TECH_DEBT.
findings.testStrategy: Render SkillGallery in jsdom, type into the search box, assert filtered cards; visual parity is guaranteed by sharing the primitive.
findings.retiresTechDebt: TECH_DEBT.md 2026-06-17 'Skills gallery reimplements the shared settings SearchBox' (L40-51)

clusters.title: Collapse copy-pasted desktop chat / TUI render helpers onto their shared modules
clusters.oneLine: SkillGroupView re-defines pretty()/preStyle (and diverges on a background var); plugin-cli panels copy truncate()/oneLine() that chat-model already exports; render-diff reimplements the dep-free tool-display helpers; testing/compactor sites inline length/4 instead of estimateTextTokens; formatTokensShort duplicated with divergent rounding.
clusters.rationale: Pure helper-reuse fixes against an existing shared module; the SkillGroupView one also fixes a visible color inconsistency. All low-risk imports/deletes.

findings.title: SkillGroupView re-defines pretty() + preStyle instead of importing from blocks/block-shared.ts
findings.problem: SkillGroupView.tsx declares its own local `pretty()` (lines 223-230) and `preStyle` (lines 210-221), which are near-identical copies of the shared helpers in apps/desktop/src/chat/blocks/block-shared.ts. ToolBlock.tsx and SubagentView.tsx already import `pretty`/`preStyle` from block-shared; SkillGroupView (and ToolGroupView, which reuses SkillGroupView's ToolRow) is the lone duplicate. Worse, the two preStyle copies DIVERGE: block-shared uses `background: var(--color-input-soft)` while SkillGroupView's copy uses `background: var(--color-surface)` — so an expanded tool body inside a skill/tool group renders a different shade than the same body in a standalone ToolBlock, a visible inconsistency.
findings.fix: Delete the local `pretty` and `preStyle` from SkillGroupView.tsx and import them from './blocks/block-shared' (matching ToolBlock/SubagentView). This also unifies the expanded-body background color across all tool renderings.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot/visual: render an expanded ToolRow inside SkillGroupView and a standalone ToolBlock; assert identical computed background on the expanded <pre>. Compile check passes after removing dead local helpers.

findings.title: truncate() and oneLine() copy-pasted in 3 panel files; @moxxy/chat-model already exports both
findings.problem: Each of SkillsPanel, ToolsPanel, WorkflowsPanel defines its own private `truncate(s,n)` and `oneLine(s)`, identical copies. The shared `@moxxy/chat-model` already exports `truncate` and `oneLine` (packages/chat-model/src/format.ts:10-18) and several files in this same package (chat/EventLine, chat/ToolCallBlock, chat/SubagentScopeView, chat/BlockLine) already import them from it.
findings.fix: Import `truncate`/`oneLine` from '@moxxy/chat-model' in all three panels and delete the local copies; adjust the column arg by one (pass NAME_COL → NAME_COL-1 at the callsite, OR standardize chat-model's truncate to inclusive-of-ellipsis) so render width is unchanged.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot/ink-testing-library render of each panel with a name longer than its column; assert the truncated string equals the pre-change output (guards the off-by-one width drift).

findings.title: Web channel render-diff reimplements toDiffRows/fileDiffSummary/diffGutterNo/fileDiffVerb instead of the dep-free @moxxy/sdk/tool-display subpath
findings.problem: render-diff.tsx deliberately re-implements rowsOf() (== toDiffRows), plusMinus() (== fileDiffSummary/plusMinus), gutter() (== diffGutterNo) and the create/update verb (== fileDiffVerb), with a comment claiming the helpers can't be imported because the @moxxy/sdk import is type-only/erased by esbuild. That justification is wrong: apps/mobile-poc/src/FileDiffView.tsx value-imports the very same helpers from '@moxxy/sdk/tool-display' (a deliberately dependency-free, browser/RN-safe subpath) and proves they bundle cleanly. So the diff-flattening logic is duplicated in the one place that didn't need to be.
findings.fix: Import toDiffRows/fileDiffSummary (or the plusMinus helper)/diffGutterNo/fileDiffVerb from '@moxxy/sdk/tool-display' in render-diff.tsx and delete the local rowsOf/plusMinus/gutter/verb. Keep the DOM/CSS-class rendering local (that part is genuinely platform-specific).
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot the web FileDiffView output for a multi-hunk diff before/after; assert gap rows and gutter numbers match the SDK helpers' output.

findings.title: Three sites inline `length / 4` token estimate instead of the SDK's estimateTextTokens
findings.problem: estimateTextTokens (provider-utils.ts) is the single home for the ~4-chars/token fallback and is already imported by the anthropic/openai/codex providers. Three other packages that depend on @moxxy/sdk re-inline the same arithmetic: attachments.ts uses `Math.round(fullText.length / 4)`, fake-provider.ts uses `Math.ceil(blob.length / 4)` (identical to the helper), and compactor-summarize computes a `tokensSaved` delta with `Math.ceil((originalChars - summary.length) / 4)`. Minor, but it's the exact pattern the helper was hoisted to retire, and the `Math.round` vs `Math.ceil` drift makes estimates inconsistent across the app.
findings.fix: Replace `Math.round(fullText.length/4)` and `Math.ceil(blob.length/4)` with `estimateTextTokens(...)` imported from @moxxy/sdk. For compactor-summarize, compute `Math.max(0, estimateTextTokens(originalText) - estimateTextTokens(summary))` (or leave as a delta if exactness there is unimportant — lowest priority of the three).
findings.testStrategy: No behavior change beyond Math.round->Math.ceil for attachments; covered by existing attachment/fake-provider tests.

findings.title: formatTokensShort duplicated in plugin-cli with divergent rounding (1.2k vs 1k, 812 vs 812)
findings.problem: usage.ts defines formatTokensShort, and packages/plugin-cli/src/session/helpers.ts defines a same-named function with DIFFERENT behavior: client-core uses `toFixed(1)` for the k band and `String(Math.round(n))` for the units band; plugin-cli uses `toFixed(0)` for k and `String(n)`. So the same token count renders as '1.2k' in desktop and '1k' in the TUI. Copy-paste that has already drifted. Neither package can import the other (plugin-cli -> client-core would be a layering inversion), so the right home is a shared @moxxy/sdk helper.
findings.fix: Move one canonical formatTokensShort into @moxxy/sdk (e.g. sdk/token-accounting or a format util) and import it from both client-core and plugin-cli; delete the two copies. Pick the desired rounding once.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot table-test the shared formatter across {812, 1_200, 3_400_000}; assert both consumers import it.

clusters.title: Deduplicate compareSemver between cli/update and desktop-host/app-update
clusters.oneLine: compareSemver is copied verbatim in two release paths; one copy drops prerelease/build suffix causing a non-deterministic latest tie-break. Hoist one tested impl.
clusters.rationale: Duplication + a latent correctness bug (release picker tie-break). Low risk; release-path so add a focused test.

findings.title: compareSemver duplicated verbatim between cli/update and desktop-host/app-update
findings.problem: compareSemver in check.ts is byte-for-byte identical to packages/desktop-host/src/app-update/resolve.ts:236-248 (same prerelease-stripping parse, same 3-field loop). The check.ts header acknowledges the copy ('A local copy so the CLI doesn't take a dependency on @moxxy/desktop-host'). Both packages already depend on @moxxy/sdk, which has no semver helper — so the duplication exists only because the shared home was never created, not because of a dependency constraint.
findings.fix: Add compareSemver (and a parseSemver) to @moxxy/sdk and import it in both check.ts and resolve.ts, deleting both local copies. Respects invariant 3 (shared logic routed through @moxxy/sdk).
findings.testStrategy: Move the existing compareSemver test cases into the sdk test; assert both call sites still pass their suites.

findings.title: compareSemver drops prerelease/build suffix → non-deterministic 'latest' tie-break in release picker
findings.problem: compareSemver parses only `s.split('-')[0]` into major.minor.patch and ignores any suffix, so e.g. `1.0.0` and `1.0.0+build2` (or two builds of the same x.y.z) compare EQUAL. In resolveDesktopRelease the final `.sort(compareSemver)` then leaves their relative order = GitHub API order, so `candidates[candidates.length-1]` (the chosen 'latest') is whichever the API happened to list last among ties — non-deterministic across calls. For straight x.y.z desktop tags this never bites, but the picker has no documented guarantee desktop tags are suffix-free.
findings.fix: For the release picker, tie-break deterministically: when compareSemver returns 0, fall back to comparing the full tag string (or the release's published_at). Alternatively document/enforce that desktop-v tags are always bare x.y.z. The bootstrap/gate uses of compareSemver (>, <=) are unaffected and need no change.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test compareSemver returns 0 for 1.0.0 vs 1.0.0+b; test resolveDesktopRelease deterministically picks the intended tag when two same-core versions are present in either API order.

findings.title: compareSemver is duplicated from packages/cli/src/update/check.ts
findings.problem: Two independent compareSemver implementations exist (resolve.ts and cli/src/update/check.ts:43). They diverge in suffix handling and could drift further. This module legitimately cannot import the cli copy (it must stay node-builtins-only for the bootstrap and the cli is a leaf binary), and @moxxy/sdk has no semver helper, so the cleanest dedupe is a tiny shared dep-free helper — but invariant 1 (sdk zero internal deps) and the bootstrap-bake constraint must be honored.
findings.fix: Extract a single dependency-free `compareSemver` into a leaf util that both can import WITHOUT pulling electron/core — e.g. keep it in app-update (already dep-free) and have cli import from @moxxy/desktop-host/app-update, OR add it to @moxxy/sdk if a dep-free home is acceptable. Lowest-risk: leave as-is but add a code comment cross-referencing the twin and a shared test vector. Do NOT introduce a cycle or make sdk depend on anything.
findings.testStrategy: Shared table-driven test asserting both implementations agree on a fixed vector (until merged).

clusters.title: Replace banned as-unknown-as private-field pokes and unchecked casts (invariant #10)
clusters.oneLine: Remove gratuitous as-unknown-as double-casts on the child-session tools/childLog path, the runner-supervisor test private-field poke, provider config casts (zai/admin), synthAssistantMessage event fabrication, and the browser error-kind launder; introduce typed carriers/generics.
clusters.rationale: Invariant #10 explicitly bans (x as unknown as {f}).f = ...; these are type-safety-only changes with no behavior change. Risk only where the cast hid a real shape mismatch (verify each).

findings.title: Banned private-field poke `(sup as unknown as {child}).child = ...` in runner-supervisor test
findings.problem: Two test lines use the explicitly-banned pattern `(sup as unknown as { child: ChildProcess | null }).child = child.proc` to inject a fake child process by writing a private field. Invariant 10 bans `(x as unknown as {f}).f = ...`. In test scope it's lower-stakes, but it couples the test to the private field name and silently breaks if the field is renamed/refactored (no compile error).
findings.fix: Add a narrow test seam to RunnerSupervisor — either a constructor-injectable spawn factory (preferred, matches the defineX/DI style elsewhere) or a `__setChildForTest(proc)` method guarded by a comment — so the test stops poking the private field. Keeps the banned pattern at zero repo-wide.
findings.testStrategy: Existing supervisor tests pass through the new seam instead of the cast.

findings.title: Two unchecked `as unknown as` casts widen ToolRegistry / EventLogReader on the child-session path
findings.problem: run-child.ts casts `parentSession.tools as unknown as ToolRegistry` (when no allowedTools filter applies) and `childLog as unknown as EventLogReader`. These double-cast through unknown to bridge a structural mismatch between the concrete type and the interface the child expects, defeating the compiler's check at a load-bearing seam (subagent tool exposure). If the underlying shapes drift, the cast hides it.
findings.fix: Make the source types structurally satisfy the target interfaces so a plain assignment (or at most a single `as ToolRegistry`) typechecks: either have ToolRegistry be the interface `parentSession.tools` already implements, or expose a `asReader()` on EventLog returning EventLogReader. If a runtime narrowing is truly needed, add a guard rather than a blind double-cast.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck is the gate; existing subagent tests exercise the path.

findings.title: Gratuitous `as unknown as` double-casts on tools and childLog (invariant #10 smell)
findings.problem: Two `as unknown as` casts are unnecessary. (1) Line 385 `log: childLog as unknown as EventLogReader` — `EventLog implements EventLogReader` (events/log.ts:13), and run-turn.ts:73 passes `log: session.log` to the identical ModeContext field with NO cast, proving direct assignability. (2) Lines 90-93 `parentSession.tools as unknown as ToolRegistry` — core `ToolRegistry` is a superset of the SDK `ToolRegistry` (it adds has/register/unregister) so it is directly assignable; run-turn.ts:71 passes `tools: session.tools` uncast. The `as unknown as` form is the banned double-cast that silently erases any future type drift.
findings.fix: Drop both `as unknown as`: write `log: childLog` directly, and either `tools: toolRegistry` with `buildFilteredToolRegistry` returning the SDK type (it already does) and `parentSession.tools` (no cast) for the unfiltered branch. If a real structural mismatch remains, narrow it to a single `as ToolRegistry` with a comment, never `as unknown as`.
findings.testStrategy: tsc must still pass after removing the casts; the existing run-child.test.ts already exercises both filtered and unfiltered tool paths indirectly.

findings.title: synthAssistantMessage fabricates a MoxxyEvent via `as unknown as MoxxyEvent` double-cast
findings.problem: The turn-end fallback constructs a synthetic assistant_message but escapes the type system with `as unknown as MoxxyEvent`, hand-filling branded EventBase fields (seq:-1, sessionId:'', source). This bypasses the event factory and silently drifts if the MoxxyEvent shape gains a required field the fold reads; the -1 seq and empty sessionId are magic values that could confuse downstream consumers if this synth event ever reaches persistence (it is appended to the log and persisted via tail()).
findings.fix: Use the SDK's event factory (or a typed makeAssistantMessage helper) to build the synthetic event with real ids/seq, eliminating the double cast. If no factory is exposed to the renderer, route the synthetic-event shape through a typed @moxxy/sdk helper so the field set stays in lockstep with MoxxyEvent.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: removing the cast must compile against the real MoxxyEvent. Add a test asserting the committed turn_complete fallback event has a non-negative seq and matching turnId.

findings.title: Weak casts: onInit logger reach-through and createClient config double-cast
findings.problem: Two unchecked casts erode type safety. (1) onInit reads the logger via `(ctx as { logger?: { warn: ... } }).logger` — an ad-hoc structural cast that bypasses the real hook-ctx type; if the lifecycle ctx ever exposes logger under a different name or signature this silently no-ops without a compile error. (2) factory.ts createClient does `new OpenAIProvider({ ...(config as Record<string, unknown>), ... })` casting the typed provider config to a loose record. Both hide contract drift from the type checker.
findings.fix: If PluginInitContext exposes a typed logger, use it directly; otherwise narrow with a small type guard. For createClient, type the config param via the provider's known config type (or the SDK's ProviderClientConfig) instead of an inline Record cast.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck only — removing the cast should compile against the real ctx/config types; no runtime test needed.

findings.title: Unchecked `config as OpenAIProviderConfig` / `as AnthropicProviderConfig` casts
findings.problem: createClient receives `Record<string, unknown>` (ProviderDef.createClient signature, sdk/src/provider.ts:260) and blindly casts it to the typed config with `config as OpenAIProviderConfig` / `config as AnthropicProviderConfig`. There is no validation that e.g. `baseURL`/`defaultModel` are strings; a malformed persisted provider config silently flows into the SDK client. Low impact because the spread immediately overrides name/models and the fields are optional, but it's a typed-surface hole that the shared-factory fix (u102-2) would centralize.
findings.fix: Narrow with a small runtime guard / zod parse of the known optional string fields (apiKey/baseURL/defaultModel) in the shared factory, or at minimum pick the known keys instead of a blanket cast.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test feeding createClient a config with a non-string baseURL and asserting it is rejected/ignored rather than passed through.

findings.title: Error `kind` laundered through `(err as Error & {kind?: string})` casts instead of a typed carrier
findings.problem: Error kinds are attached and read via repeated `(e as Error & { kind?: string }).kind = ...` (types.ts:98, install.ts:29/63) and `(err as Error & { kind?: string }).kind` then `as Err['error']['kind']` (dispatch.ts:83-87). `badParams` hardcodes `kind:'runtime'` while ErrorKind also permits 'init'/'timeout'/'navigation'; nothing constrains the assigned string to the ErrorKind union, so a typo silently flows to the wire as an unchecked cast. Borderline violation of invariant #10 (poking ad-hoc fields onto objects).
findings.fix: Introduce a small `class SidecarError extends Error { constructor(message, readonly kind: ErrorKind) }` carrier; throw it from badParams/install; in dispatch read `err instanceof SidecarError ? err.kind : 'unknown'`. Erases all the casts and makes `kind` type-checked.
findings.testStrategy: Type-only: a SidecarError with an invalid kind fails compilation; dispatch test asserts kind passthrough.

findings.title: Unchecked cast `(p as { supportsReasoning?: boolean })` reads a field absent from the typed contract
findings.problem: `providerSupportsReasoning` defeats the type system with an inline structural cast to read a property the typed `ProviderEntry` does not declare. This is exactly the kind of speculative `as`-cast plumbing the invariants flag: it compiles but can never be true (see u11-2), and it hides the missing contract field instead of surfacing it.
findings.fix: Add `supportsReasoning?: boolean` to `ProviderEntry` in @moxxy/desktop-ipc-contract (and populate it on the runner side) so the access is typed, or delete the function with the dead UI (u11-2).
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: once the field exists on ProviderEntry, the cast is removable and `tsc` still passes.

clusters.title: Make non-exhaustive switches explicit with assertNever
clusters.oneLine: Workflow-builder reducer default:return state and RequirementChecker.targetInfo switch silently swallow new variants; add assertNever so new kinds fail at compile time.
clusters.rationale: Type-safety hardening, no runtime behavior change for existing variants.

findings.title: Workflow builder reducer `default: return state` hides non-exhaustive action handling
findings.problem: The reducer switches on a 20+ variant `action.type` discriminated union and ends with `default: return state`. A new action variant added to the union compiles cleanly and is silently no-op'd at runtime instead of being a type error — exactly the failure mode invariant 10 / clean-state-model work tries to avoid.
findings.fix: Replace `default: return state` with `default: return assertNever(action)` (the same SDK helper proposed in #5). If a genuinely-ignored action is intended, narrow it explicitly so the never-check still covers the rest.
findings.testStrategy: Compile-time: adding a union member without a case fails typecheck. Existing reducer tests unchanged.

findings.title: RequirementChecker.targetInfo switch repeats `registry.list().find(name)->{kind,name,active}` 8x and lacks an assertNever
findings.problem: targetInfo() has 11 cases; 8 of them (provider/transcriber/synthesizer/mode/compactor + plugin/tool/channel/agent/command variants) all do the same shape: look the def up in its registry, return `{kind,name,active}` or null, where `active` is computed from that registry's getActive*/getActiveName. The branches differ only in which registry and which active-name accessor. The switch also has no `default: assertNever(kind)`; if a new RequirementKind is added the function silently returns undefined at runtime for it (TS may catch via noImplicitReturns but the intent isn't enforced).
findings.fix: Two-part: (a) once the def/active registries share a base (#2/#4), give the base a `describe(name): {present:boolean; active:boolean}` so targetInfo collapses to a small kind->registry lookup table; (b) add a private `assertNever(x: never): never` helper in @moxxy/sdk and end every domain switch (this one, the workflows reducer) with `default: return assertNever(kind)` to make non-exhaustiveness a compile error. No assertNever helper exists anywhere in the repo today.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that every RequirementKind has a table entry (drive it off the zod enum). assertNever is type-level (compile-time), no runtime test needed beyond a smoke case.
findings.complexity: 11-case ~50 LOC switch -> table-driven ~20 LOC + shared assertNever

clusters.title: Fix proven resource/timer/listener leaks (low behavioral risk)
clusters.oneLine: Clear leaked timers and listeners: wasm isolator Promise.race 60s setTimeout+abort listener on every successful call; WorkflowCanvas rejectTimer not cleared on unmount; isolator-subprocess/worker broker handler .then without .catch; browser sidecar queue link missing .catch; session-persistence detach final write dropped after closed flag.
clusters.rationale: Each is a confirmed or sweep-confirmed leak/unhandled-rejection with an obvious bounded fix; no protocol or state-shape change.

findings.title: Promise.race timeout leaks a 60s setTimeout (+ abort listener) on every successful wasm call
findings.problem: In createWasmIsolator().run(), the timeout/abort branch of Promise.race is never cleaned up when invoke() wins the race (the normal success path). The setTimeout(timer) for `timeMs` (default 60_000ms) is never cleared, so after a fast successful call the timer keeps the Node event loop alive for up to 60s and fires a rejection on an already-settled promise (silently dropped). The `signal.addEventListener('abort', ...)` listener is registered with { once: true } but is only removed if abort fires — on the success path it leaks on a potentially long-lived/shared AbortSignal, accumulating one dead listener per invocation. The sibling subprocess isolator does this correctly with an explicit `cleanup` Set (isolator-subprocess/src/index.ts:218-252); wasm reinvented the pattern without the cleanup.
findings.fix: Wrap invoke() in a try/finally (or use an explicit settled-guard like subprocess does): capture `timer` and `onAbort` in the outer scope and `clearTimeout(timer); signal.removeEventListener('abort', onAbort)` in a finally after the race settles. Simplest: `try { return await Promise.race([...]) } finally { clearTimeout(timer); signal.removeEventListener('abort', onAbort); }` with timer/onAbort hoisted out of the inner Promise.
findings.testStrategy: Add a vitest that runs a fast echo call, then asserts no pending timer keeps the loop alive (e.g. wrap with fake timers and assert `vi.getTimerCount()===0` after run resolves) and that `signal` has no residual 'abort' listeners (track via a spy AbortController or `signal.eventListeners`-equivalent).

findings.title: rejectTimer setTimeout never cleared on unmount (post-unmount setState)
findings.problem: `flashReject` schedules `rejectTimer.current = setTimeout(() => setReject(null), 2600)` but no effect cleanup clears it on unmount. If the builder closes within 2.6s of a rejected connection, the timer fires `setReject` on an unmounted component (React warning / wasted work). Minor leak.
findings.fix: Add `useEffect(() => () => { if (rejectTimer.current) clearTimeout(rejectTimer.current); }, []);`.
findings.testStrategy: Mount, trigger a cycle-rejected connect, unmount before 2600ms, assert no act() warning and clearTimeout called.

findings.title: Broker-request handler promise (`handleBrokerRequest(...).then(...)`) lacks a .catch in both isolators
findings.problem: Both the subprocess and worker isolators handle capability-broker requests from the sandboxed child via `void handleBrokerRequest(...).then((response) => { ... write/postMessage ... })` with NO `.catch`. Today this is benign because `handleBrokerRequest` (packages/plugin-security/src/broker.ts:117) wraps its entire body in try/catch and ALWAYS resolves with a BrokerResponse — it never rejects. But that is a hidden, undocumented coupling: any future broker op that escapes the try/catch (e.g. a synchronous throw before the await, or a refactor that moves work outside `dispatch`) would become an unhandled rejection in the security-sensitive isolation path. The `.then` callback itself in the subprocess also writes to the child's stdin (line 286) inside a try/catch — that part is fine.
findings.fix: Add a `.catch((err) => { /* report a broker-error response or log */ })` to both `.then` chains so the isolation broker path is defensively rejection-safe regardless of broker internals. Low effort, hardens a trust-boundary code path.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a broker stub that rejects; assert no unhandledRejection and that the sandboxed call still settles (error reply propagated).

findings.title: Browser sidecar request queue has no .catch on its chain link — a sync write() throw poisons all subsequent requests
findings.problem: The sidecar serializes requests by chaining each onto a module-level promise: `queue = queue.then(async () => { const reply = await dispatch(state, req); write(reply); })`. `dispatch` itself never rejects (it wraps every error into an error reply), BUT `write(reply)` calls `process.stdout.write(...)` synchronously, which can THROW (e.g. EPIPE when the parent died mid-write, or a serialization error). That throw rejects this chain link with no `.catch`, which (a) is an unhandled promise rejection and (b) leaves `queue` as a permanently-rejected promise — so every subsequent `queue.then(...)` link rejects immediately and the sidecar silently stops serving all further requests for the lifetime of the process. The browser surface and browser_session tool would both go dark with no error surfaced.
findings.fix: Append `.catch((err) => { try { write({ id: req.id, ok: false, error: { message: errMsg(err), kind: 'runtime' } }); } catch {} })` to the chained link (or wrap the body in try/catch), so a write failure (i) can't poison the serialization queue and (ii) is logged/reported instead of becoming an unhandled rejection.
findings.testStrategy: Unit: stub `process.stdout.write` to throw once, push two requests; assert the second still gets dispatched (queue not poisoned) and no unhandledRejection fires.

findings.title: Session persistence detach() fires a final scheduleIndexWrite AFTER setting this.closed — debounced write may be silently dropped
findings.problem: The unsubscribe returned by `attach()` sets `this.closed = true` then calls `this.scheduleIndexWrite()` to flush a final index row so `lastActivity` reflects close time. But `scheduleIndexWrite` debounces 250ms via setTimeout, and the timer callback calls `this.writeIndex()` which has no `this.closed` guard — so it normally runs. However, in the CLI/runner shutdown path the process can exit within that 250ms window (nothing keeps the loop alive; the timer is `.unref()`d at line 231), so the 'final' index write — the whole point of the flush — is the most likely write to be lost on a fast exit. The event-log JSONL itself is safe (those appends ride the writeQueue), but the sidecar meta (lastActivity / firstPrompt / eventCount) can be stale after a clean shutdown.
findings.fix: On detach, perform a synchronous/awaited final meta write (e.g. an immediate `void this.writeIndex()` bypassing the debounce, or expose an awaitable `flush()` the shutdown sequence can `await`) so the close-time metadata is durable. Alternatively have onShutdown await Session.close → persistence.flush().
findings.testStrategy: Attach, append one event, immediately call detach + simulate exit before 250ms; assert the meta sidecar on disk reflects the latest lastActivity/eventCount.

findings.title: Browser surface input() awaits sidecar calls but the swallowed-error .catch hides hard failures from the user
findings.problem: In the browser surface `input` handler, every user action (navigate/click/key/scroll) does `await browserSidecarCall(...).catch(() => undefined)` then `void tick()`. The `.catch(() => undefined)` is consistent best-effort, but it swallows ALL errors including a hard sidecar crash or SSRF-rejected navigation — the user clicks 'go' on a blocked URL and gets zero feedback (the next poll just re-shows the old frame). The polling tick has a FAIL_GRACE status path, but a one-off input rejection never reaches it. This is an inconsistency with the rest of the surface, which does surface 'Browser unavailable' on the poll path.
findings.fix: On an input-call rejection, `emit({ type: 'status', text: ... })` (at least for navigate, where an SSRF/format rejection is user-meaningful) instead of silently dropping, so a blocked navigation gives feedback.
findings.testStrategy: Stub browserSidecarCall('goto') to reject; assert a status payload is emitted to subscribers.

clusters.title: Evict completedTurns map on observer clients (unbounded growth)
clusters.oneLine: completedTurns grows unbounded on observer clients (never evicted) and leaks an entry when a turn completes but runTurn never consumes it; bound/evict it.
clusters.rationale: Confirmed (u120-1 high). Contained map-eviction fix in remote-session; add a test asserting eviction after turn consume.

findings.title: completedTurns map grows unbounded on observer clients (never evicted)
findings.problem: RunnerServer.handleRunTurn broadcasts `turn.complete` to ALL attached clients (server.ts:302 `this.broadcast`), not just the turn owner. On the client, RemoteSession's TurnComplete handler records every turnId that has no registered stream into `completedTurns` (remote-session.ts:217). An observer client (TUI + desktop deliberately share ONE session) never calls runTurn for those ids, so the entries are NEVER deleted — `completedTurns.delete` only fires when a matching local runTurn registers (remote-session.ts:457). A long-lived shared session therefore leaks one Map entry per turn run by every other client, for the lifetime of the connection.
findings.fix: Only stash into completedTurns when this client actually has (or is about to register) a turn for that id. Options: (a) cap completedTurns with an LRU / size bound and drop oldest; (b) only record turnIds the client itself started (track an `ownedTurns` set populated in runTurn before awaiting the reply, and ignore turn.complete for ids not in it); or (c) evict an entry after a short timeout if no stream claims it. Option (b) is cleanest and matches the documented intent ('turns that completed before their runTurn stream was registered').
findings.testStrategy: Integration test: attach an observer client, drive N turns from a driver client, assert the observer's `completedTurns` size stays bounded (expose a test hook or assert via memory). Also assert a fast local turn still completes (existing fast-turn path unbroken).

findings.title: completedTurns map entry leaks when a turn completes but runTurn never consumes it
findings.problem: When a turn_complete notification arrives for a turnId with no live TurnStream (the fast-turn race the code handles), it is stored in completedTurns and only deleted when a matching runTurn() later consumes it (line 391). If that runTurn is never issued (e.g. the caller's request failed/aborted before priming, or the turn was server-initiated), the entry is never evicted — a slow unbounded leak over a long-lived RemoteSession. Same shape for the per-turn priming via byTurn (finding 4) but that's bounded.
findings.fix: Cap completedTurns to the last N turn ids (insertion-ordered Map drop-oldest like DeliveryDedupeCache) or clear it on disconnect alongside turnStreams; entries older than the active turn window are unreclaimable today.
findings.testStrategy: Drive many turn_complete notifications with no matching runTurn and assert completedTurns size stays bounded; confirm a legit fast-turn still gets its buffered completion.

findings.title: runTurn priming order: completedTurns finish can race ahead of byTurn priming for a fast errored turn
findings.problem: When registering a fast turn, runTurn first replays mirrored events (byTurn, line 454) then, if completedTurns has the id, calls stream.finish (455-458). This is correct ordering. However the byTurn priming relies on the mirror already holding the events for that turnId; if turn.complete arrived (recorded in completedTurns) but the final event(s) were still in flight and not yet ingested at the moment runTurn registers, the stream finishes (done=true) and any event pushed afterward via the live Event handler is dropped by iterate() because it already broke out of the loop. Over a single ordered socket events precede their turn.complete, so in practice the mirror has them — but this invariant is implicit and undocumented, and a reordering of notification handling would silently truncate the last events of a turn.
findings.fix: Document the ordering invariant explicitly, or make TurnStream.finish a deferred 'no more events after the queue drains AND the recorded completion' — i.e. only honor a completedTurns finish after re-draining byTurn at registration time (already done) and guard that the live Event handler's push for an already-finished turn is intentional/no-op. A short test that interleaves a late Event after turn.complete documents the contract.
findings.testStrategy: Drive a fake transport that delivers an Event frame AFTER the turn.complete frame for the same turn and assert the late event is either consistently delivered or consistently dropped per the documented contract.

clusters.title: Small correctness fixes in testing/util helpers
clusters.oneLine: fake-provider byHash miss silently consumes a script slot (cursor++); subagent_completed coerces missing tokensUsed to 0 masking unknown-as-zero.
clusters.rationale: Low-risk localized fixes that improve test fidelity and token accounting.

findings.title: byHash miss silently consumes a script slot (cursor++ even when byHash was the intended source)
findings.problem: `const reply = this.byHash[hash] ?? this.script[this.cursor++];` increments the script cursor on every byHash miss. In a byHash-only setup (empty script), a hash that doesn't match still advances the cursor and falls through to `undefined`, producing a 'no scripted reply' error whose message reports a cursor/script context even though the test author was relying purely on byHash. This makes byHash mismatch failures confusing to diagnose, and in mixed script+byHash setups a single unexpected request silently desyncs all subsequent script-indexed replies.
findings.fix: Branch explicitly: if `byHash` is non-empty and lacks the hash, throw a byHash-specific error listing known hashes, without touching the cursor; only consume `script[this.cursor++]` when falling back to script mode. Optionally guard so a provider constructed with only byHash never advances the script cursor.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test: FakeProvider with byHash but no matching hash throws an error naming byHash and does not advance cursor; mixed script+byHash test asserts cursor only moves on script-served calls.

findings.title: subagent_completed coerces a missing/invalid tokensUsed to 0, masking 'unknown' as 'zero tokens'
findings.problem: On subagent_completed, `block.tokensUsed = typeof payload.tokensUsed === 'number' ? payload.tokensUsed : 0`. The type declares `tokensUsed: number | null` and explicitly documents 'null until completion'. Coercing a missing field to 0 (rather than null) makes a completed agent whose token usage wasn't reported render '0.0k' tokens via formatTokensK instead of omitting the segment (formatTokensK returns null for null, so the caller drops it). This silently misreports usage as zero for any provider/path that completes without a tokensUsed payload.
findings.fix: Use `... ? payload.tokensUsed : null` so an unreported total stays null and the renderer omits the segment instead of showing a false 0/0.0k.
findings.testStrategy: Add a pair-events case: subagent_completed without tokensUsed -> block.tokensUsed === null (and formatTokensK(null)===null omits the row).

clusters.title: Eliminate the O(n^2)/turn block fold in chat-model (desktop + TUI)
clusters.oneLine: Desktop Transcript and TUI ChatView re-fold the ENTIRE event log via pairToolEvents on every committed event; groupToolNodes/buildRenderNodes rebuild the whole render tree per render; countToolCalls/isSettled and the settled-prefix scan recurse the full tree per render. Incrementalize the fold (track a high-water mark, append-only) behind golden tests.
clusters.rationale: Single highest-value perf item; the fold output is load-bearing across every surface so it needs byte-identical golden tests before any incrementalization. Medium risk.

findings.title: Desktop transcript re-folds the ENTIRE event log via pairToolEvents on every committed event
findings.problem: buildRenderNodes() calls pairToolEvents() over the full events array, and the memo key is [events, extensions]. Every committed runner event (tool_call_requested, tool_result, skill_invoked, assistant_message, subagent plugin_event) produces a brand-new events array (state.ts buildSnapshot does rt.log.toArray() whenever log.version changes), so the memo misses and the whole log is re-walked from index 0. pairToolEvents rebuilds callTargets/subagents maps and the entire block tree each time. For a turn that commits k events on a session already holding n events, this is k full O(n) folds = O(k*n); over a session it degrades to O(n^2). The in-memory log grows unbounded across a live session even though disk pagination windows to 50, so n is the whole live conversation, not 50.
findings.fix: Make the fold incremental: keep a persisted folded block tree (the ChunkedBlockLog already supports O(1) append + findLast for outcome patching) and apply only the delta event in applyEvent, OR memoize pairToolEvents on a (log.version, prefixLength) high-water mark and reuse the settled-prefix blocks across calls (only re-fold the open/unsettled tail). The TUI's settledRef pattern is the model — push it down into the fold so the settled prefix is never re-walked.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test an incremental fold against the existing pairToolEvents output for a recorded multi-tool turn (must be byte-identical block tree); add a perf assertion that folding event N+1 touches O(tail) not O(N) blocks.
findings.complexity: O(n) re-fold per committed event (O(n^2)/turn) -> O(1) amortised per event (re-fold only the unsettled tail)

findings.title: TUI ChatView re-folds the whole events array on every non-chunk event (same O(n^2)/turn as desktop)
findings.problem: useEventStream appends each non-chunk event with `setEvents(prev => [...prev, event])`, creating a fresh full-session array. ChatView memoizes pairToolEvents on [events, compactTools], so each tool_call_requested / tool_result / skill_invoked / assistant_message re-walks the entire growing events array from index 0. The code comment explicitly acknowledges the O(n^2) risk for chunks and dodges it by excluding chunks, but every OTHER event still triggers the full re-walk. settledRef caps re-RENDER work but not the fold itself.
findings.fix: Share the incremental-fold solution from finding 1 (the fold lives in @moxxy/chat-model, used by both surfaces). Maintain a folded block log keyed by event seq high-water mark; on a new event, fold just that one event onto the prior tree (append/patch-by-callId via ChunkedBlockLog.findLast).
findings.testStrategy: Golden-output test: incremental fold must equal pairToolEvents(full) after each event for several recorded turns (skill scopes, live-tools, subagents, orphan results); add a counter assertion that a 500-event turn does not perform 500 full walks.
findings.complexity: O(n) per non-chunk event (O(n^2)/turn) -> O(1) amortised per event

findings.title: groupToolNodes + buildRenderNodes rebuild the whole render tree per render; extensions re-sorted each call
findings.problem: Transcript memoizes on [events, extensions], but within buildRenderNodes the extensions array is copied and sorted (`[...extensions].sort(...)`) on every fold, and groupToolNodes walks the entire node list. These are O(n) per render and ride on top of the already-flagged full re-fold (finding 1), compounding the per-event cost. The sort is O(e log e) where e is extension count (small), but the full node walk is O(n) and unmemoised across the streaming ticks that change unrelated state.
findings.fix: Fold incrementally (finding 1) so groupToolNodes/buildRenderNodes operate on the appended tail; keep extensions pre-sorted (they're appended in afterCount order already, so the sort is usually a no-op — skip it when already ordered).
findings.testStrategy: Render-node golden test unchanged; assert extension ordering preserved without the per-call sort when appends are monotonic.
findings.complexity: O(n) tree rebuild per committed event -> O(tail) incremental, sort O(e log e) -> O(1) when already ordered

findings.title: TUI settled-prefix scan walks the full folded block list every render
findings.problem: On every render ChatView walks all blocks counting the leading settled prefix (`for (const b of blocks) { if (isSettled(b)) settledCount++ else break }`), and isSettled recurses into skill-scope children and live-tools calls. This is O(blocks) (with isSettled potentially O(children)) per render, and renders happen at ~30fps during streaming plus on every unrelated state change. Because the settled prefix only ever grows monotonically (settledRef is append-only), the scan can resume from settledRef.current.length instead of restarting at 0 each time.
findings.fix: Start the settled scan at `settledRef.current.length` (the prefix already known settled never un-settles), only advancing forward. Guard the existing shrink-detection (blocks.length < settledRef length) as today.
findings.testStrategy: Existing ChatView tests; add a case asserting the Static items match a from-scratch settled scan after many incremental renders.
findings.complexity: O(blocks) per render -> O(newly-settled) per render (amortised O(1))

findings.title: countToolCalls / isSettled recurse the full block tree; called per render on the full fold
findings.problem: countToolCalls recurses every block (and skill-scope children) and isSettled likewise recurses children/calls. They're invoked on the full folded tree per render. They are cheap individually but ride on top of the per-event full re-fold (findings 1-2), so fixing the fold to be incremental should also make these operate on cached/settled subtrees rather than re-counting the whole conversation each render.
findings.fix: Cache per-scope toolCall counts and a `settled` flag on the block once it closes (skill scopes/live-tools are sealed exactly once), so countToolCalls/isSettled short-circuit on already-settled subtrees.
findings.testStrategy: Equality test of cached vs recomputed counts/settled flags across a turn with nested skill scopes and live-tools.
findings.complexity: O(tree) per render -> O(open subtree) per render once settled subtrees are cached

findings.title: groupToolNodes run-collapsing logic has no unit test
findings.problem: groupToolNodes collapses runs of >=2 consecutive top-level tool-call nodes into one tool-group, keeps a lone tool as its own block, excludes FILE_DIFF tools from grouping, and breaks the run on any non-tool node. This is real branching logic that drives the live transcript (apps/desktop/src/chat/Transcript.tsx:90) yet chatModel.test.ts covers createRuntime/applyEvent/applyAction/buildRenderNodes but never groupToolNodes. A regression in the run/flush boundary (e.g. off-by-one on run.length thresholds or losing the FILE_DIFF exclusion) would silently mis-render burst tool calls.
findings.fix: Add a chatModel.test.ts describe('groupToolNodes') covering: two consecutive non-diff tool blocks collapse to one tool-group with id `toolgroup:<first.id>`; a lone tool stays a block; a Write/Edit (FILE_DIFF_TOOL_NAMES) tool never folds; an assistant/ext node between tools breaks the run into two singletons; trailing run is flushed.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test against synthetic RenderNode arrays; assert kind/ids of the output sequence.

findings.title: topoOrder + byId recompute on every pointer-move during a node drag
findings.problem: `byId` and `order` are memoized on `state.nodes`, but `moveNode` (operations.ts:141) returns a fresh `state.nodes` array on EVERY pointer-move while dragging a card. So both memos invalidate, and `topoOrder` (an O(V+E) DAG longest-path fold) is recomputed dozens of times per second during a drag — even though a position-only move can never change the `needs` topological order. The whole node list also re-renders each frame.
findings.fix: Memoize topoOrder on a derived key that excludes geometry — e.g. a stable signature of (node.id, node.needs) joined — so position drags don't retrigger the fold. byId only needs node ids, so key it on ids too. Alternatively have moveNode keep node identity stable for everything but x/y and memo on a `needs`-only projection.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test topoOrder directly (currently untested) for stability; add a render-count assertion in WorkflowsPanel.test that dragging a node does not recompute order (spy on a memoized selector).
findings.complexity: O(V+E) per mousemove -> O(V+E) only when topology changes

clusters.title: Index EventLog and stop re-folding the whole log per provider call
clusters.oneLine: EventLog.ofType/byTurn are full-array filters with no index; applyLazyTools re-scans the full log every provider call; computeElisionState walks the log up to 4x/call (and sorts aged recalls every call); projectMessages does a linear range scan per event; estimateContextTokens re-folds the whole log per call. Add type/turn indexes + memoized elision state respecting compaction high-water marks (invariant #6).
clusters.rationale: Hot-path SDK/core perf touching the provider-call loop; must preserve compaction high-water-mark semantics and determinism. Medium risk, needs perf + equivalence tests.

findings.title: applyLazyTools re-scans the FULL event log (log.ofType filter) on every provider call when lazy tools are on
findings.problem: loadedToolNames() calls log.ofType('tool_call_requested') — EventLog.ofType does `this.events.filter(...)`, a full O(n) scan — then iterates it. collectProviderStream calls applyLazyTools on every provider call (each iteration of each turn) when ctx.lazyTools is set. So each call pays an O(n) full-log rescan just to recover the small set of load_tool names; over a turn with c calls on an n-event log that is O(c*n), and O(n^2) across a long session. ofType also materialises a new array each call (allocation churn).
findings.fix: Derive loaded-tool names incrementally: maintain a Set updated by an onEvent subscriber for tool_call_requested where name==='load_tool', or have ModeContext expose a cached loadedTools projection invalidated by log version. Alternatively give EventLog a typed index (Map<type, MoxxyEvent[]>) maintained on append so ofType is O(matches) not O(n).
findings.testStrategy: Verify loadedToolNames returns the same set via the incremental path for a log with interleaved load_tool calls; micro-bench applyLazyTools cost is flat as the log grows.
findings.complexity: O(n) full-log filter per provider call (O(n^2)/session) -> O(1) lookup (incremental Set) or O(matches) (typed index)

findings.title: EventLog.ofType / byTurn are full-array filters with no type/turn index
findings.problem: ofType<T>() and byTurn() both do `this.events.filter(...)` — a full O(n) scan and a new array allocation per call. They back several callers (token-accounting via ofType('provider_response'), tool-gating ofType('tool_call_requested'), self-update classify, remote-session byTurn(turnId) on every runTurn priming). None individually is catastrophic, but each is an O(n) scan where a maintained index would be O(matches). byTurn(turnId) in remote-session runs on every runTurn() to prime the stream and rescans the whole mirror.
findings.fix: Maintain a `Map<MoxxyEventType, MoxxyEvent[]>` and a `Map<TurnId, MoxxyEvent[]>` on append()/ingest() so ofType/byTurn are O(matches). Keep filter() as the fallback for cold/seeded logs (rebuild the index lazily on first query). Must preserve the seq-addressed semantics and the rebase/clear resets.
findings.testStrategy: Property test: indexed ofType/byTurn equal the filter result across append, ingest, rebase, clear; ensure index resets on clear() and rebase().
findings.complexity: O(n) per query -> O(matches) per query (one-time O(n) index build, then O(1) maintenance on append)

findings.title: projectMessages does eventInCompactionRange (linear range scan) per event inside the projection loop
findings.problem: projectMessages iterates every event and calls eventInCompactionRange(e.seq, compactions) which linearly scans all compaction ranges for each event — O(events * ranges). Usually ranges is small (1-2), so this is minor, but on a heavily-compacted long session it adds an avoidable inner loop to a per-provider-call O(n) projection (projectMessages already does log.slice() + computeElisionState + a resolvedCallIds prescan + the main loop, all O(n) every call). The whole projection is correct-by-invariant (#4 pure fold), but the per-event range scan is gratuitously quadratic in the number of compactions.
findings.fix: Compactions are non-overlapping ascending seq ranges — sort once and binary-search, or precompute a boundary cursor advanced alongside the event loop (both events and ranges are seq-ordered, so a single merge pass is O(events + ranges)).
findings.testStrategy: Existing projection golden tests must stay byte-identical; add a case with many compaction ranges and assert no behavioural change.
findings.complexity: O(events * ranges) -> O(events + ranges) via merge/binary-search

findings.title: computeElisionState walks the full log up to 4 times per provider call
findings.problem: computeElisionState scans allEvents once for the HWM/flags, again to build toolNameByCall + recall bookkeeping + firstUserPromptSeq, then filters allEvents again for agedRecalls and sorts them. It is invoked by projectMessages (every provider call) AND by estimateContextTokens (context-meter). That is ~3-4 O(n) passes + an O(m log m) sort per call. Inherent to the pure-fold invariant, but the multiple passes are fusable into one, and computeElisionState is recomputed from scratch every call even though its inputs below the HWM are immutable.
findings.fix: Fuse passes 1-3 into a single loop (HWM can be found first, but the second/third can share one iteration). Memoize the sub-HWM portion keyed by (log.version, hwm) since events at/below the HWM never change — only the tail above the HWM and the latest ElisionEvent can shift the result.
findings.testStrategy: computeElisionState output must be identical pre/post fusion across the existing elision test fixtures (recall pinning, conversational stubs, caps).
findings.complexity: ~4 full-log passes + sort per call -> 2 passes (HWM, then fused) and a memoised stable prefix

findings.title: estimateContextTokens re-folds whole log (computeElisionState x ~4 passes) per call
findings.problem: estimateContextTokens does: log.slice() (copy), computeElisionState (which itself does up to 4 full passes over events plus a .filter().sort() of recall results), a compaction-seq pass, and then a full event-char pass that may JSON.stringify every tool_result. It is called once per loop iteration by runCompactionIfNeeded AND runElisionIfNeeded (so ~2x/iteration), and projectMessages independently recomputes computeElisionState again — so the same fold runs 3+ times per iteration with no sharing. plugin-cli/src/context-estimate.ts had to write a whole incremental-cache wrapper specifically because "the SDK's estimateContextTokens re-walks the entire event log (including a JSON.stringify of every tool result) on every call" at ~30Hz. The SDK itself offers no memo/incremental path, so every embedding consumer re-pays O(n) (n = full event log) on every iteration; cost grows with conversation length.
findings.fix: Have runElisionIfNeeded/runCompactionIfNeeded compute computeElisionState once and thread it into estimateContextTokens (add an optional precomputed-state param) and into projection within the same iteration, OR expose the plugin-cli incremental-fold cache from the SDK so every consumer shares one O(appended) estimator. At minimum, accept the elision-state as an arg to avoid the double computeElisionState per iteration.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on a shared eventChars/computeElisionState; assert it walks the log once per iteration, not 3x. Benchmark estimate over a 10k-event log to confirm it stays linear and not multiplied.
findings.complexity: O(n) repeated 3x/iter -> O(n) once/iter (and O(appended) with the incremental cache)

findings.title: computeElisionState sorts aged recalls every call though recall results are rarely present
findings.problem: computeElisionState builds agedRecalls via events.filter(...).sort((a,b)=>b.seq-a.seq) on every call. The filter+sort runs unconditionally even when there are zero aged recall results (the overwhelmingly common case — recall is a relatively rare tool).
