Since events are already appended in seq order, a reverse iteration without an allocation+sort would suffice; the sort is O(k log k) on a freshly-allocated array each call. Combined with finding u122-2 (called 3+x/iteration), this is wasted allocation on the hot path.
findings.fix: Short-circuit when recallResultCallIds.size === 0 (skip the filter/sort/loop entirely). When non-empty, walk events backward (seq-descending order is the append order reversed) accumulating bytes instead of allocating+sorting.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark with a log containing no recalls (assert no sort) vs many recalls; unit test that unpinnedRecallCallIds is identical under both code paths.
findings.complexity: O(n + k log k) -> O(n) with early-out for the k=0 common case

findings.title: applyLazyTools filters the tool list twice into complementary partitions
findings.problem: After computing `hidden = tools.filter(... !always && !loaded)`, it computes `visible = tools.filter(... always || loaded)` — a second full pass over the same array producing the exact complement of `hidden`. Two O(n) scans plus two ALWAYS_ON_TOOLS.has + loaded.has membership checks per tool where one partition pass suffices. Runs once per provider call on every turn when lazyTools is on.
findings.fix: Single partition loop: `const visible: ToolDef[] = []; const hidden: ToolDef[] = []; for (const t of tools) (ALWAYS_ON_TOOLS.has(t.name)||loaded.has(t.name) ? visible : hidden).push(t);` then early-return when hidden.length===0.
findings.testStrategy: Existing token-efficiency.test.ts applyLazyTools cases already assert the visible/hidden split; they pass unchanged after the refactor.

findings.title: surfaceInputParamsSchema serializes the whole message via JSON.stringify on every input frame just to bound size
findings.problem: The .refine on surfaceInputParamsSchema calls JSON.stringify(m) on every surface.input frame to enforce a 1 MB cap. For an interactive terminal surface this runs per keystroke/paste; the full re-serialization of an already-parsed object is redundant work on a hot relay path (every frame is then re-serialized again by the transport send). It also allocates a throwaway string per frame.
findings.fix: Bound the size more cheaply: cap individual string fields (e.g. a `data`/paste field) with z.string().max(...) within the passthrough shape, or only run the stringify-length guard when the message type is the bulk/paste kind. A per-keystroke control frame (single char) need not pay a full JSON.stringify.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark relay of many small input frames before/after; assert oversized frames are still rejected and small frames pass without the stringify.
findings.complexity: O(message size) per frame -> O(1) for common small frames

clusters.title: Extract a generic createJsonFileStore block (mutex + atomic write + zod + quarantine)
clusters.oneLine: VaultStore, WebhookStore, ScheduleStore, provider-admin store and workflows run-store each reimplement mutex + read+zod-validate + atomic write + quarantine. Add a registry/swappable createJsonFileStore<T> to @moxxy/sdk and migrate all five; this also fixes run-store NON-unique tmp collision and centralizes corrupt-file handling.
clusters.rationale: Invariant #5 open-coded N times; consolidation puts atomicity correctness in one tested place. SDK stays dep-free (uses its own fs-utils + mutex + caller schema). Medium risk: behavior-preserving migration of live stores, needs per-store test port + shared block tests.

findings.title: Whole-file JSON store pattern (mutex + read+zod-validate + atomic write + quarantine-on-corruption) reimplemented in 5 plugins
findings.problem: VaultStore, WebhookStore, ScheduleStore, provider-admin store and the workflows run/store all independently implement the same shape: a per-instance createMutex(); a load that readFile + JSON.parse + (zod) schema-validate with a corrupt-file fallback; mutated under mutex.run(); persisted via writeFileAtomic. webhooks and vault additionally implement the corrupt/quarantine aside-rename. This is the repo's explicit invariant #5 (atomic write + per-instance promise-mutex for whole-file read-modify-write stores), open-coded N times, so a fix to one (e.g. quarantine semantics, or the run-store's non-unique tmp bug below) doesn't reach the others.
findings.fix: Add a generic `createJsonFileStore<T>({ path, schema, default, mode?, onCorrupt? })` block to @moxxy/sdk (it already owns writeFileAtomic + createMutex), returning `{ read(), update(fn) }` that does mutexed read-modify-write, zod validation, atomic write, and optional quarantine. Migrate the 5 stores onto it (registry/swappable-block friendly). SDK stays dep-free (uses only its own fs-utils + mutex + the caller-supplied zod schema).
findings.testStrategy: Port each store's existing tests onto the wrapper; add a shared block test for atomic-write durability, mutex serialization (concurrent update() calls), and corrupt-input quarantine.
findings.complexity: 5 bespoke store classes (~60-250 lines each) -> 1 shared block + thin per-domain wrappers

findings.title: 5 whole-file JSON CRUD stores re-implement the same cache+mutex+RMW pattern by hand
findings.problem: ScheduleStore and WebhookStore are nearly line-for-line identical, and provider-admin/vault/workflows-run-store repeat the same skeleton: a `cache: T[] | null`, a `createMutex()` write mutex, `invalidate()`, `list()`/`get(id)`, `create()`, `update(id, patch)`, `delete(id)`, `ensureLoaded()` (read+safeParse, ENOENT->[]), a private `mutate(fn)` that does `mutex.run(ensureLoaded; fn(cache.slice()); persist)`, and `persist()` writing `{version:1, items[]}` via writeFileAtomic. Every copy must independently get invariant 5 right (atomic write + per-instance promise-mutex); a future store that forgets the mutex or the .slice() copy silently regresses atomicity, and no test enforces it cross-store.
findings.fix: Add a generic `JsonCollectionStore<T extends { id: string }>` in @moxxy/sdk (it already exports createMutex, writeFileAtomic, moxxyPath and has zero internal deps so the invariant holds). Constructor takes { file, schema: ZodType<{version:number; items:T[]}>, itemKey?='items' }. It owns cache/mutex/ensureLoaded/mutate/persist and exposes list/get/create(entry)/update(id,patch)/delete(id). Domain stores subclass it (or compose it) to add id/createdAt minting and bespoke methods (syncSkillSchedule, recordFire). Centralizes the atomicity invariant in ONE tested place.
findings.testStrategy: One vitest suite on the generic asserting: concurrent create/update don't clobber (mutex), crash mid-write leaves prior file intact (atomic), ENOENT/corrupt -> empty, mutator gets a fresh copy. Existing per-store tests then cover only domain logic.
findings.complexity: 5 stores x ~80 LOC of boilerplate -> 1 generic ~90 LOC base + ~20-40 LOC per store

findings.title: Three call sites reimplement tmp+rename instead of the canonical writeFileAtomic; run-store uses a NON-unique tmp (concurrent-writer collision)
findings.problem: @moxxy/sdk exports writeFileAtomic (fs-utils.ts L24) explicitly documented as 'the single home for the persist-atomically invariant — every file-state writer should call this instead of hand-rolling tmp+rename'. Three writers ignore it: run-store.ts L48-50 (`const tmp = \`${file}.tmp\``), boot-log.ts L49-53 (writeAtomic sync), cli/update/check.ts L70-72 (sync). The run-store tmp is NOT unique (no pid/uuid), so two concurrent saves to the same runId would clobber each other's temp file — exactly the collision the canonical helper's pid+uuid suffix prevents (and the invariant the comment warns about).
findings.fix: run-store.ts: replace the manual tmp+writeFile+rename with `await writeFileAtomic(file, JSON.stringify(payload))` (also gives the mkdir). For the two sync sites add a `writeFileAtomicSync` to @moxxy/sdk/fs-utils (pid+uuid tmp + renameSync) and call it from boot-log.ts and cli/update/check.ts, retiring both inline copies.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test writeFileAtomicSync (target intact after a mid-write throw; tmp cleaned up). For run-store, a concurrency test issuing two save() calls and asserting both files land intact.
findings.complexity: 3 inline tmp+rename blocks -> 1 async + 1 sync shared helper

findings.title: WorkflowRunStore.save() hand-rolls tmp+rename instead of writeFileAtomic (sibling store.ts uses it)
findings.problem: save() persists a paused-workflow checkpoint via `fs.writeFile(tmp); fs.rename(tmp,file)` with a NON-unique temp name `${file}.tmp`. This duplicates the SDK's `writeFileAtomic` invariant home, and the fixed `.tmp` suffix collides if the same runId is ever re-saved concurrently (the random-UUID temp name in writeFileAtomic exists precisely to avoid that). The sibling file in the SAME package (store.ts) already imports writeFileAtomic, so this is an inconsistent re-roll, not a missing dependency.
findings.fix: Import `writeFileAtomic` (already a @moxxy/sdk re-export, `moxxyPath` is already imported here) and replace the tmp+writeFile+rename block with `await writeFileAtomic(file, JSON.stringify(payload));`. Drop the `node:fs` tmp dance.
findings.testStrategy: Existing run-store save/load roundtrip test still passes; add an assertion that no `*.tmp` file remains after a save (proves rename completed) — writeFileAtomic guarantees cleanup on failure.

findings.title: WorkflowRunStore.save hand-rolls a fixed-suffix .tmp instead of the shared atomic helper
findings.problem: save() writes to a FIXED `${file}.tmp` then rename, bypassing writeFileAtomic. The fixed temp name (no pid+uuid) means two writers targeting the same path collide on the temp file; and on a failed write the stale .tmp is never cleaned up. In practice runId is a fresh ulid per save so same-file collisions are unlikely (low severity), but it duplicates the framework's atomic-write logic with a weaker version instead of calling the single home for the invariant (fs-utils.ts notes the pid+uuid temp name exists precisely so concurrent writers never collide).
findings.fix: Replace the hand-rolled tmp+rename in save() with writeFileAtomic(file, JSON.stringify(payload)) from @moxxy/sdk.
findings.testStrategy: Unit: two concurrent save() calls don't collide on a temp path; a thrown write leaves no orphan .tmp.

findings.title: Corrupt/invalid schedules file is silently reset to empty, dropping all schedules with no signal
findings.problem: ensureLoaded swallows a failed `fileSchema.safeParse` AND any non-ENOENT read error by setting `this.cache = []` with no logger call. A single malformed entry (or a partially-written legacy file) silently discards every user schedule; the next mutation then atomically overwrites the on-disk file with the empty set, making the data loss permanent despite the comment claiming 'The bad file is left in place'. The store has no logger injected, so there is no observability.
findings.fix: On parse failure, preserve the bad file by renaming it to `schedules.json.corrupt-<ts>` before starting fresh, and surface a warn via an optional injected logger. At minimum, do not let the first mutate overwrite a file that failed to parse without backing it up.
findings.testStrategy: Write a malformed JSON file, call list(), assert []; then create() and assert the original bad file was backed up rather than clobbered.

findings.title: configure() persists an unvalidated envVar shape; provider_add validates it but the on-disk schema does not
findings.problem: provider_add's input enforces envVar matches /^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*$/ (index.ts:88-92), but the configure() API takes ProviderConfigurePatch.envVar as a bare optional string (SDK) and writes patch.envVar straight into the stored entry with no shape check. storedProviderSchema in store.ts also declares envVar as plain z.string().optional(). So a channel/runner caller can persist an envVar like 'not a name' that providerApiKeyName() will then return verbatim as the vault key, producing a key name that can never match a real POSIX env var or vault entry. The two entry-points to the same field validate inconsistently.
findings.fix: Centralize the envVar regex (export it from key-name.ts) and validate patch.envVar in configure() before merging; optionally tighten storedProviderSchema.envVar to the same regex so a hand-edited providers.json with a bogus envVar is rejected on read.
findings.testStrategy: Test configure({ envVar: 'bad name' }) rejects; test provider_add already rejects (add if missing).

findings.title: readProvidersConfig swallows ALL read errors (incl. EACCES/EIO) and silently returns empty
findings.problem: The catch-all in readProvidersConfig treats any failure — missing file, malformed JSON, schema mismatch, but ALSO genuine I/O errors like EACCES/EIO — as 'start fresh' and returns { providers: [] }. A transient permission/disk error therefore makes every runtime-registered provider silently vanish at boot (onInit re-reads this), and provider_list would report none. onInit logs a warning only if readProvidersConfig itself throws — but it never does, so the failure is fully invisible. Distinguishing ENOENT (legitimately empty) from other errors would surface real corruption instead of pretending the catalog is empty.
findings.fix: Only treat err.code === 'ENOENT' (and JSON/schema failures) as empty; rethrow other fs errors so onInit's warn path actually reports them, or at minimum log a warning inside the catch when the error is not ENOENT.
findings.testStrategy: Test: chmod the file unreadable (or mock fs.readFile to reject EACCES) and assert readProvidersConfig surfaces/logs rather than silently returning empty; keep the existing ENOENT-and-malformed-return-empty tests green.

clusters.title: Add writeFileAtomicSync to the SDK and retire the three hand-rolled sync tmp+rename copies
clusters.oneLine: boot-log.ts, app-update/resolve.ts and cli/update/check.ts each hand-roll a sync tmp+rename (some with collision-prone pid-only tmp). Add writeFileAtomicSync (pid+uuid tmp + renameSync) to @moxxy/sdk/fs-utils and call it from all three.
clusters.rationale: Closes invariant-#5 gap for sync writers in boot/update paths. Low behavioral risk but new SDK surface, so test the mid-write-throw durability.

findings.title: Three near-identical SYNC tmp+rename atomic-write helpers — no writeFileAtomicSync in the SDK
findings.problem: boot-log.ts (`writeAtomic`), resolve.ts (`writeJsonAtomic`) and cli/check.ts (`writeCache`) each privately re-implement the exact same `mkdirSync; writeFileSync(tmp); renameSync(tmp,target)` pattern with `${p}.tmp-${pid}-${Date.now()}` temp names. They can't call the SDK's `writeFileAtomic` because it is async-only and these run on synchronous boot/update paths. So the root cause is a GAP: the SDK has no `writeFileAtomicSync` sibling, forcing every sync caller to re-roll the invariant (and they pick slightly different temp-name schemes, none using the random UUID the async version uses).
findings.fix: Add `writeFileAtomicSync(target, data, opts?)` to packages/sdk/src/fs-utils.ts mirroring the async one (mkdirSync + unique-UUID tmp + writeFileSync + optional chmodSync + renameSync + rm-on-failure). Replace the three private helpers with imports. Note: cli is bundled — confirm tsup keeps the @moxxy/sdk import inlined (it already bundles sdk).
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test writeFileAtomicSync (mode honored past umask, no tmp residue on success, rollback on rename failure) alongside the existing async fs-utils.test.ts; migrate the three call-sites and run their suites.

findings.title: writeCache hand-rolls tmp+rename instead of the shared atomic-write invariant; pid-only tmp can collide in-process
findings.problem: writeCache reimplements the atomic tmp+rename pattern (invariant 5). The temp name `${file}.tmp-${process.pid}` carries only the pid, not a random component, so two in-process writers to the same cache file collide on the temp path. This is reachable: the TUI fires `refreshCheck(version)` fire-and-forget on every launch while a concurrent `moxxy update` (force) in the same process could also refreshCheck — both racing the same .tmp-<pid> path, where one rename can clobber the other writer's half-written tmp. The shared writeFileAtomic in @moxxy/sdk already solves this with pid+randomUUID temp names.
findings.fix: Replace the manual sync tmp+rename with `await writeFileAtomic(file, JSON.stringify(value, null, 2))` (writeCache's only caller, refreshCheck, is already async), or at minimum append a random suffix to the tmp name. Keep the try/catch best-effort swallow.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: two concurrent refreshCheck() calls against the same cacheFile resolve without throwing and leave a valid (parseable) cache file.

clusters.title: Add the missing per-instance promise-mutex to read-modify-write whole-file stores (invariant #5)
clusters.oneLine: savePreferences (core), plugins-admin config.yaml mutators, desktop-host chat-log NDJSON appendEvents/clearLog, tunnel-settings, config_set, runner handleProviderSetEnabled, channel-auth token file, and skills audit-log removeAuditEntry all do un-mutexed RMW (last-writer-wins / index corruption / TOCTOU). Add the per-instance mutex (and atomic write where missing) following the usage-stats/mcp precedent.
clusters.rationale: Invariant #5 violations on live state files; concurrent fire-and-forget calls lose updates. Medium risk (touches widely-used persistence) and currently untested, so add concurrency tests with each.

findings.title: core preferences.json: savePreferences read-merge-write has no mutex (last-writer-wins clobber)
findings.problem: savePreferences does loadPreferences() (read whole file) -> spread patch -> writeFileAtomic (whole-file write), split by awaits, with NO mutex. Two concurrent savePreferences calls both read the same snapshot and the second clobbers the first's field. This is a real path: the desktop/TUI persist providerName, model, mode, and disabledProviders through this same function, and a /model pick + a provider-disable toggle firing close together each drop the other's change. Atomic write is present (no truncation) but the read-modify-write invariant 5 is violated.
findings.fix: Add a module-level `const writeMutex = createMutex()` (mirroring usage-stats.ts) and run the load->merge->write body of savePreferences inside `writeMutex.run(...)`.
findings.testStrategy: Promise.all two savePreferences({model:'a'}) and savePreferences({mode:'goal'}); assert the persisted file contains BOTH fields, not just the last writer's.

findings.title: savePreferences read-modify-write has NO promise-mutex (invariant #5) and no test — concurrent fire-and-forget calls lose updates
findings.problem: savePreferences() does loadPreferences() -> merge patch -> writeFileAtomic(): a classic read-modify-write over a whole-file JSON store. It uses writeFileAtomic (the atomic-write half of invariant #5) but has NO per-instance promise-mutex serializing the RMW. Every caller invokes it fire-and-forget (`void savePreferences(...)`) from independent surfaces: runner ProviderSetActive (server.ts:396) AND ProviderSetEnabled (:416), CLI picker provider+model and mode (picker-handlers.ts:203,216; run-slash.ts:397,428), Telegram callbacks (callback-handler.ts:195,223). Two overlapping calls (e.g. desktop persists picked provider while the same flow writes disabledProviders, or user changes provider then mode in quick succession) both read the same `current`, then the later writer clobbers the earlier patch's field — a lost update. There is also no test: grep finds no preferences.test.ts and no *.test.ts importing from preferences (only mocks).
findings.fix: Wrap the RMW in a module-level promise-mutex (reuse @moxxy/sdk Mutex) so loadPreferences->merge->write runs serialized; then add packages/core/src/preferences.test.ts. Test cases: (a) round-trips a patch; (b) a second patch merges, does NOT clobber unrelated fields; (c) load tolerates missing/corrupt file -> {}; (d) mode migration via migrateModeName on load; (e) regression: Promise.all of two patches each setting a different field leaves BOTH fields present (proves the mutex serialized the RMW).
findings.testStrategy: Point HOME/preferencesPath at a tmpdir; exercise concurrent Promise.all to assert no lost update.
findings.complexity: before: unguarded RMW -> after: mutex-serialized RMW

findings.title: plugins-admin config.yaml mutators (setPluginEnabled/clearPluginState) have no mutex
findings.problem: setPluginEnabled and clearPluginState read the whole user config.yaml, mutate the plugins map, and writeUserConfig — a read-modify-write of a shared whole-file store with atomic write but NO serialization. Two concurrent enable/disable calls (e.g. /plugins toggling several at once, or CLI + TUI) both read the same baseline and the second clobbers the first's flag. Violates invariant 5. (mcp config-io.ts and provider-admin store.ts implement exactly this with a configMutex/writeMutex; this module simply omits it.)
findings.fix: Introduce a module-level write mutex and wrap the read-modify-write bodies of setPluginEnabled and clearPluginState (mirror mcp/config-io.ts mutateMcpConfig).
findings.testStrategy: Concurrently setPluginEnabled(A,false) and setPluginEnabled(B,false) against the same config path; assert both A and B end disabled in the persisted yaml.

findings.title: chat-log NDJSON store has no mutex — concurrent chat.append/clearLog race the shared in-memory index
findings.problem: appendEvents/clearLog/migrate are module-level functions over two shared module-level maps (writtenIds, lineIndexes) and mutate them across multiple awaits (stat -> appendFile -> stat) with NO serialization. The IPC handlers (chat.append, chat.clearLog) are independently dispatchable. Two concurrent chat.append for one workspace both compute extendFrom from the same pre-append size, both appendFile, and the second extends the LineIndex offsets against a stale base size — corrupting the seek cursors loadSegment relies on. Worse, a chat.append racing chat.clearLog (which rm's the file and deletes both caches) can re-append after the clear or leave the dedup set referencing a now-deleted file. Violates invariant 5 (per-instance promise-mutex for read-modify-write whole-file/derived-state stores).
findings.fix: Add a per-workspace (keyed by resolved file path) promise-mutex serializing appendEvents + clearLog + migrate for a given file, using createMutex from @moxxy/sdk. e.g. a Map<string, Mutex> keyed by fileFor(workspaceId), and run the read-stat-append-extend body and the clear body inside it.
findings.testStrategy: Fire two appendEvents(ws, batch) concurrently and an appendEvents racing clearLog; assert the NDJSON line count and loadSegment cursor offsets stay consistent (every offset points at a parseable line) and clearLog wins or loses cleanly without leaving stale dedup entries.

findings.title: appendEvents has no per-workspace write mutex; concurrent appends can corrupt the in-memory line index
findings.problem: appendEvents does read-stat-append-then-extend-index without a per-file promise-mutex (invariant #5 requires one for read-modify-write of a whole-file/derived store). Two overlapping appendEvents calls for the same workspace can interleave: both read the same idx.size, both appendFile, and both extend lineIndexes.offsets from the same base, producing duplicate/wrong byte offsets and desynced cursors until the size/mtime guard happens to rebuild. The desktop store fires appends with `void ...append(...)` (fire-and-forget, store.ts:325), so overlap is possible if two dispatches land in the same tick. Append ordering itself is also unserialised (appendFile is atomic per-call but ordering between two in-flight appends is not guaranteed).
findings.fix: Wrap appendEvents (and clearLog/migrate) in a per-workspace createMutex() like SessionPersistence.writeQueue does, so stat->append->index-extend is serialised per file. The size/mtime guard then only handles genuine out-of-band edits.
findings.testStrategy: Fire two appendEvents for the same workspace concurrently; assert the resulting file and rebuilt line index match a serial append, and loadSegment cursors stay correct.

findings.title: appendEvents has no per-file mutex — concurrent chat.append races dedup + line index
findings.problem: appendEvents is a read-modify-write on shared module state (the writtenIds Set and the LineIndex) around an `await appendFile`, but nothing serialises concurrent calls for the same workspace. The `chat.append` IPC handler (ipc/chat.ts) just awaits each call independently, so two committed-event batches that arrive overlapping (reconnect replays, WS bridge + Electron both mirroring) interleave: both read `seen` before either writes, so an event the dedup is supposed to drop gets appended twice; and both compute `extendFrom` against the same pre-append `idx.size`, so the second append's line offsets are pushed against a stale base, corrupting the cursor space loadSegment relies on. This violates invariant #5 (per-instance promise-mutex for any read-modify-write whole-file store).
findings.fix: Add a per-file promise-mutex (createMutex from @moxxy/sdk, keyed by fileFor(workspaceId)) and run the whole knownIds→filter→append→index-extend sequence inside it, mirroring DeskStore.mutex. Idempotency-by-id then holds even for concurrent batches and the line index stays consistent.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest: point MOXXY_CHATS_DIR at a tmp dir, fire Promise.all of two appendEvents calls sharing one event id plus disjoint ids, then assert the file has exactly one copy of the shared id and that loadSegment offsets parse every line; repeat with the line-index pre-warmed (call loadSegment first) to exercise the extend path.

findings.title: tunnel-settings writeTunnelSetting read-merge-write has no mutex (single-field clobber)
findings.problem: writeTunnelSetting does readWebSettings() (sync read whole file) -> spread merge -> writeFileAtomic (whole-file write). Atomic write is present, but the read-modify-write of the shared web.json has no mutex, so two concurrent writes both read the same snapshot and the second clobbers the first. web.json currently holds only `tunnel`, so the clobber is benign today (low), but the merge (`...readWebSettings(file)`) signals intent to preserve other fields, which a future second field would silently lose under concurrency. Same class as preferences-2/plugins-admin-3.
findings.fix: Wrap writeTunnelSetting's read-merge-write in a module-level createMutex().run(...).
findings.testStrategy: Concurrent writeTunnelSetting calls; assert the surviving file is well-formed and reflects one of the writes (and, once a second field exists, that the unrelated field is preserved).

findings.title: writeTunnelSetting does read-modify-write of web.json with no promise-mutex (invariant 5)
findings.problem: writeTunnelSetting reads the whole file (readWebSettings), spreads it, and writes it back via writeFileAtomic with no per-instance promise-mutex. Invariant 5 requires every read-modify-write whole-file store to be serialized by a mutex. Two concurrent web_set_tunnel calls (or a tool call racing a future writer) can interleave read/modify/write and lose an update. The atomic tmp+rename only guarantees no torn file, not no lost update.
findings.fix: Add a module-level `const writeMutex = createMutex()` (from @moxxy/sdk) and wrap the read-modify-write body of writeTunnelSetting in `writeMutex.run(async () => { ... })`, mirroring provider-admin/store.ts.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: fire two writeTunnelSetting calls concurrently (Promise.all with distinct names) against a temp file and assert the file ends in a valid, last-writer-wins state with no lost intermediate; without the mutex an interleaved read can clobber.

findings.title: channel-auth token file write is non-atomic and unserialized
findings.problem: writeTokenFile writes the generated channel auth token (0600 secret) with a plain writeFileSync — not atomic. resolveChannelToken read-checks the file then generates+writes on a miss; two channels starting concurrently with no token yet could both generate and the second's write clobbers the first (each connecting client would have been issued a different secret). Practically a one-time seed so the window is narrow (low severity), but it is a secret-bearing state file written non-atomically and with no lock, where the rest of the framework (vault, oauth credential-lock) is careful to serialize+atomic.
findings.fix: Persist via writeFileAtomic (mode 0o600); optionally guard the generate-and-write with O_EXCL ('wx') so a concurrent first-run loses cleanly and re-reads the winner's token (mirrors chat-log.ts migrate and credential-lock acquireFileLock).
findings.testStrategy: Concurrently call resolveChannelToken twice on a fresh dir; assert both observers end up with the SAME persisted token.

findings.title: Channel token file read/write is non-atomic and not mutex-guarded (invariant 5)
findings.problem: writeTokenFile does a plain fs.writeFileSync(file, ...) (channel-auth.ts:89) rather than the repo-standard atomic tmp-write + rename, and readTokenFile/resolveChannelToken/rotateChannelToken are not serialized by a per-file mutex. Invariant 5 requires atomic write (tmp; rename) + per-instance promise-mutex for any whole-file read-modify-write store. resolveChannelToken is a read-then-(maybe)rotate-and-write sequence; two concurrent channel starts (e.g. desktop bridge + mobile) racing on the same token file can each see no file and each generate+write a different token, with the loser's token silently winning, breaking an in-flight pairing. A crash mid-write can also leave a truncated/empty file (readTokenFile returns null on empty -> regenerate, dropping the pairing).
findings.fix: Write via tmp file + fs.renameSync (rename is atomic on same fs) preserving mode 0o600; guard resolve/rotate with the SDK promise-mutex keyed by the absolute file path so concurrent resolves serialize. This is the same pattern every other whole-file store in the repo uses.
findings.testStrategy: Concurrency test: fire N parallel resolveChannelToken calls on a missing file; assert exactly one token is generated and all callers see the same value. Crash-injection test: assert a half-written tmp never clobbers the live file.

findings.title: skills audit-log removeAuditEntry rewrites the NDJSON non-atomically with no lock
findings.problem: removeAuditEntry reads the skills audit NDJSON, filters out a slug, and rewrites the WHOLE file with plain `fs.writeFile` (not atomic) and no mutex — a read-modify-write of a whole-file store. The companion append path (core/src/skills/synthesize.ts:147 `fs.appendFile`) writes concurrently with no shared lock, so a remove racing an append can drop the just-appended line, and a crash mid-rewrite truncates the audit history. This is a CLI-interactive single-shot path (hence low), but it still violates both halves of invariant 5 (non-atomic write + unserialized RMW) on a security-relevant audit log.
findings.fix: Rewrite via writeFileAtomic; if concurrent append is reachable, serialize the audit log's append+rewrite behind a shared mutex (or read-then-rewrite under O_EXCL).
findings.testStrategy: Concurrently append an entry and removeAuditEntry(other-slug); assert the appended entry survives and the file stays valid NDJSON.

findings.title: removeAuditEntry rewrites created.jsonl non-atomically (violates atomic tmp+rename invariant)
findings.problem: `removeAuditEntry` reads the entire audit log, filters, and writes it back with a plain `fs.writeFile(AUDIT_PATH(), ...)` — no tmp-file + rename. Invariant 5 requires atomic write for any read-modify-write whole-file store. A crash/kill (or full disk) mid-write truncates the user's whole agent-skill audit log to a partial JSONL line, losing provenance for every other agent-created skill. The companion writer in core (`appendAudit` in skills/synthesize.ts) appends to the same file, so this is a genuine read-modify-write whole-file store.
findings.fix: Use the shared atomic-write helper (writeFile to a sibling tmp then rename) for the rewrite; reuse @moxxy/sdk fs-utils rather than reinventing.
findings.testStrategy: Test that a simulated write failure (mock rename/writeFile to throw after tmp write) leaves the original created.jsonl intact; test round-trip remove keeps all non-matching slugs.

findings.title: config_set read-modify-write lacks the per-instance promise-mutex required by invariant 5
findings.problem: config_set performs a read (readDoc) -> modify (doc.setIn) -> validate -> writeFileAtomic sequence with no mutex. Invariant 5 mandates atomic write + per-instance promise-mutex for any read-modify-write whole-file store. writeFileAtomic gives torn-write safety but NOT serialization: two concurrent config_set calls (e.g. model fires two tool calls in one turn, or a config_set races config_reload) both read the same on-disk text, each applies only its own edit on top of the stale doc, and the last writer's atomic rename clobbers the other edit (lost update).
findings.fix: Wrap the read-modify-write body of config_set (and the write side of config_init) in a per-target promise-mutex (Map<path, Mutex> or a single plugin-scoped mutex), reusing the SDK mutex helper rather than reinventing.
findings.testStrategy: Fire two config_set handlers concurrently (Promise.all) setting two different dot-paths on the same fresh file; assert both keys survive in the final file.

findings.title: handleProviderSetEnabled does an un-mutexed read-modify-write of preferences.json (TOCTOU)
findings.problem: The fire-and-forget IIFE loads preferences, mutates disabledProviders, then calls savePreferences (which itself re-loads+writes). There is no per-instance promise-mutex (invariant #5) around this read-modify-write whole-file store. A concurrent provider.setActive (line 396, which also fires savePreferences) or a second provider.setEnabled RPC can interleave between the load (line 414) and savePreferences's own internal load/write (preferences.ts:60-64), losing one update (e.g. disabledProviders flips back, or providerName is clobbered). savePreferences merges only the patch it is given, so two concurrent callers each computing `next = {...current, ...patch}` from a stale `current` race.
findings.fix: Serialize all preferences writes behind a single promise-mutex (the SDK's promise-mutex helper) — ideally inside savePreferences in @moxxy/core so every caller is covered, with the merge happening inside the critical section. At minimum, serialize the runner's two prefs-writing handlers on one local mutex so they cannot interleave.
findings.testStrategy: Concurrently fire provider.setEnabled(A,false) and provider.setActive(B) and assert both effects persist in the final preferences.json across many iterations (race repro). Unit-test savePreferences under concurrent calls with distinct patches.

findings.title: config.yaml read-modify-write has no per-instance mutex (invariant 5)
findings.problem: setPluginEnabled() and clearPluginState() each do an unguarded read-modify-write of ~/.moxxy/config.yaml: read whole file, mutate the plugins map, writeFileAtomic. The write is atomic but the RMW window is not serialized. Two concurrent enable_plugin/disable_plugin tool calls (the model can fire tools in parallel), or a tool call racing a CLI `moxxy plugins enable`, interleave their reads and the last writer clobbers the other's change — a disable can silently vanish. Repo invariant 5 mandates an atomic write PLUS a per-instance promise-mutex for any whole-file RMW store; only the atomic-write half is present.
findings.fix: Add a module-level `const configMutex = createMutex()` (from @moxxy/sdk) and wrap the read+modify+write body of setPluginEnabled and clearPluginState in `configMutex.run(async () => { ... })`. Keep writeFileAtomic. This makes per-process RMW atomic; document that cross-process races (CLI vs running session) remain best-effort behind atomic rename.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test (new config.test.ts): point configPath at a tmp file, fire Promise.all of setPluginEnabled('a',false) and setPluginEnabled('b',false), then assert loadDisabledPackageNames() contains BOTH — fails today (one is lost), passes with the mutex.

findings.title: writeUserConfig double-validates via safeParse but discards the (re-validated) parsed data quirk for undefined
findings.problem: writeUserConfig re-runs moxxyConfigSchema.safeParse(config) purely to validate, then stringifies result.data. When clearPluginState passes `{...config, plugins: undefined}`, the schema's `.optional()` keeps the `plugins: undefined` entry in result.data and relies on yaml.stringify dropping undefined keys — correct, but implicit and untested, and the explicit `{ plugins: undefined }` branch is a confusing way to express 'omit the key'. Minor maintainability/clarity issue, not a correctness bug.
findings.fix: Express deletion explicitly: build the next config object and conditionally delete the `plugins` key (`const next = {...config}; if (Object.keys(plugins).length>0) next.plugins = plugins; else delete next.plugins;`) rather than assigning undefined. Optionally drop the redundant second safeParse in writeUserConfig (input already validated on read) or keep it but comment why.

findings.title: desktop skills writeSkill uses non-atomic writeFile (truncation risk on crash)
findings.problem: writeSkill writes the skill markdown body with a plain `writeFile(path, body)` — not the tmp+rename atomic helper. A crash/disk-full mid-write leaves a truncated .md skill file. Each skill is its own file (no shared whole-file read-modify-write, so a mutex is not required), but the atomic-write invariant (5) — 'every file-state writer should call writeFileAtomic instead of hand-rolling or writing in place' — is violated. The framework already exports writeFileAtomic which this module bypasses.
findings.fix: Replace the plain writeFile in writeSkill with writeFileAtomic from @moxxy/sdk (mkdir is handled inside the helper, so ensureDir() before it becomes redundant).
findings.testStrategy: Unit: monkeypatch writeFile to throw after partial write; assert the previous skill file content survives intact (no truncation).

findings.title: deleteSkill uses a dynamic import for unlink while the module already statically imports node:fs/promises
findings.problem: deleteSkill does `const { unlink } = await import('node:fs/promises')` even though the file already statically imports readFile/readdir/writeFile from the same module at the top (line 8). Inconsistent and adds a needless per-call dynamic-import round-trip; no reason for lazy loading a built-in already pulled in.
findings.fix: Add `unlink` to the top-level static import and remove the inline dynamic import.
findings.testStrategy: Existing skills.test.ts round-trip; add a delete case to cover the path.

clusters.title: Extract generic ActiveDefRegistry / DefRegistry base for the 8 copy-paste registries
clusters.oneLine: Five one-active-def registries (compactors/cache-strategies/tunnel-providers/view-renderers/workflow-executors) are byte-for-byte copies; three flat name->def registries (agents/channels/surfaces) duplicate register/unregister/list/get/has. Extract a generic base (string-keyed, runtime-populated per invariant #11) and add the missing getActiveName/has/clearActive + workflow-executor test.
clusters.rationale: Pure structural dedup that centralizes registry correctness; low risk but touches core registry plumbing so port the existing registry tests and add the missing one.

findings.title: 5 'one active def, no instance' registries are byte-for-byte copies; no shared base
findings.problem: CompactorRegistry, CacheStrategyRegistry, ViewRendererRegistry, TunnelProviderRegistry and WorkflowExecutorRegistry each hand-roll the identical class: a `Map<string,TDef>` + `active: string|null`, register (throw-on-dup, auto-activate first), replace, unregister (clear active slot), list, setActive (throw-if-missing), getActive():TDef|null. The doc comments literally say 'Mirrors {@link CompactorRegistry}' / 'Mirrors {@link CacheStrategyRegistry}'. The repo already has `ActiveBackendRegistry<TDef,TInstance>` (active-backend-registry.ts) for the *instance-building* family (synthesizers/transcribers/embedders) but the simpler def-only family was never given its sibling.
findings.fix: Extract `ActiveDefRegistry<TDef extends { name: string }>` into core/registries (or fold as a no-build variant of ActiveBackendRegistry). Constructor takes { noun, autoAdoptFirst?=true }. Each of the 5 becomes `class CompactorRegistry extends ActiveDefRegistry<CompactorDef> { constructor(){ super({noun:'Compactor'}) } }`. ModeRegistry (which adds change-listeners + legacy-name migration) and ProviderRegistry (disabled-set + instances) stay bespoke or extend with overrides.
findings.testStrategy: Single shared vitest for the base (dup-throw, auto-activate-first, unregister-clears-active, setActive-throws-unknown, getActive-null-when-empty). Keep the 5 existing per-registry tests as thin sanity checks.
findings.complexity: 5 x ~48 LOC -> 1 base ~55 LOC + 5 x ~4 LOC subclasses

findings.title: 3 'flat name->def, no active' registries duplicate register/unregister/list/get/has
findings.problem: AgentRegistry, ChannelRegistryImpl and SurfaceRegistryImpl each hand-roll a `Map<key,TDef>` with register (throw-on-dup), unregister, list, get, has. They differ only in noun and key field (`name` vs `kind`). surfaces.ts:7 comment even says 'Mirrors {@link ChannelRegistryImpl}'. ChannelRegistry adds listWithAvailability; the base would still cover the other four methods.
findings.fix: Extract `DefMapRegistry<TDef, K extends string = string>` parameterized by { noun, keyOf:(d)=>K } providing register/unregister/list/get/has. The three registries extend it; ChannelRegistryImpl adds listWithAvailability on top. Lower priority than #2 because these are smaller and one (surfaces) keys on `kind` not `name`.
findings.testStrategy: Shared base test (dup-throw, get/has/list, unregister). Per-registry tests keep only the extras (channel availability).
findings.complexity: 3 x ~25 LOC -> 1 base ~30 LOC + 3 x ~6 LOC subclasses

findings.title: Three def-only registries are byte-identical copy-paste; extract a generic ActiveDefRegistry<TDef>
findings.problem: tunnel-providers.ts, view-renderers.ts and workflow-executors.ts are line-for-line identical apart from the type parameter, the field name, and the noun in error strings. They each reimplement the exact same Map + active-slot lifecycle (register throws-on-dup, replace overwrites, unregister clears active when it was active, list, setActive throws-on-missing, getActive returns null). cache-strategies.ts and compactors.ts in the same directory are the same shape again. This is the classic 'reinvented shared block' the repo already solved for instance-building backends via ActiveBackendRegistry (active-backend-registry.ts). Five+ copies means any fix or behavior change (e.g. adding clearActive/getActiveName, or changing unregister policy) must be made in five places and is already drifting (see u43-3).
findings.fix: Add a generic `ActiveDefRegistry<TDef extends { name: string }>` (def-only sibling of ActiveBackendRegistry) in registries/ providing register/replace/unregister/list/setActive/getActive(): TDef|null with the noun passed via constructor. Replace each of the three classes with `class ViewRendererRegistry extends ActiveDefRegistry<ViewRendererDef> { constructor(){ super('View renderer'); } }` etc. cache-strategies.ts/compactors.ts (outside this unit) can adopt it too. Preserve the exact `getActive() => null` and throw-message semantics so callers and existing tests pass unchanged.
findings.testStrategy: Keep the existing view-renderers/tunnel-providers tests and add the missing workflow-executors test (u43-2); they exercise register-dup-throws, auto-activate-first, unregister-clears-active, setActive-missing-throws against the shared base. Extend semantics.test.ts to include the new base once via a parameterized table.

findings.title: Def-only registries lack getActiveName/has/clearActive that the parallel ActiveBackendRegistry exposes
findings.problem: ActiveBackendRegistry (the instance-building sibling these three docstrings claim to 'mirror') exposes getActiveName(), has(), clearActive() and tryGetActive(). The three def-only registries expose none of these. The asymmetry is the copy-paste drift symptom: the def-only family and the instance family were forked and have diverged in surface. Not a live bug today (grep shows no caller needs getActiveName/has/clearActive on view/tunnel/workflow registries: `getActiveName` is referenced only on ProviderRegistry in semantics.test.ts), but it makes the two families gratuitously inconsistent and is the kind of gap that bites when a future caller assumes parity.
findings.fix: Resolve via u43-1: a single ActiveDefRegistry base would give all three the same surface as ActiveBackendRegistry by construction. If u43-1 is deferred, leave as-is (no functional impact) rather than hand-adding methods to three files.
findings.testStrategy: Covered by u43-1's shared-base suite.

findings.title: WorkflowExecutorRegistry has no unit test though all three sibling registries do
findings.problem: view-renderers.ts, tunnel-providers.ts and transcribers.ts each ship a co-located .test.ts, and semantics.test.ts regression-tests the active-slot invariants for Provider/Mode/Compactor/Skill. WorkflowExecutorRegistry — which gates whether a session can run any workflow (plugin-workflows registers the only `dag` executor and core does NOT seed a default, so getActive() returning null wrongly would silently disable workflows) — has zero coverage. The auto-activate-first and unregister-clears-active behaviors are load-bearing yet untested for this registry.
findings.fix: Add packages/core/src/registries/workflow-executors.test.ts mirroring view-renderers.test.ts: register-throws-on-dup, getActive() returns first after register, getActive() returns null after unregistering the active one, setActive('missing') throws, replace() overwrites. If u43-1 lands, fold into the shared base's parameterized suite instead.
findings.testStrategy: vitest run packages/core; assert the five behaviors above.

findings.title: Alias lookup, byName index, availability fallback and cache-strategy active-slot have no unit tests
findings.problem: semantics.test.ts covers Provider/Mode/Compactor/Skill (same-name replace only). Untested: CommandRegistry alias get/has/unregister cleanup and replace alias-rewrite; SkillRegistryImpl.byName / filterByTriggers / replaceAll atomic swap; ChannelRegistryImpl.listWithAvailability error fallback; CacheStrategyRegistry auto-activate-first + unregister-clears-active. These are the registries with the actual bugs flagged in u42-1, u42-2 and the most logic.
findings.fix: Add focused unit tests for: command alias round-trip + collision; skill byName/filterByTriggers/replaceAll; channel availability error-to-{ok:false} mapping; cache-strategy auto-activate + unregister-clears. These tests double as the regression guards for u42-1/u42-2.
findings.testStrategy: See fix — the tests are the deliverable.

clusters.title: Extract defineOpenAICompatProvider and delete the per-vendor copy-paste
clusters.oneLine: zai/xai/google/local each hand-roll the same createClient (config-forcing new OpenAIProvider) + validateOpenAICompatKey; provider-admin/factory.ts already generalizes it. Add defineOpenAICompatProvider to plugin-provider-openai and delegate all five (incl. provider-admin) to one path.
clusters.rationale: Invariant-safe (vendors already depend on plugin-provider-openai). Low risk; per-vendor test asserting ProviderDef.name == vendor slug, not openai.

findings.lines: zai 27-45, xai 23-39, google 22-43, local createClient; admin factory 18-37
findings.title: OpenAI-compatible provider shim (createClient + validateKey) copy-pasted across 4 built-in vendor plugins + provider-admin
findings.problem: zai, xai, google and local each hand-roll the identical defineProvider whose createClient spreads `config` into a `new OpenAIProvider({...cfg, name, baseURL, defaultModel, models})` (forcing the vendor slug so usage stats/provider events/error context don't misattribute to 'openai'), plus `validateKey: (key) => validateOpenAICompatKey(key, { baseURL })`. plugin-provider-admin/src/factory.ts:buildProviderDef ALREADY abstracts this exact body for runtime-registered vendors — so the same OpenAI-compat construction logic exists in 5 places. The per-vendor files differ only in slug/baseURL/defaultModel/models/auth-hint.
findings.fix: Add a `defineOpenAICompatProvider({ name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, auth, validate? })` factory to @moxxy/plugin-provider-openai (the package that owns OpenAIProvider + validateOpenAICompatKey — invariant-safe: vendor plugins already depend on it). Replace each vendor's createClient/validateKey boilerplate with one call, and refactor provider-admin/factory.ts:buildProviderDef to delegate to the same factory so there is one OpenAI-compat construction path. z.ai's Anthropic-compat 'plan' provider stays bespoke (different base class).
findings.testStrategy: Per-vendor unit test asserting the built ProviderDef.name equals the vendor slug (not 'openai'), baseURL/defaultModel default correctly, and validateKey probes the vendor baseURL; reuse provider-admin/factory existing tests for the shared factory.
findings.complexity: 5 copies of ~15-line createClient/validateKey body -> 1 factory + 5 one-liner call sites

findings.title: Built-in OpenAI-compatible providers hand-copy the createClient cfg-forcing block factory.ts already generalizes
findings.problem: xai, zai (openai mode) and local each write the same defineProvider whose createClient does `const cfg = config as OpenAIProviderConfig; return new OpenAIProvider({...cfg, name, baseURL: cfg.baseURL ?? VENDOR_URL, defaultModel: cfg.defaultModel ?? VENDOR_MODEL, models})` plus `validateKey: (k)=>validateOpenAICompatKey(k,{baseURL})`. plugin-provider-admin/src/factory.ts:buildProviderDef already does exactly this for runtime-registered vendors, so the framework has two parallel ways to wire an OpenAI-compat vendor and the built-ins don't use the shared one.
findings.fix: Export a `defineOpenAICompatProvider({ name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, auth, validate?=true, apiKeyFallback? })` helper from @moxxy/plugin-provider-openai. xai/zai/local then each become a single declarative call; factory.ts:buildProviderDef delegates to it too, so runtime-added and built-in vendors share one codepath (and one `config as OpenAIProviderConfig` cast site instead of three).
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the helper once (name override applied, baseURL/defaultModel defaulting, validateKey wired). Existing xai/zai/local index tests assert only the vendor constants.
findings.complexity: 3 x ~20 LOC createClient blocks + 1 factory branch -> 1 helper + 4 declarative call sites

findings.title: OpenAI-compat provider wrapper is copy-pasted verbatim across google/xai/zai — extract a shared factory
findings.problem: googleProviderDef, xaiProviderDef, and zaiProviderDef are byte-for-byte identical modulo (name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, hint): same `config as OpenAIProviderConfig` cast, same `new OpenAIProvider({ ...cfg, name, baseURL: cfg.baseURL ?? X, defaultModel: cfg.defaultModel ?? Y, models })`, same validateOpenAICompatKey wiring. Three (soon N) copies means a bug or improvement (e.g. the capability-gating fix above, or a defaultModel/validateKey tweak) must be hand-applied N times and can silently diverge. This is the exact 'reinventing a shared helper' / 'copy-paste' pattern the audit flags; moxxy convention favors a single parameterized factory.
findings.fix: Add a `defineOpenAICompatProvider({ name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, hint })` helper (export from @moxxy/plugin-provider-openai, which all three already depend on) that returns the defineProvider spec; have google/xai/zai each call it with their constants. Removes ~18 duplicated lines per package and centralizes the cast + base-URL fallback logic.
findings.testStrategy: Existing per-plugin index.test.ts (name stamping, catalog forced, apiKey auth) continues to pass unchanged after refactor; add one helper test asserting baseURL/defaultModel fallback semantics.

findings.title: OpenAI-compat provider def is copy-paste of xai/google/openai (should be a shared factory)
findings.problem: zaiProviderDef is byte-for-byte identical to xaiProviderDef (packages/plugin-provider-xai/src/index.ts:20-38) and googleProviderDef modulo (name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, hint): same `config as OpenAIProviderConfig` cast, same `new OpenAIProvider({ ...cfg, name, baseURL: cfg.baseURL ?? X, defaultModel: cfg.defaultModel ?? Y, models })` body, same validateOpenAICompatKey wiring. Any improvement (e.g. the supportsReasoning catalog fix above, or a defaultModel/validateKey tweak) must be hand-applied N times and can silently diverge. This is the exact 'reinventing a shared helper / copy-paste' pattern the audit flags. (Already noted from the xai/google side in raw unit-96.)
findings.fix: Extract a `defineOpenAICompatProvider({ name, baseURL, defaultModel, models, hint })` helper (and an Anthropic-compat sibling for the coding-plan path) in @moxxy/plugin-provider-openai / -anthropic (or a shared provider-utils), and have zai/xai/google call it. Keeps the per-vendor file to a few constants.
findings.testStrategy: Reuse existing index.test.ts assertions (provider names, model catalog, vendor-slug stamping) against the factory output; add a parametrized test over all vendors.

clusters.title: Add a shared one-shot provider-stream collector and retire the 5 hand-rolled copies
clusters.oneLine: router.ts / synthesize-draft.ts / consolidate.ts / compactor index.ts / memory draft.ts each inline a for-await text_delta collector (the SDK has collectProviderStream). Several copies also drop graceful-abort partials or stringify-throw guarding. Centralize one helper.
clusters.rationale: Consistency + several latent bugs (partial text returned as complete summary, throwing tool input aborting compaction). Medium risk: changes summary/compaction output paths, needs equivalence tests.

findings.title: Five copies of the bare-provider 'for await text_delta' collector — no shared one-shot stream helper
findings.problem: Five call-sites each hand-write the same loop to drive a one-shot `provider.stream()` and accumulate text: `for await (const event of provider.stream(...)) { if text_delta acc += delta; if error ... }`. They can't use `collectProviderStream`/`runSingleShotTurn` (those need a full ModeContext with cacheStrategy/hooks/emit/log) because these sites only hold a bare `LLMProvider`. The duplication has DIVERGED dangerously: router.ts (core) ignores the `error` event entirely and silently returns null on provider failure; consolidate.ts and synthesize-draft.ts THROW; compactor-summarize returns null; some pass `signal`, some omit it. No canonical helper exists for the bare-provider case.
findings.fix: Add a small `collectText(provider, req): Promise<{text, stopReason, error}>` helper to packages/sdk/src/provider-utils.ts (bare LLMProvider, no ModeContext) that runs the for-await loop once with consistent error semantics, then import it in all five sites. Standardize error handling (return a typed error rather than divergent throw/swallow). This fixes router.ts's silent error-swallow as a side effect.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test collectText against a fake provider emitting text_delta + error + message_end; migrate each site and re-run their existing suites (skill router, memory consolidate, compactor, workflow_create draft).
findings.complexity: 5x ~6-line loops -> 5x one call + 1 new ~12-line SDK helper

findings.title: contextChars/safeJsonLen reimplement SDK's private eventChars and already diverge on attachments
findings.problem: contextChars (lines 175-190) and safeJsonLen (192-198) are a near-verbatim copy of the SDK's private eventChars/safeJsonLen in packages/sdk/src/compactor-helpers.ts:59-92, which estimateContextTokens uses to size the projected context. The compactor's tokensSaved math (line 102-103) is explicitly meant to 'mirror the SDK estimate's per-event costing', but the copy has ALREADY drifted: SDK eventChars counts inlined user_prompt file/stdin attachment chars (compactor-helpers.ts:68-70) whereas contextChars (line 177-178) only counts e.text.length. So when a compacted prefix contains an attachment-heavy user_prompt, tokensSaved undercounts the real context freed, and any future change to the SDK's per-event costing silently desyncs the two.
findings.fix: Export a per-event context-cost helper from @moxxy/sdk (e.g. `eventContextChars(e)`), or expose it via estimateContextTokens' internals, and have contextChars call it so the compactor's savings accounting cannot drift from what projectMessagesFromLog actually sends. At minimum, mirror the attachment-counting branch.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test compacting a user_prompt with a file attachment and assert tokensSaved reflects text+attachment chars; add a shared snapshot test that contextChars and the SDK eventChars agree for each event type.

findings.title: describeEvent uses raw JSON.stringify on unknown tool input; one throwing input aborts the whole compaction
findings.problem: describeEvent's tool_call_requested branch calls `JSON.stringify(e.input)` directly on `input: unknown` (events.ts ToolCallRequestedEvent.input is typed unknown), while the sibling contextChars uses the throw-safe safeJsonLen for the identical field. A single tool call whose recorded input contains a circular reference or BigInt makes JSON.stringify throw inside compact(); runCompactionIfNeeded catches it (compactor-helpers.ts:204) and turns compaction into a permanent no-op for that log, so context then grows unbounded with no successful compaction ever emitted.
findings.fix: Replace the inline JSON.stringify in describeEvent with safeJsonLen-style guarded stringify (reuse the existing safeJsonLen or a tryStringify helper) so a malformed tool input degrades that one digest line instead of killing the whole compaction.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test compact() with a tool_call_requested whose input contains a circular reference; assert a valid CompactionEvent is still produced and the offending line is rendered safely.

findings.title: providerSummary returns partial streamed text as a complete summary on graceful abort
findings.problem: providerSummary accumulates text_delta and returns the trimmed buffer when the async-iterator ends; it never observes message_end. If ctx.signal aborts and the provider implementation ends its stream cleanly (returns from the generator rather than throwing on abort), the loop exits normally and a partial, possibly mid-sentence summary is returned and emitted as the authoritative compaction summary — a lossy/incorrect summary replaces real turns. The catch only saves the throw-on-abort providers.
findings.fix: After the loop, if ctx.signal?.aborted return null (so callers fall back to the labeled digest), and/or only accept the summary when a message_end with a non-aborted stopReason was observed.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a fake provider that yields one delta then ends after the AbortController is aborted; assert providerSummary returns null and compact() uses fallbackDigest.

clusters.title: Move the copy-pasted frontmatter/markdown mini-parsers into a shared home + test them
clusters.oneLine: core skills/parse.ts frontmatter parser is copied verbatim in plugin-memory; the chat-model hand-rolled markdown block+inline parser (rendered on every surface) has zero tests and minor bugs (ordered-list start discarded, escaped pipes in table cells, stripInline/tokenizeInline drift).
clusters.rationale: Parser logic that renders assistant output everywhere; consolidate + add the missing tests, fixing the small correctness bugs in the same PR. Low-medium risk.

findings.title: Frontmatter mini-parser is copy-pasted (plugin-memory mirrors it verbatim); belongs in zero-dep @moxxy/sdk
findings.problem: parse.ts (parseSkillFile/parseFrontmatter/parseScalar/splitArray) is duplicated almost line-for-line in packages/plugin-memory/src/parse.ts, which even carries the comment 'Local copy so the plugin stays leaf-only ... Mirrors packages/core/src/skills/parse.ts.' Two divergent copies of the same YAML-subset parser means bug fixes (e.g. plugin-memory's parseScalar splits inline arrays on bare ',' and loses nested/quoted handling that core's splitArray gets right) land in one and not the other.
findings.fix: Move the parser into @moxxy/sdk (zero internal deps — invariant #1, e.g. a new subpath alongside fs-utils) and have core/skills/parse.ts and plugin-memory/parse.ts both re-export from it. Eliminates the divergence and the maintenance hazard.
findings.testStrategy: Promote the existing parse.test.ts cases to the shared SDK module; ensure both core and plugin-memory import it and their existing tests pass unchanged.

findings.title: Entire hand-rolled markdown parser (block + inline) has zero unit tests
findings.problem: parseBlocks, normalizeInlineTables, isTableSeparator, parseTableCells/Aligns, tokenizeInline and stripInline are non-trivial parsing algorithms (fence handling, GFM table detection/normalization, list runs, paragraph gathering, combined alternation regex with multiple capture groups). They are critical rendering logic consumed by plugin-cli's TUI Markdown/Table components, yet there is no .test.ts anywhere in the repo (verified: `grep -rln parseBlocks|tokenizeInline|normalizeInlineTables|isTableSeparator --include=*.test.ts packages` returns nothing; only log.test.ts and pair-events.test.ts exist in chat-model). Any regression in the alternation order, table separator regex, or paragraph break conditions ships silently.
findings.fix: Add packages/chat-model/src/markdown/parse-blocks.test.ts and inline.test.ts covering: fenced code (open + unclosed), GFM table with/without separator, inline-glued single-line table normalization, header/separator/row column-count mismatch, ordered vs bullet list runs, heading levels 1-6 + clamping, paragraph join across lines, blank lines; and for inline: code/bold/italic/link precedence, stripInline vs tokenizeInline visible-char parity, asterisk/backtick edge cases.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot the Block[] / InlineTok[] outputs for a fixture corpus of model-emitted markdown; assert stripInline(x).length parity with the rendered glyph count for table-width cases.

findings.title: Ordered-list start number is discarded — list renumbers from 1
findings.problem: The list branch captures only the item text via `/^\s*\d+\.\s+(.*)$/` and stores `items: string[]` with no start index. A list authored as `3. a\n4. b` loses its starting ordinal; the TUI renderer (Markdown.tsx) renumbers from `${i + 1}`, so it always renders `1. 2.`. The Block type itself cannot represent a non-1 start. Minor but a real fidelity loss for numbered lists.
findings.fix: Capture the first item's numeric value and add an optional `start?: number` to the list Block (types.ts), defaulting to 1; renderer uses `start + i`.
findings.testStrategy: Parse `3. a\n4. b` and assert start === 3; renderer test asserts first bullet renders `3.`.

findings.title: stripInline / tokenizeInline 'mirror exactly' claim is only approximately true
findings.problem: The stripInline docstring asserts it strips exactly the spans tokenizeInline treats as markup. stripInline applies four independent sequential regex passes (code, then bold, then italic, then link) over the whole string, whereas tokenizeInline is a single left-to-right combined alternation. For adversarial inputs with overlapping markers (e.g. nested/odd asterisks, a `*` inside backticks already consumed) the two can diverge in which characters are removed. In practice this only feeds column-width math in table.tsx (visualLen/padCell) so the impact is at most a 1-2 char width miscalc, but the 'exactly' guarantee in the comment overstates the actual coupling and invites future bugs if a caller relies on strict parity.
findings.fix: Either (a) derive stripInline by running tokenizeInline and concatenating the rendered text of each token (single source of truth, guaranteed parity), or (b) soften the docstring to 'approximately mirrors, sufficient for width estimation'. Option (a) erases the duplication.
findings.testStrategy: Property test: for random markdown strings, assert stripInline(s) === tokenizeInline(s).map(renderedText).join('') under the chosen invariant.

findings.title: Table cell parser does not honor escaped pipes (\|) — splits cell content
findings.problem: parseTableCells splits on every literal `|`. GFM permits an escaped pipe `\|` inside a cell. A cell value containing `\|` is split into two cells, shifting all subsequent columns and (via the clamp to header length at line 41) silently dropping trailing content. Low impact since models rarely emit escaped pipes, but it is an unhandled GFM case and a silent data-shift rather than a graceful fallback.
findings.fix: Split on an unescaped-pipe regex (e.g. /(?<!\\)\|/) then unescape `\|`->`|` per cell, mirroring GFM. Keep it small and shared with the separator/align splitters.
findings.testStrategy: Parse `| a \| b | c |` with a matching separator and assert the row yields two cells [`a | b`, `c`].

findings.title: chat-model markdown block/inline parser untested (renders assistant output on every surface)
findings.problem: The chat-model markdown subsystem (parse-blocks.ts, inline.ts) parses every assistant message into the block/inline model rendered on TUI/desktop/web/telegram. The high-value folding logic around it (pair-events.ts, log.ts) is exhaustively tested, but the markdown parser itself has no co-located test. Parser bugs (unterminated code fences, nested emphasis, list/heading edge cases) degrade output rendering across all channels. Lower risk than the surface/preferences gaps because failures are cosmetic, but it is a sizeable untested pure function.
findings.fix: Add packages/chat-model/src/markdown/parse-blocks.test.ts with table-driven cases: paragraphs, headings, fenced + unterminated code blocks, lists, blockquotes, and inline.test.ts for bold/italic/code/link/escape edge cases. Pure-function snapshot/assert.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven pure-function tests; no I/O.

clusters.title: Split Node builtins out of the @moxxy/sdk main barrel into a ./server subpath + widen dep-cruiser
clusters.oneLine: The SDK main barrel statically re-exports node:child_process/fs/crypto/http, held safe today only by import-type discipline; dep-cruiser does not even cruise the desktop renderer or mobile-poc. Move Node-only exports to @moxxy/sdk/server and widen the dep-cruiser scope to catch RN/browser boundary breaches.
clusters.rationale: Protects the browser/RN boundary (invariant-adjacent). Medium care: must not break existing deep imports; coordinate with consumers. Adds CI coverage.

findings.title: @moxxy/sdk main barrel statically re-exports node:child_process/fs/crypto/http into the browser/RN surface
findings.problem: @moxxy/sdk advertises itself as a zero-dep typed public surface (keyword 'zero-deps', peerDeps only zod) and ships a dedicated './tool-display' subpath precisely so browser/RN bundles can pull rich-result types without Node builtins. But the MAIN barrel (src/index.ts) statically re-exports runtime values from four Node-only modules: spawnCliTunnel/isCliTunnelAvailable (tunnel.ts -> node:child_process), writeFileAtomic/moxxyHome/moxxyPath (fs-utils.ts -> node:fs/promises, node:os), readRequestBody/bearerTokenMatches (http-utils.ts -> node:crypto, node:http), and resolveChannelToken/bearerGuard/rotateChannelToken (channel-auth.ts -> node:crypto, node:fs, node:path). Any value import `import { X } from '@moxxy/sdk'` from a browser/RN bundle drags the entire node-builtin chain into the bundle (Metro for RN cannot polyfill node:child_process). The wall holds today ONLY by convention: client-core/chat-model use `import type` from '@moxxy/sdk' (erased), apps/mobile-poc App.tsx carefully value-imports from '@moxxy/sdk/tool-display' and uses `import type` for the barrel. One accidental value import in client-core or chat-model (both RN-consumed) silently breaks the RN/browser build.
findings.fix: Move the Node-runtime helpers behind an explicit server-only subpath (e.g. add './server' to exports mapping fs-utils/tunnel/http-utils/channel-auth) and drop their re-exports from the main barrel, OR at minimum add a forbidden dep-cruiser rule asserting `packages/sdk/src/index.ts` (and any module reachable from a browser-consumed export) must not transitively reach `node:*`. Update node-side consumers (cli/runner/channel plugins) to import from '@moxxy/sdk/server'. Keep the pure type/logic exports (events, tool-display, mode-helpers, compactor-helpers — all already node-free) on the main barrel.
findings.testStrategy: Add a vitest/CI check that resolves the './tool-display' and main-barrel type graph and asserts no `node:` specifier is reachable from browser-consumed exports; or a dep-cruiser rule from packages consumed by client-core/chat-model/mobile-poc to node:*. Build apps/mobile-poc with Metro to confirm.
findings.complexity: 1 barrel + ~exports map; consumers re-pointed -> RN/browser bundle provably node-free

findings.title: dep-cruiser scope excludes apps/desktop renderer and apps/mobile-poc — RN/browser boundary breaches would go undetected
findings.problem: The invariant enforcement (`pnpm check:deps`) only cruises `^(packages/.*/src|apps/desktop/electron)` (includeOnly at .dependency-cruiser.cjs:81; the script at package.json:18 passes only `packages apps/desktop/electron`). The desktop RENDERER (apps/desktop/src), apps/mobile-poc (React Native), apps/fixture-recorder, and apps/docs are entirely unguarded. These are exactly the bundles where the browser/RN safety invariants matter most (no node: builtins, no deep cross-package imports). Today they happen to be clean (verified: no node: value-imports in client-core/chat-model/mobile-poc; mobile-poc uses '@moxxy/sdk/tool-display' + `import type`), but there is no automated guard preventing a regression of finding boundaries-1, and no rule catches a future renderer file value-importing '@moxxy/sdk' or reaching into electron/main.
findings.fix: Extend includeOnly (and the depcruise CLI args) to cover `apps/desktop/src`, `apps/mobile-poc/src`, and add a forbidden rule: from `apps/(desktop/src|mobile-poc)` to `node:*` severity error (renderer/RN must not statically reach Node builtins). Optionally a rule forbidding apps/desktop/src -> apps/desktop/electron static imports (renderer must not import main-process code).
findings.testStrategy: Run `pnpm check:deps` after widening scope; confirm it still passes clean on current main, then add a temporary node:fs import to a renderer file to confirm the new rule fires.

clusters.title: Consolidate the hand-rolled external stores and optimistic connection-flip in client-core
clusters.oneLine: useDesks/useSessions/useConnection each hand-roll subscribe/emit/listeners; the optimistic connection-flip on session switch is duplicated across desksStore.setActiveSession and sessionsStore; queue ids (q-<rev>-<len>) can collide after dropFromQueue. Fold sessionsStore into thin selectors over desksStore (per TECH_DEBT L878) and unify the store primitive.
clusters.rationale: State-store refactor (not a pure extraction) feeding desktop + mobile; medium risk. The chatStore has NO co-located test today, so build the test harness first (see t2-test-harness).

findings.title: Optimistic connection-flip logic duplicated across desksStore.setActiveSession and sessionsStore (useSessions) paths
findings.problem: desksStore carries desk-scoped session ops (createSession/setActiveSession/renameSession/removeSession) while sessionsStore/useSessions keeps the same mutations scoped to one tracked desk; the optimistic connection-flip logic on session switch is implemented in both setActive* paths. The desktop already uses only desksStore — useSessions is retained for mobile/API parity — so the two surfaces drift independently.
findings.fix: Fold the tracked-desk sessionsStore into thin selectors over desksStore (as the TECH_DEBT entry proposes) so the connection-flip lives in exactly one place; do it when either file is next touched. Lower priority — it is a state-store refactor, not a pure extraction.
findings.testStrategy: Existing client-core store tests for both hooks must pass against the consolidated path; add a switch-session test asserting the optimistic active id updates before the RPC resolves.

findings.title: Three near-identical hand-rolled external stores (subscribe/emit/listeners Set) duplicated
findings.problem: DesksStore, SessionsStore, and ConnectionStore each reimplement the same listener-Set + subscribe + emit + Partial-patch `set` boilerplate (also duplicated again in chat-store/store.ts). The optimistic switch gesture (snapshot prev activeId + connectionStore.active$(), set optimistic, IPC, rollback on catch) is copy-pasted across DesksStore.setActive, DesksStore.setActiveSession, and SessionsStore.setActive with subtly different rollback fields — a fertile ground for the kind of partial-rollback bug u33-4 guards against.
findings.fix: Extract a tiny generic `createExternalStore<T>()` (state + subscribe + getSnapshot + set) and a shared `optimisticSwitch` helper that captures/restores both the store's active id and connectionStore.active$(); have all three stores build on it. Erases the duplication and centralizes the rollback contract.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the extracted helper once; the existing useSessions tests plus new useDesks tests cover the call sites.

findings.title: Queue ids are not unique: `q-<rev>-<len>` can collide after dropFromQueue, dropping wrong items
findings.problem: enqueue() derives the queued-turn id from `slot.rt.rev` and the CURRENT queue length: `q-${slot.rt.rev}-${slot.queue.length}`. Neither component is monotonic per-queue. After a dropFromQueue (composer 'remove pending send') the length shrinks, so a subsequent enqueue at the same rev re-mints an id already in use. Because dropFromQueue filters by id, a collision removes BOTH the new and the older turn; shiftQueue/dropFromQueue then operate on the wrong entry. rev bumps on streaming chunks often mask this, but it is a real correctness hazard whenever two enqueues land at the same rev with an intervening drop (e.g. user removes a pending send then types another while the turn is mid-flight and no chunk has arrived).
findings.fix: Use a monotonic per-store counter (e.g. `private queueSeq = 0` -> `q-${this.queueSeq++}`) or crypto.randomUUID/newBlockId() for the queued-turn id; never derive identity from mutable length or shared rev.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: enqueue two turns at a fixed rev, dropFromQueue the first, enqueue a third, assert all three ids are distinct and dropFromQueue removes exactly one entry.

findings.title: hiddenTurns can leak an id if a hidden turn errors without a turn_complete for that turnId
findings.problem: hideTurn() adds a turnId to the module-level hiddenTurns Set; it is removed only in unhideTurn() or when dispatch() sees a turn_complete tagged with that exact turnId (store.ts:269-272). A background generation that fails or is aborted such that no turn_complete with the matching turnId reaches the store leaves the id permanently in the Set. Subsequent (unrelated) events that happen to reuse/collide that turnId would be silently dropped, and the Set grows without bound across the app lifetime.
findings.fix: Have callers always pair hideTurn/unhideTurn in a finally (the AI-skill-draft caller), or also clear the hidden id on the matching send_failed/abort/error event for that turnId; consider scoping hiddenTurns per workspace and clearing it in drop().
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: hideTurn(t); dispatch a turn that errors without turn_complete(t); assert (after fix) the id is gone and later events are not dropped.

clusters.title: Fix confirmed security/boundary correctness bugs (each needs a regression test)
clusters.oneLine: isSafeViewUrl whitespace bypass (java\tscript: XSS); brokerFetch follows redirects bypassing the net allowlist (SSRF); permission-engine invalid-regex deny rule fails open; desktop-ipc remote commands accept unbounded strings (no schema); surface.* forwards unvalidated PTY text to runner; desktop skills assertSafeName misses backslash; provider_add can overwrite a built-in provider; isolator SIGTERM-only kill with no SIGKILL escalation; worker security doc contradicts behavior.
clusters.rationale: These are real confirmed security/boundary defects surfaced by the quality sweep. Each is a tight fix but security-load-bearing, so every one ships with a regression test. Medium risk by virtue of the trust boundary.

findings.title: isSafeViewUrl whitespace bypass: 'java\tscript:' passes the gate then executes as XSS
findings.problem: isSafeViewUrl only String.trim()s leading/trailing whitespace, then matches the scheme with /^[a-z][a-z0-9+.-]*:/. A scheme with EMBEDDED ASCII whitespace (tab/newline/CR/space), e.g. 'java\tscript:alert(1)', breaks the char class so the regex never sees a scheme colon and the function returns true (treated as a relative URL). render.tsx then emits <a href={rawString}> verbatim (React does not strip whitespace from href). The HTML5 URL parser strips ASCII tab/newline/CR before scheme resolution, so on click the browser navigates to javascript:alert(1). This surface is internet-exposed via tunnels and explicitly designed to be shared with third parties, so this is a click-XSS hole that defeats BOTH the parser wall and this 'second wall' (both call the same buggy function).
findings.fix: Before the checks, strip ALL ASCII whitespace and control chars the URL parser would ignore: const u = url.replace(/[\u0000-\u0020]/g, '').toLowerCase(); (or replace(/\s/g,'') plus \x00-\x1f). Then run the existing prefix/regex checks against the collapsed string. Keep the canonical SDK copy (packages/sdk/src/view-renderer.ts isSafeViewUrl) in lockstep — same edit. Note: the original raw value should still be what is rendered/blocked, but the SAFETY DECISION must be made on the whitespace-collapsed form.
findings.testStrategy: Add a render.test.ts case: link with href 'java\tscript:alert(1)', 'java\nscript:alert(1)', 'jav ascript:alert(1)', and '\x00javascript:alert(1)' must each render as plain text (no href=, no 'script'). Add a direct url-safety unit test table asserting isSafeViewUrl returns false for each whitespace/control-split scheme.

findings.title: brokerFetch follows redirects, bypassing the net allowlist (SSRF)
findings.problem: brokerFetch validates only the *initial* URL against caps.net via urlInScope, then calls fetch() with the default redirect:'follow'. An allowlisted host can 30x-redirect to an internal/forbidden host (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/ cloud metadata, http://localhost) and the broker silently follows it. The capability allowlist is the entire point of the security plugin; this defeats it for the net surface.
findings.fix: Use redirect:'manual' and re-run urlInScope on every Location hop in a bounded loop (cap hops, deny on out-of-scope or private-IP target), or at minimum set redirect:'error' so any redirect from an allowlisted host fails closed.
