Reuse the ssrf-guard logic already present in @moxxy/plugin-browser/src/ssrf-guard.ts rather than reinventing host filtering.
findings.testStrategy: Spin a local http server that 302s to http://127.0.0.1:<other>/ ; assert brokerFetch with net.mode='allowlist' hosts=[that server] denies/errs rather than fetching the redirect target.

findings.title: Invalid-regex fallback makes deny rules fail open (security gate weakens silently)
findings.problem: In matchRule, when an inputMatches value v is not a valid regex, the catch falls back to literal equality `candidate !== v`. The same code path serves BOTH allow and deny rules. For a DENY rule, a malformed pattern that doesn't exactly equal the candidate causes the rule to NOT match -> the call is no longer denied (fail-open). A user writing a deny rule like `{ cmd: 'rm -rf [' }` (unbalanced bracket = invalid regex) silently becomes a no-op deny that lets dangerous commands through, the opposite of the user's intent. A security gate must fail closed.
findings.fix: Pre-compile and validate inputMatches regexes at rule-add time (sanitizeRule) / load time and reject (or normalize) invalid patterns, so matchRule never has to guess at runtime. If runtime fallback must remain, make deny-rule evaluation fail closed: pass the rule's list intent into matchRule and, for deny rules, treat an uncompilable pattern as a match (or escape v and recompile as a literal regex) rather than requiring exact string equality.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test: deny rule with an invalid-regex inputMatches value must still deny a call whose input contains that substring; assert check() returns mode:'deny'. Add an allow-rule counterpart asserting it does NOT over-allow.

findings.title: Remote-reachable commands session.abortTurn / session.info / sessions.list accept unbounded strings (no schema)
findings.problem: validation.ts only schematizes 'dangerous' commands; its header says no-arg/already-defended commands are intentionally absent. But several commands that ARE on REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS (i.e. reachable over the LAN WebSocket bridge by anyone holding the bearer token) carry free-form string args yet have NO schema: `session.abortTurn` ({workspaceId?, turnId}), `session.info` ({workspaceId?}), `sessions.list` ({deskId?}). These are not no-arg. dispatch() validates only args[0], and with no schema validateIpcInput is a no-op (validation.ts:274-275), so a hostile remote client can pass arbitrarily large / arbitrary strings for turnId / workspaceId / deskId into the host. Unlike chat.migrate (bounded) and sessions.remove (bounded id), these have no length cap — an unbounded-string OOM / log-bloat vector over the remote transport that the surrounding commands explicitly defend against.
findings.fix: Add bounded schemas for the remote-reachable, arg-carrying commands: `session.abortTurn` -> z.object({ workspaceId: optionalWorkspace, turnId: z.string().min(1).max(256) }); `session.info` -> z.object({ workspaceId: optionalWorkspace }).optional(); `sessions.list` -> z.object({ deskId: z.string().min(1).max(256).optional() }).optional(). Consider a CI/test assertion that every IpcCommandName in REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS taking a non-undefined arg has a schema entry.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: for each command in REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS whose IpcCommands signature has a parameter, assert ipcInputSchemas[cmd] is defined; plus a validation.test case rejecting an oversize turnId/deskId.

findings.title: surface.* commands forward unvalidated input (incl. PTY text) to the runner with no boundary schema
findings.problem: None of `surface.open` (kind), `surface.input` (message → runner-owned PTY shell input / Playwright page input), `surface.resize` (size), `surface.close` (surfaceId) have a Zod schema in ipcInputSchemas, so they skip the boundary validation choke point. `surface.input.message` is shell/PTY input — unbounded, an XSS'd renderer could push arbitrarily large strings into the relay. Unlike workspace.listDir, these were not added to the schema map. The header comment notes surfaces are not on the mobile WS allow-list, so the threat is the local renderer only, which is why this is low — but it is inconsistent with the 'every dangerous command gets a boundary schema' policy and with workflows.resume (which bounds its forwarded reply to 100k specifically to avoid OOM).
findings.fix: Add bounded schemas: surface.open `{workspaceId, kind: z.string().max(64)}`, surface.input `{workspaceId, surfaceId: z.string().max(128), message: z.string().max(100_000)}`, surface.resize `{workspaceId, surfaceId, size: z.object({cols, rows}).optional()}`, surface.close `{workspaceId, surfaceId}` to validation.ts.
findings.testStrategy: Add validateIpcInput unit cases per surface command rejecting oversized/malformed payloads; assert valid payloads pass.

findings.title: assertSafeName misses backslash separator — subdir/abs path can slip through on Windows
findings.problem: assertSafeName rejects '/', '..' and non-'.md' names but does NOT reject '\\'. On Windows '\\' is a path separator, so a name like 'sub\\evil.md' (no '/', no '..', ends '.md') passes validation and path.join writes into a subdirectory of ~/.moxxy/skills instead of failing. Less severe than '..' traversal (still under the skills dir for relative forms) but inconsistent with the cross-platform intent of the guard and a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.fix: Add `|| name.includes('\\')` to the rejection condition (and consider rejecting any name where path.basename(name) !== name as a single robust check).
findings.testStrategy: Add a skills.test.ts case asserting writeSkill('sub\\evil.md', 'x') rejects with /invalid/.

findings.title: provider_add / onInit can silently overwrite a built-in provider (openai/anthropic) via replace()
findings.problem: The providerNameSchema slug regex (/^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$/) admits 'openai', 'anthropic', 'openai-codex' — the names of the built-in code providers. provider_add and onInit both do `if (alreadyRegistered) replace(def) else register(def)`, so a provider_add(name:'openai', baseURL:...) hot-swaps the real built-in OpenAI provider's def with a stored openai-compat shim against an arbitrary baseURL, and this clobber survives restarts (re-applied by onInit). There is no guard reserving built-in names. This is a correctness + trust concern: a model-callable tool can redirect the 'openai' provider to a different endpoint.
findings.fix: Reject (or refuse to replace) reserved/built-in provider names. Cheapest: only register/replace when the existing def is itself a stored one — track stored names, and for built-in collisions throw a CONFIG_INVALID ('cannot shadow built-in provider X; pick another slug'). At minimum, never replace() a def the plugin did not itself register.
findings.testStrategy: Test: pre-register an 'openai' def in FakeRegistry (simulating the built-in), call provider_add(name:'openai',...) and assert it throws / leaves the built-in def untouched; assert onInit does not clobber a built-in when providers.json contains a colliding entry.

findings.title: Timeout/abort kills child with SIGTERM only — no SIGKILL escalation; runaway handler survives
findings.problem: The whole point of this isolator's `timeMs` budget is to bound a runaway handler, but `finish()` only sends `child.kill('SIGTERM')`. A handler stuck in a tight synchronous CPU loop, or one that traps/ignores SIGTERM, keeps running unbounded (a full core, growing heap) after the parent Promise has already rejected. The kernel never reaps a busy-looping process. The worker isolator forcibly tears down the V8 isolate via `worker.terminate()`, and the wasm isolator passes the budget to `spawnSync`'s own timeout/`killSignal`, so subprocess is the weakest at actually stopping work despite being marketed as the strongest boundary.
findings.fix: In `finish`, after `child.kill('SIGTERM')`, schedule an escalation timer (e.g. `const k = setTimeout(() => { if (!child.killed) child.kill('SIGKILL'); }, 2000); k.unref?.()`). Optionally clear it on the `exit` event. Add a regression test using a handler that ignores SIGTERM (process.on('SIGTERM', ()=>{}) + busy loop) and assert the process is gone shortly after timeout.
findings.testStrategy: Fixture handler installs a SIGTERM trap then spins; after timeMs the isolator rejects AND the child PID is no longer alive (poll process.kill(pid,0)). Requires exposing/observing the pid in a test build or asserting via OS.

findings.title: Security doc-block contradicts shipped behavior: claims node:fs unblocked & only readFile brokered
findings.problem: The 'What this still does NOT enforce' JSDoc on createWorkerIsolator asserts two things that are now FALSE, on a security-boundary module where the doc IS the threat-model contract. (a) It says a handler can `import('node:fs')...readFileSync('/etc/passwd') and bypass the broker. The broker is advisory... A future loader-hook layer could block direct imports, but that's complex and Node doesn't yet have a stable API for it.' But the loader hook is actually wired: workerData.loaderUrl encodes LOADER_HOOK_SOURCE (lines 211-213) and the shim calls register(loaderUrl, import.meta.url) (lines 41-42) before importing the handler, so node:fs/child_process/net/etc. throw at resolution. (b) It says 'only readFile is brokered. writeFile, readdir, stat... will land in Phase 2.2' but all four fs ops plus fetch and exec are fully brokered (shim broker object lines 71-80; broker.ts dispatch lines 136-155). broker-e2e.test.ts directly proves node:fs import is blocked and writeFile/readdir/stat/exec round-trip. A reader sizing the isolator's guarantees will under-trust it (or mis-design a tool around a gap that no longer exists), which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a security component.
findings.fix: Rewrite the 'still does NOT enforce' block to match reality: the loader hook now blocks direct node:fs/child_process/net/http/tls (BLOCKED_HANDLER_MODULES) and all of readFile/writeFile/readdir/stat/fetch/exec are brokered. Keep only the genuinely-open gaps (env inheritance via process.env passthrough in the shim, no VM/heap-isolation beyond V8 limits, loader covers ESM-resolve only — not eval/createRequire escapes).
findings.testStrategy: Doc-only; the existing broker-e2e loader-hook describe block already pins the real behavior. Add an assertion comment cross-referencing the doc so drift is caught in review.

findings.title: Synthetic ctx.signal is a fresh never-aborted AbortController — cooperative cancel inside the handler is dead
findings.problem: The synthetic ToolContext built inside the worker sets `signal: new AbortController().signal` (line 98). That signal is never connected to the parent's abort/timeout — the parent enforces cancellation purely by worker.terminate(). A well-behaved handler that wires ctx.signal into fetch/long-loops for cooperative cancellation (the SDK's documented contract for ctx.signal) will never observe abort and will run until V8 hard-kills the thread. It also means in-flight async work has no chance to flush/cleanup before termination. The handler is told it has a real abort signal but receives a permanently-inert one.
findings.fix: Propagate abort into the worker: on parent abort/timeout, postMessage({type:'abort'}) before terminate (with a short grace period), and in the shim hold a module-level AbortController whose signal is passed to ctx; the message handler calls controller.abort(). Alternatively, document on the synthetic ctx that signal is inert under the worker isolator so handlers don't rely on it. Mirror whatever isolator-subprocess does for consistency.
findings.testStrategy: Add a fixture handler that resolves only when ctx.signal fires (e.g. await once(ctx.signal,'abort')); assert that an external ctrl.abort() resolves it via a clean message path (distinct from the hard-terminate timeout test already present).

findings.title: spawn() omits cwd — child process.cwd() is parent's dir, not call.cwd
findings.problem: The child is spawned without a `cwd` option, so `process.cwd()` inside the child equals the parent process's cwd, not `call.cwd`. The synthetic ctx passes `cwd: call.cwd` (114) and the broker scopes paths by `call.cwd`, but any handler logic that resolves a relative path against `process.cwd()` (or a relative dynamic import, or a tool that shells out expecting the working dir) silently uses the wrong directory. Intent is clearly per-call cwd isolation; the spawn just forgets to wire it.
findings.fix: Add `cwd: call.cwd` to the spawn options (line 209-212). Verify call.cwd is always defined for this isolator (it is required on IsolatedToolCall).
findings.testStrategy: Fixture handler returns process.cwd(); assert it equals the call.cwd passed in (e.g. a tmp dir distinct from the test runner's cwd).

clusters.title: Fix OAuth/provider token refresh races and stale-field bugs
clusters.oneLine: oauth_get_token refreshes without the per-credential lock (re-races rotating tokens); storeTokenSet leaves stale id_token/expires_at/scope after a partial refresh; anthropic provider OAuth refresh+401-replay has zero tests; codex encrypted-reasoning round-trip is emitted/stored but never replayed.
clusters.rationale: Token-rotation correctness with no current coverage; medium risk (auth path). Add the per-credential lock + refresh-replay tests.

findings.title: oauth_get_token refreshes without the per-credential lock — re-races rotating tokens
findings.problem: buildOauthGetTokenTool reads creds, refreshes, and stores WITHOUT wrapping the refresh+persist critical section in withCredentialLock. This is exactly the single-use-rotating-refresh-token race that credential-lock.ts + ensure-fresh.ts were built to prevent (Anthropic/Codex burn the prior refresh_token on every refresh). Two concurrent oauth_get_token calls (or one oauth_get_token + a provider's ensureFreshTokens sharing the same vault credential) can both refresh with the same stored refresh_token; the loser burns a now-dead token and logs the user out. The tool path is a second, unsynchronized writer to the same oauth/<provider>/* keys.
findings.fix: Route the tool's refresh+store through withCredentialLock(`oauth-${provider}`, ...) re-reading creds inside the lock (or better, delegate to a shared refreshAndStore used by both ensure-fresh and tools to erase the duplication). Add the isAuthRejection re-read-once recovery too.
findings.testStrategy: Concurrency test: fire two oauth_get_token handlers at an expired credential against a fake token endpoint that returns a NEW refresh_token each call and rejects (invalid_grant) on a stale one; assert exactly one network refresh and no AUTH error.

findings.title: storeTokenSet leaves stale id_token / expires_at / scope after a refresh that omits them
findings.problem: storeTokenSet only writes optional fields when they are defined (it never deletes). A refresh response that omits expires_in / id_token / scope (common: many IdPs don't re-issue id_token on refresh, parseTokenResponse then yields undefined) leaves the PREVIOUS vault value in place. For expires_at this is a correctness/security hazard: a refresh with no expires_in leaves the OLD (now in the past, or stale-future) expires_at, so isExpired() either loops refreshing forever or treats a token as fresh off stale data. For id_token it leaves a stale OIDC token that extractAccountId/extractExtras may re-derive identity from on a later read.
findings.fix: On store, for each optional key delete the vault key when the new value is undefined (vault.delete?.(`${base}/expires_at`) etc.), so the stored set exactly mirrors the live TokenSet. Alternatively write the whole credential as one JSON blob under a single key to get atomic replace semantics.
findings.testStrategy: Unit: store a tokenSet with expiresAt+idToken+scope, then store a refreshed tokenSet missing all three; readStoredCreds must return a tokenSet WITHOUT those fields.

findings.title: OAuth token refresh + 401 refresh-and-replay path has zero unit coverage
findings.problem: ensureFreshOauth() (proactive near-expiry refresh) and the reactive 401 -> refreshOauthNow() -> replay logic are the riskiest, statefulest part of this provider: they swap this.client mid-flight and re-run streamOnce. A bug here (e.g. double-yielding message_start, replaying after partial output, not surfacing a second 401) corrupts every Claude-subscription turn. The oauth-mode.test.ts suite only asserts system-block assembly; nothing drives oauthRefresh, oauthExpiresAt skew, or a thrown 401.
findings.fix: Add tests: (1) oauthExpiresAt within 60s skew -> ensureFreshOauth calls refresh and makeOauthClient gets the new token; (2) streamOnce throws an error with status:401 -> stream() calls refreshOauthNow once and replays, emitting exactly one message_start and one message_end; (3) second 401 on replay -> single error event; (4) refresh callback that throws -> error event, no replay.
findings.testStrategy: Fake client whose stream() throws {status:401} on first call and yields DONE_EVENTS on second; assert refresh invoked once and message_start count === 1.

findings.title: Encrypted reasoning round-trip is half-built: emitted/stored but never replayed into input
findings.problem: The request body sets `include: ['reasoning.encrypted_content']` (translate.ts:210) and `store: false` (translate.ts:207), and the stream consumer emits `reasoning_signature` events carrying `encrypted` (codex/sse-event-handler.ts), so the upstream loop persists a `{type:'reasoning', encrypted}` ContentBlock in assistant history (SDK ProviderMessage supports it — provider.ts:33-39). But `toResponsesInput` only forwards `text`, `image`, `document`, `tool_use`, and `tool_result` blocks — it silently drops every `reasoning` block. With `store:false`, gpt-5-family reasoning models expect the prior turn's encrypted reasoning items to be replayed as input items between tool calls; dropping them loses reasoning continuity and can provoke a 400/degraded behaviour on multi-step (tool-use) turns. Anthropic's translate.ts replays the analogous block (translate.ts:161-170); codex does not, so the feature is asymmetric and incomplete.
findings.fix: In `toResponsesInput`, for assistant messages emit a Responses `reasoning` input item (`{type:'reasoning', encrypted_content: block.encrypted}`, or the documented `{type:'reasoning', summary:[], encrypted_content}` shape) for each `reasoning` block that carries `encrypted`, positioned before the function_call items, matching codex-rs replay. Add the `reasoning` variant to the `ResponsesInputItem` union and skip unsigned/unencrypted reasoning.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: build a ProviderRequest whose assistant message contains a `{type:'reasoning', encrypted:'BLOB'}` block followed by a tool_use, assert `toResponsesInput` output contains a reasoning input item carrying BLOB ahead of the function_call. Round-trip test: feed an SSE stream that emits reasoning_signature, capture the stored block, replay it, assert it survives.

clusters.title: Harden embedder/memory correctness and recall hot-path I/O
clusters.oneLine: transformers embedder caches a failed extractor load forever (permanent brick on transient error) and normalizeShape can misalign vectors with inputs; openai embedder returns undefined dim on unknown model, silently corrupting the index; memory consolidate collision guard uses stale byName; embedding-cache reloads+parses the whole vector cache per recall and listEntries parses every .md sequentially (await-in-loop).
clusters.rationale: Memory subsystem correctness + recall-path perf. Medium risk; re-audit the subsystem and add tests for the failure-cache and shape-alignment paths.

findings.title: Failed extractor load is cached forever — embedder permanently bricked on transient error
findings.problem: ensureExtractor() stores the in-flight load as this.extractorPromise but never clears it on rejection. If the IIFE rejects (transient ONNX/download/init failure, OOM, missing native binary at first call), the rejected promise is latched: every subsequent embed() hits `if (this.extractorPromise) return await this.extractorPromise` and re-throws the SAME cached rejection for the whole process lifetime. Recall via @moxxy/plugin-memory then stays broken with no recovery even after the underlying condition clears. The OpenAI sibling has no such latch (it builds its client eagerly in the constructor).
findings.fix: Wrap the load so a rejection clears the latch: `this.extractorPromise = (async () => {...})().catch((e) => { this.extractorPromise = null; throw e; });` (or try/finally that nulls extractorPromise when extractor was not set). Keep the success-path caching of this.extractor.
findings.testStrategy: Add a vitest case: pipelineFactory that rejects on first call, resolves on second; assert first embed() throws, second embed() succeeds and factory was retried (factoryCalls===2).

findings.title: normalizeShape silently misaligns vectors with inputs when batch count != expected
findings.problem: When the extractor returns a 2D number[][] whose length does not equal the input count (line 120-122), normalizeShape returns it verbatim without any length check, while the 3D branch DOES guard (line 125 `if (nested.length !== expected) return []`). A short/long 2D batch is therefore passed straight through to the memory EmbeddingIndex, which zips vectors to text ids positionally — silently associating embeddings with the wrong records (corrupting semantic recall) rather than failing cleanly. The contract is 'Returns vectors in input order', one per input.
findings.fix: In the 2D branch verify `data.length === expected` (return [] or throw on mismatch, matching the 3D branch's invariant). Better: throw a descriptive error so a misbehaving model surfaces loudly instead of corrupting the index.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: stub factory returns 1 vector for a 2-text batch; assert normalizeShape/embed rejects or returns [] rather than a length-1 array. Cover the symmetric long-batch case.

findings.title: Unknown config model makes dim return undefined, silently corrupting the memory index
findings.problem: EmbeddingsConfig.model is an open z.string() (packages/config/src/schema.ts:83), and index.ts registers the embedder via createClient: (config) => createOpenAIEmbedder(config as OpenAIEmbedderOptions) with no validation. If a user sets embeddings.model to anything outside the three literal keys (e.g. 'text-embedding-4'), MODEL_DIM[this.model] is undefined, so the `dim` getter returns undefined. The EmbeddingProvider.dim contract is documented as 'Consumers may verify this matches their stored index'; an undefined dim defeats that check and lets a wrong/garbage index be built without any error.
findings.fix: Validate the model in the constructor (or createClient): if MODEL_DIM[model] is undefined AND no explicit dimensions override is supplied, throw a clear error ('unknown OpenAI embedding model X; set embeddings.dimensions or use a known model'). selectEmbedder already catches activation errors and falls back to TF-IDF (cli/src/setup/embedder.ts:38-45), so throwing yields a graceful, logged fallback instead of a silent undefined-dim index.
findings.testStrategy: Add a unit test: new OpenAIEmbedder({ model: 'bogus' as any }) without dimensions throws; with dimensions set it succeeds and dim returns the override. Add a config-applier/setup test asserting an unknown model falls back to TF-IDF with a warn.

findings.title: consolidateMemory's collision guard uses a stale byName, can clobber an entry merged earlier in the same run
findings.problem: `byName` is built once from the initial `all` (line 138) and never updated as the loop saves consolidated entries. The collision guard (lines 184-193) refuses to write when `byName.has(parsed.name) && !clusterNames.has(parsed.name)` to avoid destroying an unrelated memory. But an entry SAVED by an earlier cluster's merge is not in `byName`, so if a later cluster's LLM output picks that same name, the guard passes and store.save() silently overwrites the earlier consolidated entry — the exact data-loss the guard exists to prevent. The 'refuses to overwrite an unrelated memory' test (consolidate.test.ts:146) only covers collision with a PRE-EXISTING entry, not a within-run one.
findings.fix: After a successful merge save, record the produced name in a `produced` Set (or byName.set(parsed.name, ...)) and include `produced` in the collision check so a later cluster can't clobber an already-merged entry; record it as not-merged instead.
findings.testStrategy: Two tag-clusters whose fake LLM replies both return name 'foo'; assert the second cluster is recorded into:null and the first merge's body survives.

findings.title: EmbeddingIndex.load() re-reads + JSON.parses the whole vector cache on every recall
findings.problem: The EmbeddingIndex instance is memoized for the lifetime of the MemoryStore (store.ts getIndex() caches it in this.indexCache) and keeps its parsed cache in this.cache across calls. Yet recallVector() (store/search.ts:35) calls `await index.load()` unconditionally on EVERY vector recall, and load() (embedding-cache.ts:48-63) reads the entire .embeddings.json from disk and JSON.parses it, then re-inserts every entry into the already-populated in-memory Map. For a neural embedder with hundreds of memories at 1536+ dims this is a multi-MB sync-ish read + full JSON.parse on each recall, with no benefit: the store documents a single-writer-per-process assumption (store.ts:80-87), so the in-memory cache is already authoritative after the first load.
findings.fix: Add a `private loaded = false` guard in EmbeddingIndex.load() that returns early once the file has been read (set it even on ENOENT). The flush() already keeps disk in sync for this process's writes; the single-writer invariant means re-reading is unnecessary. This drops recall from O(cacheBytes) parse per call to O(1) after warmup.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on fs.readFile; assert it is invoked at most once across N successive recalls on one store instance; assert recall results stay correct (cache still consulted).
findings.complexity: O(cacheBytes) per recall -> O(1) after first load

findings.title: listEntries reads+parses every .md file sequentially (await-in-loop) on the recall hot path
findings.problem: listEntries iterates dirents and `await fs.readFile` one file at a time, then synchronously parseMdFile + zod safeParse each. recall() calls list() on every query (store.ts:233), and rows() calls listEntries() again on every save/forget. With the documented soft cap of 500 memories (DEFAULT_MAX_MEMORIES) this is up to 500 strictly-serialized disk round-trips per recall — the I/O is fully serialized when it is embarrassingly parallel. The whole A41 incremental-index effort was about avoiding O(N) per write, but recall itself still pays a serial O(N) directory scan.
findings.fix: Collect the .md dirents, then `await Promise.all(candidates.map(async (d) => { const raw = await fs.readFile(...); ... }))` and filter/build entries from the resolved results. Reads fan out concurrently; parsing stays in JS. Optionally cap concurrency (e.g. p-limit-style) if 500 simultaneous FDs is a concern, but Node handles this fine at these counts.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that writes N entries then times/asserts recall completes; or inject a fs spy and assert reads are issued before earlier reads resolve. Existing store.test.ts incremental-index test already proves correctness of the result set.
findings.complexity: serial O(n) disk latency -> parallel O(1) wall-clock batches of disk latency
findings.retiresTechDebt: A41 (follow-on: the re-index was fixed but the recall-time scan stayed serial)

findings.title: Consolidation sends literal model id 'unknown' and tolerates inverted JSON braces
findings.problem: Two minor robustness gaps in the consolidate path: (1) provider.models[0]?.id ?? 'unknown' (line 164) ships the literal string 'unknown' as the model id when the provider exposes no models, which most providers reject with an opaque API error instead of a clear message. (2) extractJson (line 225-228) checks start!==-1 && end!==-1 but not start<end; if a stray '}' precedes the first '{', slice() yields garbage and JSON.parse throws a confusing low-level error rather than the intended 'no JSON object' message.
findings.fix: Throw a clear error if provider.models is empty before streaming; in extractJson add `if (start === -1 || end <= start) throw ...` so malformed output yields the intended message.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test extractJson with input where '}' precedes '{' expects the 'no JSON object' error; test consolidateMemory with a models:[] provider expects a descriptive throw.

findings.title: memory-consolidate onBeforeProviderCall nudge hook has no test for the per-session one-shot flag
findings.problem: buildMemoryConsolidatePlugin installs an onBeforeProviderCall hook that appends a one-time system-prompt nudge once memory crosses autoNudgeThreshold, guarded by a closure `nudged` flag. consolidate-nudge.test.ts exists but the load-bearing invariants (fires exactly once even across many calls; never fires at/below threshold; appends to existing req.system without dropping it) are exactly the kind of stateful hook logic that silently regresses. Worth confirming all three are asserted.
findings.fix: Ensure consolidate-nudge.test.ts asserts: (a) no nudge at exactly threshold entries, nudge at threshold+1; (b) only one nudge across repeated calls; (c) an existing req.system is preserved with the hint appended (not replaced).
findings.testStrategy: As described in fix.

clusters.title: Fix session/channel lifecycle leaks and missing shutdown re-entrancy
clusters.oneLine: schedule run boots a full init-hook session but never closes it (daemon/port leak); self-hosted channel shutdown has no re-entrancy guard (double SIGINT runs it twice); channel-mobile startWsBridge failure leaks log subscription + session resolvers; wizard module-global stdin readline never closed; retained-child subagent registry is a module-level global so one Session.close wipes ALL sessions children; terminal getSharedTerminal create race orphans a live PTY.
clusters.rationale: Lifecycle/shutdown correctness (invariant #9 wiring). Medium risk touching process exit paths; add re-entrancy + teardown tests.

findings.title: `schedule run` boots a full init-hook session but never closes it (daemon/port + onShutdown leak)
findings.problem: runScheduleNow calls setupSessionWithConfig({cwd}) WITHOUT skipInitHooks, so every init-time daemon starts: the scheduler poller begins ticking (it can independently fire a due schedule mid-`run`, double-dispatching) and the webhooks listener binds its port. The session is then `void session`'d and never `.close()`d, so onShutdown hooks (vault flush, poller.stop, webhooks.stop) never run — cleanup relies entirely on the process exiting after the command returns. This is exactly the hazard the rest of the codebase guards against with probeSession (see setup.ts comment: 'an orphaned probe winning the webhooks port bind would silently starve the real session'); `run` reintroduces it for the duration of the firing.
findings.fix: Wrap the body in try/finally and `await session.close('schedule-run')` in the finally. `run` only needs a provider-activated session to dispatch one turn — it does not need the scheduler poller or webhooks listener running, so prefer booting without those daemons (or close immediately after the single turn) to avoid the poller double-firing and the webhooks port bind during the run.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a fake setup and assert session.close is awaited even when runSchedule throws; assert no init-hook daemon (poller/webhooks) is left running after runScheduleNow returns.

findings.title: Self-hosted channel shutdown has no re-entrancy guard (double SIGINT/SIGTERM runs it twice)
findings.problem: runSelfHostedChannel registers `shutdown` for both SIGINT and SIGTERM with no `stopping` guard, unlike its sibling runAttachedChannel (lines 79-86) which guards with `if (stopping) return`. A second Ctrl-C, or SIGINT+SIGTERM arriving close together, re-enters shutdown: webHandle.stop / runnerServer.close / handle.stop / session.close all run concurrently a second time, and a second process.exit(0) races the first. session.close() firing twice can double-flush plugin state (memory journal, vault).
findings.fix: Mirror the attached-path pattern: add `let stopping = false;` and early-return when already shutting down before running the teardown sequence.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test shutdown idempotence: invoke the handler twice and assert handle.stop / session.close are each called at most once (inject mocks for handle/session/runnerServer).

findings.title: startWsBridge failure leaks the log subscription + installed session resolvers (no teardown)
findings.problem: In start() the host is fully wired (host.wire() subscribes to session.log and installs the permission/approval resolvers on the shared session) BEFORE `await startWsBridge(...)`. If startWsBridge rejects (e.g. EADDRINUSE on the default port 8765, which is also the desktop bridge port), start() throws and the returned ChannelHandle.stop is never produced, so host.dispose() is never called: the session.log subscriber and the mobile permission/approval resolvers stay installed on a live ClientSession that the channel no longer owns. Subsequent turns will route permission prompts to a host whose bus is closed (asks park forever) and broadcast runner.event into a dead bus.
findings.fix: Wrap the post-wire startup in try/catch (or move host.wire() to AFTER startWsBridge succeeds): on any failure between wire() and returning the handle, call host.dispose() and `await this.tunnel?.close()` before rethrowing. Set this.host/this.server back to null in the catch.
findings.testStrategy: Add channel.test.ts: stub startWsBridge to reject; assert host.dispose was invoked (session.log unsubscribed, setApprovalResolver(null) called) and that the rejection propagates.

findings.title: Module-global stdin readline never closed; mutable queue/waiter state is not re-entrant or test-safe
findings.problem: The stdin prompt path stashes a single readline Interface plus mutable lineQueue/lineWaiters/stdinEnded at module scope and never calls lineReader.close(). createInterface({input: process.stdin}) puts stdin into flowing mode and registers a 'line' listener that keeps an event-loop ref. In the live CLI this is masked because bin.ts force-exits via process.exit(code) (bin.ts:286), but: (a) the listener+flowing-stdin leak persists for any caller that does not hard-exit; (b) the module-global lineQueue/lineWaiters/stdinEnded are never reset, so a second login within the same process inherits stale queued lines / a permanently-true stdinEnded; (c) once stdinEnded flips, future stdinLinePrompt calls return '' which callers treat as cancellation — a hidden global trap.
findings.fix: Encapsulate the reader + queue + waiters per buildProviderAuthContext call (closure or small class) instead of module globals, and close the readline when the login flow ends (return/throw). At minimum expose a teardown called in a finally in login.ts/init.ts, and reset stdinEnded/lineQueue/lineWaiters on each new reader so the same process can run login twice.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test piping scripted lines through two sequential stdinLinePrompt sessions in one process; assert the second reads fresh lines (not '' from stale stdinEnded) and that the readline is closed after a login completes.

findings.title: Retained-child registry is a module-level global; one Session.close() wipes ALL sessions' children
findings.problem: `retained` is a single process-wide `Map` shared by every Session in the process. `Session.close()` calls `clearRetainedChildren()` (session.ts:338) which clears the entire map, not just children belonging to the closing session. In any host that runs >1 Session per process (the desktop in-process path, tests, and any future multi-desk-in-process model), closing/resetting one session silently drops another live session's paused awaitInput children, breaking their `continue()`. The existing session.test.ts case even demonstrates this: it registers a child, then a *different* freshly-constructed Session's close() wipes it.
findings.fix: Scope the registry to the Session instance: store the `Map` on `SessionRuntime` (e.g. `session.retainedChildren`) and have `runChildTurn`/`continueChildTurn` read/write `rt.parentSession.retainedChildren`; `close()` clears only its own map. Keep the module API as thin wrappers taking a session, or move it onto SessionRuntime entirely.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test: register a retained child via session A, construct + close session B, assert session A's child is still retrievable; close A and assert it is gone.

findings.title: getSharedTerminal has a create race that orphans a live PTY
findings.problem: getSharedTerminal does `existing = shared.get(cwd)` then `await createTerminalProcess(cwd)` then `shared.set(cwd, proc)`. The await yields the event loop, so two concurrent callers for the same cwd (e.g. the desktop surface opening at the same time the agent fires the terminal tool — the documented use case) both miss the map, both spawn a shell, and the second set() overwrites the first. The first PTY/child is now unreferenced, never killed, and leaks as an orphan process. closeAllTerminals() only kills what is currently in the map, so it cannot reap the lost one.
findings.fix: Memoize the in-flight promise: keep a `Map<string, Promise<TerminalProcess>>`; on miss store the promise synchronously before awaiting, and on the created proc's exit clear both maps. Mirror the per-instance promise-mutex pattern used elsewhere for read-modify-write singletons.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: call getSharedTerminal(cwd) twice without awaiting between calls (Promise.all) and assert the two resolved processes are identical (===) and only one createTerminalProcess spawn happened (spy).

findings.title: No test covers the env-restriction security property (this isolator's headline feature)
findings.problem: The isolator's differentiating security claim over `worker` is 'the child sees only env keys in caps.env (or the allowlist); other vars are not inherited' (docblock 169-171). The test suite (index.test.ts) covers round-trip, cap denial, loader-hook blocking, abort, timeout, and global isolation — but never asserts env curation. A regression that accidentally passed `process.env` through (leaking every host API key/token into a brokered child) would be undetected. Given this is a security boundary, it warrants an explicit test.
findings.fix: Add a test: set process.env.MOXXY_SECRET='leak' in the parent, run a fixture handler that returns Object.keys(process.env) (via ctx-independent direct access — note: node:process is allowed by the loader), assert MOXXY_SECRET absent and PATH present with default allowlist; and present when caps.env includes it.
findings.testStrategy: As described; pairs with u62-2's fix verification.

clusters.title: Fix the remaining quadratic / unbounded hot paths
clusters.oneLine: TUI StreamingPreview re-splits the growing delta per chunk; terminal runCommand re-scans full output + rebuilds RegExp per chunk and scrollback concat+slice copies the cap per chunk; grep reads every file fully with no size cap/binary detection; webhooks readJsonPath re-parses the body per filter rule; UsagePanel Math.max spreads an unbounded array (RangeError); scheduler does one atomic write per changed row; DAG wave executes sequentially despite a concurrency setting; usage.perCall / seenIds grow unbounded; surfaceInputParamsSchema JSON.stringifies every input frame; ChatSurface search stringifies every tool input per keystroke; runProcess O(n^2) stdout copy.
clusters.rationale: A batch of contained complexity fixes, each O(n^2)->O(n) or bounded-growth. Medium risk only because a few touch streaming output; add micro-benchmarks/equivalence assertions.

findings.title: StreamingPreview re-splits the entire growing delta per chunk → O(n^2) over a stream
findings.problem: StreamingPreview receives the FULL accumulated streamingDelta (ChatView passes tailForViewport(streamingDelta), and tailForViewport is an identity passthrough). On every streamed chunk the component does `content.split('\n')` over the whole buffer (allocating an array of every line so far) just to find the last non-empty line, and the parent ChatView additionally calls `streamingDelta.trim()` over the full string. Both run once per delta tick, so a message streamed in N chunks does ~O(N) work N times = O(N^2) string scanning/allocation on the hot streaming render path the file's own comments call out as latency-sensitive.
findings.fix: Operate on the tail only: take `content.slice(-Math.max(innerCols*2, 256))` (or have ChatView pass an actually-truncated tail via tailForViewport) before splitting, and find the last newline with `lastIndexOf('\n')` instead of full `split`. Replace the parent `streamingDelta.trim()` truthiness guard with a cheap `streamingDelta.length > 0 && !/^\s*$/.test(...)`-on-tail check, or memoize.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test the new tail-extraction helper: feed a multi-line buffer and assert it returns the last non-empty line truncated to innerCols, and that it only inspects a bounded suffix (e.g. spy/benchmark that work is bounded regardless of total length).
findings.complexity: O(n^2)->O(n) (or O(1) per chunk on the tail)

findings.title: runCommand re-scans full accumulated output and rebuilds RegExp on every data chunk (O(n^2))
findings.problem: On every onData chunk runCommand does `acc += d` then `new RegExp(\`${marker} (\\d+)\`).exec(acc)` over the ENTIRE accumulated buffer. For a command emitting output in k chunks totalling n bytes this is O(n*k) ~ O(n^2) string scanning, plus a fresh RegExp compile per chunk. A build/test command that streams hundreds of KB makes this measurably wasteful while the tool blocks on completion detection.
findings.fix: Compile the marker RegExp once outside the closure (marker is fixed per call). Only scan the new tail: keep a search cursor and run exec from `Math.max(0, acc.length - chunk.length - markerLen)` or scan `d` plus a small carry-over buffer of the last markerLen-1 chars to catch a split sentinel, instead of re-scanning all of acc.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test runCommand against a fake TerminalProcess that emits many small chunks then the sentinel; assert completion + that the matcher is invoked on bounded-size input (or benchmark large-output case).

findings.title: Scrollback buffer concat+slice copies the full cap on every chunk once saturated
findings.problem: `this.buffer = (this.buffer + d).slice(-MAX_SCROLLBACK)` allocates a string of size (buffer+d) then a 200KB slice on every emitted chunk. Once the buffer is at the 200KB cap, each chunk does a ~200KB allocation + copy, making sustained high-throughput output O(total_output * cap) in allocation churn.
findings.fix: Append to an array of chunks and lazily join/trim in scrollback(), or keep a ring buffer / only slice when length exceeds cap + a hysteresis margin (e.g. slice when length > 1.5*cap down to cap) to amortize the copy.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark emitData with many small chunks past the cap; assert scrollback length stays <= cap and content is the tail.

findings.title: Grep reads every file fully into memory with no size cap and no binary detection
findings.problem: walk() does `await fs.readFile(full, 'utf8')` then `content.split('\n')` for EVERY non-ignored file in the tree, with no file-size limit and no binary-content skip. A repo containing a multi-hundred-MB log, a packed git object outside .git, a SQLite db, or media files will load each fully into the heap, split it into a giant line array, and run the regex over binary garbage (producing useless matches and mojibake in results). The OUTPUT_LIMIT clamp bounds the *result*, not the per-file working-set. This is a hot path the model invokes constantly.
findings.fix: Before reading: `const st = await fs.stat(full)`; skip files above a cap (e.g. 5-10 MB). After reading the first chunk (or whole small file), skip if it contains a NUL byte (binary heuristic, like ripgrep). Optionally stream large files line-by-line via readline instead of slurping.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: a dir with a 20MB file and a binary file (containing 0x00) plus a normal .ts file — assert the big/binary files are skipped and the .ts match is still returned; assert peak behavior via a size-capped fixture.
findings.complexity: unbounded per-file memory -> bounded; avoids O(filesize) work on binaries

findings.title: readJsonPath re-parses the entire request body once per jsonPath filter rule
findings.problem: shouldFire() evaluates every include/exclude rule independently, and each jsonPath rule calls readJsonPath(input.body, path) which does JSON.parse(body.toString('utf8')) from scratch. With N jsonPath rules the body (up to maxBodyBytes = 1MB) is decoded + parsed N times on the hot delivery path, on a fire-and-forth synchronous code path inside the HTTP handler before the 202 ACK. This is wasted O(N * bodySize) work where O(bodySize) is achievable.
findings.fix: Parse the body once per shouldFire() call: in shouldFire, attempt JSON.parse(body) a single time into `parsedBody` (or null), thread it (plus the string form for headers) through ruleMatches/readJsonPath so jsonPath lookups index into the already-parsed object. Cache the toString('utf8') too.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test shouldFire with a body and a JSON.parse spy: assert parse called exactly once regardless of how many jsonPath rules are present across include+exclude.

findings.title: Math.max(...series) spreads an UNBOUNDED per-call array → RangeError in long sessions
findings.problem: Line 317 computes `Math.max(...series, 0)` for the 'peak' label, spreading the FULL series. `series = perCallPrompt(events)` (line 186) pushes one number per `provider_response` event with no cap (perCallPrompt lines 144-158). For a long-lived session with very many provider responses the spread exceeds the JS engine argument limit and throws a RangeError, crashing the /usage modal render.
findings.fix: Compute peak with a reduce: `series.reduce((m,v)=>v>m?v:m, 0)` (O(n), no spread), or peak over the same tail the sparkline shows.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test a helper `peak(series)` against a large array and assert it returns the max without throwing; the inline spread throws on the same input.

findings.title: syncSkillSchedules does one full atomic file write per changed row instead of one batched write
findings.problem: Each `store.create`/`store.update`/`store.delete` inside the reconcile loops takes the mutex, JSON.stringifies the WHOLE schedules array, and does an atomic tmp-write+rename+fsync. With K changed skill schedules a single sync performs K independent whole-file serializations and K fsyncs (O(K * n) write bytes, K disk syncs). On a fresh boot with many skill schedules, or after a bulk skill edit, this is needless write amplification on the hot onInit/skill_created path.
findings.fix: Add a batch reconcile method to ScheduleStore (e.g. `reconcileSkillSchedules(wantedMap)`) that performs the whole add/remove/update diff inside a single `mutate` call -> one JSON.stringify + one atomic write. syncSkillSchedules computes the diff and hands the final array to one mutation.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on writeFileAtomic (or count fs writes) across a sync that adds 3 + updates 2 + removes 1 skill schedule; assert exactly one write. Keep existing skill-sync.test.ts assertions on added/removed/updated counts.
findings.complexity: O(K) file writes -> O(1) file writes per sync

findings.title: "DAG runs waves up to concurrency" but the wave executes strictly sequentially
findings.problem: The executor name/description (line 881) and inline comment (284-287) promise parallel waves bounded by `workflow.concurrency`, but the wave loop awaits each step one-at-a-time in a plain for-loop (`for (const step of wave) { ... await runStep(...) }`). `concurrency` only caps the batch SIZE per scheduler pass; it never produces actual concurrency. A fan-out of N independent prompt/skill/tool steps (the canonical use case, exercised by the 'fans out in parallel' test) runs in N*latency instead of ~1*latency — i.e. N serialized LLM round-trips where they could overlap. The comment justifies sequential execution by noting logic/branch/pause steps mutate shared state, but pure tool/prompt/skill steps do not and are the common parallel case.
findings.fix: Partition the wave into 'pure' steps (tool/prompt/skill/nested-workflow — no shared-state mutation) and 'serializing' steps (logic/branch/loop/awaitInput). Run pure steps via the spawner's existing `spawnAll`/Promise.all up to `concurrency`, then settle their outcomes; keep state-mutating steps sequential. Apply each step's outcome (status/vars/branch) after the batch resolves, preserving deterministic vars-merge order.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a spawner whose `spawn` resolves on a deferred that only settles once K concurrent calls are in flight; assert the fan-out of K independent steps does not deadlock (proving true overlap), and assert serial logic-step ordering is preserved.
findings.complexity: O(N) serial latency -> O(N/concurrency) wall-clock for independent steps

findings.title: usage.perCall grows unbounded (one entry per provider call) for the lifetime of the workspace slot
findings.problem: recordUsage appends to perCall on every prompt-bearing provider_response and never trims; the slot is module-level and survives workspace switches, so a long-running session accumulates one number per LLM call indefinitely, plus a fresh `[...prev.perCall, prompt]` allocation each fold. Compaction (store.ts:290) reduces latestPrompt but never compacts perCall, so the sparkline source keeps growing even after the context is shrunk. Not hot, but it is unbounded growth in long sessions and a per-call O(k) copy.
findings.fix: Cap perCall to the last N (e.g. 200) entries with a ring/slice, or store only what the sparkline renders; the totals already capture the cumulative numbers so trimming perCall is lossless for the meter.
findings.testStrategy: Feed >cap provider_response events; assert perCall.length === cap and totals still reflect all calls.

findings.title: Per-workspace seenIds Set and the live in-memory log grow unbounded for the session lifetime
findings.problem: ChatRuntime.seenIds accumulates every event id ever appended/prepended and is only cleared on /clear. The backing ChunkedBlockLog also keeps the entire live conversation in memory (toArray() materialises all of it for the snapshot). The disk layer windows to 50 events/page, but the in-memory runtime never evicts — a multi-hour desktop session holds every event + every id, and buildSnapshot's toArray() (state.ts:125) re-materialises the full array on each log.version bump. This is the n that makes findings 1/6 quadratic.
findings.fix: Bound the live window: evict from the head of the ChunkedBlockLog (and corresponding seenIds) beyond a generous in-memory cap (e.g. last 2-5k events), backed by the disk pagination that already exists for scroll-up. Have buildSnapshot hand the Transcript a windowed tail rather than the full toArray().
findings.testStrategy: Long-session simulation: append 50k events, assert in-memory log/seenIds stay capped and scroll-up still pages older events from disk.
findings.complexity: Memory + per-snapshot work O(session events) -> O(window)

findings.title: Search filter JSON.stringify's every tool input on each query change over the full event log
findings.problem: filteredEvents (useMemo keyed on [chat.events, searchQuery]) rescans the entire event array and, for every `tool_call_requested` event, calls JSON.stringify(e.input).toLowerCase() to test substring inclusion. For a long session with many large tool inputs (file writes, big args), each keystroke in the search box re-stringifies all of them. It is gated to query/events changes (not every render) so it is bounded, but it is O(events * inputSize) per keystroke with a fresh stringify allocation each time, and the stringify result is discarded.
findings.fix: Either (a) precompute a lowercased searchable string per event once (memoized on chat.events alone) and reuse it across keystrokes, or (b) match only e.name and a shallow stringify of small inputs, or (c) debounce searchQuery. Option (a) removes the per-keystroke stringify cost.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark filter over a synthetic 5k-event log with large tool inputs while typing; assert no JSON.stringify in the per-keystroke path (spy/count). Correctness: existing filter results unchanged.
findings.complexity: per-keystroke O(events*inputSize) -> O(events) after a one-time index build keyed on events only

findings.title: runProcess re-copies whole stdout buffer per chunk (O(n^2)); runProcessBinary already does it right
findings.problem: runProcess accumulates stdout with `stdout = Buffer.concat([stdout, chunk])` on every 'data' event. Each event reallocates and copies the entire accumulated buffer, so capturing N bytes across k chunks is O(N*k) ~ O(n^2) time and churns the GC. The sibling helper runProcessBinary (lines 101,126,142) already uses the correct pattern: push chunks into an array and `Buffer.concat(chunks)` once at close. runProcess just diverged. Hot for clipboard `pbpaste` reads and any osascript that returns large stdout.
findings.fix: Collect chunks into a `const chunks: Buffer[] = []`, push in the data handler, and `Buffer.concat(chunks).toString('utf8')` in the close handler — identical to runProcessBinary. This also lets the two helpers share a single core spawn routine (see u81-3 atomicity note).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: spawn a process that emits stdout in many small chunks (e.g. `node -e 'for(...)process.stdout.write(...)'`) and assert the full string is captured; a perf/regression test isn't strictly needed but a multi-chunk correctness test guards the rewrite.

findings.title: NodeStep mounts a 2nd useOnboarding() instance — duplicate probes + progress subscription
findings.problem: Onboarding.tsx already calls useOnboarding(phase) (Onboarding.tsx:45) and holds ob.node/ob.status, but NodeStep independently calls useOnboarding() (NodeStep.tsx:27). Each useOnboarding mounts: a refresh() firing TWO IPC invokes (onboarding.status + onboarding.probeNode) and a separate onboarding.install.progress subscription. So whenever NodeStep is on-screen, the app holds two parallel onboarding states, double the IPC round-trips on mount, and two listeners on the same progress stream. Beyond cost, the second instance has no `phase` arg so its `[refresh, phase?.phase]` effect never re-fires on phase change — its auto-advance semantics diverge from the parent's.
findings.fix: Lift the single useOnboarding(phase) instance in Onboarding.tsx and pass the slice NodeStep needs (node, installNode, refresh, openExternal) down as props, or via context. NodeStep should not instantiate its own hook. This collapses to one probe pair + one progress subscription and removes the phase-asymmetry.
findings.testStrategy: Render Onboarding with a mocked api(); assert onboarding.probeNode is invoked once (not twice) when the node step is active, and that only one onboarding.install.progress subscribe call is made.
findings.complexity: 2 IPC invokes + 1 subscription -> 1 each while NodeStep mounted

findings.title: writeIndex() re-runs ensureDir()+ensureLogFile() (open+close syscalls) on every 250ms index flush
findings.problem: Every debounced index write calls ensureDir() (mkdir recursive) AND ensureLogFile() (fs.open(logPath,'a') + handle.close()) before writing the meta sidecar. After the first flush these are pure no-ops semantically but still cost 2-3 syscalls each, repeated for the entire session lifetime on every chatty turn. The open/close of the .jsonl from the index path is also conceptually wrong — the index writer has no business touching the event-log file; the append path already guarantees the .jsonl exists.
findings.fix: Run ensureDir()+ensureLogFile() once (guard with a memoized `private ready: Promise<void>` created in the constructor/attach) and drop ensureLogFile from writeIndex entirely — writeFileAtomic already mkdir-p's the sidecar's dir, and the .jsonl is owned by the append path. writeIndex then just needs writeJsonAtomic.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on fs.open and assert it is not called from the index-write path after the first flush; keep existing readIndex/restore tests green.

findings.title: Session JSONL append recreates the whole meta object per event (allocation churn on chatty turns)
findings.problem: enqueueAppend runs on EVERY appended event (including the high-frequency ones the runner emits) and rebuilds the full immutable meta object via spread + `new Date().toISOString()` each time, even though the index write itself is debounced to 250ms. On a streaming turn this is per-event object allocation + a Date->ISO string formatting that is then mostly thrown away (only the last value before the debounce flush matters). JSON.stringify(event) per event is unavoidable (it's the persisted line), but the meta churn is pure waste.
findings.fix: Track eventCount/lastActivity/firstPrompt as plain mutable fields and only materialise the immutable SessionMeta object inside writeIndex() (which already runs at most every 250ms). Compute the ISO timestamp once at flush time.
findings.testStrategy: Assert the sidecar contents are unchanged after a burst of events; confirm firstPrompt is still captured from the first user_prompt.
findings.complexity: O(1) per event but with a wasted object+Date alloc -> defer to debounced flush (one alloc per 250ms)

clusters.title: Fix the remaining confirmed correctness bugs (UI + protocol + parsing)
clusters.oneLine: SkillsView load effect depends on whole s object -> refetch clobbers unsaved edits; yaml stripComments mangles # inside block-scalar prompt bodies (round-trip data loss); command-palette steppers assume multi-token names that do not exist (broken vault Args); use-turn-runner stale-closure drops a force-sent priority message; /compact never passes provider/model (forces lossy digest); telegram splitForTelegram splits composed HTML on raw boundaries breaking tags; workflows-builder loop-exit ambiguity + serialize bugs; scheduler cron timezone mismatch + skill-schedule delete/edit never propagate; askStore.respond swallows IPC failure (runner hangs); WorkspaceFiles reloadSignal IPC inside a setState updater; mode-default retryable error continues with zero backoff/unbounded; dispatchToolCall hands hooks empty cwd/env; whisper bypasses normalizeWhisperUpload; /new and /clear leave the priority slot; provider_add rollback unregisters instead of restoring; toposort keys mismatch (packageName vs declared name).
clusters.rationale: A batch of independently confirmed correctness bugs that do not share a structural theme but each warrant a targeted fix + regression test. Medium risk spread across many packages.

findings.title: SkillsView load effect depends on whole `s` object → refetch clobbers unsaved edits
findings.problem: The skill-body load effect lists `[active, s]` as deps, but `useSettings()` returns a fresh object literal on every render (see client-core/useSettings return). Any re-render of SettingsPanel — and useSettings re-renders on every `refresh()` (it sets loading=true then false) and on every SESSION_INFO_REFRESH_EVENT (runner `info.changed` push) — produces a new `s`, re-running the effect. That re-invokes `s.readSkill(active)` and calls `setBody(b)` + `setServerBody(b)`, silently discarding the user's in-progress edits and resetting `dirty`/mode while editing. The `readSkill` callback itself is stable (`useCallback([])`), so the dep is purely the object identity, not the function.
findings.fix: Depend on `[active, s.readSkill]` (a stable callback) instead of the whole `s`. Optionally guard the overwrite: only `setBody`/`setServerBody` when not `dirty`, or key the fetch to `active` only and ignore stale resolutions.
findings.testStrategy: Render SkillsView, pick a skill, edit the textarea, then fire SESSION_INFO_REFRESH_EVENT (or trigger a refresh that flips loading) and assert the edited body is preserved (dirty stays true).

findings.title: stripComments mangles `#` inside block-scalar prompt bodies → round-trip data loss
findings.problem: fromYaml() runs stripComments() over the ENTIRE document before any structural parse, including the lines that will later become `|` block-scalar content. Any prompt line that begins with `#` (e.g. a markdown heading `# Rules`) is replaced with '', and any line containing ` #...` (whitespace then hash) is truncated at the hash. Prompts are emitted as block scalars and are the primary multi-line field, so authored prompt text is silently corrupted on the host-canonical-YAML → hydrateYaml round-trip used live in useWorkflowBuilder.ts.
findings.fix: Do comment-stripping during the structural parse, not as a global pre-pass: skip stripping inside `|`/`>` block-scalar regions (track when parseBlockScalar is consuming lines), or strip per-line only in parseMap/parseSequence scalar context. Simplest: drop the global stripComments pre-pass and have splitKey/parseScalar ignore trailing bare `#` only on non-block lines.
findings.testStrategy: Add a round-trip test: a prompt block scalar containing `# Heading` and `text # inline` lines must survive serialize→fromYaml unchanged. Also a full-line `#`-leading prompt line.

findings.title: Stepper keys assume multi-token command names that don't exist; vault Args form dispatches broken args
findings.problem: COMMAND_STEPPERS is keyed by multi-token names ('vault set', 'vault remove', 'vault get', 'provider use', 'mode use'), but every slash command in the registry is single-token. plugin-vault registers `name: 'vault'` (subcommand parsed from args at runtime); there is NO 'mode'/'provider' slash command at all (those are TUI-local pickers, plugin-commands registers only info/clear/new/compact/exit/help). So no stepper key ever exact-matches a real CommandInfo.name. The startsWith fallback then mis-fires: stepsForCommand('vault') -> `'vault set'.startsWith('vault ')` is true -> returns the 2-field {key,value} form. The user fills key+value, but CommandPalette.run() builds `argString = values.map(quote).join(' ')` (steppers.ts has NO subcommand prefix), so it dispatches session.runCommand({ name:'vault', args:'KEY VALUE' }). The runner's vault handler reads `sub = 'KEY'` -> falls through to `return { kind:'error', message: 'unknown subcommand "KEY"' }`. Net: storing a secret via the desktop Actions palette is broken — every multi-arg action either targets a non-existent command or mis-builds args.
findings.fix: Model the subcommand explicitly: change COMMAND_STEPPERS to map registered single-token command name -> { subcommand?: string; steps: ArgStep[] } (e.g. vault -> { subcommand:'set', steps:[key,value] }), drop the startsWith fuzzy fallback, and have run() prepend the subcommand so it dispatches `name:'vault', args:'set KEY VALUE'`. Remove the dead 'mode use'/'provider use' entries (no such commands) or wire them to real picker actions. Add a unit test asserting stepsForCommand('vault') and the constructed argString round-trip to `set <key> <value>`.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test stepsForCommand for the real command names + a run() arg-construction test with a fake api().invoke spy asserting the exact { name, args } sent for vault set; integration: pick 'vault' action in palette, fill key/value, assert no 'unknown subcommand' error block lands in the transcript.

findings.title: Stale-closure: force-sent priority message skipped at end of the in-flight turn
findings.problem: runTurnWith is an unmemoized closure recreated every render. The drain logic in its finally block reads `priorityMessage` from lexical scope (captured at the render that created THAT closure). forceSendFirst() (Ctrl+T) calls setPriorityMessage and shifts the message OUT of queueRef. But the turn that is currently running was started by an EARLIER closure where priorityMessage was still null, so its finally sees priorityMessage===null and falls into the `else if (queueRef.current.length>0)` branch. The force-sent message is no longer in queueRef (it was shifted into the priority slot), so it is silently NOT run after the current turn — it is stranded in state until some later turn happens to drain it. The recursion inside finally also re-uses the same stale closure (priorityMessage stays null down the whole chain), so the freshly-rendered closure that DOES see the new priority value is never the one executing. This defeats the entire 'send next, alone' feature whenever it is used mid-turn (its only real use case).
findings.fix: Mirror priorityMessage into a ref (priorityRef) updated in setPriorityMessage/forceSendFirst alongside the state setter, and read priorityRef.current in the finally block (also clear it there). Mirrors the existing busy/busyRef pattern. queueRef is already a ref so it is correct.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the hook with @testing-library/react renderHook + a fake Session whose runTurn yields then resolves on a controllable promise: start a turn, call forceSendFirst() while busy, resolve the turn, assert runTurn is next invoked with the force-sent text exactly once and alone (queue not merged in).

findings.title: /compact never passes provider/model to compact() — forces lossy digest, never a real summary
findings.problem: compactSession() invokes compactor.compact(events, { log, budget, signal }) but omits the `provider` and `model` fields of CompactContext. The shipped default compactor (`compactor-summarize`) checks `ctx?.provider` to ask the model to write a real summary, and falls back to a clearly-labeled lossy digest TRUNCATION when it is absent (packages/compactor-summarize/src/index.ts:124 `if (!ctx?.provider) return null;` → line 223 'no provider available for summarization — compacting with a labeled digest truncation'). Therefore a user-invoked `/compact` ALWAYS degrades to truncation even when a provider is fully wired — silently producing worse context than the automatic path. Ironically the function already resolves the provider (resolveActiveContextWindow reads s.providers.getActive()) but throws it away instead of forwarding it.
findings.fix: Resolve the active provider once (s.providers?.getActive()) and pass `provider` plus `model: provider?.models[0]?.id` into the compact() ctx, mirroring runCompactionIfNeeded. Better: delegate the whole manual compaction to the shared SDK helper with a `force` flag rather than reimplementing it here.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test whose CompactSessionShape exposes providers.getActive() and a compactor whose compact() asserts ctx.provider is defined; assert /compact yields the provider-written summary path, not the truncation fallback.

findings.title: splitForTelegram splits already-composed HTML on raw char/newline boundaries, breaking tags/entities
findings.problem:
splitForTelegram is invoked by frame-pump on the fully composed Telegram HTML (composeFrame output), but it cuts purely on character count / nearest preceding newline. A long message can be split in the middle of an HTML tag (e.g. between `
` and `
`, or mid `` fence) or mid-entity (`&`), producing each part as invalid HTML. parse_mode=HTML then rejects the part; frame-pump's stripHtml fallback degrades it to plain text, so long messages silently lose ALL formatting (and a split fence loses its diff highlighting). It also can split an open `
` block such that neither half is valid.
findings.fix: Make splitForTelegram HTML-aware: prefer splitting only at boundaries that are outside any open tag and outside `<pre>`/`<code>` fences (close the fence in the head part and reopen it in the tail), or split the structured RenderedFrame fields (body/diff blocks) at the model layer before HTML conversion so each part is self-contained valid HTML. At minimum, never cut inside `&...;` entities or `<...>` tags.
findings.testStrategy:
Unit test splitForTelegram with input containing a >limit `
...
` block and assert every returned part is independently valid HTML (balanced tags, no truncated entity).

findings.title: Loop exit has no stored field; multiple steps with needs:[loop] yield ambiguous/inconsistent rendering
findings.problem: The loop exit is not stored — loopExitTarget() infers it as the FIRST non-body node (by array order) whose `needs` includes the loop. setLoopExit() only clears the ONE prior exit it finds. If a second non-body step also gains `needs:[loop]` (e.g. via connectNeeds(loop, x), or setLoopExit after the user manually wired another dependency), the model has two equally-valid exits: loopExitTarget renders exactly one as `loop-exit` while deriveEdges renders the other identical needs:[loop] as a plain `needs` arrow. Which one becomes the exit depends on node array order, so reordering/removing unrelated nodes can silently re-point the loop exit.
findings.fix: Make the exit explicit: store it (e.g. loop.exit?: string) or forbid more than one non-body needs:[loop] in connectNeeds/setLoopExit (reject/replace). At minimum, have setLoopExit scrub ALL non-body needs:[loop] before wiring the new target, and have connectNeeds refuse adding needs:[loop] to a non-body step (route it through setLoopExit).
findings.testStrategy: Test: setLoopExit(loop,'a'); connectNeeds(loop,'b'); assert exactly one loop-exit edge and deterministic target regardless of node order; assert 'b' is either rejected or also treated as exit consistently.

findings.title: nextFireTime with explicit timeZone reads parts in target zone but jumps in system-local zone
findings.problem: When `timeZone` is an explicit IANA zone, `decomposeInZone` correctly reads minute/hour/dom/dow IN THAT ZONE via Intl, but the jump helpers (`jumpToNextMonth`/`jumpToNextDay`/`jumpToNextHour` and the inline minute increment) mutate the cursor with system-local `Date` methods (`setHours`, `setDate`, `setFullYear`, `getMonth`). The walk therefore advances in the host's local zone while matching against the user's zone. For any user whose `timeZone` differs from the host, fire times are shifted by the UTC offset gap and `setHours(0,0,0,0)` lands on the wrong wall-clock midnight in the target zone — producing wrong fire minutes, off-by-an-hour DST errors, and in pathological cases skipped matches that walk to the 1-year cap and return null.
findings.fix: Make the cursor walk operate consistently in the target zone. Simplest correct approach: convert the cursor to the target zone's wall-clock components, do all arithmetic on those components, then map back to an absolute instant (e.g. via a fixed-offset computation per candidate, or by re-anchoring with `Intl` + offset lookup). Alternatively, since the per-minute fallback already exists, drop the field-jump optimization when `timeZone` is a non-local explicit zone and walk minute-by-minute using `decomposeInZone` for matching only (slower but correct, capped at 1 year).
findings.testStrategy: Add cron.test.ts cases passing timeZone='America/New_York' and 'Asia/Tokyo' from a host pinned to UTC (set TZ=UTC in vitest env): assert `nextFireTime('0 9 * * *', after, 'America/New_York')` returns the instant that is 09:00 New-York wall-clock, and a DST-boundary case (spring-forward day).

findings.title: Skill-schedule deletes/edits never propagate: poller never rescans, only skill_created re-syncs
findings.problem: Both doc comments claim the poller re-syncs skill schedules on every tick (index.ts L118-119 'the poller's next tick rescans'; skill-sync.ts L13-16 'The scheduler poller calls this on every tick'). It does not — `SchedulerPoller.tickWith` only reads the store and fires due entries; it never calls `syncSkillSchedules`. The only re-sync triggers are `onInit` and the `skill_created` event. Consequently: a skill that is DELETED or that DROPS its `schedule:` frontmatter leaves a stale `source='skill'` row firing forever until next restart, and editing a skill's cron in place (no `skill_created` re-emit) never updates the schedule. This is a real correctness/leak bug masked by misleading comments.
findings.fix: Either (a) actually call `syncSkillSchedules(opts.skills, store)` at the top of each poller tick when `opts.skills` is set (the idempotent no-write-when-unchanged property makes this cheap), passing skills into the poller; or (b) subscribe to skill removal/update events too. Then correct both doc comments to match reality.
findings.testStrategy: Integration test: create skill schedule via syncSkillSchedules, then drop the skill from the registry and run a poller tick (with skills wired) — assert the skill row is gone. Also assert a same-name skill with a changed cron updates without a fresh skill_created.

findings.title: askStore.respond drops the ask optimistically but swallows IPC failure — runner can hang parked
findings.problem: respond() removes the ask from the local store and emits BEFORE the ask.respond IPC resolves, then fires the IPC with `.catch(() => {})`. The runner blocks on the ask (ask-broker openAsk parks the resolver until answerAsk fires via the ask.respond handler). If the IPC round-trip fails (transport down, handler throw), the prompt has already vanished from the UI while the runner stays parked indefinitely with no surfaced error and no way for the user to re-answer — the turn wedges.
findings.fix: Await the invoke and re-insert the ask (or surface an error) on failure, OR only drop the ask after the IPC resolves. At minimum log/report the rejection instead of swallowing it so the parked runner isn't silently stranded.
findings.testStrategy: Test that respond() with a rejecting fake transport re-surfaces the ask (or emits an error) rather than leaving the store empty; add askStore.test.ts.

findings.title: reloadSignal effect fires IPC loads from inside a setExpanded updater (StrictMode double-invoke)
findings.problem: The reload effect calls `setExpanded((cur) => { for (const p of cur) if (p !== '.') void load(p); return cur; })`. The updater returns `cur` unchanged and only exists to read the latest expanded set, but it performs side-effects (load → IPC) inside the reducer. React intentionally invokes state updaters twice in StrictMode/dev and may invoke them again on concurrent re-render, so every expanded folder gets `load()`'d 2x per reload — duplicate `workspace.listDir` IPC round-trips and redundant setNodes churn. It also violates the React rule that updaters must be pure.
findings.fix: Hold the current expanded set in a ref (or lift it into the effect deps) and iterate it directly in the effect body instead of inside the setExpanded updater: keep an `expandedRef` synced to state, then `for (const p of expandedRef.current) if (p !== '.') void load(p);`. The updater should never have side-effects.
findings.testStrategy: Render WorkspaceFiles in StrictMode with a mocked api().invoke('workspace.listDir'), expand two folders, bump reloadSignal, and assert listDir is called exactly once per expanded path + once for '.' (not 2x).

findings.title: Retryable provider error continues with zero backoff and no bounded retry count
findings.problem: When collectProviderStream returns a retryable error (rate-limit/429/overloaded, transient 5xx, ECONNRESET/ETIMEDOUT — see isRetryableError in provider-utils.ts), the loop emits a `retryable` error event and immediately `continue`s. There is no delay and no bounded retry counter: the message set is rebuilt and the provider is re-hit instantly. A persistent-but-retryable condition (e.g. a sustained 429 or provider outage) becomes a tight busy-loop that hammers the provider up to maxIterations (default 500) times back-to-back, burning rate-limit budget and CPU, and likely worsening the rate-limit. The reactive-compaction overflow path has a budget (MAX_REACTIVE_COMPACTIONS=2); the generic retryable path has none. isRetryableError's own doc comment says callers should 'decide whether to back off and try again' — the back-off is never applied here.
findings.fix: Add an exponential-backoff sleep (await ctx.sleep / setTimeout-based, abort-aware via ctx.signal) before `continue` on the retryable branch, and a bounded consecutive-retry counter (reset on any clean call, like reactiveCompactions) that converts to a fatal error after N attempts. Ideally factor a shared `retryProviderCall` helper in @moxxy/sdk/mode-helpers so default/goal/deep-research all back off identically (consistency).
findings.testStrategy: FakeProvider script that yields a retryable error event then a textReply; assert the loop emits the retryable error event, waits (inject a fake sleep and assert it was called with increasing delays), then succeeds. Add a second test where the provider yields retryable errors indefinitely and assert the loop gives up with a fatal error after the bounded retry count rather than running to maxIterations.

findings.title: dispatchToolCall hands onToolCall hooks empty cwd:'' and env:{} — contract violation
findings.problem: The shared tool dispatcher builds the ToolCallContext for ctx.hooks.dispatchToolCall with hardcoded `cwd: ''` and `env: {}`. ToolCallContext (extends AppContext) declares `cwd: string` and `env: Readonly<Record<...>>` as load-bearing fields. Every loop strategy (default, goal) routes every tool call through here, so any onToolCall hook that gates on cwd/env (e.g. a path-based security/policy hook, the documented use case in goal-loop's comment) silently receives empty strings and makes the wrong decision. The tool registry itself correctly falls back to `defaultCwd` (registries/tools.ts:64), so the empty cwd reaches ONLY the hook layer — a subtle inconsistency that defeats hooks while tools still see the real cwd.
findings.fix: Add `readonly cwd: string` and `readonly env: Readonly<Record<string,string|undefined>>` to ModeContext (populated by core/run-turn from the session), then thread `ctx.cwd`/`ctx.env` into the dispatchToolCall hook ctx here instead of the empty literals. Also pass `cwd: ctx.cwd` in the tools.execute opts at line 63 so the tool sees the per-session cwd rather than registry default.
findings.testStrategy: Add a mode-default/goal integration test that registers an onToolCall hook asserting ctx.cwd === session cwd and ctx.env is the session env; today it would observe '' / {}.

findings.title: WhisperTranscriber bypasses normalizeWhisperUpload; can't WAV-wrap the project's raw-PCM MIME
findings.problem: audio.ts exists to centralize Whisper-family preprocessing (filename inference + raw PCM16->WAV wrapping via normalizeWhisperUpload). The codex sibling (plugin-stt-whisper-codex/src/transcriber.ts) calls normalizeWhisperUpload, but WhisperTranscriber.transcribe re-implements its own filename lookup against a private DEFAULT_FILENAMES table and never wraps PCM16. If a caller ever routes audio tagged MOXXY_PCM16_24KHZ_MIME ('audio/x-moxxy-pcm16-24khz') to this transcriber, it POSTs headerless raw s16le bytes with filename 'audio.bin' and Content-Type 'audio/x-moxxy-pcm16-24khz' — OpenAI rejects / mis-decodes it. Today only the Codex transcriber receives that MIME (use-voice-input.ts and desktop session.transcribe both hard-pin Codex), so it is a latent gap, not an active break — but WhisperTranscriber advertises itself as a generic Whisper-family backend and silently fails on the repo's own raw-PCM contract.
