findings.fix: Replace whisper.ts:73-79 with `const upload = normalizeWhisperUpload(audio, opts.mimeType); const file = new File([upload.bytes], upload.filename, { type: upload.mimeType });` so PCM16 is WAV-wrapped and filename inference is shared. Then delete the duplicate DEFAULT_FILENAMES table.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test in this package: transcribe(rawPcm, { mimeType: MOXXY_PCM16_24KHZ_MIME }) and assert the File handed to client.audio.transcriptions.create has a RIFF/WAVE header (first 4 bytes 'RIFF', bytes 8-11 'WAVE') and type 'audio/wav'.

findings.title: /new and /clear reset leaves the priority (force-send) message slot unrcleared
findings.problem: performSessionAction('new') clears queueRef, queueCount, busy, yolo, overlay and pending permissions, but it never clears the single-slot priorityMessage held inside useTurnRunner. If the user force-sent a message (Ctrl+T sets priorityMessage) while a turn is in flight and then issues /new, the controller is aborted, the in-flight runTurnWith finally block fires and unconditionally runs `runTurnWith(priorityMessage...)`, so a stale pre-reset message executes AFTER the session was wiped — re-seeding the just-cleared context.
findings.fix: Expose setPriorityMessage on TurnRunnerHandle and call turn.setPriorityMessage(null) in the 'new'/'clear' branch of performSessionAction; or have the abort path in runTurnWith skip the drain when the turn was aborted for a session reset.
findings.testStrategy: Render SessionView, queue+force-send a message mid-turn, dispatch /new, assert no further runTurn fires after reset.

findings.title: provider_add rollback unregisters the prior def instead of restoring it on write failure
findings.problem: When a provider with the same slug already exists (wasRegistered === true), the handler calls replace(def) to overwrite it, but on a disk-write failure the rollback path calls providerRegistry.unregister(entry.name). That DELETES the provider entirely rather than restoring the previous def, leaving the live session with NO provider under that name even though one was registered before the call. The sibling configure() API (lines 156-163) handles the exact same situation correctly by re-building the prior entry and calling replace(prev) / unregister only when it didn't exist before — so the two paths are inconsistent and provider_add's path is the buggy one. Because the wasRegistered check matches ANY same-named provider (including a built-in like 'openai'/'anthropic', which the slug regex permits), a failed write of a provider_add that shadowed a built-in would leave that built-in detached from the session.
findings.fix: Mirror configure()'s rollback: capture the prior def before mutating (e.g. const prevDef = providerRegistry.list().find(p => p.name === entry.name)) and in the catch do `if (wasRegistered && prevDef) providerRegistry.replace(prevDef); else providerRegistry.unregister(entry.name);`. Factor the register/replace+rollback dance into one shared helper used by both provider_add and configure to keep them in lockstep.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: seed the FakeRegistry with an existing 'zai' def, stub upsertStoredProvider to reject, call provider_add with the same slug, assert the registry STILL has the original def (not deleted). Repeat for the wasRegistered=false case asserting it is unregistered.

findings.title: Plugin-dep toposort resolves edges by packageName but the readiness gate keys plugins by declared name
findings.problem: A `kind:'plugin'` requirement's `name` is matched against two different namespaces. toposort.ts builds `byPackage` keyed by `manifest.packageName` (L31) and `pluginDeps` returns `req.name` looked up against that map (L66 -> L44 `byPackage.get(name)`). But the readiness gate (requirements.ts targetInfo 'plugin' case, L152-154) looks up `this.plugins.get(name)`, which is populated via `registerPlugin(plugin.name, ...)` in host.ts (L183, L202) — the DECLARED plugin name, not the package name. host.ts L187-193 documents that declared name and package name can differ. When they differ the two layers disagree on what `name` means: the ordering edge is dropped (byPackage miss -> treated as unknown dep), or the readiness gate misses (plugins map keyed by plugin.name). Latent only.
findings.fix: Standardize kind:'plugin' requirements on packageName and have registerPlugin store under packageName (host.ts already keys `loaded` by manifest.packageName for exactly this reason). Or register under both names. Document the convention on MoxxyRequirement.
findings.testStrategy: Integration test where dependent declares kind:'plugin', name:<dep packageName> while the dep's declared plugin.name differs from its packageName; assert BOTH correct load order AND that the readiness gate is satisfied.

findings.title: Appends compactor result with no defensive sessionId/turnId/source fill — type-compliant compactor emits invalid event
findings.problem: Line 177 does `await s.log.append(result as EmittedEvent)`. The compactor contract (packages/sdk/src/compactor.ts:29) returns `Omit<CompactionEvent, keyof EventBase>` — i.e. it is NOT required to provide sessionId, turnId, or source. The shared runCompactionIfNeeded explicitly defensive-fills these from ctx before appending and documents why ('a compactor that obeyed the type contract literally still emits a valid event' — compactor-helpers.ts:192-201). This plugin omits that fill, so a spec-compliant compactor that returns only {type, compactor, replacedRange, summary, tokensSaved} would append a CompactionEvent missing required EventBase fields, breaking projectMessagesFromLog/replay. The `as EmittedEvent` cast hides this at compile time.
findings.fix: Build the emittable defensively: spread result over { sessionId: s.id?/ctx.sessionId, turnId, source: 'compactor' } before append — or reuse the SDK helper (see u80-2) which already does this. Add the sessionId/turnId fields to CompactSessionShape so they are available.
findings.testStrategy: Add a /compact test whose compactor returns ONLY the Omit<CompactionEvent, keyof EventBase> fields; assert the appended event carries sessionId, turnId, and source:'compactor'.

findings.title: skill_created / skill_invoked events mint a fresh orphan turnId instead of the active turn's
findings.problem: Both the synthesize_skill and load_skill handlers append events with `turnId: session.startTurn().turnId`. session.startTurn() (session.ts:354) mints a brand-new random TurnId every call — it is NOT the turn the tool is executing in. run-turn.ts subscribes with `if (event.turnId !== turnId) return;` (run-turn.ts:40), so these events carry a turnId that does not match the running turn and get filtered out of the turn's event stream. Violates invariant #8 (filter subscribers by turnId). Net effect: desktop/TUI/any multiplexed surface never receives the skill_created / skill_invoked events for the turn that produced them.
findings.fix: Thread the active turnId through: have the tool handlers accept `(input, ctx)` and pass ctx.turnId into synthesizeSkill (add a turnId param) and into the load_skill skill_invoked append. synthesizeSkill has only this one caller, so the signature change is safe.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: drive a turn that calls synthesize_skill/load_skill and assert the emitted skill_created/skill_invoked event.turnId equals the turn's turnId (and survives the run-turn subscriber filter).

findings.title: No tests for the retained continue()/release() flow, tool filtering, or child->parent event mapping
findings.problem: run-child.test.ts only covers model resolution. Critical, branch-heavy logic is untested: (a) `continueChildTurn` — appends a user_prompt to the retained log, re-runs the strategy with skipStartEvent, then releases; the throw-on-missing path; the deferred subagent_completed. (b) `buildFilteredToolRegistry` — allowlist get/list/execute and the reject-on-disallowed path. (c) `mapChildEvent` — the per-type subtype mapping (chunk/tool_call/tool_result ok+err/error/abort) that drives all live TUI rendering. (d) the unknown-mode fallback warning + the no-default-mode hard failure. A regression in any of these silently corrupts live subagent progress or the pause/resume flow with no signal.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: spawn with retainSession then continue() and assert a second turn ran + subagent_completed fired once + entry released; release() drops without completing; allowedTools restricts list/get and rejects disallowed execute; a child emitting tool_call/tool_result/error produces the expected subagent_* parent events; unknown mode emits subagent_warning and runs on default; missing default yields the error result.
findings.testStrategy: Extend run-child.test.ts using the existing echo-model/Session harness plus a mode that emits tool_call_requested/tool_result/error to assert parent-log mapping.

findings.title: On the capping verify failure, the snapshot is restored + plugin reloaded twice
findings.problem: In the verify handler's failure branch, when existedBefore is true it already calls restoreSnapshot + unload + reload (lines 228-235). If that same failure also hits the attempt cap, escalate() is then called, which (lines 629-634) repeats restoreSnapshot + unload + reload for the existedBefore case. The work is idempotent so behavior is correct, but it doubles a recursive filesystem copy and a full plugin-host rescan on every capped MODIFY failure.
findings.fix: Pass a flag (e.g. alreadyRestored) into escalate, or set journal.state='rolled_back' before calling escalate so its restore branch is skipped; escalate should then only flip state to 'escalated' and persist.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on deps.reload/restoreSnapshot in the existing 'escalates after 2 cycles' index test and assert reload is called once per cycle, not twice on the capping cycle.

findings.title: awaitInput inside a nested workflow corrupts the resume path and strands the inner checkpoint
findings.problem: When a nested workflow pauses (runNestedWorkflow, 601-607), the inner `runExecutor` has already written its OWN checkpoint (via store.save in runExecutorLoop) and returned `result.runId` + `result.interactionAgentId`. runNestedWorkflow discards `result.runId` and only forwards `paused:true` + the inner `interactionAgentId`. The parent loop then writes a SECOND checkpoint whose `pendingStepId` is the `workflow`-type step. On resume, resumeWorkflowRun finds that workflow step and calls `spawner.continue({ childSessionId: innerInteractionAgentId })`, marking the PARENT step completed with the child's reply text — it never re-enters the nested DAG, so the nested workflow's remaining steps never run. Meanwhile the inner checkpoint file is never removed (only the parent's is), leaking until the 7-day sweep.
findings.fix: Either (a) explicitly reject awaitInput inside a nested workflow at runtime (mirror the loop-body guard at 742-751) — fail loudly rather than half-resume; or (b) properly chain: have runNestedWorkflow surface the inner `runId`, and have resumeWorkflowRun detect a workflow-typed pending step and delegate to a nested resume, then continue the parent DAG. Option (a) is the minimal correct fix and matches the schema's awaitInput-on-prompt/skill-only intent.
findings.testStrategy: Build an outer workflow whose step calls a nested workflow containing an awaitInput step; assert the run either rejects cleanly OR resumes the nested DAG to completion AND leaves zero orphaned checkpoint files in the store dir.

findings.title: onError:'retry' is behaviorally identical to 'fail'; retries fire regardless of onError mode
findings.problem: runStep retries `1 + max(0, step.retries)` times whenever `retries > 0`, completely independent of `step.onError`. After retries are exhausted, the abort gate uses `step.onError !== 'continue'` (356/760), so 'retry' takes the exact same abort path as 'fail'. The `'retry'` enum value therefore has no executor behavior distinct from `'fail'` — retrying is driven solely by the `retries` count. The 'retries a flaky step' test pairs onError:'retry' with retries:2, masking that retries:2 alone (with onError:'fail') produces identical behavior. This is a latent semantic trap: an author setting onError:'fail' + retries:3 silently gets 3 retries.
findings.fix: Make the contract explicit: either gate retries on `step.onError === 'retry'` (so 'fail'/'continue' run exactly one attempt and 'retry' uses the count), or document that retries is orthogonal and drop the redundant 'retry' enum member (keeping it only as a UI affordance). Aligning the executor with the three-valued enum removes the surprise.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test asserting onError:'fail' + retries:2 runs exactly the intended number of attempts under the chosen contract (1 if gated, 3 if orthogonal-and-documented).

findings.title: A hard step failure does not break the current wave; later wave steps still run and emit completed events
findings.problem: Inside the wave for-loop, a step failing with onError!='continue' sets `aborted = true` but does not `break`. The remaining steps already selected into `wave` continue to execute (runStep only checks the external `signal.aborted`, not the internal `aborted` flag). This wastes subagent/tool calls after the run has logically aborted and emits `workflow_step_completed` events for steps that ran after the failure, which can confuse progress/event consumers about ordering relative to the failure.
findings.fix: Add `if (aborted) break;` after the failure branch (or check `aborted` at the top of the wave for-loop) so a hard failure stops scheduling the rest of the wave. Keep onError:'continue' failures non-breaking.
findings.testStrategy: Wave of 2 independent steps where the first fails onError:'fail'; assert the second never runs (not in order[]) and no completed event is emitted for it.

findings.title: Resume re-emits workflow_started with the full step count mid-run
findings.problem: resumeWorkflowRun emits `workflow_resumed` (447), then calls runExecutorLoop which unconditionally emits `workflow_started` again (212) with the original `steps.length`. A consumer that initializes/reset progress state on `workflow_started` (e.g. a progress bar or step tracker) would reset to zero on every resume even though most steps are already settled. Current desktop/client consumers happen to key off resumed/completed/failed, so impact is limited today, but the duplicate start event is a latent inconsistency.
findings.fix: Parameterize runExecutorLoop with a `resumed` flag (or split the initial `workflow_started` emit out of the loop body so resume drives the loop without re-emitting start). Emit `workflow_started` only on a fresh run.
findings.testStrategy: Resume a paused run with an emit spy; assert exactly one `workflow_started` across the whole run lifecycle and that `workflow_resumed` precedes continued step events.

clusters.title: Decompose the god-files / god-functions into single-responsibility units
clusters.oneLine: RemoteSession (600-line, 11 client-view facades), RunnerServer (~40 handleX across 6 domains), sdk/mode-helpers.ts (797 lines, 5 concerns), DAG executor (883 lines), desktop electron main index.ts (1054 lines), WorkflowCanvas (1330-line file), Composer (443 lines/24 hooks), desktop-ipc-contract (1129-line barrel), and the inline tool-factory god-functions (coreTools 229, buildWebhookTools 344, buildBuiltinsCore 425, buildWorkflowsIntegration 375, createWindow 346). Also PluginHost.unload hardcodes 17 unregister calls. Co-locate each surface client-view with its server handler to stop drift.
clusters.rationale: High-value maintainability but large, cross-cutting, behavior-sensitive splits that each merit their own PR + careful test pinning. Propose only; sequence after the Tier-1/2 dead-code and dup removals shrink these files.

findings.title: RemoteSession god-class: one 600-line class hosts 11 unrelated client-view facades
findings.problem: RemoteSession (1106 lines total) is a single class implementing ClientSession plus 11 private makeXView() factory methods (makeProvidersView, makeModesView, makeToolsView, makeCommandsView, makeSkillsView, makeTranscribersView, makeSynthesizersView, makePermissionsView, makeMcpAdminView, makeProviderAdminView, makeWorkflowsView). Each builds a self-contained JSON-RPC-backed sub-surface (providers/modes/skills/mcp/workflows/etc.) with no shared state beyond `this.peer`. Adding any new sub-surface forces editing this one file, and the file mixes transport plumbing (TurnStream, attach, reconnect, resolver routing) with a dozen domain facades.
findings.fix: Extract each makeXView into its own module under packages/runner/src/client-views/ (one file per surface, e.g. workflows-view.ts, mcp-admin-view.ts) as a pure factory `(peer: JsonRpcPeer, info: () => SessionInfo) => XClientView`. RemoteSession keeps only transport/turn/attach/resolver logic and calls the factories. Move the facade-local interfaces into their respective files; re-export shared types through @moxxy/sdk (invariant 3).
findings.testStrategy: Existing runner protocol/round-trip tests cover behavior; add per-view unit tests that pump a stub JsonRpcPeer. No protocol bump (pure refactor).
findings.complexity: 1 file / 1106 LOC -> core ~450 LOC + ~11 view modules of 20-60 LOC each

findings.title: RunnerServer god-class: ~40 handleX RPC methods for 6 unrelated domains in one class
findings.problem: RunnerServer (757 lines) carries ~40 private handleX methods spanning turn control (handleAttach/RunTurn/Abort/SessionReset), providers (handleProviderSetActive/SetEnabled/RefreshReady/Configure), permissions, transcribe/synthesize, MCP (handleMcpListServers/EnableAndAttach/Detach), workflows (handleWorkflowList/SetEnabled/Run/ValidateDraft/Save/GetRun/Resume) and surfaces (handleSurfaceList/Open/Input/Resize/Close). It is the mirror of finding #1 on the server side: each domain group is independent and only shares `this.session` + broadcast. Every new runner capability edits this monolith.
findings.fix: Split into per-domain handler modules (provider-handlers.ts, workflow-handlers.ts, surface-handlers.ts, mcp-handlers.ts, media-handlers.ts) exporting `(ctx: { session, broadcast, ... }) => Record<RunnerMethod, Handler>`; RunnerServer composes the maps into its dispatch table. Keep turn/attach/resolver logic in server.ts. Mirror the same boundaries as the client-view split (#1) so the protocol surface stays legible.
findings.testStrategy: Runner protocol integration tests already exercise each RPC; add a dispatch-table assertion that every RunnerMethod has exactly one handler. Pure refactor, no protocol version change.
findings.complexity: 757 LOC class -> ~300 LOC dispatcher + 5 handler modules of 60-120 LOC

findings.title: sdk/mode-helpers.ts bundles 5 independent concerns into one 797-line module
findings.problem: mode-helpers.ts is the shared library every mode (default/goal/deep-research) builds on, but it concatenates five unrelated responsibilities: (a) message projection from the event log (projectMessagesFromLog/projectMessages, lines ~79-405, ~250 LOC including ProjectMessagesOptions/ProjectedMessages), (b) provider-stream collection (collectProviderStream, 432-643, ~210 LOC), (c) single-shot turn (runSingleShotTurn, 644-701), (d) stuck-loop detection (StuckSignal/StuckLoopDetector/createStuckLoopDetector, 702-782), and (e) a generic stableHash util (783-797). They share almost no internal state; the only thing binding them is being imported by mode packages.
findings.fix: Split into focused files under packages/sdk/src/mode/ (project-messages.ts, collect-stream.ts, single-shot.ts, stuck-loop.ts, stable-hash.ts) and re-export from mode-helpers.ts as a barrel so existing import sites are unchanged. stableHash is generic and should live in a util module (other callers may want it without dragging in stream/projection code). Preserves invariant 1 (no internal deps added).
findings.testStrategy: Existing mode tests + projectMessages/collectProviderStream unit tests gate behavior; barrel keeps API stable so no caller changes needed.
findings.complexity: 1x797 LOC -> barrel + 5 files of 60-260 LOC; same public surface

findings.title: DAG executor mixes serialization, scheduling loop, step kinds, and resume in one 883-line file
findings.problem: dag.ts is the single largest non-UI logic file and conflates four separable concerns: (1) state (de)serialization for resume — serializeStates/restoreStates/buildStepResults/buildRunResult (149-208); (2) the scheduler core — runExecutorLoop (209-380) + runExecutor (382-401); (3) resume orchestration — resumeWorkflowRun (403-487); (4) per-step-kind execution — runStep/runStepOnce/runNestedWorkflow/runLogicStep/runLoopStep/evaluateLoopCondition/buildSubagentSpecWithDeps (511-877). Loop/logic step semantics are intricate and would be far easier to test and evolve in isolation from the scheduler.
findings.fix: Split into executor/state-serde.ts (serialize/restore/build*), executor/scheduler.ts (runExecutorLoop/runExecutor/resumeWorkflowRun), executor/steps.ts (runStep/runStepOnce/runNestedWorkflow + buildSubagentSpecWithDeps), executor/logic-loop.ts (runLogicStep/runLoopStep/evaluateLoopCondition/buildUpstreamBlock). dag.ts keeps only the defineWorkflowExecutor registration glue. Keep ExecutorContext as the shared seam passed between modules.
findings.testStrategy: Workflow executor + resume tests cover the loop; new focused tests for logic-loop.ts (condition eval, loop body skip) and state-serde.ts round-trip. Behavior-preserving.
findings.complexity: 883 LOC -> ~5 files of 90-200 LOC; defineWorkflowExecutor stub stays in dag.ts

findings.title: buildWebhookTools is one 344-line function inlining 11 independent tool definitions
findings.problem: buildWebhookTools returns an array of 11 fully-inlined ToolDefs (webhook_create, webhook_list, webhook_delete, webhook_update, webhook_test, webhook_status, webhook_set_public_url, webhook_clear_public_url, webhook_tunnel_start, webhook_tunnel_stop, webhook_setup_guide) each with its own zod schema + handler. The whole tool surface lives in one giant function literal, so a change to one tool requires scrolling a 344-line body and every tool shares one closure scope (easy to accidentally cross-reference state).
findings.fix: Extract each tool into a small `defineWebhookCreateTool(deps): ToolDef` style factory in tools/ (one file per tool or grouped create/read/mutate/tunnel files); buildWebhookTools becomes a 15-line composition that spreads the factories. Mirrors the already-modular plugin-self-update pattern (xTool() per tool). Use defineX factories (invariant 10).
findings.testStrategy: Existing webhook tool tests cover handlers; per-factory unit tests become trivial once isolated. No behavior change.
findings.complexity: 1x344 LOC function -> ~11 factories of 15-40 LOC + 15-LOC composer

findings.title: coreTools() inlines 8 Tier-2 core-update tools in one 229-line function
findings.problem: While plugin-self-update is otherwise nicely modular (each Tier-1 tool is its own classifyTool/beginTool/verifyTool/applyTool/rollbackTool/statusTool factory), the Tier-2 core path collapses 8 tools (self_update_core_preflight/begin/write/edit/verify/apply/rollback/status) into a single 229-line coreTools() literal. This is the one outlier breaking the file's own per-tool-factory convention and is where the riskiest core-patching logic lives.
findings.fix: Split coreTools into per-tool factories (coreWriteTool, coreEditTool, coreVerifyTool, coreApplyTool, ...) in a core-tools/ subdir, matching the Tier-1 style; coreTools() becomes a thin spread. Improves auditability of the most security-sensitive (core-patching) tools.
findings.testStrategy: Existing self-update core tests (the verify-desktop-packaged path is out of scope) plus per-tool unit tests post-split. Behavior-preserving.
findings.complexity: 229 LOC function -> 8 factories of 15-45 LOC + composer

findings.title: createWindow() is a 346-line setup procedure mixing window, CSP, loopback, widget, and menu wiring
findings.problem: createWindow (185-530) and app.whenReady (732-1010, 279 LOC) are the two heaviest blocks in the 1053-line electron main entry. createWindow alone wires window construction, dev-vs-prod renderer loading, loopback-origin selection, the floating widget window, and OAuth user-agent scrubbing in one procedure. The main file already delegates most ipcMain handlers to desktop-host (only 4 handle calls remain here), so the remaining bloat is these two mega-procedures.
findings.fix: Extract create-main-window.ts (window + renderer-load + loopback decision), create-widget-window.ts, and a bootstrap.ts that owns the whenReady sequence; index.ts becomes a thin orchestrator wiring app lifecycle events to those modules. Keep deep-link/menu helpers in their own files.
findings.testStrategy: Electron main is integration-tested via verify-desktop-packaged (boot probe). Refactor is structural; the armBootProbe smoke gate guards regressions.

findings.title: desktop-ipc-contract is a single 1129-line barrel spanning ~12 domain contracts
findings.problem: The IPC contract package is one flat index.ts holding every renderer<->main interface: connection lifecycle, onboarding, prefs, workflows, mobile gateway, settings/providers/mcp/vault/skills, desks, chat, app-update, the IpcEvents map, the IpcCommands map (~430 LOC by itself, 512-1001), and the REMOTE_ALLOWED/DISALLOWED command sets. It is pure types so risk is low, but it is the file every desktop/mobile change touches and the IpcCommands interface alone is enormous.
findings.fix: Split into domain files under src/ (connection.ts, settings.ts, desks.ts, chat.ts, surfaces.ts, app-update.ts, mobile.ts) each owning its interfaces and contributing its slice of IpcCommands/IpcEvents via interface-merging or composed unions; index.ts re-exports + assembles the final IpcCommands. Keeps the single public surface while letting each domain be edited atomically. Pure-type package, no runtime risk.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck is the gate (consumers import named types); add a compile-time assertion that REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS keys are a subset of IpcCommandName. No behavior change.
findings.complexity: 1129 LOC barrel -> index assembler + ~8 domain files of 60-200 LOC

findings.title: WorkflowCanvas is a 503-line component (1330-line file) carrying pan/zoom, drag-connect, and layout
findings.problem: WorkflowCanvas.tsx is the single largest file in the repo. The exported component body is 503 lines (138-640) with 39 hook/handler sites mixing camera pan/zoom state, drag-to-connect edge creation, node layout, and selection — all in one function. The file also defines 7 sub-components (ZoomControls, InsertNodeMenu, TempLine, Edge, NodeCard[192 LOC], Handle, BodyDropHandle) that are reasonable to keep but inflate the module.
findings.fix: Extract camera/pan-zoom into a useCanvasCamera() hook and drag-to-connect into a useDragConnect() hook (apps/desktop/src/workflows/canvas/); move NodeCard/Edge/Handle/InsertNodeMenu into canvas/ component files. WorkflowCanvas keeps composition + reducer dispatch. This isolates the gesture state machines that are the trickiest part.
findings.testStrategy: WorkflowsPanel.test.tsx already covers drag-to-connect (lines 378-561); extracting the hook lets it be unit-tested directly. Component-level behavior unchanged.

findings.title: buildBuiltinsCore is a 425-line wiring function with inline tool defs and runtime concerns
findings.problem: buildBuiltinsCore wires the entire builtin plugin set into a 425-line function: a long `entries[]` registration array (providers, terminal, system-control, oauth, slash-cmds, view, subagents, plugins-admin, self-update, voice, provider-admin, mcp-admin, skills), plus inline tool definitions (e.g. set_voice/list_voices) and the live setPluginEnabledLive plug/unplug closure, plus scheduler/workflows/webhooks/security wiring. Distinct concerns (provider entries vs voice tools vs security gate) are interleaved in one scope.
findings.fix: Move the inline voice tools into the existing voice plugin (they don't belong in cli/setup); extract the entries[] into a builtin-entries.ts data module and the live-plugin enable/disable closure into plugin-toggle.ts. buildBuiltinsCore becomes orchestration that consumes those. Keeps wiring legible and stops cli/setup from owning model-tool definitions.
findings.testStrategy: CLI smoke (run-the-cli) plus setup unit tests; the builtin set is enumerable so add a snapshot of registered plugin/tool names to guard the move.
findings.complexity: 425-LOC function -> ~150-LOC orchestrator + entries data module + toggle helper; voice tools relocated

findings.title: Composer is a 443-line component with 24 hook sites mixing input, attachments, slash-cmds, and modes
findings.problem: Composer.tsx (533 LOC) has a 443-line component body holding 24 useState/useCallback/useMemo/useEffect/useRef sites. It conflates text-input state, attachment pick/drop handling, slash-command palette triggering, mode/goal modal control, and send orchestration in a single function — a classic over-grown desktop input component.
findings.fix: Extract useComposerAttachments() (pick/drop/paste state) and useSlashTrigger() hooks; lift send orchestration into a useComposerSubmit() callback module. Composer keeps layout + wiring. Reduces the hook surface per concern and makes the attachment path independently testable.
findings.testStrategy: Desktop renderer tests around composer behavior; extracting hooks enables unit tests for attachment normalization and slash detection. Behavior-preserving.

findings.title: Runner client-views and server-handlers drift apart because the two halves of each surface live far apart
findings.problem: The runner protocol is implemented as three parallel monoliths: protocol.ts (611 LOC) declares the wire types, server.ts (757) the handleX methods, remote-session.ts (1106) the makeXView facades. Each surface (e.g. workflows) is split across all three files with no co-location, so adding/changing a surface means hunting three large files and it is easy to add a server handler without the matching client view (or vice versa). This is the structural root that makes findings #1 and #2 high-churn.
findings.fix: After splitting #1 and #2 per-domain, co-locate each surface's three slices under packages/runner/src/surfaces/<name>/ (wire-types.ts, server-handlers.ts, client-view.ts) with a shared RunnerMethod enum slice. A single index assembles them. This makes each surface an atomic unit and a compile-time map enforces handler<->view symmetry.
findings.testStrategy: Add a structural test asserting every RunnerMethod has both a server handler and a client-view caller. Existing protocol round-trip tests unchanged.
findings.complexity: 3 monoliths -> per-surface folders; net LOC similar, coupling localized

findings.title: buildWorkflowsIntegration is a single 375-line setup function
findings.problem: buildWorkflowsIntegration (63-437) wires the entire workflows feature (executor registration, draft tools, run store, scheduler reuse, plugin assembly) in one 375-line function. Like buildBuiltinsCore it is a wiring monolith where independently-evolving pieces (executor wiring vs draft/save tools vs run persistence) share one scope.
findings.fix: Decompose into smaller composables: register-executor.ts, build-workflow-tools.ts, wire-run-store.ts, each returning a slice the top builder spreads. Keeps the public buildWorkflowsIntegration signature.
findings.testStrategy: Workflows setup + tool tests; snapshot the registered tool/executor names to guard the move.

findings.title: PluginHost.unload hardcodes 17 registry.unregister calls — registry list duplicated and fragile
findings.problem: PluginHost.unload (261-) manually unregisters from 17 named registries (tools, providers, modes, compactors, cacheStrategies, viewRenderers, tunnelProviders, channels, surfaces, agents, commands, transcribers, synthesizers, embedders, isolators, workflowExecutors, ...). applyPlugin (311) mirrors the same 17-registry list on the register side. Adding a new registry kind requires editing both sides plus the LoadedRecord shape; an omission silently leaks registrations on unload (a correctness hazard for hot-reload, invariant 9-adjacent).
findings.fix: Drive registration/unregistration from a single REGISTRY_KINDS table mapping kind -> (plugin field, registry, record-names field). unload/applyPlugin iterate the table so adding a registry is one entry. Removes the duplicated 17-line lists and guarantees register/unregister stay in lockstep.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that registers a plugin contributing to every registry kind, unloads it, and asserts every registry is empty (catches future leaks).
findings.complexity: Two parallel 17-line lists -> one table + 2 loops

findings.title: index.ts is a 1054-line god-file mixing 8+ unrelated main-process concerns
findings.problem: The Electron entry mixes window construction, the macOS/Win application + tray menus, focus-mode wiring, moxxy:// deep-link transport, OAuth user-agent scrubbing, the loopback HTTPS cert + CSP install, the hot-update boot probe, the WS/mobile-gateway bootstrap, and shutdown into one module with many module-level mutable singletons (pool, mainWindow, wsServer, mobileGateway, loopback, loopbackCert, trayInstance, pendingDeepLinks, rendererReady). This is hard to test (no .test.ts exists for index.ts; only floor-runner-protocol and ws-bridge are tested) and couples deep-link routing, OAuth security, and update health into one blast radius. Several of these blocks (parseDeepLink/handleDeepLink/focusMain, cleanOAuthUserAgent, armBootProbe, the OAUTH_HOST_PATTERNS builder, the cert verify proc) are pure/near-pure and already shaped like extractable single-responsibility units.
findings.fix: Extract cohesive units into sibling modules with explicit deps so each is unit-testable: deep-link.ts (parseDeepLink/handleDeepLink/focusMain + the pending buffer), oauth-window.ts (cleanOAuthUserAgent + OAUTH_HOST_PATTERNS builder + setWindowOpenHandler), boot-probe.ts (armBootProbe with injected readConfirmed/markConfirmed/markBad/appendBootLog), loopback-tls.ts (cert verify proc + certificate-error). Keep index.ts as orchestration only.
findings.testStrategy: After extraction, unit-test parseDeepLink (valid moxxy://, non-moxxy scheme, malformed), cleanOAuthUserAgent (strips Electron/app tokens, idempotent), and armBootProbe state transitions (confirm-via-DOM, timeout->markBad+relaunch) with a fake webContents.

findings.title: WorkflowCanvas is a 1300-line god-file mixing transform math, pure graph helpers, and many sub-components
findings.problem: One module holds: pan/zoom transform math, pointer gesture state machine, the canvas component, ZoomControls, InsertNodeMenu, TempLine, Edge, NodeCard, Handle, BodyDropHandle, plus pure non-React helpers (topoOrder, nodeAt, inBodyRegion, portOrigin, disconnectEdge, isEditableTarget, preview, labelOf). The pure helpers are reusable and independently testable but are buried in the component file, which is why topoOrder/disconnectEdge/finishConnection have no direct unit tests.
findings.fix: Extract the pure graph/geometry helpers into a sibling `canvas-graph.ts` (topoOrder, nodeAt, inBodyRegion, portOrigin, disconnectEdge) and the leaf presentational components (Edge, NodeCard, Handle, ZoomControls, InsertNodeMenu, TempLine) into a `canvas/` folder. Unit-test the extracted helpers.
findings.testStrategy: After extraction, add focused unit tests for topoOrder (longest-path layering, cycle fallback) and disconnectEdge (each edge kind routes to the right inverse op).

findings.title: buildBuiltinsCore is a ~420-line god-function assembling every builtin plus inline plugin definitions
findings.problem: buildBuiltinsCore mixes the static plugin manifest, several inline IIFE plugin definitions (voice-admin with two full defineTool bodies, provider-admin/mcp-admin stash wiring), the setPluginEnabledLive closure, the session.pluginsAdmin view, and scheduler/workflows/webhooks/security construction in one function. This is hard to test in isolation, couples unrelated concerns, and forces the whole file to be re-read on any single-plugin change. The voice-admin plugin in particular is a complete plugin authored inline rather than living in its own package (every other provider/mode/etc is a package).
findings.fix: Extract voice-admin into its own @moxxy/voice-admin (or plugin-voice-admin) package added to `entries` like the rest; move setPluginEnabledLive + session.pluginsAdmin wiring and the scheduler/webhooks/workflows/security sub-builders into named helper functions (one concern each). Keep buildBuiltinsCore as a thin assembler.
findings.testStrategy: After extraction, add a focused unit test for the voice-admin tools (set_voice 'system' clears, unknown name throws) — currently impossible to import without booting the whole builtins assembly.

findings.title: coreTools() is a ~230-line god-factory mixing 8 tool definitions, journal mutation, and overlay orchestration
findings.problem: coreTools defines preflight/begin/write/edit/verify/apply/rollback/status inline in one function. Each handler inlines journal read-modify-write, attempt bookkeeping, snapshotDir construction and emit wiring, so the file mixes tool-schema declaration with transaction state-machine transitions. The state transitions (provisioned->verified->staged_restart->committed/rolled_back) are not centralized, making the legal-transition invariants hard to see or test in isolation (only begin's serialize-guard and apply's verified-gate are enforced; verify silently keeps prior state on failure via `res.ok ? 'verified' : journal.state`).
findings.fix: Extract a CoreTxn state-machine module (transition(journal, event) with explicit legal transitions + a single applyTransition that persists) and have thin tool handlers call it; same for Tier-1. This makes the state machine unit-testable and keeps each tool handler single-responsibility.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the extracted transition function for each legal/illegal transition; tool handlers then just wire schemas.

findings.title: projectMessages mixes 5 distinct concerns in one ~260-line function
findings.problem: projectMessages interleaves compaction-range resolution, elision/stub projection, reasoning-block ordering for Anthropic, orphan-tool_use fallback synthesis, and stable-prefix index tracking in a single function with nested closures (flush/recordStable) and shared mutable state (pendingAssistant/pendingReasoning/pendingAssistantMaxSeq/...). This is the single most correctness-critical fold in the loop (every provider call depends on it) yet is hard to test in isolation and easy to break when adding an event type — the per-event switch and the flush() orphan loop both mutate overlapping state.
findings.fix: Extract pure sub-steps: (a) a `projectUserPrompt(event, el)` returning blocks, (b) an `OrphanResolver` precomputed once, (c) a small reducer object encapsulating pendingAssistant/pendingReasoning with explicit flush() — so each is independently unit-testable. Keep the public signature; this is an internal decomposition.
findings.testStrategy: After extraction, unit-test each sub-step (attachment expansion, orphan synthesis, reasoning ordering) directly; keep existing loop-helpers/token-efficiency integration tests as regression guards.

clusters.title: Route all home-path / proto-constant derivations through the shared helpers
clusters.oneLine: bin.ts and cli/update/check.ts re-derive the moxxy-home path and ignore MOXXY_HOME (the shared moxxyHome/moxxyPath exists); the MOXXY_PCM16_24KHZ_MIME proto constant is duplicated across 3 packages; countNodes is byte-identical in plugin-view and core (re-routed via SDK).
clusters.rationale: Consistency fixes that also correct a real env-var bug (MOXXY_HOME ignored). Low risk; route through the existing shared helper / SDK constant.

findings.title: bin.ts reinvents the moxxy-home path and ignores MOXXY_HOME (shared moxxyHome() helper exists)
findings.problem: `finalizeStagedCoreUpdate(path.join(os.homedir(), '.moxxy'))` hardcodes `~/.moxxy`, ignoring the documented `MOXXY_HOME` override. A user who sets MOXXY_HOME (which bin.ts's own --help advertises as 'override the ~/.moxxy data directory') stages Tier-2 core updates under the overridden dir but the finalize step on next boot scans the default dir, so the staged patch is never committed. @moxxy/sdk already exports `moxxyHome()` (fs-utils.ts:48) precisely to centralize this and its docstring notes plugins that inlined `~/.moxxy` 'ignored MOXXY_HOME'.
findings.fix: Import `moxxyHome` from `@moxxy/sdk` and call `finalizeStagedCoreUpdate(moxxyHome())`. Apply the same swap in self-update.ts (unit 24) so writer and reader agree under MOXXY_HOME.
findings.testStrategy: Set MOXXY_HOME to a temp dir, stage a fake core txn (state=staged_restart) there, run any CLI command, assert the journal flips to committed; assert default ~/.moxxy is untouched.

findings.title: Update-check cache path hardcodes ~/.moxxy, ignores MOXXY_HOME
findings.problem: defaultCacheFile() inlines path.join(os.homedir(), '.moxxy', 'update-check.json'), bypassing the MOXXY_HOME env override. The SDK's moxxyPath()/moxxyHome() is the documented single source of truth for the moxxy home (its doc literally says 'previously half the plugins inlined ~/.moxxy and ignored MOXXY_HOME'). With MOXXY_HOME set, the TUI banner and `moxxy update` read/write the cache in the wrong directory, so the cache is never reused and the env redirect is silently violated.
findings.fix: Import moxxyPath from '@moxxy/sdk' and return moxxyPath('update-check.json').
findings.testStrategy: Add a test setting process.env.MOXXY_HOME to a tmp dir and asserting refreshCheck/readCachedCheck round-trips the cache under that dir.

findings.title: Protocol constant MOXXY_PCM16_24KHZ_MIME duplicated across 3 packages instead of sourced from one place
findings.problem: The wire MIME tag 'audio/x-moxxy-pcm16-24khz' is a cross-package protocol value the Codex transcriber keys on, yet it is independently redeclared as a literal string in three places: here (pcm16.ts L13), packages/plugin-stt-whisper/src/audio.ts L13, and hardcoded inline in packages/plugin-cli/src/session/use-voice-input.ts L15. A drift in any one silently breaks transcription (the transcriber would no longer wrap the bytes in a WAV header) with no compile-time signal.
findings.fix: Hoist the constant to the typed public surface (@moxxy/sdk, e.g. alongside transcriber types) and have all three sites import it. SDK has zero internal deps so this is invariant-safe. At minimum, plugin-cli should import the existing export rather than redeclare the literal.
findings.testStrategy: After consolidation, a single source-of-truth import; add an assertion test in the transcriber that the constant equals the expected wire value to lock the literal.

findings.title: countNodes is a byte-identical copy of @moxxy/core's exported countNodes
findings.problem: plugin-view defines a private countNodes that is character-for-character identical to the one exported from @moxxy/core (packages/core/src/view/parse.ts:506-509, re-exported in core/index.ts:47). The same node-counting fold lives in two packages; either can drift (e.g. one starts counting differently, or adds a depth/node cap) without the other, silently changing the `nodeCount` reported to the model versus what the renderer computes internally.
findings.fix: Move the canonical countNodes into @moxxy/sdk next to the ViewNode type it operates on (packages/sdk/src/view-renderer.ts), export it from the SDK barrel, and have BOTH @moxxy/core and @moxxy/plugin-view import it. plugin-view already depends on @moxxy/sdk at runtime, so this removes the duplication without dragging in @moxxy/core, and routes the shared helper through the SDK (invariant 3).
findings.testStrategy: Add an SDK unit test for countNodes (text=1, nested element fold); keep plugin-view's present-view.test.ts nodeCount assertion (view>stack>2 text = 6) as a cross-package regression that the shared impl still matches.

clusters.title: Consolidate duplicated OAuth/compaction/onboarding flow helpers
clusters.oneLine: oauth_get_token refresh-and-store is duplicated across tools and ensureFreshToken and drops persisted extras (account_id); device-flow abort throws bare Error vs MoxxyError; openai device-flow hand-rolls the authorization_code exchange instead of the shared exchange helper; plugin-commands compactSession reimplements runCompactionIfNeeded; CliStep reinvents useOnboarding().install.
clusters.rationale: Helper-reuse consolidation in the auth/compaction paths; medium risk because it touches token persistence, so port tests + add account_id-retention coverage.

findings.title: Refresh-and-store logic duplicated across oauth_get_token and ensureFreshTokens
findings.problem: The 'refresh access token, merge rotated refresh_token, persist' sequence is copy-pasted between tools.ts (237-261) and ensure-fresh.ts refreshAndStore (67-136), but the tool copy is missing the lock (u91-1), the rotation-race retry, and the extras merge (u91-3). This is the over-coupling / reinvent-a-shared-helper smell: two divergent copies of a security-critical critical section.
findings.fix: Extract one exported refreshAndStore(profile-or-storedCreds, vault) and have buildOauthGetTokenTool call ensureFreshTokens (or the shared helper) instead of re-implementing. Erases the three divergences in one move.
findings.testStrategy: After refactor, the existing ensure-fresh tests plus a new oauth_get_token concurrency test (u91-1) both exercise the single helper.

findings.title: oauth_get_token refresh drops persisted extras (account_id) — diverges from ensureFreshTokens
findings.problem: When oauth_get_token refreshes, it calls storeTokenSet WITHOUT the meta.extras argument, so the previously stored oauth/<provider>/extras (account_id, org_id, team_slug) is NOT re-written. Because storeTokenSet only writes extras when present and never deletes, the old extras key happens to survive here — but the tool also never re-derives fresh extras the way ensureFreshTokens does (ensure-fresh.ts:123-126 merges stored extras + fresh extraction). The two refresh paths are inconsistent and the duplication is a latent bug: any future change to storeTokenSet's stale-key handling (see u91-2) would silently wipe account_id on tool-driven refreshes.
findings.fix: Unify on a single refreshAndStore helper (shared by ensure-fresh and the tool) that always re-reads, merges, and re-persists extras. At minimum pass stored.extras through in the tool's storeTokenSet call.
findings.testStrategy:
Store creds with extras={account_id:'x'}, expire, call oauth_get_token; assert oauth/

/extras still resolves to account_id after refresh.

findings.title: Device-flow abort throws bare Error; browser-flow uses MoxxyError(NETWORK_ABORTED)
findings.problem: pollUntil surfaces abort/timeout as plain `new Error(\`${label} aborted\`)` (line 45), `new Error('aborted')` (sleep, line 60/64), and `new Error(\`${label} timed out…\`)` (line 55). The sibling browser callback-server (callback-server.ts:48-57) rejects aborts with a typed MoxxyError code 'NETWORK_ABORTED' and timeouts with 'OAUTH_FLOW_TIMEOUT'. The whole rest of the package raises MoxxyError. So a turn-cancel during a device-code login bubbles an untyped Error that downstream MoxxyError.isMoxxyError / code-based handling (e.g. credential-lock.ts:102) cannot classify, producing a generic/ugly failure instead of a clean cancel or timeout message.
findings.fix: Throw MoxxyError with code 'NETWORK_ABORTED' on abort and 'OAUTH_FLOW_TIMEOUT' on deadline, mirroring callback-server.ts; include label/timeout in context. The sleep() helper can reject with the same typed error or a sentinel the loop re-wraps.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test pollUntil with an already-aborted signal asserts the rejection is a MoxxyError with code NETWORK_ABORTED; with timeoutMs:1 and a never-resolving fn asserts code OAUTH_FLOW_TIMEOUT.

findings.title: openai poll() hand-rolls authorization_code exchange instead of shared exchangeCodeForToken
findings.problem: The openai adapter's poll() inlines an authorization_code -> token exchange: builds the same URLSearchParams (grant_type=authorization_code, code, redirect_uri, client_id, code_verifier), POSTs to tokenUrl, then duplicates the exact `classifyHttpStatus(...) ?? new MoxxyError({code:'AUTH_INVALID', ...})` error handling and `parseTokenResponse(await res.json())`. This is a near-verbatim reimplementation of the exported shared helper `exchangeCodeForToken` (oauth/token-exchange.ts), which browser-flow.ts already consumes. Reinventing a shared helper is exactly the consistency violation the invariants call out, and it drifts (e.g. the helper sets `Accept: application/json` and supports clientSecret; this copy does neither).
findings.fix: Replace lines 117-143 with `return { done: await exchangeCodeForToken({ tokenUrl: opts.tokenUrl, code: data.authorization_code, redirectUri: exchangeRedirectUri, clientId, codeVerifier: data.code_verifier }) };` and import exchangeCodeForToken from ../oauth/token-exchange.js. Drop the now-dead local error/parse block.
findings.testStrategy: Existing profile.test.ts openai poll() test asserts the exchange request body fields and the parsed token; keep it green after the swap (the helper produces the same request and parseTokenResponse output). Add a case asserting a non-ok exchange throws a classified MoxxyError.

findings.title: compactSession reimplements the shared runCompactionIfNeeded compaction flow instead of reusing it
findings.problem: compactSession() hand-rolls the exact pipeline already centralized in @moxxy/sdk's runCompactionIfNeeded / resolveModelContext / estimateContextTokens: resolve context window with a models[0] fallback, build a TokenBudget, call compact(), guard on tokensSaved<=0 || empty summary, append the CompactionEvent. resolveActiveContextWindow (191-203) is a near-duplicate of SDK resolveModelContext (compactor-helpers.ts:110-117) and of plugin-cli/helpers resolveContextWindow (acknowledged in the comment at 196-197). This is reinvention of a shared SDK helper — the invariant the audit specifically flags — and means the two compaction paths can drift (e.g. the provider-forwarding bug in u80-1 exists only because this copy diverged).
findings.fix: Replace the body with a call into a shared SDK entry point (extend runCompactionIfNeeded or add a thin manual-compact wrapper) that takes the Session's compactor/log/provider and the force semantics, returning saved-count + tokensSaved so the plugin only formats the message. Drop resolveActiveContextWindow in favor of resolveModelContext.
findings.testStrategy: Move the existing /compact test to exercise the shared helper; keep a plugin-level test asserting the formatted message only.

findings.title: CliStep reinvents useOnboarding().install instead of reusing the shared controller
findings.problem: useOnboarding already exposes a full CLI-install controller — install.run() (invokes onboarding.installMoxxyCli + refresh), install.progress, install.error, install.lastExitCode, install.reset (useOnboarding.ts:87-109,144-151). CliStep ignores it and re-implements the whole thing inline: it directly invokes onboarding.status, subscribes to onboarding.install.progress with its own log buffer, and calls onboarding.installMoxxyCli with its own error/exit-code handling. This is copy-paste of a shared client helper (the audit invariant explicitly flags reinventing shared @moxxy helpers). It also drifts: CliStep caps the log buffer at slice(-200) while useOnboarding caps at slice(-499), and CliStep never calls refresh() after a successful install (so the parent's onboarding.status stays stale until a phase change).
findings.fix: Make CliStep consume the lifted useOnboarding().install controller (status.cliInstalled, install.running/progress/error, install.run) — mirroring how NodeStep uses installNode — instead of its own state + subscription. Drop the duplicated buffer/subscription.
findings.testStrategy: After refactor, assert CliStep triggers ob.install.run on click and reflects ob.install.progress; remove the now-dead local subscription. Snapshot the present/installing/failed states.

clusters.title: Fix the second batch of confirmed security/boundary defects
clusters.oneLine: broker fs ops are symlink/TOCTOU-permeable (pathInScope is lexical only); brokerExec/brokerFetch buffer output unboundedly (memory DoS); permission inputMatches regex is unanchored (allow rules over-match); wrapDeclaredTools is non-idempotent (re-running onInit double-wraps); workspace.readFile lacks the boundary Zod schema its sibling has; broker fs_write_file swallows the error message; inproc isolator timeout never aborts the handler; clipboard-image require(node:fs) throws at runtime in the ESM-only package on Linux.
clusters.rationale: Real confirmed trust-boundary defects from the sweep; each ships with a regression test. Medium risk by virtue of the security surface.

findings.title: Broker fs ops are symlink/TOCTOU-permeable — pathInScope is lexical only
findings.problem: pathInScope validates a lexically-normalized path (path.normalize / path.resolve) against globs, but never resolves symlinks. A tool with fs.read scoped to $cwd/** can read outside scope by reading a path inside cwd that is a symlink to /etc/passwd, or by racing a rename between the check and the fs.readFile call (TOCTOU). For a security boundary this is a real escape, not just a theoretical one.
findings.fix: After scope check, resolve fs.realpath(path) (and re-check the realpath against the globs) before opening, or open with O_NOFOLLOW on the final component and on each dir. Document the residual TOCTOU window. At minimum re-validate the realpath so symlink targets are scope-checked.
findings.testStrategy: Create cwd/link -> /etc/hosts, call broker fs.readFile('cwd/link') with fs.read=['$cwd/**']; assert denial. Add a rename-race test if feasible.

findings.title: brokerExec/brokerFetch buffer stdout/stderr/body unboundedly — memory DoS
findings.problem: brokerExec concatenates child stdout/stderr into in-memory strings with no cap; brokerFetch does `await res.text()` with no size limit. A brokered tool (or a hostile server/process within the allowlist) can stream gigabytes and OOM the host. The broker is the trust boundary for sandboxed tools, so it should bound resource use, not the (untrusted) caller.
findings.fix: Add a max-output byte cap (configurable, default e.g. 8MB) to exec accumulation — kill the child and reject when exceeded; for fetch, read the body via a reader loop honoring a content cap (or check Content-Length). Optionally drive the cap from caps.memMb.
findings.testStrategy: Exec a command producing >cap bytes (yes | head -c) and assert the broker rejects with a size-limit error rather than buffering all of it; fetch a large body and assert truncation/error.

findings.title: inputMatches regex is unanchored — allow rules over-match, granting broader permission than intended
findings.problem: matchRule uses `new RegExp(v).test(candidate)` with no anchoring, so the pattern matches anywhere in the candidate string. For ALLOW rules this is a privilege-escalation footgun: a rule `inputMatches: { path: 'config' }` auto-allows a Read of `/etc/passwd#config` or `~/.ssh/config-backup`. The test suite only ever exercises start-anchored patterns (`^ls`), so the unanchored behavior for substring patterns is untested and surprising. By contrast nameMatches anchors its glob with `^...$`, so the two matching paths have inconsistent anchoring semantics.
findings.fix: Document and enforce a single anchoring convention. Recommended: treat inputMatches values as full-match by wrapping `^(?:...)$` (consistent with nameMatches), or at minimum document that values are unanchored substring regexes so rule authors anchor deliberately. Add tests covering a substring pattern to lock the chosen semantics.
findings.testStrategy: Test that `inputMatches: { path: 'config' }` does NOT allow `/etc/config-evil` under the chosen anchoring (or that it does, deliberately) — pin the decision with an explicit assertion.

findings.title: wrapDeclaredTools is NOT idempotent — re-running onInit double-wraps every tool
findings.problem: The comment on wrapDeclaredTools claims 're-running after a hot plugin reload would replace previous wrappers cleanly'. It does not. wrapWithIsolator returns `{ ...tool, handler }` which preserves `tool.isolation`. On a second onInit pass, tools.list() yields the already-wrapped tool (still has .isolation), so it is wrapped AGAIN; the new wrapper closes over the previous wrapper's handler, nesting iso.run() inside iso.run(). Each reload adds another isolation layer (double cap-checks, double timeouts, doubled exec) and corrupts handler semantics.
findings.fix: Mark wrapped tools (e.g. a non-enumerable __securityWrapped symbol or a WeakSet of wrapped ToolDefs / original handlers) and skip already-wrapped tools in wrapDeclaredTools; or unwrap-then-wrap from a stored original. Add a guard `if (isWrapped(t)) continue;`.
findings.testStrategy: Call handle.plugin.hooks.onInit twice, then invoke a wrapped tool that exceeds timeMs; assert exactly one isolation layer (single timeout error, single cap-check), and that a fs.read tool runs its handler once.

findings.title: workspace.readFile lacks the boundary Zod schema its sibling listDir has
findings.problem: `workspace.readFile` is a filesystem-touching command that reads up to 1MB of file bytes off disk and streams them into the renderer, yet it has NO entry in `ipcInputSchemas` (validation.ts) so `validateIpcInput` short-circuits via `if (!schema) return`. Its sibling `workspace.listDir` (identical signature `{workspaceId, path}`) DOES have a schema bounding workspaceId<=256 and path<=4096. The validation module's stated policy is that every filesystem-touching command gets a boundary schema; readFile is an inconsistent gap. The handler's `resolveInside` (path confinement + symlink realpath guard) provides defense-in-depth so this is not directly exploitable, but an XSS'd/compromised renderer can pass an arbitrarily large unbounded `path`/`workspaceId` string straight into the handler, defeating the documented single-choke-point invariant.
findings.fix: Add `'workspace.readFile': z.object({ workspaceId: z.string().min(1).max(256), path: z.string().max(4096) })` to ipcInputSchemas in desktop-ipc-contract/src/validation.ts, mirroring workspace.listDir (path required since readFile has no sensible default).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test asserting validateIpcInput('workspace.readFile', { workspaceId: 'x'.repeat(300) }) throws, and a valid payload passes; plus a contract test that every command whose handler imports node:fs has a schema entry.

findings.title: broker_fs_write_file swallows the error message; wasm caller gets bare code 1 with no diagnostic, unlike every other bridge
findings.problem: Every other bridge (read_file, readdir, stat, exec) writes a descriptive error string to the out-pointer pair on failure via sendErr(). broker_fs_write_file uses a 4-arg ABI with no out-pointer pair, so on cap-deny or IO error it returns bare ERROR (1) and the wasm handler has zero information about why the write failed (permission deny vs ENOSPC vs bad path). The `catch {}` also fully discards the Error. This is an inconsistency in the broker error contract and makes write failures undebuggable from inside the sandbox.
findings.fix: Extend broker_fs_write_file's ABI to 6 args (pathPtr,pathLen,dataPtr,dataLen,outPtrOut,outLenOut) like broker_exec, and emit sendErr(outPtrOut,outLenOut, reason) on both cap-deny and the caught IO error, so the failure contract matches the read-side bridges.
findings.testStrategy: Add a broker-bridges test asserting an out-of-scope write returns 1 AND writes a 'fs.write capability' message to the out-pointers, mirroring the existing read-deny test.

findings.title: inproc timeout rejects but never aborts the handler — runaway work leaks past the budget
findings.problem: On a timeMs overrun the isolator only calls reject() on its own returned promise. It never aborts the signal the handler is actually observing, so the handler keeps running in the background indefinitely after the caller has already been unblocked with an 'exceeded budget' error. The header doc-comment (lines 10-12) explicitly claims it 'aborts via the propagated signal when the budget is exceeded', and CapabilitySpec.timeMs in @moxxy/sdk documents 'Aborted via ctx.signal on overrun' (isolation.ts:28) — both are false. The handler closure built in wrapWithIsolator (index.ts:217-229) binds tool.handler to ctx, and that SAME ctx.signal is passed straight into iso.run, so the timer has no controller to abort; it can only reject. Long fs/net/exec work declared with timeMs continues consuming resources unbounded.
findings.fix: Create an internal AbortController inside inprocIsolator.run, forward the incoming external signal to it (link abort), and pass the derived controller's signal to the handler instead of the raw external signal; on timeout call controller.abort(reason) BEFORE reject so the handler's in-flight fs/net/exec is actually cancelled. Requires threading a handler-facing signal through wrapWithIsolator (bind ctx with the derived signal) rather than reusing ctx.signal directly. Update both doc-comments to match whatever behavior ships.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test where the handler awaits a never-resolving promise but checks its signal.aborted: pass timeMs:20, give the handler a signal-aware body, assert the run rejects with 'exceeded budget' AND the handler observed an abort within the budget window. Currently impossible because the handler shares the un-aborted external signal.

findings.title: require('node:fs') in an ESM-only package throws at runtime on the Linux clipboard path
findings.problem: The package is `"type":"module"` and the CLI bundles it as ESM (`format:['esm']` in packages/cli/tsup.config.ts). The Linux clipboard-image branch calls `require('node:fs')`, but `require` is undefined in an ESM module, so `readClipboardImageLinux()` throws a ReferenceError the moment xclip/wl-paste returns image bytes. The throw is swallowed by the surrounding try/catch (returns null), so Linux image-paste from the clipboard silently never works and the failure is invisible — the paste just falls back to plain text.
findings.fix: Add `writeFileSync` to the top-level `import { mkdirSync, statSync, unlinkSync } from 'node:fs'` and drop the inline `require`. Then `writeFileSync(target, result.stdout)` directly.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test readClipboardImageLinux with a spawnSync stub returning a non-empty Buffer on stdout and assert it writes a file + returns a DetectedImagePath (instead of throwing). A bundle smoke test on Linux would also catch the ReferenceError.

clusters.title: Fix the second batch of confirmed correctness bugs
clusters.oneLine: workflows-builder connectNeeds cycle guard ignores branch/loop edges; command readLastRun substring match resolves the wrong workflow; mic stream leaks if MediaRecorder ctor throws; OAuthSignIn captures onSignedIn at mount; SkillRegistryImpl.replace leaks a stale byName index entry; config_get collapses falsy values to null; HTTP channel error events after listen are swallowed; telegram FramePump/voice-handler raw-length splits and un-awaited savePreferences; PermissionEditor un-guarded concurrent RMW; connectUnixSocket never times out; mcp_add_server registers before persisting; codex stager only scans first 30 releases; gemini/zai capability mis-gating; isolator-wasm broker error laundering; fixture-recorder parseFlags bug; NodeInspector un-editable Args textarea; device-flow unguarded parseInt; HTTP non-streaming turn never aborts on disconnect; Modal focus-trap missing; Skeleton couples to app-only CSS vars; PluginRegisteredEvent.kind union drift; estimateContextTokens over/under-counts.
clusters.rationale: A second batch of independently confirmed correctness bugs without a shared structural theme; each gets a targeted fix + regression test. Medium risk spread across many packages.

findings.title: connectNeeds cycle guard ignores branch/loop-body edges, so it can author a cycle the engine rejects
findings.problem: wouldCreateCycle only walks the `needs` DAG. But scheduling order is also constrained by branch targets (then/else/cases/default) and loop body/exit. connectNeeds can therefore add a `needs` edge that combined with a branch/loop edge forms a cycle the server validation later rejects — defeating the stated purpose ('refuse it here so the canvas can't author an invalid DAG'). It also lets connectNeeds add needs:[loop] to an arbitrary node, conflating with the loop-exit model (see u129-2).
findings.fix: Either narrow the docstring claim (server is the authority) or extend the reachability walk to include branch/loop-body successor edges when computing transitive dependency. Separately gate needs:[loop] additions through the loop-exit/body ops.
findings.testStrategy: Build gate(then->b), b(needs->gate via connectNeeds) and assert the cycle is refused; cross-check against server validateDraft.

findings.title: readLastRun substring match `-${name}-` resolves the wrong workflow's last run
findings.problem: `/workflows inspect <name>` picks the last-run record via `files.filter((f) => f.includes(`-${name}-`))`. The run-record filename written by engine.ts is `${stamp}-${workflow.name}-${ulid}.jsonl`, so for a workflow named `report`, the filter `-report-` also matches a sibling workflow named `daily-report` (whose file contains `...-daily-report-...`). The matches are then `.sort()`ed and the last is taken, so `inspect report` can surface `daily-report`'s last run.
findings.fix: Encode the workflow name unambiguously in the run filename and match exactly. E.g. write records into a per-workflow subdir (`dir/<name>/<stamp>-<ulid>.jsonl`) and have readLastRun list that subdir; or include a stable separator the slug cannot contain and parse the name field out of the first JSONL `run` line rather than the filename. Then match on the parsed `workflow` field.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: write run records for `report` and `daily-report`, call readLastRun('report'), assert the returned head corresponds to the `report` run, not `daily-report`.

findings.title: Mic stream leaks if MediaRecorder ctor throws after getUserMedia resolves
findings.problem: In start(), navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia() (L32) acquires a live mic stream, then `new MediaRecorder(stream, ...)` (L34) — and the AnalyserNode setup (L71-84) — can throw (unsupported mimeType combo, AudioContext ctor failure, NotSupportedError). The stream's tracks are only stopped inside the 'stop' event handler (L61), which never fires if construction throws before rec.start(). The thrown error propagates to the caller's catch (useVoiceRecorder L135) which calls fail() but never stops the tracks, so the OS mic indicator stays on and the device stays held until GC/tab close.
findings.fix: Wrap everything after getUserMedia in try/catch; on any synchronous failure call `stream.getTracks().forEach((t) => t.stop())` (and `audioCtx?.close()`) before rethrowing. Equivalently, factor a `teardown()` that stops tracks + closes the context and call it from both the 'stop' handler and the failure path.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a mocked getUserMedia returning a fake stream whose getTracks/stop are spies, and a MediaRecorder stub whose constructor throws; assert start() rejects AND every track's stop() was called.

findings.title: OAuthSignIn captures onSignedIn at mount via []-dep effect; callers pass an unstable inline closure
findings.problem: The single subscription effect runs once with an empty dep array (eslint-disabled) and the `provider.login.done` handler calls `onSignedIn?.()` from the closure captured at first mount. The comment claims 'callers pass a stable handler', but ProvidersTab's ConfigureProviderModal passes a fresh inline arrow `() => { void onRefresh(); }` on every render (apps/desktop/src/settings/ProvidersTab.tsx:236-238). If `onRefresh` (a threaded prop) ever closes over render-scoped state that changes between mount and the login completing, the success callback fires the stale version. Today this is benign only because onRefresh re-reads from the store, so the staleness is latent rather than active.
findings.fix: Store onSignedIn in a ref updated every render (`const cb = useRef(onSignedIn); cb.current = onSignedIn;`) and call `cb.current?.()` inside the handler; keep the []-dep subscription. This removes the stale-closure footgun without re-subscribing per render.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: mount OAuthSignIn with onSignedIn=v1, rerender with onSignedIn=v2, emit provider.login.done code=0 for the active loginId, assert v2 fired (currently v1 would).

findings.title: SkillRegistryImpl.replace leaks stale byName index entry when frontmatter.name changes
findings.problem: replace() overwrites byId and sets byNameIdx[newName], but never deletes the OLD name->skill entry. If a skill keeps the same id but its frontmatter.name changes (e.g. an edited skill re-registered), byName(oldName) keeps returning the stale skill forever, and byName(newName) returns the new one — two live names for one id. unregister() reads the CURRENT skill's name to clean up, so the orphaned old-name entry is never reclaimed even on unregister.
findings.fix: In replace(), read the prior skill first and drop its name from byNameIdx if the name differs: `const prior = this.byId.get(skill.id); if (prior && prior.frontmatter.name !== skill.frontmatter.name) this.byNameIdx.delete(prior.frontmatter.name);` before the two set() calls. Mirror the alias-cleanup pattern already used in CommandRegistry.replace.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: register skill id=X name=foo; replace with id=X name=bar; assert byName('foo') === undefined and byName('bar') is the new skill and list().length === 1.

findings.title: config_get collapses legitimate falsy values (false/0/"") to null
findings.problem: config_get walks the dot-path then returns `cursor ?? null` (line 140) and also short-circuits on `cursor === null || cursor === undefined` mid-walk (line 137). A leaf value of `false`, `0`, or empty string is returned as `null`, so the model reading e.g. `context.caching` (false), `security.enabled` (false), or `maxIterations` (0) cannot distinguish 'set to false' from 'absent'. This drives wrong agentic decisions when inspecting config.
findings.fix: Return `cursor === undefined ? null : cursor` at line 140; keep the mid-walk guard but only bail when the intermediate container is null/undefined (it already does). Distinguish missing vs falsy explicitly.
findings.testStrategy: Add plugin test: write a yaml with `context:\n  caching: false` and assert config_get('context.caching') === false (not null); same for 0 and "".

findings.title: Server 'error' events after listen are swallowed; running promise never rejects on failure
findings.problem: start() attaches `server.once('error', reject)` only to settle the listen promise. Once listening resolves, any later runtime server error (EADDRINUSE races aside, e.g. socket errors) hits a reject() that is a no-op on the already-settled promise, so the error is swallowed and never logged. The `running` promise only ever resolves on 'close' and can never reject, so callers awaiting handle.running can't observe a server crash — it looks like a clean shutdown.
findings.fix: Detach the listen-scoped error listener inside the listen callback, then attach a persistent error handler that logs via this.logger?.warn and rejects the `running` promise (make running a Promise that can reject).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: start the channel, emit a synthetic 'error' on the underlying server, assert logger.warn was called and handle.running rejects.

findings.title: FramePump.flush splits parse_mode=HTML on raw length, can sever a tag/entity across messages
findings.problem: flush() composes one HTML string (composeFrame: <blockquote>/<pre><code>/<b> etc.), then calls splitForTelegram(html) and sends each part with parse_mode:'HTML'. The splitter cuts at the last '\n' before the byte limit (or hard at the limit), with no awareness of open tags or '&' entities. An overflowing turn whose 4000th char lands inside a <pre><code class="language-diff"> fence or an entity yields a part with an unbalanced tag; Telegram rejects it with 'can't parse entities'. safeSend's stripHtml fallback masks the failure but loses all formatting for that tail. With multi-message diffs this is the common case, not the rare one.
findings.fix: Split at the HTML structural boundary instead of raw length: break only between top-level composeFrame parts (activity/body/diff/error already joined by '\n\n') and, for an oversized single block, close-and-reopen the surrounding <pre><code>/<blockquote> across the cut. Alternatively compose+split per logical block so a fence is never bisected.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test flush with a body that forces overflow inside a <pre><code> fence; assert every emitted part is well-formed HTML (balanced tags) and no stripHtml fallback fires.

findings.title: handleModel persists preferences via un-awaited void savePreferences — failure is silently swallowed
findings.problem: handleModel switches provider/model then fires `void savePreferences({...})` (line 195) with no await and no .catch. handleMode does the same (line 223). savePreferences does a read-modify-write of the prefs file; if it rejects (disk/permission/lock), the rejection is an unhandled promise rejection and the user is told '✓ switched' while the preference silently never persisted, so the next CLI run reverts. This also violates the persistence invariant (atomic + mutex) being verifiable — the caller can't observe the write at all.
findings.fix: await savePreferences inside the existing try block (it already catches and reports via answerCallbackQuery), or at minimum attach a .catch that logs and downgrades the success message. Apply to both handleModel and handleMode.
findings.testStrategy: Test handleModel with a savePreferences stub that rejects; assert the user is not told '✓ switched' (or an error toast surfaces) and no unhandled rejection escapes.

findings.title: Voice handler drops awaiting-approval-text capture — voice follow-up to approval is lost
findings.problem: The text handler captures the user's NEXT message as an approval text follow-up (text-handler.ts lines 61-71) by checking state.awaitingApprovalText BEFORE the busy guard. The voice handler declares awaitingApprovalText in VoiceHandlerState (line 17) but never reads it: when an approval option with requestsText is chosen and the user answers with a VOICE note instead of text, the voice path falls straight through to the busy guard (line 70) and replies 'I am still working on the previous prompt', stranding the pending approval. Symmetry with text is broken and the declared field is dead.
findings.fix: After the authorization gate and before the busy guard, mirror text-handler: if state.awaitingApprovalText is set, transcribe the audio first, then call approvalResolver.resolvePendingWithText(approvalId, optionId, transcript) and return — or, if voice-as-approval-text is intentionally unsupported, drop the field from VoiceHandlerState to remove the dead plumbing.
findings.testStrategy: Add a voice-handler test: set awaitingApprovalText, send a voice note, assert resolvePendingWithText is called with the transcript and the busy reply is NOT sent.

findings.title: PermissionEditor fires async engine read-modify-write ops with no in-flight guard or error handling
findings.problem: addAllow/addDeny/removeByName/toggle each kick off a fire-and-forget promise (`void promise.then(...)` / `void (async () => {...})()`). There is (a) no await/error handling — a rejected engine write is swallowed entirely, leaving the UI showing 'saved' though the policy file write failed; and (b) no guard against overlapping ops: rapid key presses (e.g. hold space to flip, or d then y while a prior add resolves) can interleave read-modify-write on the same on-disk policy. If PermissionEngine lacks an internal mutex, the last-writer-wins / lost-update hazard from invariant #5 applies.
findings.fix: Add a `.catch` that surfaces the failure via setMode({kind:'message', text:'error: ...'}); gate input with an `applying` flag set true before the op and cleared in finally so concurrent mutations can't interleave.
findings.testStrategy: Mock PermissionEngine.addAllow to reject; assert an error message renders and dirty/status is not set to saved. Mock a slow write and fire two flips; assert the second is ignored until the first settles.

findings.title: connectUnixSocket never resolves/rejects on a hung connect (no timeout)
findings.problem: connectUnixSocket relies solely on the socket emitting 'connect' or 'error'. If net.connect neither fires (a half-open named pipe, or a unix socket whose server is bound but not accepting), the returned Promise hangs forever. The only caller, connectWithRetry (remote-session.ts:1095), `await`s each attempt, so a single hung connect strands the entire retry loop and the channel boot — no backoff fires because the prior attempt never settled.
findings.fix: Add a bounded connect timeout: `socket.setTimeout(ms)` (or a setTimeout race) that on fire calls `socket.destroy(new Error('connect timeout'))` and rejects, mirroring the explicit lifecycle other callers (isRunnerUp) already give probe sockets.
findings.testStrategy: Bind a unix server that accepts the TCP-level connect but a fake transport that never completes, or stub net.connect to emit neither event; assert connectUnixSocket rejects within the timeout instead of hanging.

findings.title: mcp_add_server registers live tools before persisting; a persist-race throw leaves orphaned registered tools
findings.problem: The handler calls `attachServer(server)` FIRST (runtime.ts:51-73 registers the wrapped tools into the live registry and records `runtimes.set(name,...)`), then persists via `mutateMcpConfig`. The persist closure re-checks for a duplicate name against the fresh read and THROWS `CONFIG_INVALID` if a concurrent add won the race (add.ts:59-66). When that throw fires, the live tools are already registered and the runtime handle/open client already exist, but no disk entry was written and no rollback runs. Result: a session with N callable MCP tools that don't correspond to any config entry, plus a leaked open client until shutdown. The race is narrow (two concurrent mcp_add_server for the same name) but the recovery comment claims the recheck makes this safe — it protects the file, not the registry.
findings.fix: On a persist-time duplicate (or any mutateMcpConfig rejection) after attach, call detachServer(server.name) before rethrowing so the live registry + open client are rolled back to match the unpersisted state.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test buildAddServerTool with an attachServer that succeeds + a pre-seeded config containing the same name written between the initial read and the mutate (simulate via a readMcpConfig that returns empty first then populated); assert detachServer was invoked and registry has no orphan tool.

findings.title: resolveDesktopRelease only scans the first 30 releases — desktop-v* can be buried by npm cuts
