findings.problem: The GitHub Releases API is called once with `per_page=30` and no pagination/Link follow. In this monorepo, frequent @moxxy/cli npm releases share the same release stream; if 30+ newer non-desktop releases pile up after the latest `desktop-v*`, that desktop release falls off page 1 and `resolveDesktopRelease` returns null → checkForUpdate reports 'Could not find a published desktop release to update from.' even though a newer bundle exists. This is the same class of bug the doc-comment warns about (latest/ being the wrong release) but only partially mitigated.
findings.fix: Either raise to `per_page=100` (cheap, covers far more headroom) and/or follow the `Link: rel="next"` header until a desktop-v* candidate newer than currentVersion is found or pages are exhausted (bounded, e.g. max 3 pages). Pass currentVersion in so paging can stop early once a newer desktop tag is seen.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a fetchImpl returning a first page of 30 non-desktop releases plus a Link:next header, second page containing the desktop-v release; assert checkForUpdate discovers it (available:true).

findings.title: Gemini catalog mis-gates capabilities: only one model gets supportsDocuments, none get supportsReasoning
findings.problem: ModelDescriptor flags drive capability gating: supportsDocuments gates whether the desktop ships raw PDF bytes vs. extracted text (see ModelDescriptor JSDoc in sdk/src/provider.ts:126-132), and supportsReasoning gates reasoning_effort + reasoning-stream surfacing. In the Gemini catalog only `gemini-2.5-pro` carries `supportsDocuments: true`; `gemini-2.5-flash`, `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, and the entire Gemini 3 family (gemini-3-pro/flash) omit it, and NO Gemini model carries `supportsReasoning`. This contradicts the file's own header comment which states 'The Gemini 3 and 2.5 families all carry a 1M-token context window and are natively multimodal', and contradicts the cross-provider convention: every vision-capable frontier model in the OpenAI (provider.ts:51-72), Anthropic (provider.ts:75-79), and GLM catalogs carries supportsDocuments:true, and every reasoning model carries supportsReasoning:true. Gemini 2.5/3 all natively accept PDF document input and are reasoning models, so the desktop will needlessly degrade PDFs to extracted text for 4 of the 5 listed Gemini models, and reasoning-effort/stream will never be requested for any of them.
findings.fix: Add `supportsDocuments: true` to all five Gemini entries (all 2.5/3 models accept native PDF), and add `supportsReasoning: true` to the reasoning-capable entries (gemini-3-pro/flash, gemini-2.5-pro/flash; verify flash-lite). Mirror the per-model flag completeness used in the OpenAI/Anthropic catalogs.
findings.testStrategy: Extend index.test.ts to assert each Gemini descriptor that the header claims is multimodal has supportsImages && supportsDocuments, and that the reasoning-tier models have supportsReasoning — a table-driven expect over geminiModels guards against future drift.

findings.title: No GLM model advertises supportsReasoning, so GLM reasoning is never requested/surfaced
findings.problem: The GLM-5 family and GLM-4.6 are reasoning models, and the shared OpenAIProvider explicitly handles GLM/z.ai reasoning streaming via the `reasoning_content`/`reasoning` deltas (provider.ts comments at lines 48 and 281-285 call out z.ai/GLM by name). But reasoning is gated upstream on the model descriptor: mode-helpers.ts:498 does `const reqReasoning = descriptor?.supportsReasoning ? ctx.reasoning : undefined`. Because no entry in glmModels sets `supportsReasoning: true`, ProviderRequest.reasoning is always undefined for zai, so the reasoning effort is never sent and the streamed reasoning summary is never surfaced — a capability the provider code was specifically written to support is dead at the catalog level.
findings.fix: Add `supportsReasoning: true` to the GLM-5 family (glm-5.2/5.1/5) and glm-4.6 (and 4.5 tier if z.ai streams reasoning_content for them) in glmModels, matching what the OpenAIProvider already plumbs.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: assert glmModels.find(m=>m.id==='glm-4.6').supportsReasoning === true; integration-style test that a request through zaiProviderDef.createClient with reasoning enabled forwards reasoning_effort and yields reasoning_delta events from a fake GLM stream.

findings.title: parseFlags swallows the next token as a value even when it is another flag
findings.problem: Each value-bearing flag does `flags.X = argv[++i]` unconditionally, consuming whatever follows without checking it is not itself a flag. `moxxy-record --prompt --name foo --out d` sets prompt to the literal string "--name", then `foo` is parsed as a positional and hits the `else throw new Error('unknown flag: foo')` branch — a confusing error that misattributes the cause. A trailing value-flag (e.g. `--prompt` as the last arg) assigns `undefined` (argv[++i] is out of range) which only surfaces later as the generic '--prompt is required'. Users get misleading diagnostics for a simple typo.
findings.fix: Add a small `takeValue(name)` helper that peeks `argv[i+1]`, throws `"--name requires a value"` when it is missing or starts with `-`, then advances i. Reuse it for every value-bearing flag.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test parseFlags: assert `--prompt --name x` throws a value-required error rather than mis-parsing; assert trailing `--prompt` throws; assert a well-formed argv parses to the expected Flags.

findings.title: Args (JSON) textarea is un-editable: value re-derived from parsed args, reverts on invalid keystroke
findings.problem: The Args textarea is fully controlled by `value={JSON.stringify(node.args ?? {}, null, 2)}`. On change it tries `JSON.parse` and only commits on success; on failure it silently keeps the old args. But because the displayed value is recomputed from the committed args, any keystroke that transiently makes the JSON invalid (deleting a brace, typing a key before its value, adding a trailing comma) is discarded on the next render and the field snaps back to the last valid pretty-printed form. Editing existing args is effectively impossible.
findings.fix: Keep a local `useState` draft string seeded from the node's args; render the draft, parse on change, and only dispatch update-node when parse succeeds (show an inline 'invalid JSON' hint otherwise). Re-seed the draft when node.id changes.
findings.testStrategy: RTL test: type a multi-step edit that passes through an invalid intermediate state and assert the final valid JSON commits and the field retained intermediate keystrokes.

findings.title: Unguarded parseInt on interval/expires_in can poison pollUntil timing (NaN)
findings.problem: start() accepts `interval`/`expires_in` as string|number and runs parseInt with no NaN guard. A malformed server value (e.g. interval:"" or non-numeric) yields NaN: `Math.max(NaN,1)` = NaN -> intervalMs = NaN. In pollUntil, `Math.min(NaN, remaining)` = NaN -> `setTimeout(resolve, NaN)` fires immediately => tight busy-poll loop hammering the token endpoint. A NaN `expires_in` -> expiresInMs NaN -> timeoutMs NaN -> `deadline = now + NaN` = NaN -> `Date.now() < NaN` is false -> the poll loop body never runs and login fails instantly with a timeout. The RFC 8628 shared helper avoids this by only accepting `typeof === 'number'` and falling back to defaults; this adapter does not apply the same discipline to its string-coerced path.
findings.fix: Coerce then validate: `const n = typeof data.interval==='string'?parseInt(data.interval,10):data.interval; const intervalSec = Math.max(Number.isFinite(n)? (n as number):5, 1);` and the same Number.isFinite fallback for expiresInSec (default 600).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: start() given `{interval:'',expires_in:'oops'}` should return intervalMs=5000 and expiresInMs=600000 (or chosen defaults), never NaN.

findings.title: Non-streaming /v1/turn and /v1/turn/audio never abort the turn on client disconnect
findings.problem: handleTurnStream wires an AbortController to res 'close' so a hung-up client stops the model (and stops billing). handleTurn and handleTurnAudio call ctx.session.runTurn(...) with NO signal, so when the client disconnects the for-await loop keeps draining the whole turn — the model keeps generating and billing with nobody consuming the result, and the final reply() write silently fails. This is the same wasted-spend hazard the stream handler explicitly guards against, just left out of the two buffered handlers.
findings.fix: Extract a shared helper that, for all three handlers, installs an AbortController bound to res 'close' and passes signal into runTurn; the buffered handlers can abandon collection once aborted. Removing the listener in a finally as the stream handler does.
findings.testStrategy: Integration test: POST /v1/turn against a FakeProvider whose generator yields slowly, abort the fetch mid-turn, assert the session's turn signal was aborted (spy on runTurn opts.signal.aborted).

findings.title: Modal declares aria-modal but never manages focus (no initial focus, no trap, no restore)
findings.problem: Modal renders role=dialog aria-modal=true but performs no focus management: it does not move focus into the dialog on mount, does not trap Tab within the dialog (focus can escape to the underlying page behind the overlay, which is still in the tab order), and does not restore focus to the previously-focused element on close. Only ConfirmModal sets autoFocus on its confirm Button, and autoFocus is unreliable for portaled content. This is both an accessibility defect (screen-reader / keyboard users) and a focus-leak: keyboard navigation reaches obscured controls behind the modal. The component's own doc-comment positions it as the replacement for window.prompt/confirm, where the browser provided this behavior for free.
findings.fix: On mount, save document.activeElement, focus the dialog container (tabIndex=-1 ref) or its first focusable child, and on unmount restore focus to the saved element. Add a Tab/Shift-Tab handler that cycles within the dialog's focusable elements. Optionally trap with inert/aria-hidden on the app root while open.
findings.testStrategy: RTL test: render Modal, assert focus lands inside the dialog on open, Tab from last focusable wraps to first, and on unmount focus returns to the trigger.

findings.title: Skeleton couples to app-only alias CSS vars, not the canonical design-tokens
findings.problem: Skeleton's baseStyle/Card reference --color-bg-card, --color-bg-card-hover, --color-border and --radius-block. The first three are NOT defined by @moxxy/design-tokens (its css-vars.ts emits --color-card-bg, --color-card-border, --color-surface, etc.) — they exist only as aliases hand-defined in apps/desktop/src/styles.css (:root lines 104-107). Every other primitive in this same package (Button, TextInput, Modal) correctly uses the canonical tokens (--color-surface, --color-card-border, --color-card-bg). The package is documented as 'shared by the renderer and a future web channel' (AGENTS.md). A web-channel consumer that loads @moxxy/design-tokens but not the desktop's styles.css will render Skeleton with an unresolved (transparent) background and no shimmer gradient — a silent visual regression invisible to typecheck.
findings.fix: Switch Skeleton to the canonical tokens this package's siblings already use: --color-card-bg / --color-card-border (and a real hover/lift token from design-tokens) for background+border, --radius-block is fine (it IS emitted by css-vars.ts:54). This makes the primitive self-contained on @moxxy/design-tokens with no dependency on the desktop's styles.css alias layer.
findings.testStrategy: Add a design-tokens parity assertion (or a unit test) that every CSS var referenced by desktop-ui primitives is present in @moxxy/design-tokens' css-vars output; would have flagged the three missing aliases at build time.

findings.title: PluginRegisteredEvent.kind union is out of sync with PluginKind despite 'keep in sync' note
findings.problem: events.ts inlines a 14-member literal union for PluginRegisteredEvent.kind with a comment 'Keep in sync with PluginKind (plugin.ts)'. PluginKind in plugin.ts now has 19 members. The inlined union is missing 'surface', 'synthesizer', 'embedder', 'isolator', and 'workflow-executor'. Any code that emits a plugin_registered event for one of those plugin kinds cannot type the value without a cast, and the documented invariant is silently broken — exactly the kind of drift the comment was meant to prevent.
findings.fix: Replace the inlined literal union with `ReadonlyArray<PluginKind>` typed structurally — since the cycle concern is only about a *value* import, define a shared `type PluginKindName = ...` in a leaf module (e.g. ids.ts or a new kinds.ts) that both events.ts and plugin.ts import as a pure type, so the list has one source of truth and the cycle stays type-only (dep-cruiser ignores type-only edges). At minimum, add the 5 missing members.
findings.testStrategy: Add a type-level test (sdk/src/types.test-d.ts) asserting `PluginRegisteredEvent['kind'][number] extends PluginKind` and vice-versa, so future PluginKind additions fail the build until events.ts is updated.

findings.title: estimateContextTokens over-counts ToolDisplayResult outputs (skips toolResultBytes)
findings.problem: eventChars() measures a non-stubbed tool_result by JSON.stringify(e.output) for non-string outputs. For a ToolDisplayResult (Write/Edit file-diff: { forModel, display }), projection only ever sends the short `forModel` string (mode-helpers.ts:378-381: `text = e.output.forModel`), but eventChars JSON.stringifies the entire bulky `display` payload (line-by-line +/- diff slices). This makes the token estimate for recent (not-yet-elided) file-diff results wildly larger than what is actually sent, which can trip the proactive compaction/elision thresholds (estimateContextTokens drives runCompactionIfNeeded and runElisionIfNeeded) prematurely. The shared helper toolResultBytes() (elision-state.ts:17-29) was written precisely to avoid this — its doc comment says "Rich results (e.g. file diffs) only ever send their short forModel string... measure THAT so the estimate matches projection and the bulky display payload never trips elision" — but eventChars reinvents the measurement and does not call it for the non-stubbed branch.
findings.fix: In eventChars's tool_result branch, reuse the shared helper: replace the non-error path with `return toolResultBytes(e.output)` (import it from elision-state.js, already imported at top of compactor-helpers.ts). This collapses the string/JSON/display cases into the single source of truth and keeps estimate == projection.
findings.testStrategy: Add a token-efficiency test: a recent tool_result whose output is a ToolDisplayResult with a large `display` but tiny `forModel`; assert estimateContextTokens counts ~forModel.length/4, not the stringified display. Assert it equals the projected-message char count from projectMessagesFromLog for the same log.

findings.title: Feature is a skeleton: no wasm authoring path / no module actually imports the brokers; bridges only tested in isolation
findings.problem: package.json's own description says 'Calling convention is still TBD; this ships the runtime + skeleton', and the module header (lines 71-75) defers the authoring recipe to 'the first tool that actually adopts it'. No real .wasm module in the repo imports broker_fs_read_file/exec/etc.; the only coverage is broker-bridges.test.ts calling the imports directly with a fake Memory. So the end-to-end path (a wasm module declaring these imports, V8 linking them, and the sync ABI 'write (ptr,len) pair to out-pointers' contract working through a real instantiate) is unverified. Combined with u63-2 (uncoordinated allocators) the broker path is unlikely to work for any non-trivial real module as written.
findings.fix: Either (a) land one real wasm handler fixture that declares the broker imports and exercise it end-to-end in a test, closing u63-2 in the process, or (b) gate wasmIsolator behind an explicit experimental flag / drop the broker imports until a real adopter exists, so users can't select an unverified isolator. Update the stale package.json description once the convention is final.
findings.testStrategy: Add an end-to-end test instantiating a real wasm module that imports broker_fs_read_file and reads an in-scope file, asserting the bytes round-trip through linear memory.

findings.title: `/workflows run` mishandles a paused (awaitInput) run — reports it as completed
findings.problem: runCmd renders the result solely on `result.ok`. A run that pauses on an `awaitInput` step returns `ok: true` with `status: 'paused'`, `runId`, and `pendingStepId` set (dag.ts buildRunResult(ctx, 'paused', true, ...)). The command prints `✓ workflow "<name>" completed` and discards the pause/runId/pending-step info, so an operator running a paused-by-design workflow from the slash command is told it finished and is given no way to resume.
findings.fix: Branch on `result.status`: when `paused`, print a distinct message naming the pending step and its prompt, and how to resume (the workflow.resume RPC / reply UI). Only print the completed/failed heads for terminal statuses.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a `runNow` that returns a paused WorkflowRunResult; assert runCmd output mentions 'paused'/'awaiting input' and the pending step id, not 'completed'.

findings.title: useOnboarding.refresh has no catch — mount-path rejection becomes unhandled
findings.problem: refresh() wraps the Promise.all in try/finally but no catch, so if onboarding.status or onboarding.probeNode rejects (both are host IPC calls that can throw), the rejection escapes. The mount/phase-change effect calls `void refresh()` with no .catch (line 77), producing an unhandled promise rejection and leaving `status`/`node` at their last (or null) values with no error surfaced. The other callers (run/runInstallNode/saveProviderKey) await refresh inside their own try/catch, so only the auto-refresh paths are exposed.
findings.fix: Wrap the Promise.all body in a catch that records an error (or at minimum swallows it like usePrefs/useMobileGateway do on their mount fetch) so the auto-refresh path can't produce an unhandled rejection; consider settling each probe independently so one failing probe doesn't blank both status and node.
findings.testStrategy: renderHook(useOnboarding) with a transport whose onboarding.status rejects; assert no unhandled rejection and that loading flips to false.

findings.title: fileChanged watchers only built once in onReady; runtime save/enable never re-registers them
findings.problem: startFileWatchers() runs exactly once during onReady. Every runtime mutation path (view.save, view.setEnabled, the plugin's onChanged) calls only syncSchedules(), which rebuilds schedule triggers but NOT fs watchers. So a workflow whose `on.fileChanged` trigger is added/enabled/edited after boot silently never fires until the process restarts, while its schedule/afterWorkflow triggers update live. Inconsistent trigger behaviour that is hard to diagnose.
findings.fix: Call startFileWatchers() (or fold it into syncSchedules) after every store mutation, e.g. append `await startFileWatchers()` inside syncSchedules (after the schedule loop) or invoke both from save/setEnabled. Guard against re-entrancy if syncSchedules is hot.
findings.testStrategy: Integration test: boot integration, save a workflow with on.fileChanged at runtime, touch a matching file, assert runNow fired (spy on runNow). Currently it would not.

findings.title: RFC 8628 error branches (access_denied/expired_token/generic) of classifyDeviceTokenResponse untested
findings.problem: classifyDeviceTokenResponse is the core RFC 8628 §3.5 state machine shared by runDeviceCodeFlow and both device-flow adapters. Only the pending/slow_down/done paths are exercised (indirectly, via profile.test.ts:151 through rfc8628DeviceFlow.poll). The throwing branches — access_denied→OAUTH_FLOW_DENIED, expired_token→OAUTH_FLOW_TIMEOUT, and the generic error→AUTH_INVALID with provider_error context — have zero coverage, so a regression in the error-code mapping or message would ship silently. These codes drive user-facing hints and downstream retry/abort decisions.
findings.fix: Add a direct unit test on classifyDeviceTokenResponse covering each error string → expected MoxxyError code; assert slow_down increments state.intervalMs by exactly 5000 and returns pending; assert ok+access_token returns {done}.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function tests; no fetch mocking needed (pass synthetic {ok,status} + json + state).

clusters.title: Build the missing test harnesses for the load-bearing untested subsystems
clusters.oneLine: Many critical reducers/folds/parsers/protocol handlers have ZERO co-located tests: the live multi-workspace chatStore (slot reducer/queue/usage fold), SurfaceHostImpl protocol-v8 multiplexer, runner Surface RPC handlers + surface.data broadcast, desktop-host IPC surfaces, git porcelain/diff parser, provider-discovery/onboarding, the dedupe LRU+TTL cache, wizard concurrency, browser-surface polling lifecycle, self-update provenance/source-pin, and ~100 smaller per-unit test-gap findings. Stand up the harnesses as a sustained additive effort.
clusters.rationale: Additive (no behavior change) and de-risks every other cluster, but the volume is large and several harnesses are non-trivial (protocol multiplexers, multi-workspace store). Treat as an ongoing track, prioritizing harnesses that unblock Tier-2 refactors (chatStore, registries, stores).

findings.title: SurfaceHostImpl (protocol-v8 surface multiplexer) has ZERO co-located test
findings.problem: SurfaceHostImpl is the central per-Session manager for the agentic-surfaces stack (terminal/browser/files rail, the freshest and per-MEMORY most-fragile subsystem — PR #199/#205/#208 churned its timing repeatedly). It holds correctness-critical logic with no test at all: idempotent open() (a second open(kind) must return the existing instance + its snapshot), a concurrent-open dedup map (`this.opening`) so two racing open(kind) calls share ONE underlying PTY/page, per-instance unsubscribe stored in `this.unsubs` and dropped exactly on close (lifecycle invariant #9 — a leak here keeps a closed PTY broadcasting), closeAll() iterating a snapshot of keys, and error-isolated emit() (a throwing listener must not break the multiplexed frame stream). A grep across the whole repo finds no test referencing SurfaceHost / surfaces/host / SurfaceDataMessage.
findings.fix: Add packages/core/src/surfaces/host.test.ts driving SurfaceHostImpl against a fake SurfaceRegistry + a fake SurfaceDef whose open() returns a controllable instance (onData emitter, snapshot(), input/resize/close spies). Assert: (a) open(kind) twice returns the same surfaceId and only calls def.open once; (b) two concurrent open(kind) promises resolve to the same instance and def.open ran once; (c) onData frames fan out to every onData subscriber as SurfaceDataMessage with the right surfaceId/kind; (d) a throwing listener does not suppress delivery to others; (e) close() calls the stored unsub (no frames after close) and instance.close(); (f) closeAll() closes every open instance; (g) input/resize route by surfaceId and no-op on unknown ids.
findings.testStrategy: Pure in-memory unit test with fake registry+instance; no real PTY/Playwright needed. ~10 cases.

findings.title: Live desktop multi-workspace chatStore (slot reducer/queue/usage fold) has NO co-located test
findings.problem: chatStore (chat-store/store.ts, 366 LOC) is the live reducer behind the whole desktop chat surface — App.tsx, Composer, UsageModal, CommandPalette, AgentPicker, FocusWidget and client-core hooks (useChat/useSessions/useContextUsage/useDesks) all consume it via the chatStore barrel. It owns per-workspace `slots`, the prompt queue (enqueue id derivation, dequeue head/rest, remove-by-id), hiddenTurns set, compacting/autoApprove/model flags, lastSeenRev unread tracking, buildSnapshot folding (state.ts) and provider_response token folding (usage.ts). None of it is tested co-located: chatModel.test.ts exercises the DIFFERENT older per-event reducer chatModel.ts, not chat-store/*. A grep finds no test under chat-store/.
findings.fix: Add chat-store/store.test.ts (and optionally state.test.ts/usage.test.ts). Cover: per-workspace slot isolation (events for ws A never leak into ws B's snapshot); queue enqueue->dequeue ordering + remove-by-id + id uniqueness; hideTurn/unhideTurn affecting buildSnapshot; lastSeenRev/hasUnread + workspacesWithUnread; compacting/autoApprove/model setters bumping rev only on change; buildSnapshot stability across chunk-only ticks (the documented fold memo invariant in state.ts:13,30,120); usage.ts token accounting folded from provider_response.
findings.testStrategy: Instantiate the store, feed synthetic MoxxyEvent streams per workspace, assert snapshots/queue/unread; no React render needed.

findings.title: git.ts porcelain/diff parsing (files-changed rail) is parse-heavy and untested
findings.problem: git.ts backs the desktop Files-changed pane + diff viewer. It parses `git status --porcelain=v1 -z` by splitting on NUL and slicing the two-column XY status prefix, runs `git diff` (and `--no-index` against /dev/null for untracked files), and caps a single diff to avoid OOM. All of this is string/CLI-output parsing — exactly the class that breaks on edge cases (renames `R `, untracked `??`, paths with spaces/unicode, empty repo, the diff size cap boundary) — yet there is no git.test.ts. A wrong slice or a missed --no-index path silently shows the wrong file list or an empty diff in the rail.
findings.fix: Add packages/desktop-host/src/git.test.ts running against a real tmp git repo (git init; stage/modify/rename/add-untracked files; a path with a space). Assert status() returns the right {path,status} set including ' M', '??', 'A ', 'R '; diff() returns a unified diff for tracked + the --no-index diff for untracked; the size cap truncates a large diff; isRepo() true/false. Alternatively stub the git() exec to feed canned -z/porcelain output and assert parsing.
findings.testStrategy: Real tmp-repo integration test (deterministic, fast) or canned-output unit test for the pure parsers.

findings.title: Runner Surface RPC handlers + surface.data broadcast path are absent from the otherwise-thorough runner integration suite
findings.problem: The runner integration.test.ts is exhaustive for v6/v7 (attach/replay/turnId routing/abort/permission+approval routing/provider persist), but the protocol-v8 Surface stack added later is not exercised at all. The five Surface RPC handlers (SurfaceList/Open/Input/Resize/Close, server.ts:200-204,622-647) and — more importantly — the runner-side rebroadcast of SurfaceHost.onData frames as `surface.data` notifications to attached clients have no test. Since SurfaceHostImpl itself is also untested (finding 1), the entire open->frame->client multiplex path for the terminal/browser rail is unverified end-to-end. Each handler parses its params via a zod schema then delegates to session.surfaces.*; a schema/wiring regression (the kind the v8 churn is prone to) would ship silently.
findings.fix: Extend runner/src/integration.test.ts: register a fake surface in the test session, then over the real socket call surface.open and assert the returned surfaceId+snapshot; push a frame from the fake instance and assert an attached client receives a surface.data notification carrying that surfaceId/kind/payload; call surface.input/resize/close and assert they reach the instance; assert close stops further surface.data frames. Also assert bad params reject (schema parse).
findings.testStrategy: Reuse the existing real-socket harness in integration.test.ts with a fake SurfaceDef registered on the session.

findings.title: desktop-host IPC surfaces handler (renderer<->runner surface bridge) untested
findings.problem: packages/desktop-host/src/ipc/surfaces.ts bridges the desktop renderer's surface IPC commands to the runner's Surface RPCs and relays surface.data frames back to the renderer/WS-bridge/mobile. Most sibling ipc/* handlers DO have tests (sessions.test.ts, prefs.test.ts, mobile-gateway.test.ts, shared.test.ts), but surfaces.ts has none — leaving the renderer-facing half of the v8 surface path (the part users actually see in the rail) unverified at the contract boundary. Combined with findings 1 and 5, no layer of the surface stack is tested.
findings.fix: Add packages/desktop-host/src/ipc/surfaces.test.ts dispatching each surface IPC command through the contract dispatcher against a fake runner client; assert it forwards to the right RunnerMethod and that runner surface.data notifications are relayed to the renderer event bus with the correct shape. Confirm first whether logic lives here or is a thin pass-through (if purely declarative wiring, downgrade to low).
findings.testStrategy: Contract-level unit test with a fake runner peer + a capturing event bus.

findings.title: desktop-host plugin/provider boot wiring (in-process-plugins, provider-discovery) untested
findings.problem: in-process-plugins.ts and provider-discovery.ts run at desktop boot to assemble the plugin/provider set the runner-in-process uses. They are wiring/registry-population code (invariant #11: registries populated at runtime) and a miswire here silently drops a provider or plugin from the packaged app — a class of bug the MEMORY shows recurring (ipc-server-ws not bundled, providers not appearing). Neither has a co-located test; provider enable/disable persistence is tested at the runner layer but the desktop discovery/assembly path is not.
findings.fix: Add focused tests asserting the expected set of plugin/provider names is assembled given a representative config, and that disabledProviders from preferences are honored (registered-but-not-active). Keep it a list/identity assertion, not a full boot.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test the assembly function with a fake config; assert the produced plugin/provider name set.

findings.title: No unit tests for the usage fold, snapshot-cache builder, prependFresh dedup, or queue/compaction logic
findings.problem: The chat-store directory has zero test files (only chatModel.test.ts covers the lower-level runtime fold). The reducer-grade logic here is exactly the kind invariant #4 calls out as a pure fold that must be test-pinned: recordUsage() token accounting (hasUsage/hasPrompt gating, perCall vs calls divergence, totals), buildSnapshot() rev/hasOlder caching + events-reference stability across streaming-only ticks, prependFresh() seenIds-based de-dup, dispatch()'s provider_response/compaction side-channels (latestPrompt -= tokensSaved), and the queue ops. A regression in any of these silently corrupts the context meter or the transcript with no test to catch it.
findings.fix: Add chat-store/usage.test.ts and store.test.ts: assert recordUsage returns null when no usage fields, increments calls but not perCall when only outputTokens present, sums totals; assert dispatch(provider_response) updates usage but not the log; assert dispatch(compaction) reduces latestPrompt by tokensSaved and appends a notice; assert buildSnapshot reuses the cached object when rev/hasOlder unchanged and preserves the events array reference across a streaming-only tick.
findings.testStrategy: Drive the exported pure functions and the chatStore singleton directly (it is React-free) with synthetic MoxxyEvents.

findings.title: Optimistic switch gestures + usage/context math + queue drain untested
findings.problem: Several non-trivial pure/stateful units in this unit have no unit test while their siblings (useSessions, useSettings, useWorkflows, usePausedWorkflows, useWorkflowBuilder) do. Untested: DesksStore.setActive/setActiveSession/removeSession rollback-on-error gestures (which dual-write desksStore + connectionStore optimistically and must restore both on failure — easy to regress); useContextUsage.summarize (cache savings/hit-rate arithmetic mirroring sdk token-accounting) and resolveContextWindow (provider/model descriptor lookup with fallbacks); useChat send() enqueue-vs-immediate branching and the turn_complete queue-drain in ChatStoreBridge.
findings.fix: Add hook/store tests: (1) summarize() and resolveContextWindow() as pure-function table tests; (2) DesksStore.setActive/setActiveSession failure paths asserting activeId + connectionStore.active$() roll back; (3) useChat send() queueing while activeTurnId set, and ChatStoreBridge draining the queue on turn_complete.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest + a fake api() transport and a stub connectionStore/chatStore; assert state and connectionStore.setActive call order on success and failure.

findings.title: SurfaceHostImpl has zero unit tests despite owning idempotent-open, multiplex fan-out and cleanup
findings.problem: SurfaceHostImpl is core protocol-handling logic: idempotent open-per-kind, concurrent-open de-duplication via the `opening` map, multiplexed onData fan-out, per-instance unsub bookkeeping, and closeAll teardown. None of it is exercised by any test. grep over packages/**/*.test.ts for SurfaceHostImpl / surfaces.open / surfaces.list / surfaces.closeAll returns nothing; session.test.ts only mentions an unrelated 'mode badge' line. Regressions in the open-de-dup or unsub-leak paths would ship silently and only surface as leaked PTYs/browser pages in production.
findings.fix: Add packages/core/src/surfaces/host.test.ts with a fake SurfaceDef + fake SurfaceInstance covering: (a) open(kind) twice returns same surfaceId and re-snapshots without re-subscribing; (b) two concurrent open(kind) share one instance (assert def.open called once); (c) onData receives multiplexed frames tagged with surfaceId/kind and a throwing listener doesn't break the stream; (d) close() calls unsub then instance.close, removes from instances/unsubs, and swallows a close() rejection; (e) closeAll closes every open instance.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest unit suite with stub registry/def/instance and a recording onData; assert call counts and map state via the public surface only.

findings.title: open() resolves OpenSurfaceResult but never registers the instance in `opening`-failure / no rollback on subscribe path
findings.problem: Minor robustness gap: if def.open() succeeds but a later step in the IIFE were to throw (e.g. instance.onData throws synchronously, or snapshot() throws), the instance is already stored in this.instances and its onData already subscribed (unsub stored), yet the open() promise rejects and the caller never learns the surfaceId. The instance is then orphaned-but-live: it is reachable via closeAll (it is in unsubs) but the caller got an error and cannot input/resize/close it by id. Today's built-in surfaces don't throw there, so impact is latent, but the ordering (set instance + subscribe, THEN build result that may call snapshot()) leaves no rollback.
findings.fix: Wrap the post-open steps in try/catch; on failure call unsub(), delete from instances/unsubs, await instance.close().catch(...), and rethrow — so a failed open leaves no live orphan. Alternatively compute snapshot defensively before mutating maps.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: a fake def whose instance.snapshot() throws; assert open() rejects AND instances/unsubs are empty afterward (no orphan, def.open's instance.close was called).

findings.title: git status/diff parser has no unit test despite parsing porcelain + driving the diff pane
findings.problem: git.ts is critical parsing/protocol logic (NUL-delimited porcelain status, byte-capped diff streaming, untracked --no-index fallback) with zero unit coverage — there is no git.test.ts. The rename phantom bug (u56-2) would have been caught by a single fixture test, and the byte-truncation path (MAX_DIFF_BYTES) and the tracked-then-untracked diff fallthrough are untested.
findings.fix: Add git.test.ts driving the helpers against a real temp git repo (init, add, commit, rename, modify, add untracked) asserting status() rows and diff() output, plus a unit test of the porcelain parser on a captured -z byte string for the rename case.
findings.testStrategy: vitest with a tmp repo via spawnSync('git', ...); also a pure parser test on a fixed Buffer.

findings.title: git status -z rename parsing emits a phantom ChangedFile for the old path
findings.problem: status() runs `git status --porcelain=v1 -z` and splits on NUL, treating EVERY field as a `XY<space>path` record. But a rename/copy in -z mode is encoded across TWO NUL fields: `R  <new>\0<old>\0`. The first field parses correctly (status `R `, path new), but the bare `<old>` field that follows has no XY prefix — it gets mis-parsed as status=old.slice(0,2), path=old.slice(3), producing a bogus extra entry in the Files-changed pane (and a wrong path the diff viewer then can't diff) whenever the old name is >=4 chars.
findings.fix: Parse -z records statefully: when an entry's XY starts with 'R' or 'C', consume the NEXT NUL field as the old path (and don't emit it as its own ChangedFile). Use the new path for the rename row.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the parser against a captured `git status --porcelain=v1 -z` byte string containing a rename (e.g. `R  new\0old\0 M other\0`) and assert exactly two ChangedFile rows (new, other) with no phantom 'ld' entry.

findings.title: Untracked-file diff uses POSIX /dev/null, breaking the diff pane on Windows
findings.problem: The untracked/new-file diff path hardcodes `/dev/null` as the left side of `git diff --no-index -- /dev/null <file>`. On Windows there is no /dev/null, so git errors and the catch returns an empty diff — untracked files silently show no diff in the desktop pane on Windows (a platform the repo otherwise actively supports, per the cli-resolver Windows handling).
findings.fix: Use git's portable empty-tree object as the base (`git diff --no-index` against the NUL device is non-portable); e.g. `git diff -- /dev/null` → instead diff against the empty blob: `git diff --no-index -- <NUL-device-or-empty-temp> <file>` choosing 'NUL' on win32, or better `git diff $(git hash-object -t tree /dev/null) -- <file>` style with the well-known empty-tree sha 4b825dc642cb6eb9a060e54bf8d69288fbee4904.
findings.testStrategy: Add a Windows CI leg (or mock platform) asserting an untracked file yields a non-empty unified diff.

findings.title: fetchProviderModels has no fetch timeout — IPC handler can hang indefinitely
findings.problem: fetchProviderModels() is invoked synchronously from the IPC handler `settings.fetchProviderModels` (ipc/settings.ts:18-20) which feeds the desktop model picker. The bare `await fetch(url, ...)` has no AbortController/timeout. A stored OpenAI-compat provider with a wrong/dead baseURL (user-supplied via provider_add) leaves the renderer's model picker awaiting forever — there is no upper bound on the request. vaultGet() likewise spawns the CLI with no timeout, so a wedged `moxxy vault get` never resolves.
findings.fix: Wrap the fetch in an AbortController with a finite deadline (e.g. AbortSignal.timeout(8_000)) and reject with a readable 'provider /v1/models timed out' error; the caller already falls back to runner-advertised models. Add a similar timeout/kill to vaultGet's spawned child.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test fetchProviderModels with a mocked fetch that never resolves and a fake AbortSignal.timeout; assert it rejects within the budget. Add a providers.json fixture entry so the non-built-in branch is exercised.

findings.title: provider-discovery, onboarding, and prefs have zero unit tests despite parsing/security-gated logic
findings.problem: Three files in this unit have no *.test.ts (confirmed `ls src/{provider-discovery,onboarding,prefs}.test.ts` → none). They contain non-trivial logic: provider-discovery's envVarFor/builtinProviderKeyName key derivation + /v1/models parsing + sort; onboarding's vault-key matching for hasProvider (which gates whether the install wizard shows) and the `<NAME>_API_KEY` derivation; prefs' DEFAULTS merge + version pin + the mutex read-merge-write cycle. These derivations are duplicated across modules and a drift between them silently breaks onboarding detection / model picker / key save.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: prefs (defaults merge, malformed JSON → defaults, concurrent updatePrefs serialize without clobber), onboarding (readVaultKeys parses/empties, hasProvider true only when expected key present), provider-discovery (key-name derivation, /v1/models id filtering+sort, built-in returns []). Ideally extract the single `<NAME>_API_KEY` slug helper and test it once.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with tmpdir fixtures for the JSON files and a mocked spawnCli/fetch.

findings.title: `<NAME>_API_KEY` vault-key slug re-derived in 4 places (copy-paste)
findings.problem: The exact expression `${name.toUpperCase().replace(/-/g, '_')}_API_KEY` is hand-written four times across onboarding.saveProviderKey, onboarding.probeOnboarding (expectedKey), provider-discovery.builtinProviderKeyName, and provider-discovery.envVarFor. The comments even cross-reference each other as 'the same derivation'. If the convention ever changes (e.g. another forbidden char), onboarding detection and the settings key name will silently disagree.
findings.fix: Export one helper (reuse builtinProviderKeyName, or move it next to assertSafeProviderName in security.ts) and call it from all four sites. Single source of truth, one test.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test the shared helper for `-`→`_`, upper-casing, suffix.

findings.title: browser-surface.ts has zero unit tests — polling lifecycle, fail-grace, input coordinate mapping untested
findings.problem: The browser surface is the user-facing live-view block and contains non-trivial logic with no test: the inFlight de-dup guard, the FAIL_GRACE counter that decides when to surface 'Browser unavailable' vs. stay on the spinner, the snapshot() catch-up payload, and the input() coordinate de-normalization (msg.fx * vw). A regression here (e.g. surfacing the error too early, or never clearing the timer on close) ships silently — the package's other surfaces (session/web) all have tests, this one is the gap.
findings.fix: Add browser-surface.test.ts driving a fake sidecar (reuse the makeFakeSpawn helper / inject deps.spawnFn): assert (a) first failure does NOT emit status, FAIL_GRACE-th failure with no prior frame DOES; (b) a successful frame resets `fails` and updates snapshot(); (c) close() clears the interval (no further calls) and dataSubs; (d) input click maps fx/fy to vw/vh pixel coords sent to the `mouse` method.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with fake timers (advance past FRAME_INTERVAL_MS) + injected fake sidecar call.

findings.title: getSidecar() silently ignores deps on the second+ caller — surface and tool can be wired with mismatched spawnFn
findings.problem: getSidecar caches a module-level singleton on first call and returns it for all subsequent calls, ignoring the `deps` of later callers. The tool handler (getSidecar(deps)) and the surface (browserSidecarCall(...deps)) each pass deps independently; whichever runs first wins. In production index.ts builds both with the same opts so they agree, but in tests (or any future caller that wants a distinct spawn override) the second caller's spawnFn/sidecarPath is silently dropped, which is a confusing footgun that can make a test pass against the wrong (real) spawn.
findings.fix: Either (a) assert/warn when getSidecar is called with deps that differ from the cached instance's, or (b) make the singleton keyed/scoped (pass the sidecar instance explicitly through plugin construction rather than a module global). Minimum: document that deps are honored ONLY on first spawn and that closeBrowserSidecar() resets it.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: call getSidecar with spawnFnA, then browserSidecarCall with spawnFnB, assert spawnFnB is NOT used (current) — then after fix assert a warning/throw, or that the explicit instance is used.

findings.title: Surface swallows all frame errors permanently once one frame has succeeded
findings.problem: Once `last` is set (one frame ever received), the catch branch can never emit a status again: `++fails === FAIL_GRACE && !last` is false forever after `last` is populated. If the browser page later dies (crash, sidecar exit, navigation to a hung page), the pane keeps showing the last stale frame with no indication the live view is dead — the error is swallowed indefinitely. Combined with `fails` only being compared by strict `===` (so it only ever fires on the exact 4th consecutive failure even pre-`last`).
findings.fix: Use `>=` and emit a 'frame stale / browser disconnected' status on sustained failure even when a prior frame exists (e.g. after N consecutive fails, regardless of `last`), so a dead page is visible rather than frozen on a stale screenshot.
findings.testStrategy: Test: deliver one good frame, then make the fake call reject for >FAIL_GRACE ticks, assert a 'stale/disconnected' status is emitted.

findings.title: dispatch.test.ts only covers goto-SSRF; the rest of the protocol handler is untested
findings.problem: dispatch.test.ts exercises only the `goto` SSRF-guard branch. The other ~13 protocol methods (text whole-document evaluate path, frame, mouse/key/scroll coordinate input, screenshot mediaType shape, the error-wrapping in `dispatch`'s outer catch that maps a thrown `kind` to the Err union, the `default` unknown-method branch, and teardown idempotency) have zero unit coverage. This is the one true protocol-handler in the unit and a frequent source of wire-shape regressions (the reply `{mediaType,base64,...}` shapes are consumed by surface + tool callers).
findings.fix: Add table-driven dispatch tests using makeFakeHandle (extend it with mouse/keyboard/viewportSize stubs) asserting reply shape + that `badParams`-thrown errors surface kind:'runtime', and that an unknown method returns kind:'runtime'.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest cases per method against a fake PlaywrightHandle; assert exact Ok/Err shapes.

findings.title: Surface-only methods (mouse/key/scroll) skip all input validation — undefined coords reach Playwright
findings.problem: `mouse`, `scroll`, and `key` are reachable only via `browserSidecarCall` (the surface), which passes raw params with NO zod validation (only the agent-facing `actionSchema` validates, and it does not include these methods). `mouse` does `page.mouse.click(x, y)` with `x,y` cast from `req.params` and no check — if the surface ever sends a malformed/partial message, `click(undefined, undefined)` is forwarded to Playwright, producing an opaque runtime error instead of a clean `badParams`. `scroll` defaults `dy ?? 0` and `key` guards with `badParams`, so the protection is inconsistent across sibling methods.
findings.fix: Add `badParams` guards for `mouse` (require finite x,y) for parity with `key`, or validate surface params with a shared zod schema at the dispatch boundary.
findings.testStrategy: Dispatch unit test: send `mouse` with missing x/y and assert an Err with kind:'runtime' rather than an unhandled Playwright throw.

findings.title: Security-critical provenance + source-pin logic (detectCoreInstall, provisionWorkspace) has zero tests
findings.problem: detectCoreInstall/findCoreScopeDir parse @moxxy/core package.json to derive version, gitHead and repoUrl, and provisionWorkspace enforces the integrity guarantee that the cloned source HEAD equals the installed gitHead (line 254: `!head.startsWith(ref) && !ref.startsWith(head)`). This is the trust boundary for Tier-2 core patching — if the source-vs-binary pin is wrong, the agent builds and overlays from an arbitrary commit. None of these functions is exercised by any test. The test suite (core-update.test.ts) covers shortName/findRepoPkgDir/safeRepoPath/overlay/detectNewDeps/journal but skips the resolution + pin path entirely.
findings.fix: Add unit tests against a temp fixture: (a) findCoreScopeDir for both the global-install layout (parent IS @moxxy) and the workspace layout (ancestor has node_modules/@moxxy); (b) detectCoreInstall returning null on missing/corrupt package.json and parsing gitHead/repoUrl + normalizeGitUrl; (c) provisionWorkspace's HEAD-pin mismatch rejection using a local fixture git repo with a known divergent commit.
findings.testStrategy: vitest with tmpdir fixtures + a throwaway `git init` repo to assert the mismatch branch returns ok:false with the 'source mismatch' message.

findings.title: DeliveryDedupeCache (LRU + TTL eviction algorithm) has zero unit tests
findings.problem: DeliveryDedupeCache is the idempotency gate that prevents the agent from re-running a prompt on every webhook retry. Its two eviction algorithms are non-trivial: (a) maxEntries overflow drops the oldest via Map insertion-order, (b) evictExpired() iterates and break-s on the first non-expired entry, which is correct ONLY because check() preserves time-ascending insertion order (delete+re-set on hit, set at tail on insert). None of this is tested — grep of *.test.ts finds no reference to DeliveryDedupeCache.
findings.fix: Add dedupe.test.ts covering: first key returns true then false on repeat; TTL expiry (inject ttlMs + fake clock or wait) re-admits a key; maxEntries overflow evicts the oldest while keeping recent; duplicate-hit refreshes recency; evictExpired stops at first fresh entry (insertion-order invariant).
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with vi.useFakeTimers / Date.now mock to drive ttl; assert size() and check() return values across eviction boundaries.

findings.title: Wizard control-flow and stdin line-queue concurrency logic have zero unit tests
findings.problem: The setup wizard (OAuth-vs-apiKey branching, OAuth retry loop, key-validation retry/accept logic) and the stdin prompt queue/waiter matching (early-arriving lines queued, close resolving pending waiters to '') are non-trivial control logic with no test in packages/cli/src/wizard. runSetupWizard takes an injectable SetupWizardController specifically so it can be driven in tests, but none exist. Regressions in the retry/accept branches (u30-3) or the queue ordering (u30-2) would ship silently.
findings.fix: Add wizard.test.ts driving runSetupWizard with a fake controller + mocked @clack/prompts covering: apiKey happy path, OAuth happy path, OAuth retry-then-succeed, OAuth retry-decline → bail, key reject→retry, key reject→accept. Add auth-context.test.ts for stdinLinePrompt covering line queued-before-waiter, waiter-before-line, close-resolves-pending-empty.
findings.testStrategy: vitest with vi.mock('@clack/prompts') returning scripted answers and a stub SetupWizardController recording calls; assert config path returned and controller methods invoked in the right order.

findings.title: groupSimilarPrompts is O(n^2 * tokens): re-tokenizes the whole group on every entry
findings.problem: For each entry it walks every existing group and rebuilds that group's token Set via `group.flatMap((e) => tokenize(e.originatingPrompt))` — re-tokenizing every prompt already in the group, every time. With n entries this is O(n^2) tokenize calls plus the per-token string split. Only matters for large audit logs but is trivially avoidable.
findings.fix: Memoize tokens per entry once (Map<entry, Set<string>> or precompute an array of {entry, tokens}); maintain a running token Set per group and union the new entry's tokens on placement instead of rebuilding.
findings.testStrategy: Add a unit test asserting grouping output is unchanged after the refactor for a fixed set of prompts (snapshot the group membership).
findings.complexity: O(n^2 * t) -> O(n^2) set lookups with O(n) tokenization (or O(n*g) with running sets)

findings.title: Pure parsers/renderers (resolveId, groupSimilarPrompts, renderPlist, renderUnit) have no unit tests
findings.problem: Several pure, bug-prone functions have zero coverage: resolveId (sessions.ts:149 — numeric-index/suffix/prefix/exact precedence + ambiguity fallback), groupSimilarPrompts/tokenize (skills.ts:200-224 — overlap>=2 clustering), and the unit/plist renderers renderPlist (launchd.ts:32) + renderUnit (systemd.ts:24) including escapeXml/quote. Only update.ts in this unit has a test file. These are exactly the deterministic string/algorithm units that regress silently.
findings.fix: Add table-driven unit tests: resolveId across exact/suffix/prefix/index/ambiguous; groupSimilarPrompts clustering thresholds; renderPlist/renderUnit golden output incl. XML/shell escaping of args+env.
findings.testStrategy: New vitest specs asserting fixed inputs map to fixed outputs; cover the escaping edge cases noted in u25-5 and the ambiguous-id fallthrough in u25 sessions.

findings.title: Core completion-detection + output-cleaning logic (runCommand/cleanOutput) has no unit test
findings.problem: The only test (index.test.ts) checks that the surface/tool exist and that the zod schema validates — it never exercises the actual algorithm: the sentinel completion protocol (runCommand), the exit-code parse, the timeout path, or the line-filtering in cleanOutput (which strips the echoed command, the marker line, and the printf line). This is the load-bearing, regression-prone logic of the plugin (a parser/protocol handler per the audit brief) and is wholly untested. The TerminalProcessImpl fan-out, scrollback cap, and exit-dedup in pty.ts are likewise untested.
findings.fix: Add unit tests driving a fake TerminalProcess: (a) emit output then sentinel line, assert {output, exitCode, timedOut:false} and that echoed command/marker/printf lines are stripped; (b) never emit sentinel, assert timeout -> {exitCode:null, timedOut:true}; (c) cleanOutput edge cases (command containing the marker substring, multi-line output). Plus TerminalProcessImpl tests for scrollback cap and emitExit idempotency.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with an in-memory TerminalProcess stub implementing onData/onExit/write/scrollback.

findings.title: computeElisionState recall-pin/maxRecallBytes cap + adaptive auto-disable have no direct unit test
findings.problem: computeElisionState is the single source of truth for the elision fold (HWM, recalledCallIds/Seqs, the pinned-recall maxRecallBytes cap, and the adaptive effectiveElideConversational = elideConversational && seqRecalls < threshold switch). It is consumed by both projection (what we send) and the token estimate (what we think we send) — a divergence silently overflows the context. Yet there is no test that imports computeElisionState directly; coverage is only indirect via projectMessagesFromLog in token-efficiency.test.ts. The subtle, security-of-context-relevant branches (aged-recall newest-first byte accumulation into unpinnedRecallCallIds at lines 123-134; the seqRecalls>=threshold auto-disable at line 138) are exactly the kind of fold logic the invariants demand be unit-tested.
findings.fix: Add an elision-state.test.ts that unit-tests computeElisionState directly: (a) HWM picks the max elidedThrough across multiple elision events; (b) recall callId/seq bookkeeping; (c) maxRecallBytes cap correctly stubs the oldest aged recalls once cumulative bytes exceed the cap (newest-first); (d) effectiveElideConversational flips false once seqRecalls reaches conversationalRecallThreshold; (e) toolResultStubbed/conversationalStubbed boundary cases (TINY_TURN_CHARS, firstUserPromptSeq anchor, neverElide).
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function table tests with hand-built event arrays; assert each ElisionState field. No mocks needed.

findings.title: extractMarkdownBlock (LLM-output unwrapping) has no direct unit test
findings.problem: draftSkill strips a ```markdown fenced block out of model output via extractMarkdownBlock before parsing frontmatter. This unwrapping is the brittle seam between model text and the schema validator, but it has no own test (synthesize.test.ts feeds raw frontmatter that never exercises the fenced-block branch). A model wrapping output in ```md or emitting nested fences would silently mis-extract and produce a confusing 'invalid frontmatter' error.
findings.fix: Add a small unit test for extractMarkdownBlock (export it or test via draftSkill): bare text, ```markdown-fenced, ```md-fenced, and content-containing-backticks cases.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven test over the four input shapes asserting the extracted inner block.

findings.title: loadPreferences mode-migration branch (migrateModeName) is untested
findings.problem: `loadPreferences` rewrites a persisted `mode` via `migrateModeName(prefs.mode)` to translate legacy loop-* names to the current mode-* names. This is a correctness-bearing migration (a stale `loop-tool-use` value must map to `default`, etc.) but there is no test file for preferences.ts at all (only session/run-turn/usage-stats/requirements have tests in this unit). A regression in the migration call would silently leave users on a non-existent mode after upgrade.
findings.fix: Add preferences.test.ts: (a) missing/malformed file -> {}; (b) a legacy mode value round-trips through migrateModeName to the migrated name; (c) savePreferences merges a patch without clobbering unrelated fields and writes atomically (assert tmp+rename via fs spy or by reading the file back). Use a temp HOME or pass an explicit path-injection (note: load/savePreferences hard-code preferencesPath() with no path arg — see u44-5).

findings.title: preferences load/save hard-code preferencesPath() with no injectable path (unlike usage-stats)
findings.problem: Sibling store `usage-stats.ts` parameterizes every fn with `filePath: string = usageStatsPath()`, which is what makes it cleanly unit-testable against a tmp dir (see usage-stats.test.ts). `preferences.ts` does NOT — `loadPreferences()` and `savePreferences()` always resolve `preferencesPath()` (real `~/.moxxy`), forcing any test to mutate the user's real home or monkeypatch os.homedir. The two best-effort JSON stores in the same unit are inconsistent in their testability seam.
findings.fix: Add an optional `filePath: string = preferencesPath()` parameter to both functions, mirroring usage-stats. This is non-breaking (default preserves current behavior) and unblocks u44-4's test.
findings.testStrategy: After adding the param, the u44-4 tests drive it against a tmp file; assert default still targets preferencesPath() when omitted.

clusters.title: Long-tail: minor correctness / robustness nitpicks
clusters.oneLine: Low-severity correctness and robustness nitpicks across many files (swallowed errors, minor edge cases, defensive-fill gaps) that did not rise to the targeted bug clusters. (116 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Low severity, low risk; address opportunistically when the owning file is next touched (TECH_DEBT journal).

findings.title: content-length from a chunked/compressed response yields a wrong/absent progress total
findings.problem: `const total = Number(res.headers.get('content-length')) || undefined` is used only for the progress bar total. For a transfer-encoding: chunked response (or a proxy that strips content-length) this is NaN→undefined (handled), but for a response whose content-length reflects a transport-compressed size different from the received bytes, the progress bar can finish at <100% or report received>total. Purely cosmetic (integrity is sha256-bound), so low severity, but worth noting the total is never reconciled with the actual received length.
findings.fix: Optional: after readAll, if received !== total emit a final progress event with total=received so the bar lands at 100%. Not required for correctness.
findings.testStrategy: Stager test with a body stream whose total header is absent/mismatched; assert final progress received is reported and no throw.

findings.title: Timeout/abort kill is indistinguishable from a normal exit — callers cannot detect a timed-out child
findings.problem: When the timeoutMs timer fires (or the signal aborts), the helper calls child.kill('SIGTERM') but does NOT set `settled` or otherwise flag the cause. The subsequent 'close' event then resolves the promise normally with `exitCode: code ?? -1`. So a process killed for exceeding its 15s/30s timeout returns the same shape as a process that genuinely exited with a non-zero/-1 code. Every tool (applescript 30s, screenshot 15s, click 10s, etc.) only inspects exitCode/stderr, so a hung osascript that was force-killed is reported to the model as a generic non-zero exit with empty stderr rather than a clear 'timed out after Nms' error. This makes stuck-UI-automation failures confusing and unactionable.
findings.fix: Track a `let timedOut = false` (and/or `aborted`) flag set in the timer/abort callbacks; on close, either reject with a MoxxyError('TOOL_ERROR', `${cmd} timed out after ${timeoutMs}ms`) or add a `timedOut`/`aborted` boolean to ProcResult so tools can surface a precise message. Apply to both runProcess and runProcessBinary.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: runProcess('sleep',['5'],{timeoutMs:50}) and assert the result/rejection signals a timeout (not a silent exitCode -1). Also test abort via an AbortController.

findings.title: tickOnce return count undercounts: error fires and counts only via onFired
findings.problem: tickOnce derives its returned count by wrapping `onFired` and counting invocations. `onFired` is called once per schedule that ran (including error outcomes, since runSchedule returns ok:false rather than throwing). But if runSchedule THROWS (e.g. store.update fails mid-run), tickWith's catch swallows it and onFired is never called, so a schedule that genuinely fired-and-failed is not counted. The CLI `schedule run`/tickOnce contract ('number of schedules that ran') is thus slightly wrong on store-level failures.
findings.fix: Count due-and-attempted schedules directly in tickWith (increment a counter before the try, or return the count from tickWith) rather than piggy-backing on onFired, so the figure reflects attempts regardless of outcome.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a store whose update() throws; assert tickOnce still returns 1 for one due schedule.

findings.title: Redundant clearTimeout(timer) before settle() in every error branch
findings.problem: Each non-success branch of handleRequest calls `clearTimeout(timer)` (lines 72, 95, 110) immediately before `settle(...)`, but settle() already clears the timer and removes the abort listener as its single cleanup chokepoint (lines 28-29). The redundant calls contradict the file's own stated 'single cleanup chokepoint' design comment and are dead/copy-paste — harmless but a maintenance trap: a future reader may believe settle does not clear, or may forget the manual clear on a new branch and assume a leak.
findings.fix: Delete the three redundant `clearTimeout(timer)` lines in the error branches; rely solely on settle() for all cleanup, matching the success path and the documented chokepoint.
findings.testStrategy: Existing callback-server.test.ts state-mismatch/error tests still pass; add an assertion that no unhandled timer fires after rejection (process exits cleanly).

findings.title: Markdown link extraction drops single-quoted and unquoted href URLs
findings.problem: The anchor rewrite regex hardcodes a double-quoted href (`\bhref="([^"]+)"`). Anchors written with single quotes (`href='...'`) or unquoted hrefs match the later generic tag-strip instead, so the link TEXT survives but the URL is silently lost — the markdown output claims to 'keep ... links' but drops a common subset. Real-world HTML frequently uses single quotes.
findings.fix: Broaden the capture to `href=(?:"([^"]+)"|'([^']+)'|([^\s>]+))` and coalesce the groups, or at minimum accept `["']`. Add a test for single-quoted and unquoted hrefs.
findings.testStrategy: Add htmlToMarkdown cases asserting `[text](url)` output for href in single quotes and unquoted form.

findings.title: makeWsApi.invoke silently drops positional args beyond the first
findings.problem: MoxxyApi['invoke'] is typed (command, ...args: Parameters<IpcCommands[K]>) but the WS adapter forwards only args[0] as JSON-RPC params (`client.request(command, args[0])`). The server (ipc-server-ws ws-command-bus.ts:89) symmetrically reconstructs args as `[params]`, so the single-arg assumption is a consistent protocol decision today and all REMOTE_ALLOWED_COMMANDS take 0/1 args. The hazard is future-proofing: if any remotely-allowed command ever gains a 2nd positional parameter, this adapter will drop it with no type error (the `as MoxxyApi['invoke']` cast at line 87 erases the mismatch) and no runtime guard, producing a silent wrong-arity call rather than a failure.
findings.fix: Keep the single-arg wire shape but make the truncation explicit/safe: either assert args.length <= 1 (throw on >1 so a multi-arg command surfaces loudly), or thread the full args array as params and have the server spread it — pick one and document it in both the client and ws-command-bus as the canonical wire arity.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test invoking with two args against a fake client and assert it throws (or that both args reach the request) per chosen contract; mirror on the server side.

findings.title: Stale-lock takeover has a TOCTOU window (acknowledged best-effort, but two takers can collide)
findings.problem: On finding a stale lock, the code rm()s it then `continue`s to retry open('wx'). Between stat()->stale and rm(), two processes can both decide the lock is stale; one rm()s the real holder's freshly-recreated lock. This widens the race the lockfile was meant to narrow. Per the module header the file lock is explicitly best-effort (the in-process mutex + vault read-merge-write + invalid_grant retry are the real guards), so impact is bounded, but it is a genuine correctness gap worth noting.
findings.fix: Make takeover atomic: rename the stale lock to a unique temp name, re-stat the temp to confirm we grabbed the same file, then open('wx') the canonical path; if rename fails (someone else took over) just loop. Or accept current behavior and add a comment that double-takeover is tolerated by the in-process mutex.
findings.testStrategy: Hard to test deterministically; add a comment documenting the tolerated window, or a rename-based takeover unit test asserting only one of two concurrent takers proceeds.

findings.title: brokerExec timeout-kill path leaves child running and resolves nothing extra, but timer not cleared on abort
findings.problem: In brokerExec the abort listener `onAbort` calls child.kill but does NOT clear the timeout timer, and the timeout path calls child.kill+reject but relies on the subsequent 'close' to clear the timer (it does). The real gap: on abort, the Promise is only settled later via 'close'/'error'; if the child ignores SIGTERM, neither the timer (still pending if abort fired before timeout) nor the promise is force-settled, and there is no SIGKILL escalation. A wedged child keeps the broker request pending indefinitely.
findings.fix: On abort, reject immediately and schedule a SIGKILL fallback (setTimeout -> child.kill('SIGKILL')); clear all timers in both settle paths. Mirror the kill-safety pattern used elsewhere (security-invariants kill-safety).
findings.testStrategy: Spawn a child that traps SIGTERM (sh -c 'trap "" TERM; sleep 30'), abort the signal, assert the broker rejects promptly and the child is SIGKILLed.

findings.title: telegram_send_message constructs a fresh grammy Bot per call and never closes it
findings.problem: The telegram_send_message tool handler does `const bot = new Bot(token); await bot.api.sendMessage(...)` on every invocation. A new Bot instance is created and discarded each call with no `bot.stop()`/cleanup. While the Bot API client itself is lightweight (no long-polling started), repeated invocations from scheduled prompts allocate a new grammy Bot (which lazily resolves bot info, holds an API transformer chain) per send; it is wasteful and inconsistent with caching a single Api client.
findings.fix: Use grammy's lightweight `Api` class directly (`new Api(token).sendMessage(...)`) instead of a full `Bot`, or memoize a single Bot/Api per token at module scope keyed by token.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (refactor); assert send still works via a mocked Api in a tool test.

findings.title: systemd Environment= values are not escaped/quoted (latent injection if a spec sets env)
findings.problem: renderUnit emits `Environment=${k}=${v}` with the raw value. systemd splits Environment= on whitespace and treats `"`/`\` specially, so an env value containing a space, quote, or newline produces a malformed unit (split into multiple vars) or a broken file. The ExecStart path uses the `quote()` helper, but the env lines bypass all escaping. No current caller passes env (serveSpec sets none), so this is latent, not live — but it is an asymmetry waiting to bite when scheduler/telegram specs gain env.
findings.fix: Wrap each Environment= value with systemd quoting (double-quote and escape embedded quotes/backslashes, reject newlines) mirroring the launchd escapeXml symmetry.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test renderUnit with an env value containing a space and a quote; assert the rendered line is a single valid Environment= directive.

findings.title: managedNodeBinDir sort uses a non-total-order comparator to prefer the pinned version
findings.problem: The comparator `(a,b) => a.startsWith(pinned) ? -1 : b.startsWith(pinned) ? 1 : 0` is not a strict weak ordering: for two non-pinned entries it returns 0 (fine), but it can report a<b and b<a inconsistently when only one side is pinned vs another pinned pair, and engine sort stability for the 'prefer pinned' goal is not guaranteed across V8 versions. The intent (pinned folder first, else any node-v*) is better expressed by partition, not sort.
findings.fix: Replace the sort with: `const pinnedDir = candidates.find(e => e.startsWith(pinned)); const ordered = pinnedDir ? [pinnedDir, ...candidates.filter(e => e !== pinnedDir)] : candidates;` then iterate.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that creates both a pinned and an older node-v* folder and asserts managedNodeBinDir returns the pinned one.

findings.title: HTML entities decoded in text nodes but NOT in attribute values (inconsistent)
findings.problem: decodeEntities is applied only to text tokens in build() (line 210). Attribute values parsed by parseAttrs() are taken verbatim and never entity-decoded. So `<text>a & b</text>` renders `a & b`, but `<input label="a & b" .../>` keeps the literal `a & b`. This is a behavioral inconsistency the agent cannot predict; an entity-encoded ampersand/quote in any label/title/placeholder shows up raw. It is NOT a security hole — the web frontend sets attributes via React props (no HTML entity decoding), so an entity-encoded scheme like `href="javascript:..."` is never decoded to `javascript:` and so cannot bypass isSafeViewUrl — but the divergence is surprising and untested.
findings.fix: Decide the contract explicitly: either decode entities in attribute string values too (call decodeEntities in coerceValue for string/enum types, AFTER the URL-safety check stays on the decoded value) or document that attributes are verbatim. Decoding-after-safety-check would still reject a decoded `javascript:` scheme, so it is safe; add a test for `href="javascript:..."` to lock the behavior either way.
findings.testStrategy: Add cases: an attribute with `&`/`<` asserting the chosen decode behavior, and an entity-encoded `javascript:`/`data:text` href asserting it is still rejected (no scheme-smuggle regression).

findings.title: AskPrompt can respond with an empty optionId when approval options are missing
findings.problem: Both allow() and deny() fall back to `''` for the approval optionId (`ask.approval?.options[0]?.id ?? ''` and `... ?? ask.approval?.defaultOptionId ?? ''`). If an approval ask arrives with an empty/absent options array, askStore.respond is still invoked with `{ optionId: '' }`, which is not a valid ApprovalOption id and leaves the parked runner to resolve an undefined decision. The ask is also dropped locally (respond filters it out) so the user can no longer act on it.
findings.fix: Guard: if no resolvable optionId exists, do not call respond (keep the ask pending) or surface an error; only forward when a concrete option id is found.
findings.testStrategy: Feed an approval AskRequest with options: [] and assert deny()/allow() does not call api().invoke('ask.respond', ...).

findings.title: collectKey accepts a provider-REJECTED key when user declines retry, conflating it with network-error case
findings.problem: collectKey distinguishes a provider rejection (result.ok === false, printed in red) from a validator-unreachable error (caught, printed yellow). But both fall through to the same 'Try a different key?' prompt, and on decline both return the unvalidated `value`. The comment ('sometimes the network is the problem, not the key') only justifies the unreachable case; for an explicit rejection this silently persists a key the provider already said is bad.
findings.fix: On an explicit provider rejection (result.ok === false), either loop back to re-prompt unconditionally, or make the decline path bail() rather than returning the rejected key. Only the unreachable-validator (catch) branch should fall through to 'accept unvalidated'.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test collectKey with a testKey returning {ok:false} and a stubbed confirm answering 'no', asserting it does NOT return the rejected key; separate test for the throwing testKey returning the value on decline.

findings.title: Reasoning of the budget-exhausting call is dropped because the budget check returns before the reasoning emit
findings.problem: The token-budget backstop (lines 206-228) `return`s before the `if (reasoning)` emit at lines 233-244. So on the iteration that crosses GOAL_TOKEN_BUDGET, the model's reasoning summary for that final provider call is never persisted to the log. This is cosmetic (the run is ending anyway and no further provider call needs the signed block), but it is an inconsistency with every other exit path, which emits the assistant/system message after the reasoning. If the budget exit is ever changed to `continue` instead of `return`, the missing reasoning_message would also break the Anthropic content[0] signed-thinking ordering on the next call.
findings.fix: Move the `if (reasoning)` emit block above the token-budget check (just after `reactiveCompactions = 0;` at line 203), so a finalized reasoning_message is always logged for a successful provider call before any exit decision — matching the intent of the comment at lines 230-232.
findings.testStrategy: Add a goal-loop test where the budget-exhausting iteration also returns reasoning (FakeProvider with a reasoning delta) and assert a reasoning_message event precedes the goal_budget_exhausted plugin_event.

findings.title: WorkflowStore has no concurrency guard around load()/read-modify-write ops
findings.problem: load() clears `byName` then refills it; setEnabled()→save() does a read (byName.get) then a file write then a byName.set. There is no per-instance mutex (invariant #5). Concurrent calls — e.g. an autonomous trigger re-sync (`onChanged`→reload) racing a user `/workflows enable`, or two toggles — can interleave a `byName.clear()` against an in-flight `save()` read, leaving the in-memory map briefly empty or stale relative to disk. Each workflow is its own file so on-disk corruption is unlikely, but the in-memory registry can desync from disk.
findings.fix: Serialize load() and the mutating methods (create/save/setEnabled/delete) behind a per-instance promise-mutex (reuse @moxxy/sdk mutex) so a reload cannot interleave with a save.
findings.testStrategy: Fire load() and setEnabled() concurrently in a test against a tmp dir and assert the final byName state matches disk; add a mutex and assert no empty-map window.

findings.title: Fence/inline placeholders use ` FENCEn `/` INLINEn ` text tokens that can collide with literal user content and consume surrounding spaces
findings.problem: markdownToTelegramHtml replaces code with space-delimited literal placeholders ` FENCE0 `/` INLINE0 ` and later splices back via regex ` FENCE(\d+) `/` INLINE(\d+) `. (1) If the model output literally contains the substring ` FENCE0 ` or ` INLINE3 ` (e.g. discussing this code, or an enum value), it gets replaced with the wrong/empty content (`?? ''`). (2) The placeholder injects/consumes a leading and trailing space, so `a`+inline-code+`.` can gain a spurious space before the punctuation, and an inline code at string start loses no space but mid-text spacing is altered. The collision path is low-probability but silently corrupts output with no test for it.
findings.fix: Use a placeholder that cannot occur in normal text (e.g. a private-use unicode sentinel like `\uE000${i}\uE001`) and trim/normalize the injected spaces, or tokenize via an array/offset map rather than string substitution.
findings.testStrategy: Add tests: input containing the literal text `FENCE0` outside code survives unchanged; inline code adjacent to punctuation (`use `x`.`) does not introduce a stray space.

findings.title: `moxxy channels` list path discards user argv flags (--config / --verbose / --model)
findings.problem: runList() boots the probe with `argvToSetupOptions({ flags: {} }, …)`, a hardcoded empty argv, instead of the real `argv`. As a result `moxxy channels --config ./custom.config.ts` (or `--verbose`) silently ignores those flags when listing channel availability — a user pointing at a non-default config gets availability computed against the default config. Every other path in this file and run-channel.ts threads the real `argv` through `argvToSetupOptions(argv, …)`.
findings.fix: runList should accept the parsed `argv` and pass it: `runList(argv)` → `argvToSetupOptions(argv, { … })`. The bare-`{flags:{}}` form is only appropriate when no argv is available, which is not the case here (runChannelsCommand has argv).
findings.testStrategy: Add a test invoking the channels-list path with `--config <tmp>` and assert the temp config's channel settings are reflected in the printed availability/`configured` markers.

findings.title: parseArgv greedily consumes the next token as a flag value, mis-parsing boolean flag + positional
findings.problem: For a value-less flag form `--name <token>`, the parser always treats the following non-dash token as the flag's value. So `moxxy plugins --reload install foo` binds reload='install' and drops 'install' from positionals; likewise `moxxy --allow-all bash` (allow-all is boolean per --help) yields flags['allow-all']='bash'. There is no flag schema, so the parser cannot distinguish boolean from value flags, and the heuristic silently mis-assigns. Help even lists boolean shortcuts (--allow-tools, --allow-all) that can swallow a following positional.
findings.fix: Either accept a known-boolean flag set (e.g. help/h/version/v/yes/y/verbose/allow-all/...) that never consumes a value, or require `=` for all value flags. Minimal: keep behavior but add tests pinning the documented semantics so future commands know booleans must use `--flag=...` or trailing position.
findings.testStrategy: Add parseArgv cases: `['plugins','--reload','install']` expected positional contains 'install'; `['--allow-all','bash']` expected allow-all===true and positional ['bash'] (after introducing the boolean set).

findings.title: openModelPicker mutates session.readyProviders as a side effect of opening a picker
