findings.problem: openModelPicker re-probes every provider's credentials via credentialResolver and writes the fresh Set back to sess.readyProviders. This is a read-modify-write of shared session state triggered merely by opening the /model UI, racing with any concurrent boot probe or a parallel openModelPicker (e.g. quick re-open). It also performs N credential resolutions (network/keychain for OAuth providers) every time the picker opens, with no caching/debounce.
findings.fix: Cache the probe result with a short TTL or only re-probe on explicit user request; treat readyProviders as derived state rather than mutating it from a UI-open path, or guard against concurrent probes.
findings.testStrategy: Open the picker twice rapidly with a slow resolver; assert no interleaved partial readyProviders writes.

findings.title: Loose-install fallback reports the frozen-lockfile error message on its own failure path
findings.problem: provisionWorkspace falls back from `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` to a loose `pnpm install`; if the loose install also fails, the returned message interpolates install.output (the frozen run) rather than loose.output, so the surfaced diagnostic is from the wrong invocation and may mislead the operator about why provisioning failed.
findings.fix: Return trunc(loose.output, 400) (the actual failing run) in the fallback-failure branch.
findings.testStrategy: Covered indirectly once provisionWorkspace has tests (u106-1); assert the message reflects the loose run when both fail.

findings.title: safeRead returns untrimmed body while listEntries/readEntry return trimmed body
findings.problem: safeRead returns `body: parsed.body` (raw) but readEntry (line 67) and listEntries (line 54) return `parsed.body.trim()`. Today this is benign because safeRead's only caller (store.ts:193 writeEntry) uses it solely to recover createdAt and discards the body. But the inconsistent contract is a latent trap: any future caller that reads the body via safeRead gets different whitespace than via get()/list(), with no signal that the two paths differ.
findings.fix: Make body handling consistent (trim in safeRead too, or document that safeRead intentionally returns raw body and is metadata-only). If unifying via u88-2's shared helper, expose a `trimBody` flag and pass it explicitly so the divergence is intentional and visible.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test asserting safeRead and readEntry agree on body for the same file (or asserting the documented difference).

findings.title: Filter evaluation runs before dedupe and re-parses body; ordering also re-runs verify-passed work on every retry
findings.problem: In handle(), order is verify -> shouldFire -> dedupe -> fire. shouldFire (which JSON-parses the body, see u116-1) runs on EVERY delivery including duplicate retries that the dedupe gate will immediately drop. For a noisy provider that retries aggressively, the expensive filter parse executes for every retry even though the dedupe key would short-circuit it. Reordering dedupe before shouldFire would skip the parse for known-duplicate deliveries.
findings.fix: Compute idempKey and run dedupe.check BEFORE shouldFire when an idempotency key is present (a duplicate need not be re-filtered). Keep verify first (cheap, and you don't want to dedupe-record unverified requests). Combine with u116-1 so the single parse only happens for non-duplicate, verified deliveries.
findings.testStrategy: Server test: send the same delivery id twice with a jsonPath filter; assert shouldFire's parse is invoked only on the first (spy on JSON.parse or on a filter seam).

findings.title: Slow-reader eviction in send() is silent to the JsonRpcPeer — a dropped request response leaves a pending caller
findings.problem: When SlowReaderGuard returns 'terminate', `WsTransport.send` calls `ws.terminate()` and returns without signaling failure. This is benign for notifications (broadcast is best-effort, client reconnects), but `send` is also used to deliver JSON-RPC RESPONSE frames to a client request. If a response is dropped this way the server-side handler has already resolved, while the client's pending request only resolves when the socket close propagates a reject. The terminate does fire 'close' (so the client eventually rejects), so it is recoverable, but the path is undocumented for the request/response case and relies on close propagation for cleanup rather than an explicit error.
findings.fix: After terminate, proactively invoke emitClose (or rely on documenting that terminate's 'close' is the cleanup path). At minimum add a comment that response delivery on an evicted reader is intentionally lossy and recovered via reconnect, so a future reader doesn't assume send() is reliable for responses.
findings.testStrategy: Test that a peer evicted mid-response emits onClose so the runner's pending-request map rejects; assert no pending entry is left dangling.

findings.title: mergeConfigs concatenates arrays, silently violating documented 'later wins' for replace-semantics fields
findings.problem: mergeInto concatenates arrays (existing ++ value) for ALL array fields. The header comment says 'later wins on scalars; arrays are concatenated' so additive fields (permissions.allow/deny) are intentional, but provider.fallbacks and skills.extraDirs also concat across user+project scopes, so a project cannot OVERRIDE a user-level fallback list — it can only append, producing duplicate/contradictory ordering. There is no way to express replacement, and the precedence doc ('project config > user config') is false for any array field.
findings.fix: Either document that array fields are always additive (and dedupe fallbacks), or make replace-vs-concat per-field (concat for permissions allow/deny; replace for provider.fallbacks/skills.extraDirs). Add a test asserting the chosen semantics for fallbacks.
findings.testStrategy: mergeConfigs({provider:{name:'a',fallbacks:['x']}},{provider:{name:'a',fallbacks:['y']}}) — assert the intended result (replace -> ['y'] or dedup-concat) and lock it.

findings.title: loadDir aborts the entire discovery scan if a file is removed mid-scan
findings.problem: `fs.readFile(full, 'utf8')` at line 80 is not wrapped in try/catch. The outer `loadDir` only catches the `fs.readdir` failure. If a `.yaml` is unlinked (e.g. a concurrent `/workflows rm`, or the atomic write's `.tmp` window) between readdir and readFile, the ENOENT rejects, propagates up through `discoverWorkflows`/`store.load()`, and aborts loading ALL remaining sources/workflows — turning a transient race into a total discovery failure. Parse failures are tolerated (logged + skipped) but I/O failures are not.
findings.fix: Wrap the per-file readFile in try/catch; on error, `logger?.warn?.('workflow: unreadable file, skipping', { path: full })` and `continue`, matching the invalid-parse handling.
findings.testStrategy: Test that discoverWorkflows over a dir with one valid file and one path that ENOENTs on read returns the valid file rather than rejecting.

findings.title: Module-level titleRefreshTimer not cleared when ChatStoreBridge unmounts
findings.problem: scheduleSessionTitleRefresh uses a module-global `titleRefreshTimer` set by a setTimeout that fires desksStore.refresh() 1s later. ChatStoreBridge's cleanup (lines 132-137) unsubscribes the IPC listeners but never clears this pending timer. If the bridge unmounts (HMR, multi-window teardown) within the 1s window, the timer still fires desksStore.refresh() against a possibly torn-down transport. Harmless in steady state but a latent timer leak across the module's lifetime.
findings.fix: Clear titleRefreshTimer in ChatStoreBridge's effect cleanup (clearTimeout + null it), or make the timer instance-scoped via a ref inside the bridge.
findings.testStrategy: Render ChatStoreBridge, dispatch a user_prompt on an auto-named session, unmount within 1s, assert desksStore.refresh is not invoked after unmount.

findings.title: String(input[k]) collapses objects/arrays to '[object Object]', so inputMatches can't match structured input
findings.problem: matchRule coerces the candidate with `String(input[k] ?? '')`. When the tool input field is an object or array (common for structured tool args), this yields '[object Object]' or comma-joined elements, so any inputMatches regex against a structured field silently can never match what the author expects. The author gets no error and the rule quietly behaves as a non-match — for a deny rule that is again a fail-open surprise.
findings.fix: Either (a) restrict inputMatches to string-valued fields and skip/warn on non-string values, or (b) coerce via JSON.stringify for non-primitives so the regex sees the serialized form. Document the chosen behavior. Tie deny-rule non-matches to a fail-closed stance per u40-1.
findings.testStrategy: Test inputMatches against a call whose field is an object/array and assert the documented behavior (match on JSON form, or explicit non-match with no silent allow for deny rules).

findings.title: Module-global cachedJiti is keyed by the first cwd seen, stale for later .ts configs elsewhere
findings.problem: getJiti memoizes a single jiti instance in module-global `cachedJiti`, created with the dirname of the FIRST .ts config loaded (factory(cwd, ...)). A later loadConfig for a .ts config in a different directory reuses the jiti bound to the original cwd, so jiti's module resolution / interopDefault context is wrong for the second project. Affects long-lived hosts (desktop/runner) that load configs for multiple workspaces in one process.
findings.fix: Key the cache by cwd (Map<string, jiti>) or drop memoization (jiti construction is cheap relative to .ts compile). Given .ts configs are rare, a per-cwd Map is the safe minimal change.
findings.testStrategy: Load two .ts configs from two temp dirs in one process and assert each resolves its own relative imports; today the second silently uses the first dir's resolution base.

findings.title: autoLayout longest-path recursion can stack-overflow / mis-layer on cyclic input
findings.problem: autoLayout's resolve() recurses over needs with a per-call `seen` cycle guard returning depth 0 on a back-edge, but `seen.delete(id)` on unwind means a diamond/shared-subgraph is re-walked for each path (exponential in the worst case for dense DAGs) and a large/deep imported graph recurses without an explicit stack. hydrate() runs this on any workflow lacking ui.layout (hand-authored or freshly imported), i.e. attacker/user-controlled YAML.
findings.fix: Compute depths iteratively via Kahn topological order (already needed elsewhere) with an explicit cycle break, giving O(N+E) and no recursion-depth risk.
findings.testStrategy: Layout a 1000-node linear chain and a wide diamond; assert it completes fast and columns are monotonic.

findings.title: lastError is sticky: an early error-stopReason assistant_message poisons a later successful turn result
findings.problem: Both runners set `lastError` on any assistant_message with stopReason==='error' (or an error event) but never clear it. If a turn emits an early assistant_message that ends in 'error' and a later assistant_message that succeeds (multi-round turns / retries), `text` is overwritten by the success but `lastError` remains, so a successful run is reported as failed (scheduler inbox / webhook reply shows a spurious error).
findings.fix: Reset lastError to null when an assistant_message arrives without an error stopReason (the latest assistant_message is authoritative for the final outcome), or only consult the final assistant_message's stopReason.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a fake runTurn yielding assistant_message{stopReason:'error'} then assistant_message{stopReason:'stop'}; assert result has no error.

findings.title: key.ts single-char keystroke ignores its own KEY_CODES catalog for chars that map to named keys
findings.problem: A key resolves via KEY_CODES only on an exact lowercase name match; otherwise any length-1 string goes to `keystroke`. This is correct, but combined with the catalog `space`/`tab`/`return` being the ONLY way to send those keys, passing e.g. key:' ' (a literal space) is keystroked as a space char rather than the System Events space key — generally equivalent, but for `tab`/`return` the model must use the named form. The description documents named keys but does not warn that single literal whitespace bypasses key-code handling, a minor footgun for chords like ctrl+space (key:' ',modifiers:['control']) which would keystroke a space with control held instead of pressing the Space key code.
findings.fix: Optional hardening: when key.length===1 and the char is whitespace (' ','\t','\r','\n'), route to the corresponding KEY_CODES entry so modifier chords behave as key presses. At minimum document that named keys are required for whitespace chords.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: key:' ' with modifiers:['control'] produces a `key code 49 using {control down}` script after the fix.

findings.title: Block scalar parser ignores fold/strip chomping nuances and loses leading indentation
findings.problem: parseBlockScalar joins folded (`>`) lines with a single space unconditionally and uses the first non-blank line's indent as blockIndent for ALL lines. A block-scalar body whose lines are indented MORE than the first line will have that extra indentation silently stripped (slice(blockIndent) on a deeper line keeps it, but a SHALLOWER-but-still-deeper-than-parent line under-slices). Folding also doesn't preserve blank-line paragraph breaks. For prompts (the main consumer) this can alter whitespace-significant content. Lower severity than u129-1 since the emitter never produces `>` and emits consistent indentation, but host-canonical YAML may.
findings.fix: Compute blockIndent as the minimum indent of the block body (or parentIndent+offset), preserve relative indentation, and implement fold's blank-line-as-newline rule. Or document that only `|`/`|-` from the canonical emitter are supported and reject `>`.
findings.testStrategy: Round-trip a block scalar with indented sub-lines and blank-line paragraph breaks.

findings.title: Screenshot temp filenames can collide under concurrent calls (Date.now() not unique within a ms)
findings.problem: Both the capture PNG and the output file are named with `process.pid` + `Date.now()`. Two concurrent computer_screenshot invocations in the same process within the same millisecond would generate identical paths, so one call's screencapture/sips could read or overwrite the other's temp file, returning corrupt or wrong-region bytes. Tool calls are user-prompted so concurrency is unlikely, hence low severity, but the uniqueness guarantee is real-world brittle.
findings.fix: Append a random suffix (e.g. `crypto.randomUUID()` or an incrementing module counter) to both temp names, or use a single mkdtemp dir per call. The SDK has fs-utils helpers for atomic temp paths worth reusing.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test generating two filenames back-to-back asserts inequality; or fire two screenshot handlers concurrently against a mocked runProcess and assert distinct paths.

findings.title: Status stays 'open' for the whole backoff window after a link drop
findings.problem: handleClose() reconnects with up to 30s exponential backoff but never transitions status away from 'open' while waiting. setStatus('reconnecting') only fires when the scheduled timer fires connect(). So after the socket dies, this.socket is null yet client.status (and any onStatus observer) still reports 'open' for the entire delay (up to RECONNECT_MAX_DELAY_MS = 30s). A UI driving a connection indicator off onStatus will show 'connected' for up to 30s after the link is actually dead, and won't show 'reconnecting' until the next attempt begins. Requests issued in that window also silently queue (status not disconnected/closed) rather than reflecting the degraded state.
findings.fix: In handleClose(), after deciding to reconnect (before scheduling the timer), call this.setStatus('reconnecting') so the gap between drop and the next attempt is observable. Leave the per-attempt setStatus('reconnecting') in connect() (idempotent via the equality guard at line 166).
findings.testStrategy: Extend json-rpc-client.test.ts: open the socket, push 'open' to a captured statuses array, then socket.close() and assert client.status === 'reconnecting' (and statuses contains 'reconnecting') BEFORE advancing fake timers — currently it would still read 'open'.

findings.title: matchesGlob compiles a fresh RegExp per pattern per path on every check
findings.problem: matchesGlob rebuilds and recompiles the regex from the glob string on every call. checkFsCap calls it for each path x each pattern (O(paths*patterns) recompiles), and pathInScope recompiles on every broker fs syscall. The glob set per tool is fixed, so the regex could be compiled once and cached.
findings.fix: Memoize compiled RegExp by pattern string in a module-level Map, or precompile patterns once per cap into RegExp[] and reuse across paths/calls.
findings.testStrategy: Behavioral tests unchanged; add a micro-benchmark or assert cache hit via spy if desired. Existing cap tests guard correctness.

findings.title: Connection-cap check (verifyClient) and the connections counter race; cap can be exceeded
findings.problem: The max-connections gate is enforced in `verifyClient` by reading the mutable `connections` counter, but `connections` is only incremented later in the `'connection'` handler (after the upgrade completes). Between two near-simultaneous upgrade handshakes, both read `connections < maxConnections` and both are admitted, so the live count can transiently exceed `maxConnections`. The same counter is also what `clientCount()` reports to the pairing UI. It is a soft cap, not a hard one, and the documentation/comments present it as a firm limit.
findings.fix: Increment a provisional counter inside verifyClient when returning true (and decrement it if the socket never reaches 'connection', e.g. via the wss 'headers'/connection bookkeeping), or gate on `wss.clients.size` plus an in-flight-upgrade counter. Simplest: increment `connections` optimistically in verifyClient right before returning the auth result, and decrement on the upgrade-failure path. Given it is loopback + token-gated by default, keep severity low but make the cap actually hard.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: drive N+1 concurrent upgrade requests (don't await the first's 'connection' before opening the next) against a server with maxConnections=N and assert at most N sockets end up OPEN.

findings.title: 401-retry replay does not re-test for 401; a second 401 falls through to a generic non-auth error
findings.problem: On the first 401, `refreshNow()` runs and the request is replayed (provider.ts:124-134). The replayed `response` is then evaluated only by the `!response.ok` branch (line 136), which classifies retryability via `classifyHttpStatus` and emits `Codex /responses returned 401: ...`. A second 401 is therefore surfaced as a generic HTTP error rather than the clear 'OAuth credentials revoked — run moxxy login' guidance, and 401 is classified non-retryable by the SDK status classifier, which is correct but the message is unhelpful for the most common cause (expired/revoked grant after refresh failed silently). The provider.test.ts:346 case asserts only that some error mentions 401, so the weak message is not caught.
findings.fix: After the replay, if `response.status === 401`, emit a targeted error event (`'ChatGPT OAuth credentials were rejected after refresh; run `moxxy login openai-codex` to re-authenticate.'`, retryable:false) before the generic branch, mirroring ensureFresh's message.
findings.testStrategy: Extend the double-401 test to assert the error message references re-authentication / `moxxy login openai-codex`, not just the substring '401'.

findings.title: renderResult discards MCP image/resource payloads, returning only placeholders
findings.problem: An MCP tool that returns an image or resource block yields only `[image:mime]` / `[resource]` to the model — the actual base64 data and resource contents are dropped. MCP servers (screenshot, chart, file-fetch tools) legitimately return image/resource content; the model is told an image exists but can never see it, silently degrading those servers. The rest of moxxy already has a `document`/image ContentBlock + ToolDisplayResult path for surfacing rich content.
findings.fix: Decide intentionally: either document that MCP image/resource results are unsupported, or surface them — e.g. return a ToolDisplayResult whose `forModel` keeps the placeholder while `display` carries the image, and/or pass through resource text when the resource block has inline text. At minimum stop silently swallowing `block.data`.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test renderResult with an image block and a resource-with-text block; assert the data is not lost.

findings.title: PermissionDialog has an empty useEffect with a misleading comment (dead plumbing)
findings.problem: An empty `useEffect(() => { /* comment */ }, [])` does literally nothing — the comment claims it 'auto-focuses the dialog by capturing input on mount' but the body is empty; focus is actually achieved by useInput (line 25). It's dormant 'just in case' plumbing that misleads readers into thinking a mount side-effect exists.
findings.fix: Delete the empty useEffect (and the unused `useEffect` import becomes removable).
findings.testStrategy: Behavior-preserving deletion; existing render covers it.

findings.title: A single malformed server entry makes readMcpConfig silently discard the ENTIRE catalog
findings.problem: readMcpConfig safeParses the whole file against mcpStoredConfigSchema and on any failure returns `{servers: []}` — every other valid server entry disappears too. Because the array element schema requires `name: z.string().min(1)`, one hand-edited entry missing/empty name causes ALL configured MCP servers to vanish from boot, list, enable/disable, and removal (removeServerFromConfig also reads through this and would then no-op). The user gets no warning at the readMcpConfig layer (onInit's catch only fires on a thrown error, but this path returns a value, not throws). Severity is low because it's documented as intentional, but the blast radius (all servers, not the one bad row) is surprising and silent.
findings.fix: Either (a) parse leniently per-entry (filter out invalid rows, keep valid ones) or (b) at minimum emit a logger.warn from readMcpConfig (or have callers detect the empty-vs-missing-file distinction) so a corrupt file doesn't silently strand every server. Given .passthrough() already tolerates extras, dropping only the offending row is the better fix.
findings.testStrategy: Test readMcpConfig with a file containing one valid + one nameless server; assert the valid one survives (after fix) rather than the whole catalog being emptied.

findings.title: describeEntry computes nextFireAt from a different baseline than the poller's isDue, so UI can show a misleading next-fire
findings.problem: describeEntry uses `since = lastRunAt ?? max(createdAt, now)` while isDue (poller) uses `since = lastRunAt ?? createdAt`. For a never-run cron schedule whose first fire is already past (created during downtime), the tool reports a next-fire in the future (because it anchors at now), while the poller will actually fire it immediately. The displayed nextFireAt contradicts behavior.
findings.fix: Share one helper (e.g. export a `computeNextFire(entry)` used by both isDue and describeEntry) so the displayed next-fire and the firing decision agree. Decide deliberately whether to anchor at createdAt (shows the catch-up fire) — and document it.
findings.testStrategy: Unit: a cron entry created 2h ago with first-fire 1h ago and no lastRunAt — assert describeEntry().nextFireAt matches the instant isDue would fire (<= now).

findings.title: Resize drag registers window listeners that leak if the rail unmounts mid-drag
findings.problem: startDrag adds pointermove/pointerup listeners on window and sets document.body.style.userSelect='none'; they are only removed by the pointerup handler. If the ContextRail unmounts while a drag is in flight (e.g. the user collapses the rail via keyboard, or the active workspace changes and the parent stops rendering the rail), pointerup never fires for this closure, leaving a dangling pointermove listener calling setRailWidth and userSelect stuck off. There is no effect-cleanup tying these listeners to the component lifetime.
findings.fix: Scope the listeners with an AbortController stored in a ref and abort it in a useEffect cleanup, or attach the listeners to setPointerCapture on the handle element so they auto-release. At minimum restore userSelect in an unmount cleanup.
findings.testStrategy: In a test, pointerDown on the handle, then unmount the component, then dispatch a window pointermove and assert setRailWidth was not called after unmount and document.body.style.userSelect is reset.

findings.title: Empty prompt-frame action is silently swallowed with no ack to the browser
findings.problem: On a prompt frame with whitespace-only text the handler returns without driving a turn AND without sending any frame back to the browser. The browser input box has no signal that the prompt was rejected, so a user who submits blank/whitespace gets no feedback (vs. action frames which always ack). Minor UX inconsistency, not a crash.
findings.fix: Either trim/reject at the frontend, or send a lightweight status/ack frame for ignored empty prompts so the surface can clear its pending state. Low priority.
findings.testStrategy: Channel test: send a whitespace-only prompt frame, assert no turn driven (existing) and optionally that a rejection signal is delivered.

findings.title: spawnCliTunnel matches urlRegex per stdout chunk — misses a URL split across chunks
findings.problem: onData runs `urlRegex.exec(buf.toString('utf8'))` against each individual stdout/stderr chunk in isolation. A pipe 'data' event is not line-buffered: if the assigned public URL straddles two chunk boundaries (large URL, slow/fragmented flush, or a chunk that ends mid-URL), no single chunk matches and the tunnel times out / rejects even though the CLI did print the URL. cloudflared/ngrok normally flush the URL on one line so this is rare, but it is a latent flake with no buffering safeguard.
findings.fix: Accumulate a rolling buffer string across data events (capped, e.g. keep last ~8KB) and run urlRegex against the accumulation, not the lone chunk. Reset/cap to bound memory while settled=false.
findings.testStrategy: Add a tunnel.test.ts case that spawns a node child writing the URL in two process.stdout.write() calls split mid-host (e.g. 'https://abc-1' then '23.example.test\n') and assert it still resolves.

findings.title: dispose() leaves the permission resolver pointing at the disposed host
findings.problem: dispose() clears the approval resolver (setApprovalResolver(null)) and drains pendingAsks once, but never detaches the permission resolver (setPermissionResolver only accepts a non-null resolver, so it CAN'T be nulled). A permission check that arrives after dispose() (e.g. a turn that wasn't aborted in time, or a re-used session) calls openAsk(), which broadcasts to the now-closed bus and parks a resolver in pendingAsks that is never drained again — the awaiting check hangs forever instead of failing closed.
findings.fix: Add a `disposed` guard at the top of openAsk(): if disposed, resolve immediately to {mode:'deny'} (fail closed) instead of parking. Optionally swap setPermissionResolver to install a static deny resolver on dispose, or extend the SDK to accept a nullable permission resolver.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: dispose() then invoke permissionResolver.check(...) and assert it resolves to {mode:'deny'} (does not hang).

findings.title: bootMobile() invoked as a side effect inside useState lazy initializer
findings.problem: The ENV-URL fast path runs `bootMobile(ENV_URL, ENV_TOKEN)` (which mutates global transport/platform config via configureTransport/configurePlatform) from inside the `useState` lazy initializer. Side effects in render are a React anti-pattern: under any future StrictMode/double-invoke or re-mount the transport gets reconfigured unexpectedly, and the effect is invisible to React's dependency tracking.
findings.fix: Move the ENV-URL boot into a `useEffect(() => { if (ENV_URL) bootMobile(...) }, [])` and seed `connected` from `!!ENV_URL` without the side effect, or call bootMobile once at module scope before the component mounts.
findings.testStrategy: Mount App under StrictMode with ENV_URL set; assert configureTransport is called exactly once.

findings.title: Constructor seeds events without aligning base to seed[0].seq — latent index/seq desync
findings.problem: The constructor pushes seed events but leaves base=0, relying on the unstated invariant that seed[0].seq === 0 (so seq === base + index holds for at()/slice()/ingest()). Nothing validates this. The only current caller (build-session.ts -> restoreEvents) re-sequences survivors to 0..n-1, so it holds today — but a future caller seeding a tail slice (e.g. seq 50..) would get silently wrong at()/slice() seq-addressing and an ingest() contiguity gate off by 50, with no error. rebase() exists precisely for this case but the constructor offers no way to combine seed + non-zero base, and no guard against the mismatch.
findings.fix: In the constructor, when seed.length > 0 set `this.base = seed[0].seq` (or assert seed[0].seq === 0), so seq-addressing is correct regardless of caller. Optionally assert contiguity of seeded seqs in dev.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test: `new EventLog([eventWithSeq5...])` then `at(5)` returns it and `length`/`slice` line up; and that ingest() of seq 5+length is accepted.

findings.title: worker message/error/exit listeners not added to cleanup set (rely solely on terminate)
findings.problem: The run() Promise registers a persistent worker.on('message') listener (line 262) plus once('error')/once('exit') (lines 285,289), but only the timer and the parent-signal abort listener are added to the cleanup Set (lines 252,260). After finish() runs, settled-guards prevent re-entry and worker.terminate() is fired, so in practice the listeners stop mattering — but the cleanup contract is asymmetric: 'message' is a non-once listener kept alive until terminate resolves, and terminate is fire-and-forgotten (void worker.terminate()). If terminate() ever rejects/hangs, the worker + its listeners linger. Minor, but inconsistent with the otherwise-careful cleanup discipline.
findings.fix: Add worker.removeAllListeners() (or explicit off() calls) into the cleanup set, and attach a .catch on worker.terminate() to swallow/log rejection. Keeps cleanup symmetric and avoids a dangling worker if terminate() faults.
findings.testStrategy: Hard to unit-test directly; assert via a leak-style test that after N sequential run() calls the process has no extra live workers (worker_threads has no public count, so this is best verified by review).

findings.title: Piped-backend write() can throw EPIPE between shell exit and the exit listener firing
findings.problem: write() guards on `this.alive`, but `alive` only flips false when the 'exit'/'error' event fires. Node delivers process exit asynchronously, so there is a window where the child has already exited (stdin closed) yet alive is still true. `this.child?.stdin.write(data)` then writes to a closed pipe and can throw EPIPE synchronously/emit an unhandled 'error' on stdin, crashing the host. The PTY path is similarly unguarded but pty.write tends to be tolerant; the pipe path is the exposed one.
findings.fix: Wrap the write in try/catch (ignore EPIPE) and attach a no-op 'error' handler to child.stdin in the constructor so a broken-pipe error never goes unhandled.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: spawn pipe backend, kill the child, then call write() and assert it does not throw / does not emit an unhandled error.

findings.title: copyUrl setTimeout not cleared on unmount → setState-on-unmounted warning
findings.problem: `copyUrl` schedules `setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 1500)` with no cleanup. If the user copies and then switches tabs / closes Settings within 1.5s, the timer fires `setCopied` on an unmounted component (React warning; harmless but sloppy, and a small leaked timer). The sibling `QrCode` effect correctly uses an `alive` flag, so this is an inconsistency within the same file.
findings.fix: Track the timer in a ref and clear it on unmount, or use an effect: store the timeout id and return a cleanup that clears it; or set a `copiedAt` timestamp and derive via effect.
findings.testStrategy: Render MobileTab with gateway on, click Copy, unmount immediately, assert no act/state-update warning (vi spy on console.error).

findings.title: runUpdate awaits onUpdateCli without try/catch — a rejecting promise strands the spinner
findings.problem: runUpdate() does `const result = await onUpdateCli();` with no try/catch. The prop type is `() => Promise<UpdateCliResult>`, which permits a rejected promise. If onUpdateCli ever rejects (rather than resolving { ok:false }), the function throws, status stays 'updating' forever (disabled spinner, no error, no manual escape hatch), and React logs an unhandled rejection. The error UI (status==='error') is only reachable through the resolved { ok:false } path.
findings.fix: Wrap the await in try/catch: on catch, `setError(toErrorMessage(e) ?? 'The update failed…'); setStatus('error');` so any rejection falls back to the same error + manual-command surface instead of a permanent spinner.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test passing `onUpdateCli={vi.fn().mockRejectedValue(new Error('boom'))}`; click the button; assert the role=alert error appears and the button leaves the disabled 'updating' state.

findings.title: Late node-probe inserts the node step mid-list; linear cursor can misalign
findings.problem: ctx.nodeProbed = (ob.node !== null). On first paint ob.node is null, so the node step's applies() is false and it is filtered out of flow.steps. When onboarding.probeNode later resolves to 'missing', the node step (id position 1) appears, growing the active list AFTER the cursor was initialized. useStepFlow tracks position by a raw integer cursor (step-flow.ts:87) indexing into the live `active` array, not by step id — so if the active list mutates while cursor>0 the cursor points at a different step than intended. In the normal first-run flow node resolves while Welcome (cursor 0) is shown, so the shift is benign; but any path where the probe resolves after the user has advanced (slow/Windows probe, recovery re-entry) risks routing the user to the wrong step or skipping node.
findings.fix: Anchor linear navigation to step id rather than integer index when the applicable set can change (track currentId, recompute index from it on each resolve), OR gate the whole Onboarding render until ob.node has been probed (node!==null) so the step list is stable before the linear walk starts. The fix touches step-flow (unit 33); flow.ts/Onboarding.tsx is the trigger.
findings.testStrategy: Drive resolveFlow in linear mode: start with node filtered out, advance cursor to 1, then flip ctx so the node step now applies, and assert the resolved currentId is still the intended step (not a silent shift).

findings.title: Turn hiding races IPC ordering: hideTurn(id) runs only after the runTurn invoke resolves
findings.problem: start() awaits `api().invoke('session.runTurn')` and only calls `chatStore.hideTurn(id)` after the promise resolves. The host's session-driver starts the event pump and emits runner.event/user_prompt via log.subscribe independently of when the invoke RPC reply lands. The code relies entirely on the IPC round-trip being slower than the first emitted event; if an event for this turn reaches the main useChat bridge before hideTurn is set, it leaks into the visible transcript (the very thing this hook exists to prevent). The comment 'Hide BEFORE we start reading' overstates the guarantee — hiding happens after the RPC, not before the turn runs.
findings.fix: Have the host hide the turn at source, or return the turnId synchronously and let the renderer hide it before the runner begins emitting — e.g. split into a `session.beginHiddenTurn` that registers the hidden id on the host before the pump starts. At minimum, document the latency dependency honestly.
findings.testStrategy: Integration test emitting a runner.event synchronously in the same microtask as the runTurn resolve and asserting chatStore drops it; or assert host registers the hidden id before the pump's first session.runTurn await.

findings.title: session-action 'exit' directive is silently ignored by the desktop palette
findings.problem: session.runCommand can return `{ kind:'session-action', action:'exit' }` (the /exit command, contract index.ts:762 lists action 'new'|'clear'|'exit'). run() only branches on 'clear' and 'new'; 'exit' falls through with no handling — the desktop neither closes the session nor surfaces anything beyond a generic notice card. Either intended (desktop has no 'exit') or a quiet gap; worth an explicit decision rather than silent fall-through.
findings.fix: Either explicitly no-op 'exit' for desktop with a comment, or hide the /exit command from the desktop palette via the channels filter, or map it to a window/session close. Document the decision inline.
findings.testStrategy: Test run() with a mocked 'session-action'/'exit' result asserting the intended desktop behavior (no crash, no stray empty action_result).

findings.title: mirrorForeignTurn does not filter event-log subscribers by turnId (invariant #8)
findings.problem: mirrorForeignTurn subscribes to session.log and re-broadcasts every `assistant_message` whenever `!busy`, using a coarse boolean to distinguish our own turns from foreign ones. Invariant #8 requires filtering event-log subscribers by turnId when multiplexing turns on one Session. The `busy` boolean is set in runUserTurn's try/finally; the final assistant_message of our own turn is emitted during the turn (busy=true) so it is normally skipped, but there is no turnId correlation: any assistant_message dispatched in the window after busy flips false in the `finally` (e.g. a late/async-dispatched event, or replay/resync on a RemoteSession) will be mirrored, risking a duplicate post of our own turn's prose into lastChatId.
findings.fix: Track the turnId of the turn this channel initiated (set in runUserTurn) and in mirrorForeignTurn skip events whose `event.turnId` equals an own-turn id, instead of (or in addition to) the busy boolean. This makes the dedup robust to async event ordering and matches invariant #8.
findings.testStrategy: Feed an assistant_message with the channel's own current turnId while busy=false and assert no sendMessage; feed one with a different turnId and assert it mirrors.

findings.title: installFullAppUpdate leaves a download-progress listener on the singleton autoUpdater after it returns
findings.problem: The function calls removeAllListeners('download-progress') only at the START of each attempt, then attaches a new listener. On the happy path it schedules quitAndInstall and returns with the listener still attached; on a thrown error (no installer / download fails) it rejects with the listener still attached. autoUpdater is a process-wide singleton also used by initShellUpdater. The closure captures opts.onProgress, so a failed/abandoned attempt pins that callback (and whatever it closes over, e.g. an IPC reply channel) until the next installFullAppUpdate call clears it. It is self-healing on retry but is a latent listener/closure leak between attempts.
findings.fix: Wrap the body in try/finally and removeAllListeners('download-progress') in the finally (the happy path defers quitAndInstall to setImmediate, so detaching synchronously before that fires is safe). Or capture the listener fn and autoUpdater.off it in finally.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a fake autoUpdater recording listener add/remove counts; assert listenerCount('download-progress') === 0 after both a successful and a failing call.

findings.title: Identity-persist effect keyed only on user.id can miss late-hydrating displayName
findings.problem: The effect that persists the Clerk identity into desktop prefs reads user.fullName / primaryEmailAddress / username but its dependency array (lint disabled) is only [user?.id, prefs?.clerkUserId]. Clerk hydrates the User object progressively: user.id can be available one render before fullName/email resolve. The effect fires on the first render where id !== prefs.clerkUserId, persists whatever displayName fields exist at that instant (possibly null/username fallback), and then never re-fires for the same id when the richer name arrives — so clerkDisplayName can be persisted as a less-preferred fallback and stays stale until the next launch with a changed id (which itself short-circuits on `prefs?.clerkUserId === user.id`).
findings.fix: Add a resolved-displayName value to the dep array (e.g. compute `const resolvedName = user?.fullName ?? ... ?? null` and depend on `[user?.id, resolvedName, prefs?.clerkUserId]`), or gate the write so it also fires when the chosen name differs from prefs.clerkDisplayName for the same id. Then drop the eslint-disable.
findings.testStrategy: RTL test: mount with a Clerk user mock exposing {id} only, assert update called with clerkDisplayName=null/username; then rerender with fullName populated (same id) and assert update is called again with the full name.

findings.title: Truncated-stream flush silently drops function calls whose name never arrived
findings.problem: The post-loop flush only re-emits tool_use_end for pending entries where emittedStart===true. If the server sent output_item.added(function_call) and arguments.delta frames but the item never carried a `name` (so emittedStart stayed false) and the stream then truncated before .done, that call is dropped entirely with no tool_use_start/end and no error — the model's tool request is silently lost. This is the same failure class the flush was written to defend against, but it leaves a gap for the no-name case.
findings.fix: On flush, for a pending entry with emittedStart===false but a non-empty name buffered, emit the tool_use_start before tool_use_end (mirroring the .done branch at sse-event-handler.ts:100-103); if there is genuinely no name, at minimum log a one-shot MOXXY_DEBUG note so a dropped call is observable rather than silent.
findings.testStrategy: Feed a stream ending after arguments.delta with an item that never carried a name; assert the call is either emitted or visibly logged, not silently swallowed.

findings.title: ModeRegistry.replace of the active mode does not notify onActiveChange listeners despite the def changing
findings.problem: replace() calls activate() only when nothing is active. When the ACTIVE mode's def is replaced in place (same name, new behavior — e.g. a hot-reloaded mode plugin), activate() is not called at all, and even if it were, activate() early-returns when this.active === mode.name. So a runner observing onActiveChange (used to broadcast InfoChanged) is never told the active mode's definition swapped under it. Remote clients can keep displaying / driving the stale mode behavior.
findings.fix: Decide intended semantics: if replacing the active mode should notify, have replace() fire the changeListeners when name === this.active (e.g. call a notify() that doesn't have activate's name-equality early-return). Document the chosen behavior either way.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: register mode 'm' (auto-active), subscribe onActiveChange, replace('m', newDef), assert listener fired exactly once.

findings.title: viewId from surface.nextViewId() is returned to the model but ignored by every consumer
findings.problem: present_view calls surface.nextViewId() and returns the value as PresentViewResult.viewId, but the actual view-delivery path (the web channel's Projector) generates its OWN viewId from the tool_result event (`v_${turnId}_${++viewSeq}`) and never reads the result's viewId. There are thus two independent, never-reconciled viewId generators; the surface's counter (`v_srv_*` in channel.ts:253/349) is advanced purely to populate a result field no machinery consumes. It is not strictly dead (the model sees it in the tool result) but it is dormant plumbing that can mislead — the id the model is told differs from the id the frontend actually uses.
findings.fix: Either (a) drop nextViewId() / result.viewId entirely and let the projector own viewId allocation (single source of truth), or (b) if the model is meant to reference a stable view id, have the projector reuse the result.viewId instead of minting its own. Pick one; today both exist and disagree.
findings.testStrategy: If removing: delete the field and assert present-view.test.ts no longer expects viewId. If unifying: add a channel test asserting the frame.viewId equals the result.viewId for the same callId.

findings.title: Boot probe re-arms only on the first did-finish-load; an OAuth-recovery reload before confirm is not re-probed
findings.problem: armBootProbe uses webContents.once('did-finish-load'). installAccountPortalRecovery (319) can reload the window to the app root mid-flow, and did-start-loading resets rendererReady. If the FIRST load is the stranded Account-Portal page (which never mounts #root, so the DOM check stays false) and the recovery reload happens after that first did-finish-load, the probe is already consuming its single deadline against the wrong document and may time out and poison a perfectly good bundle, or — if recovery lands just in time — confirm correctly by luck. The probe's health signal is tied to one specific load, not 'the app eventually mounted'.
findings.fix: Keep a single overall deadline but re-evaluate health across loads: register did-finish-load with .on (not .once) and clear/re-poll on each, or poll on a wall-clock interval independent of which document is current, confirming as soon as #root mounts regardless of how many intermediate loads occurred. Cancel on confirm.
findings.testStrategy: Drive a fake webContents that fires did-finish-load on a portal page (no #root), then a reload that fires did-finish-load on the real app (mounts #root) before the deadline; assert markConfirmed is called and relaunch is NOT.

findings.title: Reinstall note text is hardcoded to 'app older than runner' even when the real cause is no updater wired
findings.problem: canUpdate = appNewerThanRunner && !!onUpdateCli. When appNewerThanRunner is true (e.g. versions unknown → defaults true) but onUpdateCli is undefined, canUpdate is false and the user sees 'This app is older than the moxxy runner it found' — a factually wrong diagnosis (the app may be newer/equal; the updater just wasn't passed). Two distinct conditions (older app vs. no updater) collapse into one misleading message.
findings.fix: Branch the note on the actual reason: only show 'app is older, reinstall' when appNewerThanRunner is false; when onUpdateCli is simply absent, show a neutral 'update the CLI manually (see details)' note. Alternatively assert onUpdateCli is always present in the terminal path via the type.
findings.testStrategy: Render protocol-incompatible with unknown versions (serverVersion:null, clientVersion:null) and no onUpdateCli; assert the note does NOT claim the app is older than the runner.

findings.title: cursor mixes EventLog.length (index count) with slice(seq) — only correct when base===0
findings.problem: onInit captures `cursor = ctx.log.length` and onShutdown folds `ctx.log.slice(cursor)`. In the real EventLog, `get length()` returns `this.events.length` (a count of HELD events) but `slice(from)` interprets `from` as an absolute seq (`events.slice(from - base)`). These agree only because authoring logs have base===0. If this plugin ever ran against a rebased log (EventLog primed via partial-replay `rebase`, runner protocol v6 `replay.start`, where baseSeq>0), `cursor` (an index count) would be passed to `slice` as a seq and clamp to 0, re-folding the ENTIRE restored prefix and double-counting all prior usage into ~/.moxxy/usage.json. Currently latent: the plugin is registered only in CLI builtins (authoring/runner side) where base===0, never on a client mirror — so the bug is dormant, not active. But the invariant 'usage runs only on a base-0 log' is undocumented and silently load-bearing.
findings.fix: Make the cursor a seq, not a length, so it is base-correct regardless of rebasing. Either add a `baseSeq`/`nextSeq` accessor to EventLogReader and capture `cursor = ctx.log.baseSeq + ctx.log.length` (the seq of the next-to-be-appended event), or have the plugin fold via a seq-addressed cut it computes from the live tail. Minimally, document in EventLogReader that `length` is an index count and `slice` is seq-addressed, and assert base===0 (or read baseSeq) here.
findings.testStrategy: Add a unit test using an EventLogReader whose baseSeq>0 (simulate a rebased mirror): seed restored events at seq 100..102, onInit, append live event seq 103, onShutdown; assert only the single live call is merged. With the current code this test fails (folds all 4).

findings.title: runTurn errors fully swallowed including AbortError vs real failures
findings.problem: The catch block discards every error from session.runTurn with only `void err`, relying on the comment 'surfaced via error events'. AbortError (from Esc) and genuine transport/runtime errors are treated identically and never logged. If runTurn ever throws WITHOUT having emitted an error event (e.g. it throws before the event loop starts, or a bug in the provider stream), the turn ends silently with no user-visible signal and no diagnostic, making such failures undebuggable.
findings.fix: Distinguish abort (controller.signal.aborted) — which is the expected Esc path and can stay silent — from other errors, and route non-abort errors through the session/TUI logger (debug level) so they are at least diagnosable, even though the user-facing surface is the error-event stream.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: make runTurn reject with a non-AbortError and assert the injected logger is called; make it reject after signal.abort() and assert it is NOT logged as an error.

findings.title: pair-flow registers SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers on every run and calls process.exit; leaks listeners + bypasses caller
findings.problem: runPairFlow attaches process-level SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers and calls process.exit(0) inside the shutdown closure. (1) These listeners are never removed; if the setup wizard loops back into pair flow (or another flow registers its own), MaxListeners warnings and double-shutdown can occur. (2) process.exit(0) hard-terminates the CLI from inside a library helper, bypassing the subcommand's normal return-code propagation and any outer cleanup. The earlier `await handle.running` (L124) is unreachable once a signal fires (process exits in the handler).
findings.fix: Use `process.once` for the signal handlers and remove them on normal completion; prefer resolving/returning a code and letting the CLI entrypoint own process.exit, rather than exiting from the helper.
findings.testStrategy: Hard to unit-test signals; assert handlers are registered with `once` and removed after handle.running resolves (spy on process.on/once).

findings.title: poll() fetches are not abort-responsive (no signal threaded into the request)
findings.problem: The framework wires ctx.signal into start() and into pollUntil's sleep (poll-until.ts:49 sleep(...,signal)), so the WAIT between polls aborts. But the actual poll fetches do not receive a signal: openai-device-flow.ts poll() issues two unsignalled fetches (pollUrl and the token exchange), and rfc8628-device-flow.ts poll() issues one unsignalled fetch. On abort during an in-flight poll request (slow/hung token endpoint), the request runs to completion instead of cancelling. Root cause is the DeviceFlowAdapter.poll(init,state) contract not passing signal; these two adapters cannot honour it even if they wanted to.
findings.fix: Thread the abort signal into poll: add `readonly signal?: AbortSignal` to PollState (or pass it as a third poll arg), have pollUntil populate it from opts.signal, and pass `...(state.signal?{signal:state.signal}:{})` into each poll fetch in both adapters. Low priority since the inter-poll sleep already short-circuits within one interval.
findings.testStrategy: Test: abort the signal mid-poll (mock fetch that resolves after the signal fires) and assert poll()/pollUntil rejects with an abort error rather than completing the request.

findings.title: jiti instance is a module-global singleton keyed to the first cwd, ignoring later cwds
findings.problem: `jitiInstance` is a module-level `let` cached on first `getJiti(cwd)` call (L11/L14) and never re-created for a different cwd; the cwd argument is consulted only on the first call. In a hypothetical multi-cwd-in-one-process scenario a second loader would reuse the first session's jiti rooted at the wrong cwd.
findings.fix: Key the cache by cwd (Map<string, jiti>) or make jiti an instance field created inside createPluginLoader.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: two loaders with distinct cwds loading a .ts plugin; assert jiti factory invoked once per cwd.

findings.title: Constructor mutates global process.env.HF_HOME — global side effect, last-constructed wins
findings.problem: The constructor writes `process.env.HF_HOME = opts.cacheDir` as a side effect. This mutates process-wide global state from what the EmbedderDef contract (sdk/embedding.ts:31) says should be a cheap createClient. If two TransformersEmbedder instances are constructed with different cacheDir, the last one silently rebinds the cache dir for ALL of them (and for any other library reading HF_HOME). It is also order-dependent and racy, and is never restored.
findings.fix: Pass the cache directory to @huggingface/transformers via its env/config object at pipeline-load time inside ensureExtractor() (e.g. set env.cacheDir on the imported module just before factory()), or store cacheDir and apply it scoped to the load, rather than mutating global process.env in the constructor.
findings.testStrategy: Construct two embedders with distinct cacheDir, assert neither clobbers a shared global; verify the configured dir reaches the pipeline loader (spy on the import/factory).

findings.title: createUnixSocketServer fans out to every onConnection handler but only ever needs one
findings.problem: onConnection pushes into an unbounded `connectionHandlers` array and the accept callback loops over all of them, yet the TransportServer/RunnerServer contract treats this as a single-consumer hook (server.ts registers exactly one). The array can grow if any caller registers repeatedly, and the multi-dispatch implies a fan-out semantic that nothing supports (two RunnerServers over one transport would both wrap each socket in a JsonRpcPeer). It is latent plumbing with no consumer.
findings.fix: Either store a single handler (`let connectionHandler` and last-write-wins / throw on double-register, matching Transport.onFrame/onClose which are documented single-handler) or document and bound the multi-handler semantic. Single-handler is consistent with the rest of this file.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test asserting a second onConnection register either replaces or rejects; verify a single accepted connection invokes the handler exactly once.

findings.title: Browser pane forwards keys to the page but never preventDefault — Tab/arrows leak to the host UI
findings.problem: The onKeyDown handler forwards typing + control keys to the remote page via surface.input, but never calls e.preventDefault(). So Tab still moves focus off the browser pane (into other desktop UI), ArrowUp/Down/Backspace still scroll/navigate the renderer, and printable chars may trigger app-level shortcuts. The user types into the embedded page AND the desktop chrome reacts.
findings.fix: When a key is forwarded, call e.preventDefault() (and likely e.stopPropagation()) inside the matched branch so the keystroke is consumed by the pane and not also processed by the host.
findings.testStrategy: Fire a keydown for 'Tab' on the frame box in a jsdom test and assert defaultPrevented === true when the surface is ready.

findings.title: No abort check between synthesis approval and emitting the synthesized message
findings.problem: After the synthesis approval gate, collectSynthesis runs a full (potentially multi-second, 4096-token) provider call. There is no ctx.signal.aborted check after it returns before emitting the assistant_message. If the user aborts during synthesis, the loop still emits a full assistant_message rather than an abort event. Every other phase in this file guards on ctx.signal.aborted (lines 42, 90, 110, 191), making this an inconsistency, not just a gap.
findings.fix: After `if (synthesisText === null) return;`, add `if (ctx.signal.aborted) { yield await ctx.emit({ type: 'abort', ... reason: 'aborted during synthesis' }); return; }` to match the rest of the loop's abort discipline.
findings.testStrategy: Headless test that aborts ctx.signal during the synthesis provider call (fake provider that flips the AbortController) and asserts an abort event, not an assistant_message, is emitted.

findings.title: Ctrl+C hard-exits via process.exit(0) from inside the input parser, bypassing cleanup
findings.problem: On byte 0x03 the parser calls `process.exit(0)` directly. This is a synchronous hard exit from deep inside a stdin data handler: it bypasses any onShutdown/Session.close, runner unix-socket teardown, raw-mode restore, and Ink unmount. If a runner/PTY/socket is owned by this session, the child/socket can be orphaned and the terminal may be left in raw mode on some shells.
findings.fix: Replace the inline exit with a `ctx.onInterrupt?.()` callback that performs graceful teardown (restore stdin, unmount Ink, close session/runner) and then exits, mirroring the standard onShutdown->Session.close lifecycle invariant.
findings.testStrategy: Inject an onInterrupt spy via ParseCtx and assert it is called (not process.exit) for 0x03; integration-test that the runner socket is closed on Ctrl+C.

findings.title: mcp_remove_server runs detachServer outside the config mutex, racing a concurrent re-add of the same name
findings.problem: The handler persists the removal inside `mutateMcpConfig` (mutex-held) but then calls `detachServer(name)` AFTER the critical section ends. A concurrent mcp_add_server for the same name could complete its attach + persist in the gap between this removal's persist and its detach; the subsequent detach would then close the just-attached client and unregister the freshly-added tools, leaving config with an entry but no live tools (or a half-state). The runtime map is not protected by the same mutex as the config file, so add/remove of the same name are not serialized end-to-end.
findings.fix: Either perform detach inside the same mutex-held mutator, or key the runtime ops by the same configMutex so add/remove/detach for a given name are fully serialized. Minimum: document that runtime attach state and config are only loosely coupled and detach is best-effort.
findings.testStrategy: Hard to unit-test the true race; add an integration-style test interleaving add+remove of one name and asserting the final runtime state matches the final config state.

findings.title: Unknown tag emits N+1 redundant errors (one per attribute) on top of the tag error
findings.problem: convert() pushes `unknown tag <X>` (line 319) then unconditionally calls coerceAttrs(b, undefined, errors). With spec undefined, coerceAttrs flags EVERY attribute on the unknown element as `unknown attribute "..."` (lines 299-302). So `<evil a="1" b="2"/>` produces three errors (`unknown tag`, plus two `unknown attribute`) for one root cause, polluting the error list the agent reads back. on* attribute checks still fire (good), but the per-attr 'unknown attribute' spam is noise.
findings.fix: When spec is undefined in convert(), skip attribute coercion (still recurse into children, or short-circuit the subtree) — the tag error already conveys the problem. Alternatively, in coerceAttrs, when spec is undefined only run the on*/URL security checks and suppress the per-attr 'unknown attribute' errors.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that `<view><evil a="1" b="2"/></view>` yields exactly one `unknown tag <evil>` error and no per-attribute 'unknown attribute' noise (while still rejecting an on* attr on an unknown tag).

findings.title: Model switch mutates shared Session provider state from an unguarded callback — races concurrent turns
findings.problem: handleModel calls session.providers.replace/setActive synchronously across an `await resolver(providerId)` (lines 188-191). If a turn is in flight on the same Session (another channel or this one before /cancel), swapping the active provider mid-turn changes which client the running loop talks to on its next round. There's no busy check here (unlike text/voice paths which gate on busy). The credential resolve await also opens a window where two rapid model clicks interleave replace/setActive.
findings.fix: Guard the provider swap behind the channel's busy flag (refuse or queue mid-turn switches), or serialize provider mutations with a small mutex; at minimum reject the switch with a 'busy — finish/cancel the current turn first' toast when a turn is active.
findings.testStrategy: Simulate two model: callbacks resolving concurrently with a slow credentialResolver; assert setActive is not interleaved into an inconsistent state.

findings.title: localhost tunnel URL does not bracket IPv6 hosts
findings.problem: open() builds `http://${opts.host}:${opts.port}` with no IPv6 bracketing. If opts.host is an IPv6 literal (e.g. '::1'), the result 'http://::1:4040' is not a valid URL — the port delimiter is ambiguous and most parsers reject it. Should be 'http://[::1]:4040'.
findings.fix: Wrap the host: `const h = opts.host.includes(':') ? \`[${opts.host}]\` : opts.host;` then `url: \`http://${h}:${opts.port}\``. Equivalently use net.isIP(opts.host)===6 to decide bracketing.
findings.testStrategy: Add a case to localhost.test.ts asserting open({host:'::1',port:4040}).url === 'http://[::1]:4040' and that URL parsing of the result succeeds.

findings.title: randomCode has modulo bias and silently loses range for digits >= 10
findings.problem: randomCode draws 4 bytes (a uint32, max 2^32-1 ≈ 4.29e9) and takes `% 10**digits`. For digits >= 10, 10**digits > 2^32 so the high digits can never be non-zero (the code's leading digits are effectively constant) — entropy is silently capped. For digits below 10 there is a (small) modulo bias toward lower values. It is only called with digits=6 today (telegram pairing), where bias is negligible and security-uncritical, so this is latent rather than active.
findings.fix: Draw enough entropy for the requested width and reject-sample to remove bias, or document/clamp the supported range. Minimal: derive byte count from digits (`Math.ceil(digits * Math.log2(10) / 8)`), read that many bytes into a BigInt, reject values in the biased tail, then mod. Or assert digits <= 9 with a clear error.
findings.testStrategy: Add crypto.test.ts cases: randomCode(10) produces 10-char strings whose first digit is not always '0' (currently fails); statistical chi-square-lite over many draws at digits=6 to confirm no gross bias.

findings.title: broker_fs_write_file forces UTF-8 round-trip, corrupting any non-UTF-8 byte payload
findings.problem: Write reads the data region via readStr() (TextDecoder, UTF-8) then writeFileSync(filePath, data, 'utf8'). Any byte sequence that isn't valid UTF-8 (e.g. a wasm handler emitting binary output) is lossily replaced with U+FFFD before hitting disk. The read path correctly returns raw bytes (sendBytes on readFileSync result), so write is asymmetric with read and silently corrupts binary writes.
findings.fix: Read the data region as raw bytes and write the Buffer directly: `const data = new Uint8Array(memOf().buffer, dataPtr, dataLen).slice(); writeFileSync(filePath, Buffer.from(data));` — preserving arbitrary byte payloads and matching the read bridge's byte fidelity.
findings.testStrategy: Write a payload containing 0x80,0xFF (invalid UTF-8) via broker_fs_write_file and assert the on-disk bytes match exactly.

findings.title: stripHtml decodes only 4 named entities — numeric/' entities survive plain-text fallback
findings.problem: stripHtml is the plain-text fallback when Telegram rejects HTML. It un-escapes & < > " but not numeric (', ') or other named entities. Diff/code content escaped elsewhere (render.ts escapes only & < >, but markdownToTelegramHtml may emit ' for apostrophes/quotes) leaks literal ' into the fallback message. Order is also wrong: & is replaced first, so a literal '&lt;' would double-decode to '<'.
findings.fix: Decode entities last-to-first (</>/"/' before &) and add numeric entity decoding, or route through a single entity-decode pass. Simpler: strip tags, then decode in the order <,>,",',' and finally &.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test stripHtml('a &lt; b 'q'') === "a < b 'q'".

findings.title: Near-miss (resolved): @moxxy/runner depends on a concrete mode block — confirmed dev-only, registry-driven at runtime
findings.problem: Initial scan flagged @moxxy/runner statically importing @moxxy/mode-default (a block package) and @moxxy/testing, which would contradict invariant 11 (modes are runtime-discovered via the registry, not statically wired). On inspection this is a NON-issue and is recorded here only to document the boundary was checked: both @moxxy/mode-default and @moxxy/testing appear ONLY in runner's devDependencies and are referenced exclusively from test files — no non-test source in packages/runner/src references them. Runner's production dependency graph is exactly { @moxxy/core, @moxxy/sdk, zod }, so the runner ships without any concrete mode/block baked in and resolves modes through the registry as required.
findings.fix: No change required. Optionally add a comment in runner/package.json noting mode-default is a test-only fixture so a future reader does not promote it to a runtime dep.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (documented as verified-clean)

findings.title: Foreground daemon stops the poller but never closes the session (onShutdown hooks skipped)
findings.problem: runDaemonForeground boots a full session (setupSessionWithConfig with init hooks → scheduler poller AND webhooks listener) but its shutdown only calls `scheduler.poller.stop()`, never `session.close()`. The webhooks listener and any other onShutdown hooks (vault flush etc.) are never torn down gracefully — they rely on `process.exit(0)` killing the process. This diverges from serve.ts's runUntilSignal which correctly `await setup.session.close(signal)`.
findings.fix: Capture the full setup handle and `await session.close(signal)` in the shutdown path before process.exit, mirroring serve.ts. Add a force-exit unref'd timer as serve.ts does so a hung close can't strand the port.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on session.close in a fake setup; send SIGINT to the foreground daemon and assert close is awaited before exit.

findings.title: onCompact can setState after unmount; cleanup relies on modal staying open
findings.problem: `onCompact` awaits a runner round-trip (`session.runCommand` compact), then unconditionally calls `setCompacting(false)` and `setNote(...)` in finally. The modal has an `onClose` prop and a backdrop; if the user closes it (or the workspace switches) mid-compaction the component unmounts and these setState calls fire on a dead component (React warns; in StrictMode noise). Unlike sibling ActionRow.tsx — which carefully tracks a generation/ref to invalidate post-unmount async — this handler has no mounted guard.
findings.fix: Add a `mountedRef`/`cancelled` guard (set false in a cleanup effect) and skip the renderer-side setNote/setCompacting if unmounted. The `chatStore.setCompacting(workspaceId,false)` global unlock MUST still run regardless (it is workspace-scoped, not component-scoped).
findings.testStrategy: Render UsageModal, click Compact, unmount before the mocked runCommand resolves, assert no act() warning and that chatStore.setCompacting(id,false) still ran.

findings.title: compactedEvents count derives from replacedRange seqs as if they were a dense count
findings.problem: Line 178 computes `compactedEvents = result.replacedRange[1] - result.replacedRange[0] + 1` and reports it as the number of compacted events. CompactionEvent.replacedRange is documented (events.ts:171-184) as an INCLUSIVE [fromSeq, toSeq] of event `seq` VALUES, not array indices, and 'seq === arrayIndex is not guaranteed for mirrors or partial views'. On a sparse/mirrored log the difference of seqs overstates the actual count of replaced events. Cosmetic today (display string only) but the arithmetic encodes a wrong assumption that could be copied elsewhere.
findings.fix: Either label the value as a seq span, or count the actual events the range covers (events.filter(e => e.seq >= range[0] && e.seq <= range[1]).length) for an accurate message.
findings.testStrategy: Construct a log with a seq gap inside the replaced range and assert the reported count matches actual replaced events, not the seq span.

findings.title: Channel options merged with `as never` cast + raw argv.flags spread (weak typing, flag bleed)
findings.problem: Both code paths spread `...argv.flags` directly into the channel's options (`{ ...configOpts, ...argv.flags }`) and start the channel with `... as never`, erasing all type checking on the channel start payload. Every global CLI flag (e.g. --standalone, --model, --yes) leaks into channel options, and the `as never` cast means a wrong-shaped start payload is never caught at compile time. The duplicated create/options block across the two paths is also copy-paste.
findings.fix: Extract a shared `buildChannelStartArgs(argv, configOpts, session)` helper; pass only the channel's declared option keys rather than the entire argv.flags; type the start payload via the ChannelDef's start signature instead of `as never`.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level test (.test-d.ts) asserting channel.start args type-check without `as never`; runtime test that an unrelated global flag does not appear in the options handed to a stub channel.

findings.title: Numeric fields dispatch NaN on empty input (retries / maxIterations)
findings.problem: `onChange={(e) => patch({ retries: Number(e.target.value) })}` and the loop maxIterations field both do `Number(e.target.value)`. Clearing the input yields `Number('') === NaN`, which is dispatched into state. NaN survives JSON-serialization as null and round-trips badly; validation.ts does not guard maxIterations/retries, so a NaN can reach the saved YAML.
findings.fix: Coerce safely: `const n = Number(e.target.value); patch({ retries: Number.isFinite(n) ? Math.max(0, Math.min(3, n)) : 0 })` (and clamp maxIterations to 1..50), or guard in the reducer ops. Prefer clamping centrally so all surfaces benefit.
findings.testStrategy: RTL: clear the retries field and assert the dispatched action carries a finite clamped value, not NaN.

findings.title: loadDir aborts an entire skill directory tree if one .md file is unreadable
findings.problem: Inside loadDir, fs.readFile (line 55) is unguarded. The outer readdir is wrapped in try/catch (returns []), but a single .md that errors on read (permission, race-deleted, symlink loop) throws out of loadDir and propagates up through discoverSkills, aborting discovery of every remaining skill in every remaining source. One bad file makes all skills disappear.
findings.fix: Wrap the per-file read+parse in try/catch, logger?.warn and `continue` on failure (matching the invalid-frontmatter skip at :58-61), so one unreadable file degrades to skipping just that file.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: a directory with a valid skill plus an entry that throws on read (e.g. a directory-typed .md or chmod 000) still returns the valid skill.

findings.title: image-cache dir grows unbounded — clipboard PNGs are never reaped
findings.problem: Every clipboard/drag-drop image is written to `moxxyPath('image-cache')/clip-<ts>-<rand>.png` and never deleted (only the AppleScript-coercion failure path unlinks its own temp). Over a long-lived install, pasted screenshots accumulate forever in the moxxy data dir. No size cap, TTL, or sweep.
findings.fix: On ensureCacheDir(), opportunistically reap clip-*.png older than e.g. 24h (or keep newest N). Alternatively write to OS tmpdir so the OS reaps it.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test the reaper: seed the cache dir with old + new fake files, run ensureCacheDir, assert old ones removed and recent retained.

findings.title: estimateContextTokens double-pass over events allocates compactedSeqs Set unboundedly
findings.problem: estimateContextTokens iterates events twice: a first pass (lines 35-42) expands every compaction's replacedRange into a compactedSeqs Set (one entry per covered seq), then a second pass (43-55). For a long session with large compaction ranges (replacedRange can span thousands of seqs after several compactions), compactedSeqs grows to O(total compacted seqs) per call — a fresh full-range Set allocation every invocation. Since compactions partition disjoint seq ranges, a membership test could use the range bounds directly (binary-search or a single merged interval list) instead of materializing every integer. Minor, but it amplifies the per-iteration cost flagged in u122-2.
findings.fix: Collect compaction ranges as [from,to] intervals (already disjoint and ascending) and test e.seq against them with a small interval check, avoiding the per-seq Set materialization. Or fold this into the single shared elision/compaction state from u122-2.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test estimateContextTokens with a compaction covering a wide range; assert identical result and (via a wrapped Set) that no per-seq materialization occurs.
findings.complexity: O(sum of compacted range widths) -> O(number of compaction events)

findings.title: createMcpPlugin connects servers strictly serially; one slow server delays all
findings.problem: Servers are connected and tool-listed one at a time in a for-await loop. With N MCP servers each paying a spawn/handshake/listTools round-trip, total boot latency is the sum, not the max. The lazy admin runtime path exists precisely to avoid this, but createMcpPlugin (the non-admin embedding used by setup) still pays full serial cost. Not a correctness bug, but a startup-latency cost that grows linearly with server count.
findings.fix: Connect+list in parallel with Promise.allSettled (or Promise.all with the existing catch that closes opened clients), preserving the partial-failure cleanup. Keeps ordering by mapping then flattening. Bounds boot at the slowest server, not the sum.
findings.testStrategy: Test with two fake clients whose listTools resolve on staggered timers; assert both wrapped tool sets are present and that a throwing second client still closes the first (existing index.test.ts cleanup case).

findings.title: countTokens skips OAuth freshness/401 handling, silently degrading to estimate after token expiry
findings.problem: stream() proactively refreshes the OAuth bearer (ensureFreshOauth) and replays on 401, but countTokens() calls this.client.messages.countTokens directly with no ensureFreshOauth and no 401 refresh. Once the subscription token is stale, every countTokens hits the catch and falls back to estimateTextTokens — so context-budget decisions (compaction/elision) run on a coarse estimate until the next stream() refreshes the client, with no signal that this happened.
findings.fix: Call `await this.ensureFreshOauth()` at the top of countTokens when this.oauth is set (mirroring stream); optionally one reactive refresh-and-retry on isUnauthorized before falling back to the estimate.
findings.testStrategy: OAuth provider with a refresh callback + oauthExpiresAt in the past; assert countTokens triggers refresh before calling the SDK.

findings.title: Declared dim can disagree with the actual model output dimension (no verification)
findings.problem: dim is derived from a hardcoded KNOWN_DIMS table or an explicit override, never from the real model. If KNOWN_DIMS is wrong/stale for a model, or a user picks a non-listed model and supplies a wrong `dimensions` override, the EmbeddingProvider.dim (which sdk/embedding.ts:14-18 says consumers use to verify their stored index) silently lies. Memory's index-dimension check is then satisfied by a false value while real vectors have a different length.
findings.fix: After the first successful embed, optionally assert/learn the true dimension and reconcile with the declared dim (warn on mismatch). At minimum document that KNOWN_DIMS must stay in sync; consider returning 'dynamic' and letting the consumer learn dim from output for unknown models instead of trusting an unverified override.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test asserting a configured dim that disagrees with the stub's vector length is detected (warn/throw), preventing silent index corruption.

findings.title: start() called twice overwrites this.server and leaks the prior listening server
findings.problem: start() unconditionally creates a server and assigns this.server = server, with no guard against an already-running instance. A second start() (e.g. supervisor restart that forgot to stop) silently overwrites this.server, so stop() only closes the second server and the first keeps its port bound and sockets open — a resource/listener leak that also leaves the configured port held.
findings.fix: Throw (or no-op return the existing handle) if this.server is already set; on stop() null out this.server and reset boundPortValue to 0.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: call start() twice, assert the second rejects or that exactly one listener is bound; after stop(), assert boundPort===0.

findings.title: Activation fires focus.resize twice (once at width 232, again once hasTranscriber resolves)
findings.problem: The resize effect depends on `[stage, hasTranscriber]`. On inactive→active, `hasTranscriber` is still `null` (the `session.hasTranscriber` probe is async), so the first effect run resizes the window to ACTIVE_WIDTH_WITH_MIC (232). When the probe resolves to `false`, the effect re-runs and resizes to ACTIVE_WIDTH_WITHOUT_MIC (196). On a no-transcriber runner this is a visible width snap right after the panel opens — the very flicker the lifted-state comment (lines 72-74) was meant to avoid.
findings.fix: Either gate the first active resize until `hasTranscriber !== null` (skip the resize while null and let the resolved value drive the single resize), or render the active stage only after the probe completes. Keeps activation a single resize call.
findings.testStrategy: Extend FocusWidget.test.tsx: with hasTranscriber→false, assert only ONE focus.resize with stage active fires (width 196), not a 232-then-196 pair.

findings.title: Browser sidecar hand-rolls a promise-chain mutex (createMutex) and loses its rejection-resilience
findings.problem: The Playwright sidecar serializes per-page requests with `let queue = Promise.resolve(); ... queue = queue.then(async () => { const reply = await dispatch(state, req); write(reply); })`.
