This is exactly `createMutex`/`Mutex.run` from the SDK (invariant #5's serialize-mutators home), but the hand-rolled version does NOT guard against a rejected link: if the `.then` callback ever throws (e.g. `write()` hits EPIPE or a circular-JSON serialize), the chain becomes a rejected promise and EVERY subsequent request is silently dropped — the sidecar stops responding with no error reply. createMutex's `.then(fn, fn)` + chain-reset is built precisely to survive this. The package already depends on @moxxy/sdk and the sidecar is built with plain tsc (imports resolve), so adoption is free.
findings.fix: Import `createMutex` from '@moxxy/sdk', create one instance at module scope, and run each request through `mutex.run(async () => { write(await dispatch(state, req)); })`. Removes the bespoke queue and gains poison-resistance.
findings.testStrategy: Add a sidecar test that makes one queued request's write() throw, then asserts the NEXT request still gets a reply (proves the lock survived) — currently it would hang.

findings.title: TOCTOU between pidCommand identity check and process.kill — PID may be reused before SIGTERM
findings.problem: freeTcpPortIfMoxxy resolves the holder PIDs, then asynchronously fetches each pidCommand, filters by looksLikeMoxxy, then process.kill(SIGTERM)/SIGKILL. Between the `ps` snapshot and the kill the PID can exit and be reused by an unrelated process, so the kill could signal a non-moxxy process despite the identity gate. The gate is the security control for self-healing port stealing; the window is small but real.
findings.fix: Re-verify identity immediately before each kill: re-read pidCommand and re-check looksLikeMoxxy right before process.kill, skipping any PID whose command no longer matches. Accept the residual race as inherent to POSIX signalling, but narrow it.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with mocked captureStdout/process.kill: simulate a PID whose command changes from a moxxy command to a foreign one between the gate check and the kill; assert the kill is skipped.

findings.title: Child-event streaming silently swallows every parent-log append failure
findings.problem: `streamChildEventToParent` awaits `parentSession.log.append`. EventLog.append already swallows individual listener errors, but if append itself rejects (or any wrapping throws), the rejection propagates into the childLog.subscribe callback, where EventLog.append (events/log.ts:74-78) swallows it wholesale. There is no warning/log on a dropped progress event, so a parent-log write failure during a long subagent run vanishes with zero observability — the TUI just stops updating with no trace.
findings.fix: Wrap the append in streamChildEventToParent in a try/catch that at minimum increments a dropped-event counter or logs once via the session logger, so chronic forwarding failures are diagnosable. Keep it non-fatal.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a parent log whose append rejects for one event; assert the child run still completes and a warning/counter is recorded.

findings.title: resumeNow bypasses the inFlight guard, allowing a resume to race a fresh run of the same workflow
findings.problem: runNow uses `inFlight.add(name)` to refuse a concurrent fire of the same workflow. resumeNow performs a full workflow execution (drives the rest of the DAG, delivers to inbox) but never consults or sets inFlight, keyed by runId rather than name. A schedule/afterWorkflow/manual fire of the same workflow concurrent with an operator resume can therefore run two executions of one workflow simultaneously, doubling side effects and inbox deliveries.
findings.fix: Resolve the workflow name from the checkpoint at resumeNow entry and apply the same inFlight guard (or a runId-scoped lock), releasing in a finally.
findings.testStrategy: Test: start a paused run, then concurrently call resumeNow(runId) and runNow({name}); assert one is refused / they serialize and inbox isn't double-delivered.

findings.title: last-run pre renders steps via JSON.stringify on every render, no fallback for empty result
findings.problem: The last-run panel chooses `output || error || JSON.stringify(steps, null, 2)`. If a run produced no output, no error, and steps is undefined/empty (e.g. a workflow that registered but produced no step records), JSON.stringify(undefined) yields the literal string 'undefined' rendered to the user, and JSON.stringify runs on every component re-render (toggle, refresh, generating-modal open) rather than being memoized off lastRun identity.
findings.fix: Guard the steps branch (e.g. `steps?.length ? JSON.stringify(steps, null, 2) : 'No output.'`) and, if lastRun.result.steps can be large, useMemo the stringified value keyed on wf.lastRun to avoid recomputing on unrelated state changes (editing/generating toggles).
findings.testStrategy: Add a WorkflowsPanel.test.tsx case with a lastRun whose result has no output/error and empty steps; assert a sane placeholder renders instead of 'undefined'.

findings.title: listDir does not re-validate that each child symlink stays inside the workspace root
findings.problem: resolveInside() realpath-guards the requested directory itself, but listDir's per-entry loop calls stat(path.join(abs, name)) and reports name+kind for entries WITHOUT re-checking each entry's realpath against root. A symlink inside the workspace pointing to an out-of-tree directory is reported as a 'dir' (and a symlink to a file outside is reported as a 'file'). listDir leaks only names/kinds (not contents — readFile re-runs resolveInside, so opening such an entry is correctly blocked), but the directory listing still discloses the existence/kind of symlink targets outside the sandbox, which the module's own doc-comment claims to prevent.
findings.fix: Use readdir(abs, { withFileTypes: true }) and for symlink entries (dirent.isSymbolicLink()) realpath the target and drop it (or mark it) if !isInside(root, real); otherwise keep classifying via the dirent type without an extra stat (also removes the N extra stat() syscalls).
findings.testStrategy: Add a workspace-fs.test.ts: create a symlink inside a temp workspace pointing to an out-of-tree dir, assert listDir omits (or flags) it; assert in-tree symlinks still list.
findings.complexity: O(n) stat -> O(n) dirent (fewer syscalls) + per-symlink realpath

findings.title: ApprovalDialog clamps scrollOffset for render but never resets the stale state, leaking offset across content
findings.problem: scrollOffset is clamped to maxOffset only for the rendered `offset` local; the stored scrollOffset state is never reset when the user enters/exits textEntry or when content effectively changes. Since request is fixed per mount this is mostly benign, but the j/k handlers (137-141) let scrollOffset grow unboundedly past maxOffset (it caps only at render time, not in state), so after paging far down, k must be pressed many times before any visible movement occurs.
findings.fix: Clamp in the setter: `setScrollOffset((o) => Math.min(maxOffset, o + step))` (compute maxOffset from rendered.length, which can be memoized per body).
findings.testStrategy: Simulate many PgDn then one PgUp; assert the visible window moves up by exactly one page.

findings.title: ApprovalDialog j/k scroll silently shadows options whose hotkey is 'j' or 'k'
findings.problem: Body scroll (j/k or PgUp/PgDn) is matched BEFORE the hotkey lookup (line 147) and digit-jump. The inline comment claims this is intentional 'so a binding like hotkey=j doesn't accidentally shadow scroll' — but the consequence is the inverse and is a real correctness gap: if a loop strategy defines an option with hotkey 'j' or 'k', that hotkey becomes permanently unreachable (it scrolls instead of selecting). The dialog is generic and options come from arbitrary strategies, so this is reachable.
findings.fix: Only treat j/k as scroll when the body actually overflows (rendered.length > MAX_BODY_LINES) AND no option claims that hotkey; otherwise fall through to the hotkey match. Or reserve PgUp/PgDn for scroll and drop the j/k overlap when any option uses those letters.
findings.testStrategy: Render ApprovalDialog with options including hotkey:'j', short body; simulate 'j' keypress; assert onDecide called with that option (not a scroll no-op).

findings.title: extractPaths file:// handling drops query/host and ignores percent-encoding
findings.problem: extractPaths treats any string starting with 'file://' by slicing off the literal prefix: `value.slice('file://'.length)`. For 'file:///work/x.ts' this yields '/work/x.ts' (correct by luck of the triple slash), but for 'file://host/x' it yields 'host/x' (relative, resolved against cwd — wrong), and percent-encoded paths (file:///a%20b) are not decoded, so a path that IS in scope after decoding may be checked in encoded form (or vice-versa, an escape). String-slicing a URL is fragile here.
findings.fix: Parse with new URL(value) and use url.pathname (after decodeURIComponent), rejecting file URLs with a non-empty host; fall through to deny on parse failure.
findings.testStrategy: Add cases: file://evil/secret, file:///a%2e%2e/etc/passwd, and assert correct in/out-of-scope decisions.

findings.title: ProviderRegistry.replace on the active provider leaves a transient 'active but no instance' window
findings.problem: replace() drops the cached instance (correct, so the new createClient runs) but leaves `active` pointing at the name. Between replace() and the next setActive(), getActive() throws `Active provider has no instance` and tryGetActive-equivalent paths break. The only production caller (runner server.handleProviderSetActive) immediately follows replace() with setActive(), so it is safe today, but replace() alone is a public method and the broken interim state is undocumented.
findings.fix: Either rebuild the instance eagerly inside replace() when name === active, or document that replace() of the active provider must be followed by setActive(). Prefer documenting + an invariant note since eager rebuild needs the config which replace() doesn't have.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: register+setActive 'a'; replace('a'); assert getActive() throws a clear message (or, if rebuild added, still returns an instance).

findings.title: Final index write on detach uses an unref'd 250ms timer; close-time lastActivity can be lost on immediate exit
findings.problem: The detach callback calls scheduleIndexWrite() to flush close-time lastActivity, but scheduleIndexWrite always defers via setTimeout(...,250).unref(). Because the timer is unref'd, if the process exits within 250ms of detach (the common case on `moxxy resume`/one-shot shutdown), the close-time index update never runs and lastActivity reflects the last event, not the close. Minor (picker ordering only), but the documented intent ('lastActivity reflects the close time even if no events arrived') is not actually guaranteed.
findings.fix: On detach do a direct `void this.writeIndex()` (await it in an async close path if callers can await), bypassing the debounce, rather than scheduling an unref'd timer.
findings.testStrategy: Attach, append no events, detach, then immediately read the sidecar in the same tick chain and assert lastActivity advanced past startedAt.

findings.title: ServerFrame 'view' frame's `replaces` field is silently ignored by the frontend reducer
findings.problem: The protocol's view frame carries replaces: string | null (the prior viewId to swap in place) and turnId, but applyView ignores both — it caches by (name ?? viewId) and decides in-place vs push solely by comparing the new key to the top-of-history key. If the server intends to REPLACE the current view with one that has a different name/viewId (the documented `replaces` semantics), the frontend instead pushes a new history entry, so goBack reveals a stale view that the server believed was replaced. This is latent because today the server likely re-sends the same name, but the wire contract and the reducer disagree.
findings.fix: Either honor replaces in applyView (when set and it matches the current top entry's viewId, replace that history slot instead of pushing), or remove replaces from the view frame if in-place is fully determined by name equality. Document the chosen invariant.
findings.testStrategy: Extend view-store.test.ts: a view frame whose name differs but replaces===currentViewId should update in place (history length unchanged), not push.

findings.title: Bash leaves stdout/stderr `pause`-able streams flowing; no guard if spawn returns null pipes under odd stdio
findings.problem: Minor robustness: `child.stdout.on('data', out.push)` and `child.stderr.on(...)` dereference the streams without a null check. With the fixed `stdio: ['ignore','pipe','pipe']` they are always non-null at runtime, so this is safe today, but it is a non-defensive dereference and TS-narrowed only by the literal stdio array. More notably, on a `child.on('error')` (spawn EACCES/ENOENT) the bounded sinks/pipes are never explicitly destroyed; Node GCs them, so not a leak, but the handler relies on 'error' OR 'close' firing exactly once — which it does. Flagging as low-confidence defensive note, not a confirmed bug.
findings.fix: Optional: guard `child.stdout?.on(...)`. No functional change needed given the fixed stdio; safe to leave as-is. Keep if hardening for future stdio variants.
findings.testStrategy: Existing bash tests cover error/abort/timeout/close paths; no new test required unless stdio becomes configurable.

findings.title: Host scratch allocator (fixed base 65536) and the module's own alloc() share one Memory with no coordination — heap corruption
findings.problem: The host writes broker results into the wasm instance's linear memory at a hardcoded `SCRATCH_BASE = 65536` (start of page 2) via a bump pointer, while the module's own `alloc(size)` export hands out addresses the host uses for the input region (line 172) and that the module presumably uses for its own heap. Nothing reconciles the two allocators: if the module's `alloc`/heap grows past 65536 (a real AssemblyScript/Rust heap in a non-trivial handler easily exceeds one 64KiB page), host scratch writes will overwrite live module data and vice-versa, silently corrupting either the handler's state or the brokered result the handler is about to read. The ABI gives the module no way to learn where host scratch lives, and gives the host no way to learn the module's high-water mark. The echo test fixture sidesteps this entirely because its `alloc` is a constant `i32.const 1024`, so the collision is never exercised.
findings.fix: Have the host obtain scratch regions from the module's own allocator instead of a fixed base: call `exports.alloc(bytes.length)` inside reserveScratch (passing the module's alloc into buildWasmHostImports) so input, output and broker scratch all come from a single coordinated allocator. Failing that, require the module to export its heap_base/heap_top and assert host scratch starts strictly above it. At minimum document the invariant 'module heap must never exceed 64KiB' and assert `memory.buffer.byteLength` growth doesn't cross the scratch base.
findings.testStrategy: Hand-encode (or generate via AssemblyScript) a fixture whose alloc returns monotonically increasing addresses past 65536, run a broker call, and assert the brokered result is read back uncorrupted and the handler's own allocations survive.

findings.title: rendered flag means 'a surface is attached', not 'the view was delivered'
findings.problem: rendered is computed as `surface != null` (rendered: surface != null, line 76). The handler never pushes the view anywhere — delivery happens asynchronously when the Projector observes the tool_result. So present_view reports rendered:true whenever a web surface is co-attached, even if no browser is connected to it (the documented blank/static-page case from PR #205 memory). The model is told the screen was rendered when it may only have been queued for a not-yet-connected client. The PresentViewResult JSDoc ('Whether a live surface received the view') overstates what is known at return time.
findings.fix: Rename to surfaceAttached (or document that rendered === a surface is attached, not delivery-confirmed) so the model is not misled into thinking the user definitely sees the screen; or have the handler actually hand the AST to the surface and report a real delivered/queued distinction.
findings.testStrategy: Update present-view.test.ts naming/assertions; add a case where a surface with a null/idle client is attached and assert the (renamed) flag reflects attachment, not delivery.

findings.title: autoApprove is never reset on /new and bypasses always-allow persistence
findings.problem: session.setAutoApprove({enabled}) flips a host-level boolean that is never reset by session.newSession (which only aborts turns + resets the session). A client that enabled auto-approve and then started a fresh session keeps auto-approve silently on. Additionally, in auto-approve mode the resolver returns {mode:'allow'} without ever persisting an allow, which is fine, but the asymmetry (manual allow_always persists, auto-approve does not) is undocumented and easy to misread.
findings.fix: Reset this.autoApprove=false inside the session.newSession handler (mirror desktop SessionDriver semantics) so a fresh session starts from the safe default; document the intent.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: setAutoApprove(true) → newSession → permission check now opens an ask instead of auto-allowing.

findings.title: Table width math uses string code-unit length, mis-sizing wide (CJK/emoji) cells
findings.problem: visualLen and padCell measure width with `stripInline(s).length` (UTF-16 code units), not display columns. A column of CJK or emoji content is sized at half its rendered width, so the dim `─/┼` rule and the ` │ ` separators no longer align with the wrapped cells, breaking the grid the component exists to draw. Truncation to `width-1 + '…'` likewise slices mid-grapheme.
findings.fix: Introduce a small display-width function (ansi-stripped + wcwidth-style east-asian-wide detection) in @moxxy/chat-model and use it in visualLen/padCell/truncation; or pull in an existing string-width dep already vendored by Ink.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test padCell/visualLen with CJK and emoji inputs asserting padded display width equals declared column width.

findings.title: Image attachment map only cleared on non-slash submit; leaks placeholders across slash commands
findings.problem: imageAttachmentsRef and nextImageIdRef are only reset inside resolveAttachments, which SessionView calls only for non-slash messages. If a user pastes images (registerImage stores a pending promise + bumps the id counter) and then sends only slash commands, the map grows and id numbering never resets, so later real submissions reference stale/incorrectly-numbered placeholders. Growth is bounded per session but the numbering drift is a UX correctness bug.
findings.fix: Clear the pending image map when a slash command is dispatched (or whenever the input buffer is cleared without a resolve), or key placeholders to the current buffer rather than a monotonically-growing session counter.
findings.testStrategy: Register an image, dispatch a slash command, register another image, submit; assert placeholder numbering restarts and only the live image resolves.

findings.title: Inbox filename keyed only on ISO timestamp + trigger name — bursts overwrite each other
findings.problem: writeInbox builds the path as `${stamp}-${trigger.name}.md` where stamp is `new Date().toISOString()` (ms resolution). Two deliveries of the same trigger within the same millisecond (a webhook batch/retry storm is exactly the burst case) produce identical filenames, and writeFileAtomic does tmp+rename which overwrites — silently losing one fire's output. The available deliveryId (passed in but used only in the YAML header) is the natural uniqueness source and is dropped from the filename.
findings.fix: Include a uniqueness suffix in the filename: prefer the deliveryId when present, else a short random suffix (randomUUID().slice(0,8)). e.g. `${stamp}-${trigger.name}-${deliveryId ?? rand}.md`.
findings.testStrategy: Test writeInbox twice with a frozen clock (same stamp) and assert two distinct files exist; with same deliveryId assert dedupe upstream already prevents the second fire.

findings.title: First-event append can race ahead of ensureDir() on a fresh sessions dir -> spurious degraded warning
findings.problem: attach() kicks off ensureDir()->ensureLogFile() as a detached promise chain (void ...catch()), then synchronously registers the log.subscribe listener. enqueueAppend() pushes fs.appendFile through writeQueue without awaiting that chain. fs.appendFile with flag 'a' creates the FILE but not its parent directory, so if the very first event arrives before ensureDir() resolves on a machine where ~/.moxxy/sessions does not yet exist, the append fails ENOENT, latches writeDegraded, and logs the loud 'history persistence degraded' warning. It self-recovers (the debounced writeIndex also calls ensureDir, and the next append succeeds), but the warning is misleading and the first event may be lost.
findings.fix: Serialize the first append behind ensureDir: store the `ensureDir().then(ensureLogFile)` promise as `this.ready` and have enqueueAppend/enqueueTruncate run `this.writeQueue.run(() => this.ready.then(() => fs.appendFile(...)))`, or simply `await this.ensureDir()` inside the queued task before the first append. Cheap because mkdir({recursive:true}) is idempotent.
findings.testStrategy: Construct SessionPersistence with dir set to a non-existent nested path, attach, immediately log.append a user_prompt before any tick; assert persistence.degraded stays false and restoreEvents returns the event.

findings.title: reasoning_effort gated by max_completion_tokens regex drops effort for OpenAI-compatible reasoning backends
findings.problem: reasoning_effort is only sent when `usesCompletionTokens` (model id matches /^(?:gpt-5|o1|o3)/) AND reasoningEffort is set. The OpenAIProvider class is the shared reuse point for OpenAI-compatible vendors (xai/zai/google/local/provider-admin all instantiate it — confirmed via grep), and the file's own comments (lines 44-48, 281-285) state DeepSeek-R1 / z.ai-GLM / vLLM / Ollama reasoning backends DO stream reasoning. Their model ids (e.g. glm-4.6, deepseek-reasoner) never match the regex, so a user-requested `reasoning.effort` is silently discarded for exactly the backends that honor it. The token-field-naming heuristic and the reasoning-effort gating are conflated into one condition; they are independent concerns.
findings.fix: Decouple the two: send reasoning_effort whenever `emitReasoning && reasoningEffort` is set (gating on the model descriptor's supportsReasoning at the call site already, via req.reasoning), independent of the token-field-name choice. Keep the separate `tokenLimitKey` heuristic for max_(completion_)tokens only.
findings.testStrategy: Add a stream() test with a non-gpt-5 model id (e.g. 'glm-4.6'), req.reasoning={effort:'high'}, capture the create() body, assert reasoning_effort:'high' is present.

findings.title: ListPicker 'land cursor on current' effect omits filtered from deps, can stomp/miss on first paint
findings.problem: The effect that moves the cursor onto the `current` option reads `filtered` and `query` but deliberately lists only [activeTabId, sourceOptions] as deps. On first mount the searchable default `query=''` path is fine, but if `filtered` is derived async (sourceOptions identity changes without activeTabId changing — e.g. provider list streams in) the effect may run with a stale `filtered` snapshot and place the cursor on a stale index, or not re-run when the current option's position shifts. The narrowed deps are an explicit lint-suppression that trades correctness for avoiding keystroke-stomp.
findings.fix: Track a `didInitCursor` ref per tab and compute the initial current-index directly from sourceOptions (not the filtered/query-dependent list), so the effect is a pure function of [activeTabId] + a one-shot guard, eliminating the stale-closure ambiguity.
findings.testStrategy: Mount ListPicker with options whose `current` option is mid-list; assert cursor === that index on first frame; re-render with a new sourceOptions where current moved; assert cursor follows or is intentionally pinned per spec.

clusters.title: Long-tail: add unit tests for the untested per-unit logic
clusters.oneLine: Pure parsers/reducers/folds/state-machines across the repo (prompt-editor reducer, escape parser, oauth orchestrators, mcp wrap, telegram channel logic, codex/anthropic stream folds, picker/queue reducers, AppleScript serialization, token-accounting fold, etc.) that lack co-located tests. (75 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Additive, no behavior change. Lower priority than the Tier-3 test-harness items that unblock refactors; do opportunistically when each file is touched.

findings.title: DeepLinkBridge drain-before-subscribe ordering and store push are untested
findings.problem: The comment claims 'Subscribe happens BEFORE the drain so the main side can safely flip to live-push the moment it answers the drain with no lost-link race' — but the code calls api().subscribe first, then awaits api().invoke('deepLink:drain'). A link delivered live between the subscribe registration and the drain resolution can be pushed both as a live event and (if main buffered it before flipping) again in the drained batch. Because the store only keeps `last`, duplicate pushes are harmless today, but the ordering invariant is asserted only in prose, with no test.
findings.fix: Add a test with a fake api() whose subscribe + invoke('deepLink:drain') are controllable, asserting (a) subscribe is registered before drain resolves, (b) a link arriving during the window still reaches deepLinkStore exactly once observable via latest().
findings.testStrategy: Mock @moxxy/client-core api(); drive ordering manually; assert deepLinkStore.latest().

findings.title: Security-critical no-shell spawn helpers (runProcess/runProcessBinary/ensureDarwin) have zero unit tests
findings.problem: shell.ts is the load-bearing process-execution layer for the whole plugin and exists specifically to avoid shell-injection (array-form args, no `bash -c`). Its abort propagation, timeout kill, stdin write, exit-code mapping, and binary stdout preservation are all untested. The only test file (tools.test.ts) exercises zod schemas exclusively and never imports shell.ts. Regressions in abort/timeout/leak handling (see u81-1, u81-2) would ship silently. ensureDarwin's throw-on-non-darwin gate — the documented cross-platform contract — is also untested.
findings.fix: Add shell.test.ts: (a) runProcess captures stdout/stderr + exitCode for a known node one-liner; (b) input is written to stdin; (c) AbortSignal kills the child; (d) timeoutMs kills the child; (e) runProcessBinary preserves exact PNG-like bytes; (f) ensureDarwin throws a MoxxyError with code TOOL_ERROR off-darwin and is a no-op on darwin (mock process.platform). Multi-chunk stdout case doubles as the u81-1 regression guard.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest suite spawning `process.execPath` (node) with `-e` snippets; use AbortController and a 50ms timeoutMs to keep it fast and deterministic.

findings.title: Security/health-critical deep-link parsing and boot-probe revert logic have zero unit tests
findings.problem: parseDeepLink() parses OS-delivered (untrusted) moxxy:// URLs at a trust boundary and forwards host/path/params to the renderer, and armBootProbe() decides whether a hot-updated bundle is healthy or must be poisoned + relaunched onto the floor. Both are load-bearing (the self-update saga in TECH_DEBT history turned on exactly this poison/confirm logic) yet neither has a test — index.ts has no .test.ts. A regression that mis-parses a scheme or that confirms a white-screened bundle (or poisons a healthy one) ships silently.
findings.fix: Add index.test.ts (or extract per u6-1 then test). Cover parseDeepLink: only moxxy: scheme accepted, params extracted, malformed -> null. Cover armBootProbe with an injected fake webContents + fake confirmed/bad stores: DOM-mounted -> markConfirmed; never-mounted -> markBad + relaunch; already-confirmed fast path; window destroyed mid-poll -> no relaunch.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with a stub BrowserWindow.webContents whose executeJavaScript returns scripted booleans and a fake clock (vi.useFakeTimers) to drive the poll deadline.

findings.title: Vault key-name validation regex has no unit test
findings.problem: AddKeyForm gates submission on a security-relevant name validation (`/^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9._/-]*$/` plus a `!includes('..')` traversal guard) that mirrors the IPC schema. This validation is the renderer's first line against path-traversal-ish key names, yet there is no test for it (settings tests cover ProvidersTab/MobileTab/AddWithAgent/useAgentTask only). A regression loosening the regex would silently pass.
findings.fix: Add a unit test exercising valid names, names with spaces, leading dot/dash, and `..` segments to assert `canSubmit` / the disabled state.
findings.testStrategy: Render AddKeyForm, type each candidate name, assert the Save button enabled/disabled and the validation hint text.

findings.title: Prompt editor reducer (insert/delete/kill/yank/paste, word-motion) has zero unit tests
findings.problem: reducer.ts is a pure, edge-case-dense state machine (cursor clamping, backslash-strip newline, kill/yank buffer, word-back/forward boundaries, paste accumulation) and is exactly the kind of fold the audit flags for test-gap. There is no reducer.test.ts (only parse-input/external-insert/BufferLines tests exist). Regressions in cursor math or the line-continuation backslash logic would ship silently.
findings.fix: Add reducer.test.ts covering: insert at mid-cursor, delete-back at 0 (no-op), insert-newline with stripBackslashAtEnd at end vs mid-line, kill-to-line-end/start + yank round-trip, word-back/forward across punctuation, paste-start/append/end with and without overrideText, and `set` cursor clamping.
findings.testStrategy: vitest table-driven cases asserting {buffer, cursor, killBuffer} after each action.

findings.title: topoOrder and disconnectEdge have no direct unit test (only indirect coverage)
findings.problem: topoOrder (longest-path topological layering with a cycle-guard fallback) and disconnectEdge (maps each of 7 edge kinds back to its inverse op) are the only non-trivial algorithms in the unit. WorkflowsPanel.test.tsx exercises the canvas through the DOM but never asserts topoOrder's ranking for diamond/cycle graphs nor that disconnectEdge dispatches the correct op for case/default/loop-body edges. A regression in ordering or in one disconnect branch would pass the current suite.
findings.fix: Export (or extract per u15-6) topoOrder and disconnectEdge and add table-driven unit tests: topoOrder over a diamond and over a cyclic loaded graph; disconnectEdge for all 7 edge kinds asserting the dispatched action shape.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest unit suite alongside the extracted helpers.

findings.title: migrateLegacyChats one-time data migration parser is untested (data-loss path)
findings.problem: migrateLegacyChats parses legacy localStorage JSON blobs, version-gates on LEGACY_VERSION===2, then on success DELETES every matched key and sets a migrated flag. A bug in the version gate, the key filter, or the remove ordering would permanently destroy a user's chat history. The migration deletes keys it collected (including wrong-version/corrupt blobs) unconditionally once the migrate IPC succeeds. There is no unit test for this destructive parser.
findings.fix: Add chatPersistence.test.ts with a fake KeyValueStore: assert v2 blobs are forwarded to chat.migrate; assert keys are removed only after a successful migrate and the flag is left UNSET when migrate throws (the documented retry path); assert wrong-version/corrupt blobs are not silently lost in a way that diverges from the documented contract.
findings.testStrategy: Inject a fake kv + __setApiOverride fake transport; cover success, transport-throw (flag stays unset, keys retained), and corrupt-blob paths.

findings.title: Ask-surface ref-count store has no test despite gating a runner-blocking surface
findings.problem: askSurface is a global ref-count (owners += / -=) deciding whether App.tsx renders the fallback ask UI. Its own doc says the runner BLOCKS on every ask.request until answered and 'an ask must never be invisible — but it also must not render twice'. A mismatched claim/release (e.g. double-decrement, or owners going negative) would silently make the global fallback render-or-not incorrectly, hanging a blocked runner. There is no unit test for the increment/decrement/visibility transition.
findings.fix: Add a small RTL test: mounting a useClaimAskSurface consumer flips useAskSurfaceClaimed() to true; unmounting flips it back to false; two concurrent claimers require both to unmount before it drops to false (owners stays >0).
findings.testStrategy: vitest + @testing-library/react renderHook on both hooks, mount/unmount sequences asserting the boolean.

findings.title: Hand-rolled YAML codec has only one edge-case test; parser branches largely uncovered
findings.problem: yaml.ts is a bespoke emitter+parser (the only non-trivial parser in the unit) and the sole codec test is a single round-trip of a flat object. Untested branches: nested sequences (emitArray recursion + parseSequence), inline-map splice (emitArray line 67-70), `|-`/`>`/`>-` block scalars and the fold/strip flags, flow `[a, b]` parsing, single-quoted unquote with `''` escape, keys needing quoting (emitKey), and the `#`-in-block-scalar case from u129-1. This is exactly the parser/fold logic the audit flags as must-test.
findings.fix: Add a yaml.test.ts: round-trip nested arrays, inline maps, all block-scalar header variants, flow sequences, quoted/escaped keys+values, and a block scalar containing `#`.
findings.testStrategy: Property/round-trip tests fromYaml(toYaml(x))===x over a fixture matrix covering each branch above.

findings.title: command.ts, engine.ts, and tools.ts have no unit tests
findings.problem: The package tests cover schema, template, store, draft, logic-response, run-store, and dag — but the `/workflows` command dispatcher (subcommand routing, run/inspect/new/rm output), the run-record writer + readLastRun round-trip, and the 8 workflow_* tool handlers (validation, name-must-match-yaml check in workflow_update, error mapping) are untested. These are exactly the parsing/dispatch/handler surfaces flagged by the audit, and findings u118-1/u118-2 live in this untested code.
findings.fix: Add command.test.ts (drive handle() with fake deps for list/run/inspect/new/rm + unknown subcommand), engine.test.ts (runWorkflow writes a parseable JSONL record; recordDir:null skips), and tools.test.ts (workflow_update rejects name mismatch; workflow_run errors when no spawner).
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with a fake WorkflowStore and tmp recordDir; assert CommandOutput kinds/text and emitted plugin_event subtypes.

findings.title: No tests for nested-workflow pause, true wave concurrency, or the stall-detection path
findings.problem: Three correctness-critical behaviors of this scheduler have no unit coverage: (1) a nested workflow that PAUSES on awaitInput (the resume-corruption case in u117-2); (2) actual concurrent execution of an independent wave (the existing fan-out test only checks ordering, which passes under serial execution); (3) the stall guard at 275-281 that aborts with 'workflow stalled' when pending steps have no runnable path. Folds/schedulers of this complexity should have these paths pinned.
findings.fix: Add tests: (a) nested workflow with awaitInput → assert defined behavior (per u117-2 fix); (b) a deferred-spawner overlap test for waves; (c) a workflow whose pending step depends on a failed step under onError:'continue' that becomes unrunnable, asserting the 'workflow stalled' abortReason.
findings.testStrategy: As above.

findings.title: resolveSpec merge/precedence logic has no direct unit test
findings.problem: resolveSpec() is the unit's core pure logic: it merges a model-supplied AgentSpecInput with the registered AgentDef, with caller fields winning over kind defaults, kind falling back to the built-in DEFAULT_AGENT, and label defaulting to def.name. None of this is tested directly. The sibling dispatch-agent.test.ts only exercises end-to-end spawning through core (createFakeSession + collectTurn) and the model-override warning path; it never asserts that def.systemPrompt/def.allowedTools/def.maxIterations/def.mode flow through when the caller omits them, that caller values override the kind, that an unknown agentType falls back to DEFAULT_AGENT (def.name='default'), or that agentType is preserved verbatim. A regression in the precedence (e.g. accidentally letting def win over caller) would pass the existing suite because every test uses getAgent: () => undefined, so the AgentDef branch of the merge is never reached.
findings.fix: Export resolveSpec (or test it via a getAgent that returns a populated AgentDef) and add unit tests: (a) caller field overrides def field for each of systemPrompt/model/mode/allowedTools; (b) def field used when caller omits; (c) def.maxIterations flows through even though it is absent from the input schema; (d) unknown agentType -> DEFAULT_AGENT, label defaults to 'default'; (e) agentType is echoed as the requested string even on fallback.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function unit tests over resolveSpec with a stub DispatchAgentDeps whose getAgent returns a fully-populated AgentDef and undefined for unknown names; assert each field of the returned SubagentSpec.

findings.title: Security-sensitive path-confinement (workspace-fs) and stale-socket sweep have no unit tests
findings.problem: workspace-fs.ts is a security boundary (cwd confinement + symlink-escape guard + binary/oversize handling) and sweep-sockets.ts performs process-killing side effects (SIGTERM/SIGKILL of PIDs from lsof). Neither has a .test.ts (all sibling modules in the unit do). The confinement guard (resolveInside throwing on '..'/abs/symlink-escape), the MAX_READ_BYTES truncation, the NUL-byte binary detection, and the sweep's dead-vs-alive probe + unlink path are exactly the fold/parser/permission-gate logic the audit flags as must-test.
findings.fix: Add workspace-fs.test.ts covering: '..'/absolute/symlink-escape rejection, in-tree read, MAX_READ_BYTES truncation flag, NUL-byte binary placeholder, IGNORED_DIRS/hidden filtering. Add sweep-sockets.test.ts covering dead-socket-unlink (no kill) and the SweepLog accumulation against a temp sockets dir (skip the real SIGKILL by injecting the kill fn or pointing at a sacrificial child).
findings.testStrategy: Tempdir-based vitest suites as above; for sweep, factor kill()/pidListeningOn() to be injectable so process.kill isn't exercised on real PIDs.

findings.title: format.ts pure helpers (summarizeArgs/buildCompactSummary/formatElapsed/formatTokensK) have zero unit tests
findings.problem: format.ts is all pure, branch-heavy formatting/aggregation logic consumed by BOTH the TUI and desktop renderers (summarizeArgs, buildCompactSummary with plural/insertion-order grouping, compactPreviewLine, formatElapsed with the s/m/h boundaries, formatTokensK null/<0/<1000/k branches). log.ts and pair-events.ts each have a dedicated test file; format.ts has none. A regression in e.g. the formatElapsed minute/hour padding, the formatTokensK negative/null gate, or buildCompactSummary's first-vs-subsequent verb-casing + count pluralization would ship silently and corrupt every tool-call header and subagent token row.
findings.fix: Add format.test.ts covering: formatElapsed boundaries (59s, 60s->'1m 00s', 3599s, 3600s->'1h 00m', negative->'0s'); formatTokensK(null|undefined|-1)->null, 999->'999', 65300->'65.3k'; summarizeArgs string/object/empty/null + ARG_SUMMARY_MAX cap; buildCompactSummary grouping (verb lowercased after first, one vs other noun, inFlight ellipsis); compactPreviewLine previewKey hit vs fallback.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven vitest cases on the exact boundary inputs above; assert exact strings so padding/casing/pluralization can't silently drift.

findings.title: Pure parsers stepsForCommand/quote/humanize have no unit tests
findings.problem: steppers.ts holds the palette's only non-trivial logic — schema resolution (stepsForCommand), shell-style arg quoting (quote), and the label humanizer — yet there is no *.test.ts anywhere under command-palette/ or composer/. quote() is security-relevant (it builds the args string the runner re-parses, so a quoting miss could split/inject args), and stepsForCommand's matching is exactly where u4-1's bug lives. Folds/parsers like these are precisely what the invariant set flags for unit coverage.
findings.fix: Add steppers.test.ts: quote round-trips (plain token, spaces, embedded double-quote, empty string), stepsForCommand for each real command name + a non-matching name returning [], and humanize for 'vault set' -> 'Vault set'. These also lock the u4-1 fix.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest table-driven cases per function; assert quote('a b') === '"a b"' and quote('he"llo') escapes the inner quote.

findings.title: matchSlash ranking algorithm (exact > prefix > alias) has no unit test
findings.problem: matchSlash is pure ranking/parsing logic that drives the slash autocomplete and the ghost-suffix completion in PromptInput. Its three-bucket ordering (exact name, name-prefix, alias-prefix), the leading-'/' guard, the empty-needle 'return all' branch, and the limit slice are all untestable-by-eye edge cases with no coverage.
findings.fix: Add SlashCommands.test.ts asserting: exact name ranks first; prefix matches preserve catalog order; alias-only match appears after name matches; empty needle returns all (capped to limit); non-'/' input returns []; limit truncates.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven vitest over a fixed SlashCommand[] fixture asserting the returned name order for each query.

findings.title: Resolver fall-through routing (scoped vs unscoped, client-doesn't-handle) has no unit test
findings.problem: resolvePermission/resolveApproval implement subtle routing: scoped turn + client handles -> RPC to owner; scoped turn + client does NOT handle approvals -> defaultApproval (NOT fallback, 'headless semantics', line 714); unscoped turn -> fallback resolver. The integration tests cover only the happy path (client installed a resolver, server falls back when none installed). The 'scoped-but-client-doesn't-handle-approvals returns the default option rather than pestering the host fallback' branch and the 'permission client unavailable -> deny' / 'approval RPC throws -> defaultApproval' catch branches are untested. These are security-relevant decision gates (a wrong fall-through could auto-allow a tool or surface another client's prompt on the host).
findings.fix: Add unit tests driving RunnerServer.resolveApproval/resolvePermission directly (or via a turn whose owning client set approval:false) asserting: scoped+!handles returns the default option; a thrown PermissionCheck RPC yields {mode:'deny'}; a thrown ApprovalConfirm yields defaultApproval.
findings.testStrategy: Drive a turn from a client that called setResolver({approval:false}) and assert the mode that calls approval.confirm receives the default option without the host fallback firing; simulate a peer whose PermissionCheck rejects and assert deny.

findings.title: createClaudeCodeClient does not assert that beta headers + identity preamble reach AnthropicProvider
findings.problem: provider.test.ts verifies the client name and that the inner Anthropic client carries `authToken`/null `apiKey`, but never asserts that `oauthBeta` (CLAUDE_OAUTH_BETA) and `systemPreamble` (CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM) are actually forwarded. These two values are load-bearing: per constants.ts a subscription token is REJECTED by the Messages API unless `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` is sent and the system prompt leads with the exact CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM line. A silent drop/typo in the forwarding object would compile fine and only surface as a runtime 401/400 against the real Anthropic endpoint, which no unit test would catch.
findings.fix: Add a unit assertion that the constructed AnthropicProvider received the expected beta array and preamble (inspect the provider's stored config the same way the existing test reaches into `.client`, or expose a small readable field). This pins the contract that makes OAuth-mode Claude work at all.
findings.testStrategy: Extend provider.test.ts: `const c = createClaudeCodeClient({oauthToken:'t'})` then assert the internal config carries `oauth-2025-04-20`/`claude-code-20250219` and the CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM preamble.

findings.title: No tests for MCP call timeout, post-abort late-resolve, or wrapMcpServerToolsLazy
findings.problem: runMcpCallWithFallback is the load-bearing safety net that prevents a hung MCP server from wedging the agent's tool loop forever (per its own doc comment), yet wrap.test.ts only covers the immediate-abort case. The timeout branch (L44-48), the settle-once guard (a late callTool resolution after abort/timeout must NOT double-settle), and the entire lazy code path (wrapMcpServerToolsLazy, including first-call connect via getClient and abort-before/after-connect) are untested. A regression that drops the clearTimeout, double-settles, or breaks the lazy connect would ship silently.
findings.fix: Add vitest fake-timer tests: (a) call never resolves -> advance MCP_CALL_TIMEOUT_MS -> rejects with /timed out/; (b) abort fires, then the underlying promise resolves -> handler still rejects with /aborted/ and does not throw 'already settled'; (c) wrapMcpServerToolsLazy: getClient invoked only on first handler call, cached for the second, abort-before-connect throws without calling getClient.
findings.testStrategy: vi.useFakeTimers + a controllable deferred client.callTool.

findings.title: collectAgents subagent-event fold (a stateful reducer over plugin_event log) has no unit test
findings.problem: collectAgents is a non-trivial pure fold over the event log: it threads a Map keyed by childSessionId, transitions running/done/failed, drops orphan tool events that precede the started event, and parses 5 distinct subtypes. This is exactly the kind of reducer the audit flags as needing coverage, and none of the test files under plugin-cli/src/components cover it (only EventLine/StreamingPreview/BufferLines/parse-input have tests).
findings.fix: Add a unit test feeding a synthetic plugin_event sequence (started→tool_call→tool_result→completed, plus error/abort and orphan-without-started cases) asserting the resulting AgentRecord[] states, activity rows, and ordering.
findings.testStrategy: Direct call collectAgents([...events]) in vitest; assert record count, state, activity[].text, and that orphan tool events are dropped.

findings.title: fmtNext date/cron formatting helper has branchy logic and no unit test
findings.problem: fmtNext has four behaviorally distinct branches (cron → nextFireTime since lastRunAt|createdAt; invalid-cron → '(never — invalid)'; one-shot enabled → runAt ISO; else '(done)') plus a non-null `entry.runAt!` assertion in the sibling listSchedules. None of it is unit-tested. Regressions in next-fire formatting would silently mislead users about when a schedule fires.
findings.fix: Add format.test.ts covering: valid cron with/without lastRunAt, invalid cron (null nextFireTime), enabled one-shot, disabled/expired one-shot. Optionally guard `s.runAt!` in listSchedules behind a defined check.
findings.testStrategy: Pure unit tests over fmtNext with fabricated ScheduleEntry values + a stubbed/real nextFireTime; assert exact strings.

findings.title: Core channel logic (frame pump, slash dispatch, pairing, text dispatch, turn runner) has no unit tests
findings.problem: Only callback-handler and voice-handler have tests. FramePump (edit-throttle/messageId state machine, HTML/plain fallback, split-tail send), runSlash (registry-vs-local dispatch, model/mode pickers, publishBotCommands merge+dedupe), PairingHandler (state-machine wrapper, vault persist, greeting), handleTextMessage (auth gate, awaiting-text capture, /cancel, busy guard), and runUserTurn (turnId subscribe/filter + finally cleanup) are all untested. These are stateful protocol handlers whose bugs (e.g. u110-1, u110-3) are exactly the kind unit tests would have caught.
findings.fix: Add focused unit tests: FramePump.flush/scheduleEdit with a fake Bot api; runSlash registry dispatch + /yolo + picker keyboard contents; PairingHandler.confirmCode happy/mismatch/expired with a fake vault; handleTextMessage auth-gate + awaiting-text + /cancel branches; runUserTurn turnId filtering (events with wrong turnId must NOT render) + unsubscribe-in-finally.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with grammy Bot.api mocked (the existing tests already model this) and a fake Session/log.subscribe.

findings.title: noneIsolator (security passthrough gate) has no unit test
findings.problem: noneIsolator is a security-relevant code path: it is the registered isolator for tools resolved to strength 'none' and is the baseline the requireDeclaration / required-strength gate compares against (ISOLATION_RANK.none = 0). It has zero direct unit coverage — grep over packages/plugin-security/src/*.test.ts shows inprocIsolator is tested in isolators/inproc.test.ts but noneIsolator only appears via the registry/matrix tests indirectly. A regression that made it accidentally enforce (or throw) would not be caught by a focused test.
findings.fix: Add a 2-line test asserting noneIsolator.run(call, handler, caps, signal) returns handler(call.input) verbatim and ignores caps (e.g. an fs-violating input still runs, proving passthrough semantics are intentional and stable).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: pass a cap-violating input ({file:'/etc/passwd'}) with a handler returning a sentinel; assert the sentinel is returned (no enforcement), distinguishing it from inproc.

findings.title: validateAddServerInput per-kind required-field guard (the flat-schema enforcement) is untested
findings.problem: Because addServerInput is intentionally flat (no discriminated union, to satisfy OpenAI's function-call validator), the per-kind required-field enforcement lives entirely in `validateAddServerInput`: stdio requires `command`, http/sse require `url`. These runtime guards are the only thing preventing a malformed McpServerConfig. The add.test.ts only exercises the happy stdio and http paths; the two MoxxyError branches (missing command for stdio, missing url for http/sse) have no direct test. A refactor that drops one branch would not fail any test.
findings.fix: Add a schema.test.ts asserting validateAddServerInput throws CONFIG_INVALID with the documented message when stdio omits command and when http/sse omits url, and that autoSkill is stripped from the returned McpServerConfig.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function unit test, no I/O needed.

findings.title: Chunked uint8ArrayToBase64 (the stack-overflow mitigation) has no test
findings.problem: uint8ArrayToBase64 exists specifically to avoid `String.fromCharCode(...allBytes)` blowing the V8 stack on multi-second clips (per its own doc comment), chunking at 0x8000. This is load-bearing encoding on the voice-capture path, but only pcm16Peak is covered by pcm16.test.ts — the base64 chunker and the chunk-boundary case are untested. A regression (e.g. wrong subarray slicing at the final partial chunk) would corrupt the base64 sent to the transcriber.
findings.fix: Add tests: empty buffer → ''; a buffer larger than 0x8000 (e.g. 0x8001 bytes) round-trips through atob back to the original bytes; verify equality with a reference encoding. Optionally test audioToPcm16's float→int16 clamp/scale with a stubbed AudioContext.
findings.testStrategy: vitest: generate a >32KB Uint8Array of known bytes, encode, decode via Buffer.from(b64,'base64'), assert byte-for-byte equality including the partial trailing chunk.

findings.title: setupNativeResolution has no unit test despite mutating NODE_PATH + internal Module._initPaths
findings.problem: setupNativeResolution mutates process.env.NODE_PATH and calls the internal/undocumented Module._initPaths(). It is fully guarded, but it runs unconditionally at every packaged boot (bootstrap.ts:75) and a regression (e.g. prepend vs append, or breaking the existsSync filter) would silently break keychain native-dep resolution — falling back to passphrase prompts. No test exercises the NODE_PATH composition or the empty-candidates no-op.
findings.fix: Add a unit test using a tmpdir floorRoot: with a node_modules dir present, assert NODE_PATH gains the path APPENDED (not prepended) and that an existing NODE_PATH is preserved with the delimiter; with no candidate dirs, assert NODE_PATH is left untouched. Stub/spy is unnecessary for env mutation; _initPaths call can be tolerated.
findings.testStrategy: Described above; restore process.env.NODE_PATH in afterEach.

findings.title: clipboard-image (the module with the require-in-ESM bug) has zero unit tests
findings.problem: clipboard-image.ts contains spawn-based platform logic, AppleScript stdout parsing, 0-byte-file rejection, and the Linux write path — none covered by a test (no clipboard-image.test.ts). The require-in-ESM defect (u77-1) and the silent-fallback behavior went unnoticed precisely because nothing exercises the Linux branch.
findings.fix: Add clipboard-image.test.ts injecting a spawnSync stub: darwin 'ok'/'no_image'/0-byte cases, linux xclip-hit / wl-paste-fallback / both-absent. Make spawnSync injectable like voice-input's spawnImpl to test without real binaries.
findings.testStrategy: As above — the Linux-hit case would have surfaced u77-1 immediately.

findings.title: useLatestBlock snapshot/cache logic (live-stream-wins, reverse scan, content memo) has no direct unit test
findings.problem: `readLatestBlock`/`latestLineFromSnapshot` encode non-trivial rules: streaming assistant text wins over committed events; otherwise reverse-scan to the newest non-empty assistant_message OR user_prompt; and a content-keyed module cache returns a reference-stable LatestBlock (required for useSyncExternalStore to avoid infinite re-render). FocusWidget.test.tsx only exercises the happy assistant_chunk path end-to-end; the reducer rules and the cache stability/eviction (null path deletes the entry) are untested in isolation.
findings.fix: Add useLatestBlock unit tests against a fabricated ChatSnapshot: (a) streamingText present → assistant wins; (b) only user_prompt → user; (c) empty/whitespace content skipped; (d) same who+length+prefix but differing tail does NOT return a stale cached block (or accept the collision and document it). Pure-function-extract latestLineFromSnapshot is already test-friendly.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test latestLineFromSnapshot + readLatestBlock with hand-built snapshots; assert reference stability when key unchanged and a new reference when content changes.

findings.title: OAuthSignIn login state machine (prompt/browser/done/cancel/loginId filtering) has no unit test
findings.problem: OAuthSignIn implements a non-trivial protocol state machine: loginId minting + filtering of provider.login.output/prompt/done by the active id, the browser-vs-token branch keyed on /browser/i in the question, answer submission, cancel-on-unmount, and done code===0 vs !==0 transitions. The only existing coverage (ProvidersTab.test.tsx:109-119) asserts the Sign-in button renders — it never drives a login. A regression in loginId filtering (stale run bleeding into a new one) or the done/error branch would pass CI silently.
findings.fix: Add OAuthSignIn.test.tsx with a fake api (like useAgentTask.test.tsx): start() then emit output/prompt for the active and a foreign loginId (assert foreign ignored), assert browser button appears for a /browser/ question, submit('') and answer paths, and done code=0→onSignedIn / code!=0→error.
findings.testStrategy: Drive each transition via the emit() helper pattern already used in useAgentTask.test.tsx and assert phase/log/prompt/onSignedIn.

findings.title: Embedder selection / preference application / session-restore branching have no dedicated unit tests
findings.problem: selectEmbedder (lazy dynamic-import registration + TF-IDF fallback ladder), applyPreferences (provider/mode re-apply ordering + silent skip of stale prefs), and buildSession (resume-vs-sticky restore: explicit --resume errors on missing log, sticky resume falls back to fresh) are all branchy correctness-bearing logic with no test file. Regressions in the resume/fallback semantics (a core replayability invariant) would go uncaught.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: embedder fallback to tfidf when name unknown/import fails and 'none' short-circuit; applyPreferences skips a pref naming an unregistered plugin and applies provider-before-mode; buildSession throws MoxxyError for missing --resume log but returns fresh log for missing sticky sessionId.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest with a stub Session exposing minimal registries; mock restoreSessionEvents to throw and assert branch behaviour.

findings.title: Multi-branch top-level render gate (onboarding/splash/CLI-missing/recovery/connected) has no unit test
findings.problem: App.tsx is the single decision point for which top-level screen renders, with at least seven mutually exclusive branches (prefsLoading splash, onboarding-incomplete, first-boot splash, cli-missing/no-provider recovery onboarding, never-connected ConnectionScreen, connected shell, reconnect banner). The branch ordering and the lastConnected fallback (lines 142-157, 210-211) encode subtle invariants — e.g. 'don't drop to full-screen Splash on a workspace switch once connected once'. None of this is covered: apps/desktop has 16 test files but none target App.tsx. A regression in branch ordering would silently re-introduce the cold-start flicker the comments describe.
findings.fix: Add App.test.tsx rendering App with mocked client-core hooks (usePrefs/useConnection/useActiveWorkspaceId) to assert: prefsLoading->Splash, onboarding-incomplete->Onboarding, connected->ChatSurface, and that a snapshot=undefined transition after a prior connect keeps the shell mounted (no Splash).
findings.testStrategy: Use @testing-library/react with vi.mock of @moxxy/client-core to drive each gate branch and assert the rendered screen by role/test-id.

findings.title: Diff line classification + git status-color logic have no unit test
findings.problem: DiffView.lineStyle (the only pure parsing logic in the surfaces) and FilesPane.statusColor classify diff/status lines by prefix matching. These are the kind of small prefix parsers that silently mis-classify edge cases (a content line that legitimately starts with '+++' inside a code block, '---' as a markdown rule, untracked '??' vs 'A'/'D' status codes). They are untested; the only shell test is SidebarCollapse.test.tsx.
findings.fix: Add a small unit test asserting lineStyle returns the expected color/weight for file-header / hunk / add / del / context lines, and statusColor for '??','A','D','M' status codes.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function table test over representative diff lines and git status codes.

findings.title: config_set runtime-applier path, config_reload, and config_validate have no unit tests
findings.problem: The applier wiring (the seam that hot-applies config to a live session) is the load-bearing part of this plugin and is untested. plugin.test.ts covers config_path/show/get/set/init only. There is no test that config_set invokes the applier and surfaces its applied/pending result, that an applier throw is caught and reported as `pending: ['reload-failed: ...']` (lines 180-185), nor that config_reload/config_validate behave with and without an applier.
findings.fix: Add tests building buildConfigPlugin({ cwd, applier }) with a spy applier: assert config_set returns its runtime result; assert a throwing applier yields the 'reload-failed:' pending entry; assert config_reload returns the no-applier sentinel when applier omitted; assert config_validate returns {ok:false,...} for an invalid on-disk config.
findings.testStrategy: vitest with a mock ConfigApplier resolving/rejecting; assert returned shapes.

findings.title: fetchLatest transport (timeout/abort, !ok, malformed body) has no unit test
findings.problem: registry.ts has no registry.test.ts. fetchLatest is the network seam for both `moxxy update` and the TUI banner and carries non-trivial fail-soft logic: AbortController timeout, clearTimeout in finally, null on !res.ok, null on non-string json.version, null on throw. None of these branches are directly exercised (check.test.ts only stubs fetchImpl at the check layer and never hits a 404 or a malformed body through registry). A regression (e.g. dropping the clearTimeout, or throwing on a 404 body) would ship unnoticed.
findings.fix: Add registry.test.ts covering: 200 with {version} → string; 200 with no version → null; 404/500 → null; fetchImpl that rejects → null; and a timeoutMs:0 / hanging fetch path asserting the AbortController fires (timer cleared, returns null).
findings.testStrategy: vitest with injected fetchImpl stubs + a fake timer to assert the abort path and that clearTimeout runs in finally.

findings.title: cwdForWorkspace's id-vs-session matching + fallback routing has no unit test
findings.problem: `cwdForWorkspace` encodes non-trivial routing: match a desk by `d.id === workspaceId` OR `d.sessions.some(s => s.id === workspaceId)`, else fall back to active desk, else `process.cwd()`. This is the cwd that scopes both filesystem browsing and git operations, so a routing bug silently points reads/diffs at the wrong workspace. It is exported and reused by git.ts, yet no test in desktop-host references cwdForWorkspace or registerWorkspaceFsHandlers (grep over *.test.ts returns nothing).
findings.fix: Add a unit test for cwdForWorkspace covering: (a) match by desk id, (b) match by owning session id, (c) fallback to active desk when id is unknown, (d) process.cwd() fallback when no active desk.
findings.testStrategy: Drive with a fake DeskStore returning crafted desk/session shapes; assert returned cwd per case.

findings.title: Reactive-compaction-on-overflow and retryable-error paths have no unit test
findings.problem: index.test.ts covers text turns, tool turns, denial, throw, stuck-loop pairing, maxIterations cap, end_turn-with-tools, and hook system injection — but never exercises the error branch (lines 94-129): the context-overflow → force-compact → retry path (MAX_REACTIVE_COMPACTIONS, reactiveCompactions reset on clean call) and the generic retryable `continue`. This is non-trivial control flow (budget counter, reset semantics, the `compacted` guard that falls through to a fatal emit when compaction can't help) with zero coverage, so a regression in the overflow-recovery logic would ship silently.
findings.fix: Add tests: (a) provider yields a context-overflow error once then succeeds → assert a retryable error event with 'compacted older turns, retrying' and that the turn completes; (b) overflow that cannot be compacted (compactor returns false) → assert the fatal error event fires after the budget; (c) clean call after one reactive compaction resets the budget (a later overflow can compact again).
findings.testStrategy: Drive via collectTurn with a FakeProvider whose script includes a context-window error event (message matching isContextOverflowError) and a stub compactor that the test can make return true/false.

findings.title: gatherSignals event-log fold is untested (classify tests inject synthetic signals)
findings.problem: gatherSignals is the only code that reads the live EventLogReader: it folds tool_call_requested into a callId->name map, then walks a lookback window correlating failed tool_result events back to their call names and collecting error messages. classify.test.ts tests classify() with hand-built ClassifySignals and never exercises this fold, so the callId correlation, the lookback boundary (Math.max(0, log.length - lookback)), and the failed-but-unregistered detection have no regression guard.
findings.fix: Add a test building a real EventLog (or a stub EventLogReader) with tool_call_requested + failing tool_result + error events and assert failedTools/errorMessages, including the lookback truncation when the log exceeds the window.
findings.testStrategy: Construct events via core EventLog factory or a minimal EventLogReader fake; assert the derived ClassifySignals.

findings.title: deriveSuggestions/pickTopic regex heuristic has zero unit tests
findings.problem: deriveSuggestions() and pickTopic() are pure, branch-heavy parsers (reverse event scan + three layered regexes for backtick/quote/Capitalised-word extraction) that drive user-visible suggestion chips. There is no test asserting which branch fires for each last-block type, nor that the regexes extract the intended span. Regression risk is high because the logic is exactly the kind that silently drifts (e.g. an empty-events fall-through, or a regex that matches the wrong group).
findings.fix: Add suggestions.test.ts covering: empty events -> COLD_START_SUGGESTIONS.slice(0,3); last assistant_message with a backticked token -> 'Show an example of <token>'; tool_result/tool_call_requested -> the tool triple; error -> the error triple; and pickTopic precedence (backtick > quote > Capitalised, returns null on plain prose).
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function vitest cases feeding crafted MoxxyEvent arrays + raw strings; assert exact returned arrays.

findings.title: recall `seq` and `turnId` branches (and renderEvent fold) have no unit tests
findings.problem: recall.test.ts only covers the `callId` happy path, the idempotency belt, and the unknown-callId throw. The `seq` branch (ctx.log.at(seq) — seq-vs-index addressing, the exact thing the EventLog base/rebase logic makes subtle), the `turnId` branch (byTurn + renderEvent join), the 'provide one of' validation throw, and renderEvent's per-type formatting are all untested. recall is a protocol-ish reducer over the event log (an invariant-4 derived view) and is the kind of fold this audit flags for coverage.
findings.fix: Add tests: recall by seq returns renderEvent text for a user_prompt/assistant_message/tool_result; recall by turnId joins all renderable events of a turn; missing-seq and no-arg cases throw the documented messages; summarize:true truncates at SUMMARY_CHARS.
findings.testStrategy: Reuse the existing `reader()` fake-log helper in recall.test.ts to feed multi-turn events and assert seq/turnId outputs.

findings.title: consumeResponsesSse streaming fold has no direct unit test for tool-call / terminal / flush paths
findings.problem: The load-bearing Responses-API SSE fold (multi-chunk frame buffering, tool-call assembly across output_item.added->arguments.delta->arguments.done, the end_turn->tool_use upgrade via sawToolCall, the truncated-stream pending flush, terminal-frame short-circuit, abort handling, and usage accumulation) is a protocol handler with no direct unit test. sse-event-handler.test.ts only covers the per-event mapper, and provider.test.ts exercises the consumer only with trivial single `response.completed` frames — none of the tool-call or terminal-edge logic. A regression here silently drops tool calls (the exact bug the sawToolCall/flush code was written to prevent, per the inline comments).
findings.fix: Add stream-consumer.test.ts that drives consumeResponsesSse() with a ReadableStream of crafted frames: (a) output_item.added(function_call)+arguments.delta+arguments.done -> asserts tool_use_start/delta/end + message_end stopReason==='tool_use'; (b) function_call with NO .done frame -> asserts the pending flush still yields tool_use_end; (c) a frame split across two chunk reads -> asserts correct reassembly; (d) response.failed -> asserts single error event and no trailing message_end; (e) aborted signal -> asserts the abort error event.
findings.testStrategy: The new test IS the regression guard; assert exact ProviderEvent sequences for each scenario.

findings.title: Package has zero tests and no vitest config
findings.problem: packages/desktop-ui ships no test setup (no vitest.config.ts, no test script in package.json — only build/typecheck/clean). The primitives are mostly pure style-mapping with little algorithmic logic, so coverage value is modest, but the two behavioral pieces — Modal's Escape-to-close + portal SSR guard (returns <></> when document is undefined) and the variant/className merge precedence in Button — have no regression guard. The package is a shared dependency of the whole desktop renderer, so a silent prop-merge or portal regression propagates widely.
findings.fix: Add a vitest.config.ts (jsdom) + a small smoke suite: Escape calls onClose, overlay mousedown-on-self closes but mousedown-on-content does not, Button merges v.style then caller style (caller wins) and joins classNames, IconButton always emits btn-icon.
findings.testStrategy: The added suite is itself the regression guard.

findings.title: No test exercises nextFireTime with an explicit IANA timeZone or month/year wraparound
findings.problem: cron.test.ts covers only system-local cases. The most error-prone paths — explicit `timeZone` (the Intl branch + the zone/jump mismatch in u103-1), `jumpToNextMonth` (e.g. `0 0 1 1 *` walking across a year boundary), and the DOM/DOW vixie-OR semantics when both are restricted — have zero coverage. These are exactly the fold/algorithm cases the audit flags as critical-but-untested.
findings.fix: Add tests: (1) `0 9 * * *` with explicit timeZone (see u103-1), (2) `0 0 1 1 *` from mid-year -> next Jan 1, (3) a DOM+DOW both-restricted case asserting OR semantics (e.g. `0 0 13 * 5` fires on the 13th OR any Friday).
findings.testStrategy: Deterministic Date inputs; assert exact returned instant/components.

findings.title: matchesGlob/hostMatches/pathInScope traversal & wildcard edges untested
findings.problem: matchesGlob (the core authorization predicate) and hostMatches gate every fs/net decision, yet several security-critical edges have no test: path traversal collapsing ('$cwd/../etc' style after normalize), the documented '/**'-also-matches-parent special case, single-* not crossing '/', and hostMatches rejecting 'evilexample.com' vs pattern 'example.com'. pathInScope/urlInScope (the broker's per-syscall gate) have NO direct unit tests at all — cap-check.test.ts only covers checkFsCap/checkNetCap/maskEnv.
findings.fix: Add a table-driven test for matchesGlob (literal, *, **, parent-dir special case, traversal) and for pathInScope/urlInScope directly, including negative cases (sibling-prefix dirs like /work2 vs glob /work/**).
findings.testStrategy: Parameterized vitest table over (path, glob, expected) and (host, pattern, expected); include /work2/x vs /work/** => deny, *.example.com vs example.com => deny.

findings.title: Approval-gate logic (redraft-cap, cancel, headless auto-approve) has zero unit tests
findings.problem: index.test.ts covers parsers and the happy-path end-to-end flow but never exercises runQueryApprovalGate / runSynthesisApprovalGate directly. The redraft-cap branch (`nextCount > MAX_REDRAFTS` -> redraft-cap-exceeded) is non-trivial off-by-one-prone control flow that gates real user-facing behavior (abort after N redrafts), and the headless auto-approve path (`if (!ctx.approval) return approve`) is a security/UX invariant. None of these branches are tested.
findings.fix: Add unit tests for runQueryApprovalGate driving a fake ApprovalResolver: approve, cancel, redraft-with-feedback (asserts feedback + incremented count), and redraft N+1 -> redraft-cap-exceeded. Plus a headless (no resolver) auto-approve assertion for both gates.
findings.testStrategy: vitest with a stub `ctx.approval.confirm` returning scripted decisions; assert returned QueryGateOutcome.kind and redraftCount for each branch including the boundary at MAX_REDRAFTS.

findings.title: Reasoning-delta streaming path (reasoning_content/reasoning) has no unit test
findings.problem: The emitReasoning branch — which reads delta.reasoning_content ?? delta.reasoning and emits reasoning_delta, plus the reasoning_effort request wiring — is critical protocol-translation logic with no coverage in provider.test.ts. A regression in field-name handling (vendors split between reasoning_content and reasoning) or in the gating would silently drop visible reasoning for compatible backends.
findings.fix: Add tests: (a) chunk with delta.reasoning_content emits reasoning_delta when req.reasoning is truthy; (b) delta.reasoning (alt field) also handled; (c) reasoning ignored when req.reasoning is absent/false.
findings.testStrategy: Drive fakeOpenAI with reasoning_content chunks under req.reasoning={effort:'low'} and assert emitted reasoning_delta events.

findings.title: memory.ts fold/format pure helpers (groupByType, formatRelative, formatSize, prune cutoff) untested
findings.problem: memory.ts contains non-trivial pure logic with boundary behavior — groupByType (ordered fold with default-type bucket), formatRelative (today/1d/<30d/months/years boundaries), formatSize (B/KB/MB thresholds), and the prune-stale cutoff math — none of which has a unit test (no memory.test.ts exists). These are exactly the parser/fold/boundary functions that regress silently. groupByType also has a subtle correctness edge: it pre-seeds only the four `order` types, so an entry whose `type` is outside that order array is still bucketed (via the `?? []` fallback) but appended at the END of the map and would render after the ordered groups — untested.
findings.fix: Add memory.test.ts covering: groupByType ordering + an out-of-order type, formatSize at 1023/1024/1MB boundaries, formatRelative at 0/1/29/30/364/365 days. Consider exporting these helpers (currently module-private) for direct testing.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function unit tests with fixed inputs; for formatRelative inject a fixed `Date.now()` via a clock param or test around tolerance.

findings.title: matchEscape terminal-sequence parser is untested (kitty modifiers, CSI variants)
findings.problem: matchEscape is a protocol parser decoding raw terminal escape sequences (kitty keyboard protocol modifier bitmasks, CSI arrows/home/end/delete, alt-arrow word motion, SS3, meta+key) and returns a consumed-length used to advance the input stream. A mis-decoded length leaves junk in the buffer or eats a key. parse-input.test.ts exercises only two kitty paths indirectly; matchEscape itself (and hasCtrlModifier/hasShiftModifier bit math) has no direct test.
findings.fix: Add escape.test.ts asserting action+len for each branch: kitty Shift+Enter (CSI 13;2 u), Shift+Tab (CSI 9;2 u), Ctrl+letter, plain arrows, alt-arrows, CSI 3~ delete, SS3 home/end, meta b/f/backspace, and an unknown partial CSI returning null vs consuming to terminator.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven cases mapping input string -> {action, len, letter?}; include modifier-bit boundary cases (modifiers 1..8).

findings.title: stableStringify / hashRequest determinism has no direct unit test
findings.problem: hashRequest is the load-bearing key for both FakeProvider.byHash dispatch and RecordedProvider fixture lookup; its correctness rests entirely on stableStringify producing identical output for objects with differently-ordered keys. That key-ordering invariant is the whole point of the helper, yet it is never asserted. A regression (e.g. dropping the `Object.keys(...).sort()`) would silently break every fixture-replay test by producing record/replay hash mismatches.
findings.fix: Add hash.test.ts: (1) two equivalent ProviderRequests built with keys in different insertion order produce identical hashRequest output; (2) changing model/system/messages/tool name|description changes the hash; (3) a tool's extra fields beyond name+description do NOT change the hash (documenting the intentional narrowing on line 9).
findings.testStrategy: Vitest unit test as described; run under `vitest run` in packages/testing.

findings.title: Cost-math fold (foldResponses: billedInputEq/savedRatio/cacheHitRate/cacheEffective) has no unit test
findings.problem: token-accounting.test.ts only exercises summarizeTokensByModel. The entire `foldResponses` derivation backing summarizeSessionTokens and summarizeSessionTokensFromEvents — totalPrompt, billedInputEq (read 0.1x + write 1.25x), uncachedInputEq, cacheHitRate, savedRatio, and the non-trivial cacheEffective heuristic (`!(calls>=5 && totalCacheCreation>0 && cacheHitRate<0.05)`) — is untested. This is financial/cost-reporting logic with multiplier constants and a boundary heuristic; a regression (e.g. flipping a multiplier or the >=5/0.05 thresholds) would silently misreport cache savings and the 'cache broken' alarm with no test failure.
findings.fix: Add unit tests over summarizeSessionTokensFromEvents covering: all-uncached input, a healthy cache (high read ratio → cacheEffective true, savedRatio>0), the broken-cache trip (>=5 calls, writes>0, near-zero reads → cacheEffective false), the <5-calls and zero-writes exemptions (must stay true), and the totalPrompt=0 divide-by-zero guards (cacheHitRate/savedRatio default to 0).
findings.testStrategy: Vitest table of synthetic provider_response event arrays asserting each derived field, especially the cacheEffective boundary at calls===4 vs 5 and cacheHitRate===0.05.

findings.title: Picker dispatch + provider-switch credential logic has no unit test
findings.problem: makePickerHandler routes model/mode/mcp/plugins selections and handleModelSelected contains non-trivial, security-adjacent logic: a readyProviders gate that blocks switching to unconfigured providers, a providers.replace(def)+setActive dance to flush cached instances so freshly-resolved credentials take effect, and a credentialResolver round-trip. None of this is covered by a test (only run-slash and InteractiveZone have suites). A regression that drops the replace() or the readiness gate would silently strand a switched provider without auth.
findings.fix: Add picker-handlers.test.ts covering: unready provider → notice + no setActive; same-provider model switch → no replace(); cross-provider switch → resolver called, replace+setActive called, preferences saved.
findings.testStrategy: Mock session.providers/credentialResolver and assert call order and the readiness-gate branch.

findings.title: admin/index.ts onInit boot loop, enableAndAttach, and listServers have zero unit coverage
findings.problem: The new factory `buildMcpAdminPluginInternal` carries the load-bearing boot logic: the onInit loop that skips `disabled` servers, auto-refreshes a missing/empty cache (`refreshServerCache`), warn-and-continues on per-server failure, and lazy-attaches; plus the `enableAndAttach`/`detach`/`listServers` API used by CLI/TUI/desktop/runner. Grep shows no test imports `buildMcpAdminPlugin`/`buildMcpAdminPluginWithApi` from admin/index. The only onShutdown test (index.test.ts:61) exercises the LEGACY admin.ts shim, not this code path. So the disabled-skip, cache-refresh-on-miss, and per-server error isolation in onInit are unverified — a regression that, e.g., attaches disabled servers or aborts the whole boot loop on one bad server would ship green.
