findings.fix: Add admin/index.test.ts: build the plugin with a fake registry + a mocked runtime, write a config with one disabled, one cacheless, one cached server, run onInit, and assert lazy stubs registered only for the right servers, that a per-server refresh failure logs+continues, and that listServers/enableAndAttach return the expected join of config + runtime state.
findings.testStrategy: As above; reuse the runtime.test.ts mocking style (vi.mock('../client.js')).

findings.title: parseFlags (the only non-trivial pure logic) has no unit test
findings.problem: The sole test (index.test.ts) is an import smoke check that `record` is a function. `parseFlags` — which has the real edge-case logic (required-flag validation, comma-split allow-tools trimming/filtering, value consumption, unknown-flag rejection) — is never exercised. `parseFlags` is not exported, so it cannot be tested without a small refactor. Findings u18-1 and u18-2 would have been caught by such tests.
findings.fix: Export `parseFlags` (or move it to a `flags.ts` sibling) and add table-driven tests covering: required-flag errors, allow-tools split/trim/filter, unknown-flag rejection, --help, and the value-swallowing / NaN cases from u18-1/u18-2.
findings.testStrategy: vitest table cases over parseFlags(argv) covering happy path + each error branch.

findings.title: deriveSuggestions/pickTopic regex heuristics have no unit test
findings.problem: suggestions.ts is pure, parser-like logic: deriveSuggestions walks the event log backward branching on event type, and pickTopic runs three ordered regexes (backtick span, quoted span, capitalised-word run) to pluck a topic. This is exactly the kind of self-contained algorithmic logic that should be unit-tested, but there is no suggestions.test.ts in apps/desktop/src/chat (only JumpToLatest.test.tsx and Transcript.test.tsx exist). Regex edge cases (unbalanced backticks, smart quotes, capitalisation runs longer than 5 words) and the backward-scan branch ordering are untested, so a refactor could silently break the suggestion chips.
findings.fix: Add suggestions.test.ts covering: empty events -> COLD_START first 3; last assistant message with/without a backticked/quoted/capitalised topic; tool_call_requested -> 'Explain what just happened' branch; error branch; and pickTopic's three regex tiers + null fallback.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven vitest cases feeding crafted MoxxyEvent[] and asserting the returned suggestion arrays; direct pickTopic cases for each regex tier.

findings.title: No test for onShutdown-without-onInit (cursor defaults to 0 → folds whole log)
findings.problem: The cursor closure defaults to 0. If onShutdown ever fires without a preceding onInit (an abnormal lifecycle, e.g. a dispatcher that skips init on an error path), the plugin folds the entire log from index 0, re-counting any restored/resumed prefix. The three existing tests all call onInit first, so this default-0 fall-through branch is untested. The 'counted exactly once on resume' guarantee in the file's docblock hinges entirely on onInit having run.
findings.fix: Either (a) add a test asserting the documented happy-path ordering and accept default-0 as deliberate, or (b) guard: track an `initialized` flag and skip the merge (or treat the whole log as restored) when onShutdown fires without onInit, to avoid silent double-count on an abnormal lifecycle.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that calls only `onShutdown` (no onInit) and asserts the resulting behavior is the chosen contract (no merge, or explicit full-fold), pinning it against regression.

findings.title: Goal-loop termination guards (token budget, iteration cap, reactive compaction) have no unit coverage
findings.problem: goal-loop.ts is the safety-critical driver of an autonomous, auto-approved loop, yet index.test.ts only covers happy paths (goal_complete, mid-run auto-approve, policy-deny, stall, stuck-orphan). Several load-bearing termination/recovery branches are untested: the cumulative-token-budget backstop (lines 206-228, emits goal_budget_exhausted + stops), the iteration-cap exit (lines 359-377, emits goal_max_iterations), and the reactive-compaction-on-context-overflow retry path (lines 177-202, bounded by MAX_REACTIVE_COMPACTIONS=2). A regression that breaks any of these (e.g. budget check moved after a `continue`, or the cap off-by-one) would silently let goal mode run unbounded — the exact failure these guards exist to prevent.
findings.fix: Add goal-loop tests via FakeProvider: (a) a provider that reports usage exceeding GOAL_TOKEN_BUDGET (inject a low budget or a high-usage script) and assert goal_budget_exhausted + a final system assistant_message + no goal_completed; (b) a maxIterations override (ctx.maxIterations=2) with a perpetually-tool-calling script asserting goal_max_iterations + fatal error; (c) a provider emitting a context-overflow error then succeeding, asserting the retryable 'compacted older turns, retrying' error then completion, and that it gives up after MAX_REACTIVE_COMPACTIONS.
findings.testStrategy: Self-describing — the fix IS the test additions; assert on emitted plugin_event subtypes and stop conditions.

findings.title: runOauthLogin orchestrator has no unit test (browser/device branching, timeout capping)
findings.problem: runOauthLogin is the top-level login orchestrator (picks browser vs device flow, caps device timeout by device_code expiry, builds extras/account_id, persists). grep of the package test files shows zero references to runOauthLogin. The device-timeout cap (Math.min(init.expiresInMs, DEFAULT_DEVICE_TIMEOUT_MS)) and the OAUTH_FLOW_NOT_SUPPORTED rejection for headless-without-deviceFlow are untested protocol/branching logic.
findings.fix: Add run-login.test.ts with a fake DeviceFlowAdapter + fake vault asserting: (a) headless=false uses browser flow; (b) headless=true with no deviceFlow throws OAUTH_FLOW_NOT_SUPPORTED; (c) timeoutMs is capped at the smaller of expiresInMs and the default; (d) account_id/extras persisted.
findings.testStrategy: Stub runAuthorizationCodeFlow / adapter.start+poll via injected fakes; assert stored vault keys and thrown codes.

findings.title: NdjsonTransport drops malformed frames silently with no test
findings.problem: onData parses each line and on JSON.parse failure sets parsed=undefined to 'drop malformed frames rather than tearing the link down' — a deliberate, security-relevant resilience behavior (a peer cannot crash the runner with a bad line). There is no unit test pinning it: a future refactor could start throwing or forwarding undefined to the frame handler without any test catching the regression.
findings.fix: Add a test that writes a raw `garbage{not json\n{"ok":1}\n` and asserts only `{ok:1}` is delivered (malformed line dropped, link survives, good frame after still arrives).
findings.testStrategy: Raw net.connect client writes one invalid then one valid NDJSON line; assert exactly one frame received equal to the valid one and the transport stays open.

findings.title: No test for /compact failure/empty/no-compactor branches or token formatting boundaries
findings.problem: commands.test.ts covers the happy /compact path but not: no active compactor (returns error), empty log (returns 'nothing to compact: event log is empty'), compactor throw (returns kind:'error'), tokensSaved<=0 / empty summary ('nothing to compact yet'), and the formatTokenCount boundaries (M/k/trimFixed of 1.0). These are the branches most likely to regress when refactoring toward the shared helper (u80-2). The /help single-command-detail path (index.ts:102-112) is also untested.
findings.fix: Add tests for each compactSession early-return/error branch and a small table test for formatTokenCount (999, 1000->'1k', 1_500->'1.5k', 1_000_000->'1M'). Add a /help <command> detail test.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest cases per branch with fakes returning the relevant compactor/log shapes.

findings.title: Queue drain/priority/abort reducer logic has zero unit tests
findings.problem: useTurnRunner contains the non-trivial turn-queue state machine (priority-runs-alone, batch-merge of remaining queue, per-turn AbortController, recursive drain) with no test coverage at all (grep of *.test.* in the package finds only use-voice-input.test.ts and run-slash.test.ts). This is exactly the folds/reducers/protocol-handler class the audit flags, and finding u79-1 shows the logic is currently incorrect — a regression here is invisible.
findings.fix: Add use-turn-runner.test.ts covering: (a) single turn drains nothing, (b) queued messages merge into one follow-up turn joined by \n\n with concatenated attachments, (c) force-send runs the head ALONE before the rest, (d) dropFirst removes head without running, (e) abort via turnControllerRef cancels only the active turn.
findings.testStrategy: renderHook with a fake Session exposing a controllable async-generator runTurn; assert the sequence of (text, attachments) passed to runTurn across drains.

findings.title: migrateModeName (legacy mode-name migration gate) has no unit test
findings.problem: migrateModeName is the single funnel that maps legacy/persisted mode names (tool-use->default, deep-research->research, plan-execute/bmad/developer->default) to current names. It is consumed by core/preferences.ts (loading ~/.moxxy/preferences.json) and core/registries/modes.ts (mode lookup fallback). A silent regression in this map resurfaces the original bug it fixes: 'Mode not registered' crashes when loading an old config. No test pins the mapping or the identity-passthrough.
findings.fix: Add a small unit test asserting each legacy key maps to its target and that an unknown name passes through unchanged.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven test over LEGACY_MODE_NAMES entries plus one unknown name -> identity.

findings.title: ONBOARDING_STEPS gate predicates (full vs recovery routing) have no direct test
findings.problem: ONBOARDING_STEPS encodes the load-bearing routing decision between the first-run linear walk and the recovery gate (each step's applies/satisfied). step-flow.test.ts covers the generic engine but nothing asserts that THIS step table routes correctly — e.g. that in a recovery gate (full=false) only the unmet prerequisite (cli/provider/node) applies, that welcome/workspace/done are excluded outside first run, or that node only applies when probed-and-missing. A silent edit to a predicate (a regression source given u9-4's timing subtlety) would ship unnoticed.
findings.fix: Add a flow.test.ts that feeds representative OnboardingCtx values (full first-run; cli-missing recovery; no-provider recovery; node-missing) through resolveFlow(ONBOARDING_STEPS, ctx, mode, cursor) and asserts the active step ids + completion edge for each.
findings.testStrategy: Table-driven test over OnboardingCtx permutations asserting resolveFlow's steps[].id and isComplete; pure, no React.

findings.title: Security-critical url-safety allow-list has no direct unit test
findings.problem: url-safety.ts is the render-time XSS gate and is duplicated verbatim from the SDK with an explicit 'KEEP THE TWO IN LOCKSTEP' contract. It has no dedicated unit test; it is only indirectly exercised through render.test.ts for a couple of happy/literal cases. There is no test asserting parity with the SDK canonical function, so silent drift (or a missed scheme like the whitespace bypass in u72-1) is undetectable, and no edge cases (mailto:, tel:, data:image vs data:text, vbscript:, protocol-relative //, embedded whitespace) are covered at this boundary.
findings.fix: Add packages/plugin-channel-web/src/frontend/url-safety.test.ts with a truth table over allowed/blocked schemes for both 'href' and 'src', plus a parity assertion importing isSafeViewUrl from @moxxy/sdk (test-only, node env) and fuzzing identical inputs to guarantee the two copies agree.
findings.testStrategy: vitest table test covering: https/http/mailto/tel/relative/fragment => true; javascript:/vbscript:/data:text => false; data:image/* => true only for attr==='src'; plus a property-based parity check against the SDK copy.

findings.title: wrapWithPolicy Proxy passthrough of non-check methods (e.g. abortAll) is untested
findings.problem: `wrapWithPolicy` returns a Proxy that intercepts only `check` and `policyCheck`; all other resolver members (`abortAll`, channel-specific helpers) are meant to pass through via `Reflect.get`. The intercepted paths are tested (session.test.ts:146) but the passthrough is not. If a future edit broke `Reflect.get` (e.g. wrong `receiver`/`this` binding for a method that reads private state), a channel's `abortAll()` would silently fail to reach the underlying resolver — a real correctness/security-adjacent regression with no test to catch it.
findings.fix: Add a test: wrap a resolver exposing `abortAll: vi.fn()` (and a method that reads `this`), call it through `session.resolver`, and assert it dispatched to the underlying resolver with correct `this`.

findings.title: recallVector persistent-cache stitch path (cache hit reuse on second recall) has no targeted test
findings.problem: The `if (index)` branch is the most intricate logic in the unit: it splits the corpus into cached hits + misses, embeds `[...misses, query]` in one batch, derives the query vector by positional index `queryIdx = misses.length`, and stitches cached + fresh vectors back into entry order via freshByEntryIndex. The custom-embedder test (vector-recall.test.ts:69) exercises only the FIRST recall (all misses) and never asserts that a SECOND recall reuses cached vectors and re-embeds only the query. A regression that, e.g., mis-indexes queryIdx after a partial-miss, or returns stale cached vectors, would pass all current tests.
findings.fix: Add a test with a spy EmbeddingProvider counting embed() inputs: (1) save 3 entries, recall -> assert embed received 3 corpus texts + query; (2) recall again unchanged -> assert embed received ONLY [query] (all cache hits); (3) save a 4th entry, recall -> assert embed received [new entry, query] and the stitched ranking still places the right entry first. Also assert .embeddings.json persists across a fresh store instance.
findings.testStrategy: self (this finding adds the missing tests)

findings.title: session/settings/onboarding IPC handlers have no IPC-level test (mobile-reachable; attachment gate wiring untested)
findings.problem: Only mobile-gateway, prefs, sessions and shared have *.test.ts in this directory. session.runTurn wires the security-critical attachment authorization (authorizeAttachments + dropped-warn), settings.providers builds the renderer's whole provider table from getInfo()+admin merge (ready/enabled/active/kind/keyName mapping), and onboarding.providerAuthKind has a built-in-OAuth fallback path — none have a handler-level test. The underlying authorizeAttachments has its own unit test (attachment-authz.test.ts), which is why this is low not high, but the IPC wiring (that the gate is actually invoked with the right cwd before buildAttachments, and that `dropped` is honored) is unverified.
findings.fix: Add session.test.ts asserting runTurn calls authorizeAttachments with `[cwd]` and forwards only `authorized` to driver.runTurn; settings.test.ts asserting the providers-mapping shape (ready/active/kind/keyName) and that providerCatalog de-dupes builtins+admin; onboarding.test.ts covering the providerAuthKind registry-hit and OAUTH_FALLBACK paths.
findings.testStrategy: Stub RunnerPool/RemoteSession (sessions.test.ts already has the fakes) + spy on authorizeAttachments; assert call args and forwarded values.

findings.title: HookDispatcher chaining/short-circuit reducers under-tested (rewrite vs deny precedence, request threading)
findings.problem: dispatchToolCall encodes non-trivial precedence: a later 'deny' short-circuits and overrides any earlier 'rewrite', and 'allow' (undefined verdict) is ignored. dispatchBeforeProviderCall and dispatchToolResult thread the accumulator through each plugin. lifecycle.test.ts covers timeout/safety but not the deny-overrides-rewrite ordering or request/result threading across multiple plugins.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: (a) plugin A rewrite, plugin B deny -> final deny; (b) A deny short-circuits so B's hook never runs; (c) beforeProviderCall/toolResult: A mutates, B sees A's output, final reflects B.
findings.testStrategy: Drive HookDispatcherImpl.setPlugins with stub plugins returning known verdicts/transforms and assert the reduced output.

findings.title: Reasoning/thinking stream translation + cache-usage capture + abort are untested
findings.problem: The content_block handling for thinking blocks (thinking_delta -> reasoning_delta, signature_delta accumulation -> reasoning_signature at content_block_stop, redacted_thinking passthrough) and the message_start cache-token capture (cacheReadTokens/cacheCreationTokens) and the abort path (signal.aborted -> single 'aborted' error event) are protocol-critical folds with no unit test. provider.test.ts only covers text_delta + tool_use. A regression in signature accumulation silently breaks the interleaved-thinking round-trip (Claude then 400s the next tool-use turn).
findings.fix: Add fixture-driven tests for: a thinking block emitting reasoning_delta then reasoning_signature with the concatenated signature; redacted_thinking -> reasoning_signature{redacted:true,encrypted}; message_start carrying cache_read_input_tokens -> message_end.usage.cacheReadTokens preserved through message_delta; an aborted signal yielding exactly one {type:'error',message:'aborted'}.
findings.testStrategy: Extend the existing fakeAnthropic() fixture array with thinking/signature events and an AbortController in req.signal.

findings.title: TelegramApprovalResolver (security/approval gate) has no unit test
findings.problem: TelegramApprovalResolver is the approval gate surfaced to loop strategies (plan-execute) over Telegram. Its confirm()/resolvePending()/resolvePendingWithText()/abortAll() logic — including the no-decider fallback to defaultOptionId, the decider-failure path that resolves with `cancel`, and abortAll resolving every pending with the default — is security-relevant (it decides whether a plan/tool proceeds). There is a permission.test.ts and pairing.test.ts but NO approval.test.ts (verified: only format/pairing/permission/render/subcommands + channel/callback + channel/voice tests exist).
findings.fix: Add approval.test.ts covering: no-decider fallback returns defaultOptionId/options[0]/'approve'; resolvePending and resolvePendingWithText resolve and remove the pending and return false for unknown ids; decider rejection resolves with the default + error text and deletes the pending; abortAll resolves all pending with the default and clears the map.
findings.testStrategy: Vitest unit tests against TelegramApprovalResolver directly with a stub decider (resolving and rejecting).

findings.title: parseTokenResponse field-normalization + refresh-rotation behavior untested
findings.problem: parseTokenResponse is the single normalizer feeding every flow (auth-code, refresh, device). Its branchy logic — missing access_token→PROVIDER_UNKNOWN_RESPONSE throw, optional refresh_token, expiresAt = now + expires_in*1000, default tokenType 'Bearer', optional scope/idToken — has no direct unit test. refreshAccessToken's documented invariant (providers MAY omit refresh_token; caller must preserve the prior one) is also unverified, and a regression dropping expiresAt or refreshToken would corrupt token persistence/refresh.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: missing access_token throws PROVIDER_UNKNOWN_RESPONSE; full response maps every field; absent expires_in → no expiresAt key; absent token_type → 'Bearer'. Add a refreshAccessToken test (with injected fetchImpl) asserting a refresh response omitting refresh_token yields a TokenSet without refreshToken so callers can preserve the prior one.
findings.testStrategy: Pure function for parseTokenResponse; refreshAccessToken takes fetchImpl param (token-exchange.ts:58) so inject a stub Response.

findings.title: config.ts enable/disable persistence has zero unit tests
findings.problem: config.ts is the persistence layer that gates whether a plugin loads at boot and survives restarts (loadDisabledPackageNames, setPluginEnabled, clearPluginState, readUserConfig). It is security/lifecycle-relevant (a failed disable means a plugin the user turned off keeps running) yet has no test file — catalog.ts, install.ts, search.ts, shared.ts all have .test.ts siblings; config.ts does not. Edge cases go unverified: ENOENT -> {} fallback, the clearPluginState `plugins: undefined` round-trip through safeParse+stringify, the `settings?.enabled === false` (vs undefined) filter, and an invalid-config throw.
findings.fix: Add config.test.ts covering: round-trip set/load of a disabled flag against a tmp configPath; ENOENT -> loadDisabledPackageNames() returns empty set; clearPluginState removing the last plugin produces a config with no `plugins` key; setPluginEnabled preserves unrelated existing config keys; invalid YAML/config throws.

findings.title: audio.ts WAV/normalize helpers + WhisperTranscriber.run() error classification have no in-package tests
findings.problem: whisper.test.ts covers the happy-path transcribe branches and plugin registration, but the package ships pure, security-relevant byte-layout logic (pcm16MonoToWav WAV-header construction, normalizeWhisperUpload's PCM16-vs-passthrough + filenamePrefix branch) with no unit test in THIS package — they are only indirectly exercised by the codex package's tests. The run() error-translation ladder (APIUserAbortError re-throw, APIConnectionError/APIError -> classifyNetworkError/classifyHttpStatus -> MoxxyError) is also untested here, despite being the package's correctness contract for failures.
findings.fix: Add audio.test.ts asserting: WAV header fields for pcm16MonoToWav(24kHz); normalizeWhisperUpload PCM16 branch (WAV-wrapped, filename 'moxxy.wav' with prefix); passthrough branch filename inference. Add a whisper.ts test where create() rejects with a fake APIError(status) and a fake APIConnectionError, asserting the thrown MoxxyError code and that APIUserAbortError propagates unchanged.
findings.testStrategy: Self-describing: the new tests are the regression guard.

findings.title: MobileChannel.start/stop/rotateToken + the deny-before-host permissionResolver have no unit test
findings.problem: single-session-host.ts, pairing.ts and tunnel.ts are well covered, but the channel orchestration itself is untested: the start() ordering (register before accepting connections, origin allow-list assembly, tunnel-origin append, loopback-only hint), stop() teardown ordering, rotateToken() re-keying the live server, and the constructor's deny-by-default permissionResolver (`this.host ? ... : {mode:'deny'}`) that `moxxy serve --all` reads before start(). These are security-relevant (origin allow-list, deny-before-client) and exactly where regressions hide.
findings.fix: Add channel.test.ts with a stubbed startWsBridge/tunnel provider: assert permissionResolver.check returns {mode:'deny'} before start and delegates to the host after; assert setAllowedOrigins gets [...local, tunnelOrigin] when a tunnel opens and is untouched when the tunnel fails; assert stop() disposes the host, closes the tunnel then the server, and resolves `running`.
findings.testStrategy: self (this finding adds the test).

findings.title: describeCauseChain depth-cap and nested-cause folding untested
findings.problem: describeCauseChain walks an Error.cause chain with a hard depth cap of 5. The cap (a guard against cyclic/very-deep cause chains) and the per-link formatting are reducer-like logic with no direct unit test, so a regression that drops the cap (infinite loop on a self-referential cause) or mis-renders links would pass CI.
findings.fix: Add a test building a 7-deep cause chain and a self-referential cause (a.cause=a) with debug:true; assert exactly 5 `caused by` lines and no hang.
findings.testStrategy: As above; also assert non-debug output omits the chain entirely.

findings.title: AppleScript string serialization and key-chord script building have zero unit coverage
findings.problem: The only tests (tools.test.ts) exercise zod schema parsing, not the load-bearing string algorithms: `toAppleScriptString` (escape + newline->`& return &` splitting) and key.ts's KEY_CODES lookup / single-char keystroke / modifier->`command down` mapping. A regression in escaping (e.g. an unescaped quote) would silently produce a broken or injection-prone AppleScript and no test would catch it.
findings.fix: Add unit tests: toAppleScriptString of `a"b`, multi-line, and backslash inputs produce expected literals; key.ts produces `key code 36 using {command down}` for {key:'return',modifiers:['cmd']} and `keystroke "/"` for a single char; unknown multi-char key throws.
findings.testStrategy: Pure-function table tests; export the helpers or test through the handler with runProcess mocked.

findings.title: buildProviderAdminPluginWithApi.configure() has zero unit-test coverage
findings.problem: configure() is the channel/runner-facing ProviderAdminView path (used by RemoteSession provider.configure over runner protocol v7+). It contains non-trivial logic: patch-merge of baseURL/defaultModel/envVar/models, the defaultModel-in-models consistency check, the live-registry-first-then-disk ordering, and a rollback-on-write-failure path. index.test.ts exercises every tool (provider_add/list/remove/test) and onInit, but never calls buildProviderAdminPluginWithApi or its configure() — so the merge semantics, the 'no stored provider named X' error, the defaultModel validation, and the rollback are all untested. This is exactly the kind of fold/merge+gate logic the audit flags as a critical untested path.
findings.fix: Add a configure.test.ts: (a) configure unknown name → throws CONFIG_INVALID; (b) partial patch merges only provided fields; (c) defaultModel not in (merged) models → throws; (d) successful patch updates both registry def and disk; (e) write-failure rollback restores the prior def (covers u93-1's analogue here too).
findings.testStrategy: Use the existing FakeRegistry + tmp cfgPath harness from index.test.ts; assert registry.defs and readProvidersConfig state after each configure().

clusters.title: Long-tail: consistency / naming / small-duplication nitpicks
clusters.oneLine: Low-severity consistency deviations, naming, and small copy-paste that did not form a cross-cutting dedup theme. (49 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Cosmetic/structural nitpicks; fold into the relevant refactor PR when the file is touched.

findings.title: Status-table rendering (key/value rows + col padEnd + write loop) copy-pasted across 3 commands
findings.problem: runDaemonStatus, runServeStatus, and runStatus each build `rows: Array<[string,string]>`, compute `const col = Math.max(...rows.map(([k]) => k.length))`, then loop writing `${bold(k.padEnd(col))}  ${dim(v)}`. The exact same renderer is reproduced three times (and the daemon variant duplicates getDaemonStatus, which itself just re-wraps getServiceStatus). Drift risk and noise.
findings.fix: Extract a shared `printKeyValueTable(rows: ReadonlyArray<[string,string]>)` helper (e.g. in colors.ts or a small format util) and call it from all three. Consider dropping getDaemonStatus and calling getServiceStatus(SCHEDULER_SERVICE) directly in daemon.ts.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot-test the extracted printer for a representative rows array; verify the three commands still render the same output.

findings.title: slugify duplicated between command.ts and store.ts
findings.problem: command.ts defines a `slugify` (240-241) that is byte-for-byte the same as store.ts's `slugify` (174-180). The `newCmd` scaffold path and the store's `uniqueFilename` path must agree on slugging or a `/workflows new <name>` file and a later `store.create` of the same name can land on different filenames.
findings.fix: Export one `slugify` from a shared module (loader.ts or store.ts) and import it in command.ts; or route `/workflows new` through the store so a single slug authority exists.
findings.testStrategy: Assert command.newCmd and store.create produce the same on-disk filename for the same input name.

findings.title: read->parse->safeParse triplicated across safeRead, listEntries, readEntry
findings.problem: The exact sequence `fs.readFile(utf8) -> parseMdFile -> memoryFrontmatterSchema.safeParse -> branch on success` is copy-pasted three times (safeRead lines 16-20, listEntries inner loop lines 47-50, readEntry lines 63-66). Each copy can drift: indeed safeRead returns `body: parsed.body` untrimmed while the other two return `parsed.body.trim()` (see u88-3). Three call sites means three places a future frontmatter-validation or parsing change must be edited.
findings.fix: Extract a private `parseEntryFile(filePath, { trimBody }): Promise<{frontmatter; body} | null>` that does read+parse+safeParse and returns null on safeParse failure; have safeRead/listEntries/readEntry call it. ENOENT handling can stay per-caller (listEntries vs readEntry differ on whether ENOENT propagates).
findings.testStrategy: Existing store.test.ts round-trip + 'rejects invalid frontmatter' tests cover the merged helper; add one test for a malformed file returning null.

findings.title: ToolRegistryImpl.execute formats input ZodErrors cleanly but lets output ZodErrors throw raw
findings.problem: Input validation deliberately uses safeParse and reformats issues into a single-line model-friendly message (lines 87-96, with a comment explaining the 20-line ZodError noise problem). The output validation on line 112 uses outputSchema.parse(result), which throws the raw multi-line ZodError. An output-schema mismatch is a plugin bug, but it surfaces as the same red noise the input path was specifically written to avoid, and with no indication which tool/field failed.
findings.fix: Use outputSchema.safeParse and, on failure, throw an Error like `Tool ${name} produced invalid output: ${issues}` reusing the same issue-formatting helper as the input path (extract it to a small shared function).
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: tool with outputSchema requiring a field, handler returns a bad shape; assert the thrown message is single-line and names the tool + offending path.

findings.title: reclaimStaleSocket re-implements isRunnerUp's connect-probe (copy-paste)
findings.problem: reclaimStaleSocket inlines a connect probe (net.connect + once('connect')->true / once('error')->false + destroy) that is byte-for-byte the same liveness check exported as isRunnerUp() in socket-path.ts. Two copies of the same 'is something answering this address' logic drift independently; the exported one is the canonical, tested version.
findings.fix: Have reclaimStaleSocket call `await isRunnerUp(socketPath)` after the existsSync gate instead of re-rolling the probe (import from ./socket-path.js). Removes ~9 lines and keeps a single liveness definition.
findings.testStrategy: Existing 'reclaims a stale socket file' test (unix-socket.test.ts:88) plus isRunnerUp tests cover both paths; no behavior change expected.

findings.title: eventChars/safeJsonLen and the fullWalk algorithm are a hand-copied clone of the SDK's private estimator
findings.problem: `eventChars`, `safeJsonLen`, and `fullWalk` are a verbatim re-implementation of @moxxy/sdk compactor-helpers' PRIVATE `eventChars`/`safeJsonLen`/`estimateContextTokens` body (down to the +12 error-message fudge and the user_prompt attachment rule). The SDK does not export `eventChars`, so the cache wrapper must duplicate it; the two will silently drift if the SDK's token model changes (e.g. a new event type that contributes chars), and only the parity test catches it. Invariant 'reinventing a shared @moxxy/sdk helper'.
findings.fix: Export `eventChars` (and the stub-decision walk) from @moxxy/sdk so the TUI cache wraps the canonical fold instead of re-deriving it. Then context-estimate only owns the WeakMap cache + incremental fold, with `per = sdk.eventChars` by default.
findings.testStrategy: Existing context-estimate.test.ts already asserts parity with sdkEstimate; after the SDK export, the duplicated body is gone and the parity test guards the single source.

findings.title: Inline `args?.workspaceId ?? pool.activeWorkspaceId()` duplicates the shared resolveSupervisor fallback
findings.problem: shared.ts deliberately centralizes workspace resolution in resolveSupervisor/resolveCtx (with the documented 'default to active workspace' + not-connected guards), and most handlers route through it. But connection.retry, session.abortTurn and session.setAutoApprove re-implement the `args?.workspaceId ?? pool.activeWorkspaceId()` fallback inline and call pool.get/drivers.get directly. This is copy-paste of the exact logic the helper exists to own, so a future change to the resolution rule (e.g. how 'active' is chosen) silently skips these three call sites.
findings.fix: Route connection.retry through `resolveSupervisor(pool, args?.workspaceId)?.forceRetry()`. For session.abortTurn/setAutoApprove, add a tiny `resolveDriver(pool, workspaceId)` helper in shared.ts (mirrors resolveSupervisor but returns drivers.get(id)) so the active-fallback rule lives in one place.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test resolveSupervisor/new resolveDriver default-to-active behaviour (shared.test.ts already exercises resolveCtx the same way); assert the three handlers delegate to it.

findings.title: App product name 'MoxxyAI Workspaces' duplicated as a literal across 6+ sites in two files
findings.problem: The user-facing product name is hard-coded as the bare string 'MoxxyAI Workspaces' in bootstrap.ts (app.setName) and repeatedly in index.ts (app.setName, BrowserWindow title, tray header label, tray tooltip, the macOS app-menu label). The bootstrap comment explicitly warns it MUST stay in sync with index.ts, but nothing enforces that — a single edit that misses one site reintroduces the exact userData-dir divergence the comment describes (staged updates become invisible). Copy-paste of a sync-critical constant.
findings.fix: Define APP_PRODUCT_NAME once in a tiny shared floor-safe module (sibling to update-key.ts / floor-runner-protocol.ts, baked into the bootstrap) and import it in both files. Keeps the immutable bootstrap dependency-light while removing the silent-drift risk.
findings.testStrategy: Unit assert bootstrap and index reference the same exported constant; a grep-based lint (or test) that no other 'MoxxyAI Workspaces' string literal remains.

findings.title: dotColorForTool only resolves mcp/tool; DotColors.skill/subagent/other are picked by callers, leaving the helper a partial abstraction
findings.problem: DotColors exports five semantic colors (mcp/skill/tool/subagent/other) but dotColorForTool() can only ever return mcp or tool. The skill/subagent/other branches are resolved ad hoc in the renderers (BlockLine.tsx uses DotColors.skill, SubagentScopeView/SubagentGroupView use DotColors.subagent directly), so the 'color the dot by subsystem' decision is split between this helper and three call sites. The doc comment above DotColors describes skill/tool/other/pending/failed semantics but omits subagent ('blue'), so the table and the helper have drifted.
findings.fix: Either keep dotColorForTool tool-only (rename to dotColorForToolName and document it covers only the tool/mcp axis), or fold the skill/subagent dot-color decision behind a single dotColorForBlock(block) helper so all five colors are chosen in one place. Update the DotColors doc comment to include subagent.
findings.testStrategy: If centralized, unit-test dotColorForBlock per block kind; otherwise no behavior change to test.

findings.title: Inner consumption-loop catch does not special-case abort like the Anthropic provider does
findings.problem: When the stream consumption loop throws because of an in-flight abort (the SDK rejects the iterator with an AbortError once req.signal fires), the catch at 232 runs toFriendlyError() and emits a generic provider error instead of the clean { message: 'aborted', retryable: false } event. The Anthropic provider (provider.ts:425-426) explicitly checks `signal?.aborted` in its catch and yields the clean aborted event. This is an inconsistency in cancel semantics across providers; callers that key off message==='aborted' to suppress error UI on user cancel get a noisy classified error from OpenAI instead.
findings.fix: Mirror Anthropic: in the inner catch, `if (req.signal?.aborted) { yield { type:'error', message:'aborted', retryable:false }; return; }` before the toFriendlyError fallback. Apply the same guard to the create() catch at 158-160.
findings.testStrategy: Test that abort()ing mid-stream yields message:'aborted', not a classified provider error.

findings.title: PermissionEditor `visible` useMemo is a no-op identity wrapper around rows
findings.problem: `const visible = useMemo(() => rows, [rows])` returns rows unchanged with rows as the only dep — it computes nothing and memoizes nothing (identity). It is dead ceremony that implies filtering/derivation that doesn't exist, and misleads future readers into thinking `visible` differs from `rows`.
findings.fix: Delete the useMemo and use `rows` directly (or implement the intended viewport/filtering if that was the plan).
findings.testStrategy: Trivial; covered by existing render — removing it is behavior-preserving.

findings.title: Docs/description say 'JSONL' but recorder writes pretty-printed .json fixtures
findings.problem: The file header (lines 2-5) and package.json description claim fixtures are JSONL ('writes the recorded ProviderEvents to a JSONL fixture'), and the readdir filter at line 104 matches `*.json`. But RecordedProvider actually writes a single pretty-printed JSON object per fixture (`JSON.stringify(fixture, null, 2)` to `${testName}.${hash}.json`, record-replay.ts:84,99) — not newline-delimited JSON. The wrong format name misleads anyone debugging or hand-editing fixtures (a real JSONL parser would fail on the multi-line object).
findings.fix: Reword the header comment and package.json description to 'pretty-printed JSON fixture' (or '.json fixtures') to match the actual on-disk format.
findings.testStrategy: Docs-only; verify wording matches the .json extension the filter already uses.

findings.title: DEFAULT_FILENAMES in whisper.ts duplicates WHISPER_FILENAME_BY_MIME in audio.ts verbatim
findings.problem: whisper.ts defines a private DEFAULT_FILENAMES map (lines 16-27) that is byte-for-byte identical to WHISPER_FILENAME_BY_MIME in audio.ts (lines 18-29), within the same package. Copy-paste of the same lookup table that the package already exports; the two will drift (e.g. a new codec added in one place only).
findings.fix: Remove DEFAULT_FILENAMES from whisper.ts. After applying u108-1, filename inference flows through normalizeWhisperUpload; if u108-1 is not taken, import whisperFilenameFor from './audio.js' instead.
findings.testStrategy: Existing tests already assert filename routing indirectly via the create() request; add an explicit case for an unmapped MIME -> 'audio.bin'.

findings.title: Grep and Glob diverge on symlink handling and cycle-safety despite sharing the walk concern
findings.problem: Glob.walk resolves realpath + tracks a `visited` set (cycle-safe) and explicitly stats symlinks so dir-symlinks recurse and file-symlinks match (glob.ts:54-105). Grep.walk does neither: a symlink to a directory has Dirent.isDirectory()===false and isFile()===false, so grep's `if (!entry.isFile()) continue` silently skips ALL symlinks — it never searches symlinked files or directories, and has no realpath/visited cycle guard at all. The two walkers were meant to 'stay in sync' (util.ts:44-48 comment about IGNORED_DIR_NAMES) but their traversal semantics differ, so Grep silently misses content Glob would find.
findings.fix: Extract a single shared directory-walker (cycle-safe, symlink-aware, IGNORED_DIR_NAMES-aware) used by both Grep and Glob; or at minimum make Grep follow symlinks with the same realpath/visited guard. Either route deletes the copy-paste divergence.
findings.testStrategy: Add a grep test mirroring glob's symlink test: a file-symlink whose target contains the pattern should be found; a dir-symlink cycle must terminate.

findings.title: Steps bypass useOnboarding's saveProviderKey/refresh helpers, leaving status stale
findings.problem: ProviderStep calls api().invoke('onboarding.saveProviderKey', ...) directly (ProviderStep.tsx:97) rather than useOnboarding().saveProviderKey, which additionally calls refresh() so the parent's ob.status.hasProvider updates. Same pattern as CliStep (u9-2). Net effect: after a successful key save the parent Onboarding's hasProvider gate doesn't refresh until an unrelated phase transition fires useOnboarding's effect, so the recovery-gate auto-resolution relies on a side channel instead of the explicit refresh the helper provides. (The hook's saveProviderKey export consequently has zero callers — dead, but that lives in unit 33's useOnboarding.ts, reported there.)
findings.fix: Route ProviderStep's key save through the lifted useOnboarding().saveProviderKey (which refreshes status), or call ob.refresh() after activateProvider() so the parent gate re-evaluates immediately.
findings.testStrategy: Mock api(); after saveKey resolves, assert onboarding.status is re-probed so hasProvider flips true without waiting on a phase change.

findings.title: AgentPicker duplicates the session.info fetch logic in two places (refresh + effect)
findings.problem: `refresh()` (L40-45) and the in-effect `fetchInfo()` (L49-56) are byte-for-byte the same IPC call differing only by the effect version's `cancelled` guard. The duplication invites drift (e.g. a future error-handling change applied to one and not the other) and the un-guarded `refresh()` can setState after unmount if invoked from `onPickProviderModel` right before a workspace switch.
findings.fix: Hoist a single `loadInfo(signalRef)` helper (or reuse the shared `useSessionInfoBridge` hook from client-core if it covers this) and route both the mount effect and the post-pick refresh through it, sharing the mounted guard.
findings.testStrategy: Render AgentPicker, mock session.info, trigger a provider/model pick then immediately unmount; assert no post-unmount setState warning.

findings.title: originalPrompt uses only the FIRST user_prompt while the planner is fed ALL of them
findings.problem: runPlanningPhase captures originalPrompt as the first user_prompt in the log (research-loop.ts:270-275), and that single string is what synthesis (buildSynthesisInput) and follow-up planning (buildFollowupUserMessages) anchor on. But buildInitialUserMessages (query-phase.ts:75-79) pushes EVERY user_prompt in the log as a planner message. In a multi-turn session the planner plans against the full conversation, yet synthesis is told the 'Original question' is only the first prompt — they can diverge, producing a report anchored on a stale question.
findings.fix: Pick one source of truth: either anchor originalPrompt on the LAST user_prompt (the active turn's prompt) for both planner and synthesis, or pass the same derived prompt into buildInitialUserMessages so planner and synthesis agree.
findings.testStrategy: Test with a log containing two user_prompt events; assert the synthesis input's 'Original question' matches whichever prompt the planner was driven from.

findings.title: RecordedProvider.writeFixture uses non-atomic writeFile (no tmp+rename)
findings.problem: writeFixture writes the fixture JSON directly with fs.writeFile, not the repo's standard atomic write (writeFile tmp; rename) per invariant 5. If the recorder process is interrupted mid-write (Ctrl-C during a long Anthropic record run), a half-written fixture file can be left behind that then fails JSON.parse on the next replay, masquerading as a corrupt-fixture error rather than a missing one.
findings.fix: Write to `${path}.tmp` then `fs.rename` into place, or reuse the SDK's atomic-write helper. Single-writer test fixtures make this low-severity, but it aligns with the repo invariant and avoids torn fixtures on interrupt.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test that mocks fs to throw after the tmp write and asserts the final path is untouched; plus existing record-replay round-trip test still passes.

findings.title: EmbeddingIndex partially reinvents @moxxy/sdk CachedEmbeddingProvider
findings.problem: The SDK exports CachedEmbeddingProvider (sdk/src/embedding-cache.ts) — a content-hash embedding cache with serialize()/hydrate() for cross-process persistence. EmbeddingIndex re-implements the same hash-keyed embed-miss caching by hand. It does add genuinely-extra concerns (name-keyed entries enabling prune(), embedder-name+dim invalidation, atomic disk persistence), so it is not a pure duplicate, but the core content-hash lookup/set logic is duplicated rather than composed on top of the shared helper.
findings.fix: Either layer EmbeddingIndex's disk/dim/prune logic over a CachedEmbeddingProvider instance (compose, don't copy the hash-cache core), or document in a header comment exactly why the shared helper is insufficient (name-keyed prune + dim invalidation) so future readers don't 'fix' it by deleting one.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (refactor preserving existing embedding-cache.test.ts round-trip + invalidation tests).

findings.title: triggerSummary duplicated verbatim in command.ts and tools.ts (drifting separator)
findings.problem: `triggerSummary` is copy-pasted in both command.ts (201-210) and tools.ts (342-351). They have already drifted: command.ts joins with `'+'` while tools.ts joins with `' + '`, and command.ts ends with `parts.join('+')` vs tools.ts `parts.join(' + ')`. Two copies of the same trigger-rendering logic invite further divergence.
findings.fix: Extract one `triggerSummary(on)` helper (e.g. in loader.ts or a small format.ts) and import it in both command.ts and tools.ts.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test the shared helper across all trigger shapes; remove the duplicates.

findings.title: recall.ts imports `z` from 'zod' directly instead of '@moxxy/sdk' like every sibling tool
findings.problem: The SDK re-exports zod (`export { z } from 'zod'` in packages/sdk/src/index.ts:444) precisely so plugins use ONE zod instance; all 7 other built-in tools (bash/edit/grep/write/glob/read/sleep) import `z` from '@moxxy/sdk'. recall.ts alone imports `{ z } from 'zod'`. If the resolved zod copy ever differs from the SDK's (duplicate in the tree, version skew), `instanceof ZodType` checks inside the SDK's schema-handling (zodToJsonSchema, defineTool validation) can silently fail to recognize recall's schema, and it is an unexplained inconsistency in an otherwise uniform module.
findings.fix: Change recall.ts line 2 to import `z` from '@moxxy/sdk' (merge with the existing line-1 sdk import), matching all sibling tools.
findings.testStrategy: Existing recall.test.ts already exercises the schema path; add a lint rule (no-restricted-imports for 'zod' inside plugin packages) to prevent regression.

findings.title: UsageModal re-implements formatTokensShort (with a divergent M precision)
findings.problem: `fmt(n)` is a near-verbatim copy of `formatTokensShort` already exported from @moxxy/client-core/chat-store/usage.ts. The two diverge: this copy uses `.toFixed(2)` for the millions case while the shared one uses `.toFixed(1)`, so the same token count renders '3.40M' in the modal but '3.4M' in the compaction notice store.ts emits. Token formatting should read identically across the surface.
findings.fix: Re-export `formatTokensShort` from @moxxy/client-core's index, import it in UsageModal, delete the local `fmt`. Decide one M precision (1dp) and apply everywhere.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test formatTokensShort for boundary inputs (999, 1000, 1_000_000, 3_400_000); assert UsageModal renders the same string as the store notice for an identical count.

findings.title: Duplicated fixed-bottom-center inline-style block across GlobalAskFallback and ReconnectBanner
findings.problem: GlobalAskFallback (lines 272-283) and ReconnectBanner (lines 314-345) both hand-roll the same 'position:fixed; left:50%; bottom:24; transform:translateX(-50%)' floating-pill positioning as inline style objects, diverging only in zIndex (60 vs 50) and padding. This is copy-paste of layout primitives that the rest of the app expresses via the shared stylesheet / design-tokens; inline duplication means a positioning tweak must be made in two places and theme tokens are bypassed.
findings.fix: Extract a shared FloatingBottomCenter wrapper (or a CSS class in styles.css) parameterized by zIndex; both call sites use it.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (visual/refactor); covered by existing render smoke once a shared class is used.

findings.title: absPath join logic duplicated between FilesPane.detailFor and WorkspaceFiles.FileRow
findings.problem: The cwd+relPath -> absPath construction `cwd ? `${cwd.replace(/\/+$/, '')}/${rel}` : rel` is copy-pasted in two places (FilesPane.detailFor and WorkspaceFiles.FileRow onClick). Divergence risk: if one is fixed for trailing-slash/Windows-separator edge cases the other silently keeps the bug. Path joining is exactly the kind of helper that should be shared.
findings.fix: Extract a single `joinWorkspacePath(cwd, rel)` helper (alongside FileInsertDetail in WorkspaceFiles, or a shared util) and call it from both sites.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the extracted helper for trailing slash, no-cwd fallback, nested paths; assert both call sites use it.

findings.title: collectExtraFlags leaks launcher-internal flags (no-wizard, __skipWizard) into channel.start()
findings.problem: collectExtraFlags drops a reserved set `{model, config, verbose, standalone, attach}` and forwards everything else into the channel's `start()` options. But the launcher also consumes `no-wizard` and `__skipWizard` (used by run-channel.ts:63-64 and service.ts which spawns `moxxy telegram --no-wizard`). These are not in the reserved set, so a channel started via the service launcher receives `{ 'no-wizard': true }` in its start options. Channels that validate/enumerate their options, or that happen to use a `no-wizard` key, could misbehave; at minimum it is an undocumented option contaminating the channel surface.
findings.fix: Add `'no-wizard'` and `'__skipWizard'` (and `'wizard'`) to the reserved set, or better, invert the model so start-flag forwarding uses an explicit allow-list / the channel-declared flag schema rather than a deny-list that must track every launcher-internal flag.
findings.testStrategy: Extend client-mode.test.ts: assert `collectExtraFlags(argv({ 'no-wizard': true, foo: 'bar' }))` === `{ foo: 'bar' }`.

findings.title: Internal Claude constants (CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM, CLAUDE_OAUTH_BETA, CLAUDE_CODE_SERVICE_NAME) leaked through public surface but unused externally
findings.problem: index.ts re-exports CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM, CLAUDE_OAUTH_BETA, CLAUDE_CODE_SERVICE_NAME, and CLAUDE_TOKEN_ENV_VARS, but a repo-wide grep shows only CLAUDE_TOKEN_ENV_VARS is consumed externally (cli/provider-credentials.ts). The other three are implementation details (request-shaping constants + a display string) that widen the package's public API for no consumer, making future refactors of the Anthropic-request stamping a breaking-change concern they need not be.
findings.fix: Narrow the index.ts re-export to just CLAUDE_TOKEN_ENV_VARS (and CLAUDE_CODE_PROVIDER_ID if needed by builtins wiring). Keep CLAUDE_CODE_SYSTEM/CLAUDE_OAUTH_BETA module-internal — provider.ts already imports them straight from constants.js.
findings.testStrategy: Remove the re-exports, run repo typecheck + build; nothing external references them so it stays green.

findings.title: probeNode doc says 250ms budget but the timeout is 2000ms
findings.problem: The JSDoc claims a 'Fast (250ms budget)' node probe, but the actual setTimeout fires at 2_000ms. A wizard relying on the documented bound could feel a 2s stall on a hung node. Minor doc/code drift.
findings.fix: Reconcile: either drop the timeout to 250ms or correct the comment to 2s.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (doc).

findings.title: wrapOneTool and wrapOneLazyTool are near-identical copy-paste
findings.problem: The two builders differ only in how they obtain the client: the eager one closes over a live `client`, the lazy one calls `await getClient()`. Everything else (name, description, schema, permission, abort checks, runMcpCallWithFallback, renderResult) is duplicated verbatim. Future edits to the handler (e.g. timeout tuning, error formatting, display-result support) must be made in two places and will drift.
findings.fix: Collapse to one builder parameterized by a `resolveClient: () => Promise<McpClientLike>` thunk; the eager path passes `() => Promise.resolve(client)`. wrapMcpServerTools maps with the resolved thunk, wrapMcpServerToolsLazy maps with getClient. Removes the second copy.
findings.testStrategy: Existing wrap.test.ts plus a new lazy-path test (see u86-2) both pass against the unified builder.

findings.title: setServerDisabled/removeServerFromConfig hand-roll the read-modify-write that mutateMcpConfig already encapsulates
findings.problem: config-io.ts defines a generic `mutateMcpConfig` helper (lines 55-64) that runs read+modify+write as one critical section under `configMutex`, with a reference-equality no-op skip. But `setServerDisabled` (76-87) and `removeServerFromConfig` (93-102) re-implement the exact same pattern by calling `configMutex.run` directly with their own readMcpConfig + writeMcpConfig. This is copy-paste of the load-bearing concurrency boilerplate; future changes to the mutex/write contract (e.g. the no-op-skip optimization) must be remembered in 3 places, and the two hand-rolled copies always write even on a logical no-op (setServerDisabled always writes even when `disabled` is unchanged).
findings.fix: Rewrite setServerDisabled and removeServerFromConfig in terms of mutateMcpConfig, returning `{next: cfg, result}` with the same reference when nothing changed (e.g. name not found, or disabled flag already equals target) so the no-op write is skipped.
findings.testStrategy: Existing config-io.test.ts covers both; add a case asserting setServerDisabled(name, same-value) does not rewrite the file (capture mtime or spy writeFileAtomic).

findings.title: useThemePreference uses an inline per-render subscribe (resubscribe churn) — diverges from sibling stores
findings.problem: useThemePreference passes a freshly-allocated inline arrow `(cb) => { listeners.add(cb); return () => listeners.delete(cb); }` as the subscribe arg to useSyncExternalStore. A new subscribe identity on every render makes React tear down and re-add the listener every commit, unlike every other module store in this unit (useSidebarCollapsed, useRailWidth, useWorkspaceCollapsed) which all pass a stable module-level `subscribe`. The returned cleanup also yields `listeners.delete(cb)` (a boolean) where a `() => void` is expected.
findings.fix: Hoist a module-level `function subscribe(fn){ listeners.add(fn); return () => { listeners.delete(fn); }; }` (note braces so the cleanup returns void) and pass it to useSyncExternalStore, matching the sibling stores. emit() already iterates `listeners`.
findings.testStrategy: Existing useTheme.test.tsx still passes; add an assertion that listeners.size returns to 0 after unmount and does not grow across re-renders.

findings.title: Two near-identical child-process spawn+collect runners (run / runCmd) plus duplicate trunc/truncate
findings.problem: core-update.ts `run()` and verify.ts `runCmd()` are byte-for-byte the same spawn-pipe-collect-timeout-SIGKILL implementation modulo the result field names (code/output vs exitCode/output) and timeout constant. trunc()/truncate() are likewise duplicated. This is copy-paste that will drift; both reimplement a process runner the SDK does not yet expose (only spawnCliTunnel exists in @moxxy/sdk).
findings.fix: Extract a single `runProcess(cmd,args,cwd,timeoutMs): Promise<{code:number;output:string}>` (and one truncate) into a shared module within the plugin, or promote a generic spawn-collect helper to @moxxy/sdk so other plugins (tunnel, scheduler) can reuse it. Neither file imports core, so a local shared util keeps invariant 2 intact.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the extracted runProcess for exit code, combined stdout/stderr capture, ENOENT 'error' path, and timeout SIGKILL; existing verify/core tests then cover callers.

findings.title: Surface emit() loop lacks the per-callback try/catch that TerminalProcessImpl uses
findings.problem: The surface fan-out `emit` iterates dataSubs and calls each cb with no guard. TerminalProcessImpl.emitData (pty.ts:96-105) deliberately wraps each listener in try/catch with the comment 'a bad viewer must not break the stream'. The surface layer — the actual viewer-facing fan-out — drops that protection, so one throwing viewer callback aborts delivery to all subsequent viewers and can leave the data/exit subscription in an inconsistent state.
findings.fix: Wrap each `cb(payload)` in try/catch matching emitData, swallowing per-viewer errors.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: add two data subscribers, make the first throw, assert the second still receives the payload.

findings.title: session.runCommand casts the session through `as unknown as` to satisfy the command handler
findings.problem: The runCommand handler passes `this.session as unknown as Parameters<typeof def.handler>[0]['session']`. This double-cast erases the type relationship between ClientSession and the command handler's expected session shape, defeating the contract that would otherwise catch a drift between the two surfaces (a banned-pattern per invariant 10's spirit, though here it's a cast not a private-field poke).
findings.fix: Tighten the command-handler session type (or ClientSession) so the registered command's expected session is structurally assignable from ClientSession, removing the `as unknown as`. If the command registry truly needs a wider session, introduce a shared narrowing type in @moxxy/sdk and route both through it.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: a .test-d.ts asserting the command handler accepts a ClientSession without an explicit cast.

findings.title: WS subprotocol/bearer constants duplicated from @moxxy/sdk channel-auth (drift risk)
findings.problem: WS_SUBPROTOCOL, WS_BEARER_PREFIX and bearerProtocol() are byte-for-byte copies of MOXXY_WS_SUBPROTOCOL, MOXXY_WS_BEARER_PROTOCOL_PREFIX and encodeWsBearerProtocol() in packages/sdk/src/channel-auth.ts (lines 150-165). The duplication is deliberate and documented (this package must stay Node-dep-free to bundle under Metro, and @moxxy/sdk currently pulls Node-flavoured deps), so it is NOT a violation to collapse blindly. But there is no compile-time link keeping the two strict-percent-encode implementations in lockstep — if the SDK encoder ever changes (e.g. encodes another char), the host (which decodes via tokenFromWsProtocolHeader) and this client silently disagree and auth breaks at runtime only.
findings.fix: Either (a) extract the three constants + encoder into a tiny dependency-free subpath of @moxxy/sdk (mirror the existing @moxxy/sdk/tool-display dependency-free subpath pattern) and import it here, or (b) if the boundary must stay, add a cross-package equivalence test that imports encodeWsBearerProtocol from @moxxy/sdk and bearerProtocol from this package and asserts identical output over an edge-char corpus, so drift fails CI instead of silently breaking pairing.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test feeding a fuzz/edge corpus (chars !'()*, +, =, /, unicode) through both encoders and asserting equality; round-trip each through tokenFromWsProtocolHeader and assert it recovers the original token.

findings.title: Voice download failure logs a hardcoded placeholder instead of the real HTTP status
findings.problem: On a failed audio download the warn meta is { status: 'ok=false' } (line 97), a literal string, even though response carries no status field on the test shim but the real fetch Response does. Operators get no diagnostic signal (404 vs 403 vs network). Inconsistent with the rest of the file which logs err: String(err).
findings.fix: Widen FetchAudioResult to include optional status/statusText and log the actual value (or log url + response.status when available).
findings.testStrategy: Test the !response.ok branch asserts the logged meta reflects the stubbed status.

findings.title: Content cache key uses length + 64-char prefix slice — can return a stale block on same-prefix edits
findings.problem: The memo key is `${who}:${text.length}:${text.slice(0,64)}`. Two distinct messages with the same author, identical length, and an identical first 64 characters produce the same key, so the cache returns the previously-stored block and the preview shows stale text. Unlikely but possible for streamed answers that are edited/replaced at equal length (e.g. compaction or re-render replacing a same-length line).
findings.fix: Key on the full text (string compare is cheap relative to a render) or on a cheap full-content hash, not a truncated prefix. Since LatestBlock is small and only the latest line is cached per workspace, full-text keying has no meaningful cost.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: two same-(who,length,prefix) but tail-differing texts dispatched in sequence must yield two different rendered previews.

findings.title: ElisionEvent interface is the only MoxxyEvent member not re-exported from the SDK barrel
findings.problem: events.ts defines and includes ElisionEvent in the MoxxyEvent union (events.ts:195, 275), but index.ts re-exports every sibling event interface (UserPromptEvent ... PluginEvent) EXCEPT ElisionEvent. A consumer that wants to narrow/annotate an elision event by its named type cannot import it and must fall back to `MoxxyEventOfType<'elision'>`. Inconsistent public surface for one protocol event.
findings.fix: Add `ElisionEvent` to the `export type { ... } from './events.js'` block in index.ts.
findings.testStrategy: No runtime test needed; a type-only re-export. Could add to types.test-d.ts importing ElisionEvent from the package root to lock it in.

findings.title: buildBuiltinsCore reads UNRESOLVED rawConfig; ${vault:..} placeholders in those fields won't resolve
findings.problem: setup.ts computes a placeholder-resolved `config` (resolveConfigPlaceholders) and passes it to registerPlugins/config plugin, but passes the UNRESOLVED `rawConfig` to buildBuiltinsCore. Fields read straight off rawConfig — security.isolator/perTool/perPlugin, plugins['@moxxy/plugin-self-update'].options.{repoUrl,maxTxnRetained,allowCoreUpdate}, channels.web.tunnel — therefore see raw `${vault:NAME}` strings if a user used a placeholder there. The injected vault makes per-plugin secret resolution work, but these scalar config fields are silently un-substituted, deviating from the resolved-config contract every other consumer relies on.
findings.fix: Pass the resolved `config` into buildBuiltinsCore (rename the field), or document explicitly that these fields are placeholder-exempt. Most likely the resolved config should be used for parity with registerPlugins/config-applier.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: build config with `security.isolator: '${vault:ISO}'` (or web.tunnel placeholder), open a vault with that key, run setupSessionWithConfig, assert the builtin sees the resolved value.

findings.title: Duplicated HTML-escape helpers across format.ts, render.ts (and ad-hoc unescape in html.ts)
findings.problem: escapeHtml is reimplemented identically in format.ts (L94-96) and render.ts (`escape`, L228-230); escapeAttr lives only in format.ts; html.ts hand-rolls the inverse unescape in stripHtml. Three copies of the same escape/unescape table drift independently (note format's escapeHtml does NOT escape `"` while escapeAttr does — easy to mis-pick). This is exactly the kind of shared helper that should be centralized; an `@moxxy/sdk` or a single local `html-escape.ts` would erase the duplication.
findings.fix: Extract a single `htmlEscape`/`attrEscape`/`htmlUnescape` module within the plugin (or reuse an sdk helper if one exists) and import it from format.ts, render.ts, and html.ts.
findings.testStrategy: Existing format/render tests should pass unchanged after centralizing; add one test asserting escape and unescape round-trip on `< > & "`.

findings.title: getInstallHint hardcodes apt for all Linux, silently wrong on Fedora/Arch/etc.
findings.problem: On linux the helper always returns `sudo apt install <pkg>`, regardless of the actual distro/package manager (dnf, pacman, zypper, apk). Users on non-Debian distros are shown a command that simply doesn't exist. perPlatform only keys on NodeJS.Platform ('linux'), so a caller cannot disambiguate by package manager either.
findings.fix: Either (a) detect the package manager (probe for dnf/pacman/zypper/apk on PATH) and pick the matching command, or (b) for linux return a manager-neutral hint ('install <pkg> with your distro package manager (apt/dnf/pacman/...)') matching the existing 'manual' fallback tone. Option (b) is the low-risk minimum.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test getInstallHint('ffmpeg','linux') asserting the new neutral/probed output; existing darwin/win32 cases unchanged.

findings.title: CommandRegistry.register does not detect a primary name colliding with an existing alias
findings.problem: register() guards a new alias against both commands.has(alias) and aliases.has(alias), but when registering a new command's PRIMARY name it only checks commands.has(name), not aliases.has(name). A command whose name equals an alias already claimed by another command registers silently; get(name) then returns the direct command (direct lookup wins over alias), shadowing the alias with no error — the opposite direction of the guard the code clearly intends to enforce.
findings.fix: In register(), extend the duplicate guard: `if (this.commands.has(cmd.name) || this.aliases.has(cmd.name)) throw new Error(...)`. (replace() may keep upsert semantics.)
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: register cmd A with alias 'x'; then register cmd named 'x' and assert it throws 'already in use'.

findings.title: Re-implements shared buildBrokerEnv helper + duplicates DEFAULT_ENV from plugin-security
findings.problem: plugin-security exports `buildBrokerEnv(caps, optsEnv)` and `BROKER_DEFAULT_ENV` specifically so 'every isolator that spawns a child curates env the same way' (broker.ts:300-321 comment). This isolator instead hand-rolls the identical curation loop (203-207) and redefines the same allowlist as a local `DEFAULT_ENV` constant (161). This is copy-paste of a shared @moxxy helper — the exact reinvention the consistency lens targets — and it means the two env lists can silently drift (e.g. if BROKER_DEFAULT_ENV gains a key, the subprocess child won't get it). It also ignores `caps`-driven precedence semantics that buildBrokerEnv centralizes.
findings.fix: Import `buildBrokerEnv` from '@moxxy/plugin-security' and replace lines 182,202-207 with `const env = buildBrokerEnv({ env: caps.env ?? opts.defaultEnvAllowlist }, undefined);` (or extend buildBrokerEnv to accept a fallback allowlist). Delete the local DEFAULT_ENV. Keeps the env contract single-sourced.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: spawn a handler that returns Object.keys(process.env); with caps.env=['PATH'] assert the child sees only PATH (+ Node-injected) and not an arbitrary secret env var set in the parent. Same fixture proves both this fix and u62-4.

findings.title: Orphaned clear() docblock sits above rebase() — doc/method mismatch
findings.problem: The large JSDoc block at lines 123-136 describes clear() ('Drop every event from the log... used by /new... onClear subscribers fire... truncates JSONL'), but the method that immediately follows it is rebase() (line 144), not clear(). A second docblock (137-143) for rebase() is wedged in between. The actual clear() is defined far below at line 154 with no doc. A reader (or doc tooling) attributes the clear() prose to rebase(), and clear() appears undocumented. Likely a refactor that moved rebase() in without relocating the comment.
findings.fix: Move the lines 123-136 docblock down to immediately above `clear(): void` (line 154); leave the lines 137-143 rebase docblock where it is.
findings.testStrategy: Doc-only move; existing log.test.ts clear/rebase cases remain green. Optionally a lint rule for misplaced JSDoc, but not warranted.

findings.title: Commands allowlist basename match accepts any path with a matching basename
findings.problem: When caps.commands=['echo'], the check `allowlist.includes(path.basename(command))` allows ANY command whose basename is 'echo' — including /attacker/controlled/echo or a cwd-relative ./echo — not just the trusted system binary. This is documented behavior in the SDK type, but it weakens the allowlist meaningfully (a tool with subprocess+commands can be tricked into running a planted binary of the same name). At minimum it deserves a hardening note / opt-in absolute-only mode.
findings.fix: Document the basename-match tradeoff at the call site; consider requiring absolute-path entries to match the exact resolved path, and treat basename entries as 'resolve via PATH and pin the resolved absolute path'. Or add a strict mode that only honors absolute-path allowlist entries.
findings.testStrategy: Test that allowlist ['echo'] denies '/tmp/evil/echo' under strict mode while still allowing the resolved system echo.

findings.title: Duplicated JSON.parse + _rawPartial fallback for tool args in two places
findings.problem: The exact 'parse entry.args as JSON, fall back to { _rawPartial: entry.args } on SyntaxError' block is copy-pasted in handleSseEvent (the function_call_arguments.done branch) and again in the consumer's truncated-stream flush loop. Two copies of the same fragile parse-with-fallback can drift (e.g. one gets a fix the other doesn't), and the second copy is precisely the path with no test (see u98-1).
findings.fix: Extract `function parseToolArgs(args: string): unknown` into stream-types.ts (or a small shared module) and call it from both sites.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test parseToolArgs directly: valid JSON object, empty string -> {}, malformed JSON -> { _rawPartial }.

findings.title: `dialog.showOpenDialog(window ?? null!, ...)` non-null-asserts a possibly-null window
findings.problem: Both native-picker handlers compute `window = getFocusedWindow() ?? getAllWindows()[0]` (which can be undefined when no window exists) and then pass `window ?? null!` to showOpenDialog. The `null!` asserts non-null on an intentionally-null value — a confusing, lint-defeating idiom. Electron's showOpenDialog overload accepts being called with no parent window, so the honest call is `showOpenDialog(opts)` (or `window ? showOpenDialog(window, opts) : showOpenDialog(opts)`), not a coerced `null!`.
findings.fix: Drop the `null!`: call the no-parent overload when window is absent — `window ? dialog.showOpenDialog(window, opts) : dialog.showOpenDialog(opts)`.
findings.testStrategy: Type-check passes without the assertion; manual: launch picker with all windows closed (e.g. via tray) and confirm it opens parentless rather than crashing.

findings.title: ask.respond response.mode enum is duplicated from SDK PermissionMode and can silently drift
findings.problem: The `ask.respond` schema hardcodes `z.enum(['allow', 'allow_session', 'allow_always', 'deny'])` for response.mode. This is a copy of @moxxy/sdk's `PermissionMode = 'allow' | 'allow_session' | 'allow_always' | 'deny'` (packages/sdk/src/permission.ts:3), which the contract already imports (index.ts:27). If the SDK adds/renames a verdict, the runtime gate here silently rejects the new value while the compile-time type accepts it — a security gate desyncing from its own type. AskResponse.mode is typed `PermissionMode` but the Zod literal list is unanchored to it.
findings.fix: Export a `PERMISSION_MODES` const tuple from @moxxy/sdk (e.g. `export const PERMISSION_MODES = ['allow','allow_session','allow_always','deny'] as const; export type PermissionMode = typeof PERMISSION_MODES[number];`) and build the Zod enum from it (`z.enum(PERMISSION_MODES)`), so type and runtime gate share one source.
findings.testStrategy: Add a type-level test (`satisfies readonly PermissionMode[]`) plus a unit test asserting the schema accepts exactly the SDK's mode set; both fail if the SDK list changes without updating the gate.

findings.title: Defensive `events.findLast?.()` optional-chaining hides a silent empty-assistant path
findings.problem: `events.findLast?.(...)` optional-chains a method that is guaranteed present on Node >=20.10 (repo engines field). The guard is dead under the supported runtime, and if it ever WERE undefined, finalAssistant becomes undefined and the handler silently returns assistant:'' (a 200 with empty content) instead of surfacing an error — a confusing failure mode for callers.
findings.fix: Drop the optional chaining: `events.findLast((e) => e.type === 'assistant_message')`. The findLast?.()+ternary type-narrowing can collapse to a single typed lookup.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: removing `?.` must still typecheck; existing router/integration tests cover the assistant extraction.

findings.title: Local include re-lists src/**/*.test.ts; mergeConfig concatenates, leaving a duplicate glob
findings.problem: The preset (@moxxy/vitest-preset) already sets include = ['src/**/*.test.ts', 'test/**/*.test.ts']. Vitest's mergeConfig concatenates arrays rather than replacing them, so the effective include becomes ['src/**/*.test.ts','test/**/*.test.ts','src/**/*.test.ts','src/**/*.test.tsx']. The duplicate 'src/**/*.test.ts' is harmless (glob results are de-duplicated by path), but the override is misleading: it reads as if it replaces the preset list when it actually appends. The only NET addition needed is the .test.tsx pattern.
findings.fix: Set test.include to just ['src/**/*.test.tsx'] (relying on mergeConfig append) OR keep the explicit list but drop the redundant 'src/**/*.test.ts'. Better: add '.test.tsx' to the shared preset's include + coverage.exclude so every package picks it up and this override can be deleted entirely.
findings.testStrategy: Run `pnpm --filter @moxxy/client-core test` and confirm all 9 suites still collect; assert vitest's resolved config has no behavioral change (no missing suites).

clusters.title: Long-tail: minor perf nitpicks
clusters.oneLine: Low-severity perf observations (small redundant work, minor allocations) below the threshold of the targeted perf clusters. (41 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Marginal wins; bundle opportunistically with nearby work.

findings.title: mergeUsageStats reads the whole usage file under the mutex even for an empty delta
findings.problem: When `delta` has no keys, the function still enters the mutex's critical section and performs `await loadUsageStats(filePath)` (a disk read + JSON.parse of the entire aggregate) before returning `current`. The early-out check `if (keys.length === 0) return current;` is placed AFTER the load, so the empty-delta fast path does pointless I/O and serializes against concurrent merges. `@moxxy/plugin-usage-stats` calls this on every shutdown, and a session with no recorded usage produces an empty delta.
findings.fix: Move the `if (Object.keys(delta).length === 0) return loadUsageStats(filePath)` (or return early without reading if a caller doesn't need the file) before acquiring/inside-but-before-reading; better: short-circuit before entering `mergeMutex.run` entirely when the delta is empty, since no write occurs and no read-modify-write ordering is needed.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on fs.readFile; call mergeUsageStats({}) and assert readFile is not invoked (or invoked outside the mutex). Existing 'ignores empty deltas' test already asserts the result equality; extend it to assert no I/O.
findings.complexity: n/a (removes one disk read + parse on the empty-delta shutdown path)

findings.title: withSessionTitles re-reads every auto-named session's .meta.json on every list call (no cache)
findings.problem: withSessionTitles / withSessionTitlesOverview run readFirstPrompt() per auto-named session on EVERY sessions.list / desks.list — one async readFile + JSON.parse per session. This is the sidebar render hot path; it re-does identical disk I/O on every poll/refresh with zero memoization, scaling O(sessions) file reads per list even though sidecars are immutable for a given session log (only change when the first prompt is written once).
findings.fix: Add a tiny per-process LRU/Map cache keyed by `${sessionId}` -> { mtimeMs, title }; stat the sidecar (cheap) and only re-read+parse when mtime changed. Or cache the derived title until an invalidation signal (session.info.changed / first user_prompt) arrives. Bound the cache by session count.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: call withSessionTitles twice against the same dir with a spy on fs.readFile; assert the second call performs no (or only stat-level) reads when the sidecar mtime is unchanged.
findings.complexity: O(sessions) disk reads per list -> O(changed) reads per list

findings.title: index.load() re-reads + re-parses .embeddings.json from disk on every recall
findings.problem: Inside the mutex, recallVector always calls `await index.load()` before lookups. EmbeddingIndex.load() reads the whole .embeddings.json, JSON.parses it, and merges every entry into the in-memory Map — on EVERY recall, even back-to-back recalls in the same process where the in-memory cache is already authoritative (the store is the only writer, per the documented single-writer assumption). For 500 cached vectors this re-parses a large JSON blob and re-inserts 500 Map entries per query. The merge is idempotent (keyed by name) so it is not a correctness bug, only wasted I/O+parse on the hot path.
findings.fix: Make EmbeddingIndex.load() load-once (guard with a `loaded` flag, reset only on external-change detection) given the single-writer invariant the module already relies on; or have recallVector load only on first use per store instance. Keep an explicit reload path for forget()'s rebuild if needed. Note: embedding-cache.ts is outside this unit, so the minimal in-unit change is to gate the load() call in recallVector on a first-time flag passed in.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on fs.readFile for .embeddings.json across two recalls in one store and assert it is read once; verify ranking unchanged.
findings.complexity: O(cacheSize) JSON parse + Map rebuild per recall -> O(1) after first load

findings.title: recall() re-reads + re-parses every memory file from disk on every call, ignoring the in-memory row cache
findings.problem: recall() calls this.list() -> listEntries() (io.ts:30-59) which readdirs the dir and fs.readFile + parseMdFile + zod-parses EVERY .md file on each recall. The store already maintains an in-memory indexRows cache (store.ts:87) for MEMORY.md, but recall bypasses it (indexRows lacks bodies, which recall needs). This is O(N) disk reads + N markdown parses + N zod parses per recall, repeated identically across back-to-back recalls in a session.
findings.fix: Extend the in-process entry cache to optionally hold bodies (or add a separate full-entry cache invalidated by writeEntry/forget), so repeated recalls within one process reuse parsed entries. Given the documented single-writer assumption this is safe and turns recall's read cost into O(1) after warmup.
