findings.testStrategy: Spy fs.readFile; assert recall N times after one save reads each file at most once total; assert ranking unchanged.
findings.complexity: O(N disk reads + N parses) per recall -> O(1) after warmup

findings.title: NDJSON buffer uses O(n) slice per frame; pathological under many small frames in one chunk
findings.problem: onData repeatedly does `this.buffer = this.buffer.slice(newline + 1)` once per frame found. For a single 'data' chunk containing k frames of total length L, this is O(k*L) string reallocation (each slice copies the remaining buffer). On the runner's hot event-broadcast path large replays/bursts arrive as one chunk, so this is quadratic in the worst case. JSON.stringify+'\n' on send is fine; the read side is the cost.
findings.fix: Track a cursor index into buffer and only slice the unconsumed remainder once after the loop (or split on '\n' keeping the trailing partial). Drops per-chunk cost from O(k*L) to O(L).
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark/assert: feed one chunk of 10k small frames and assert all parsed; micro-benchmark to confirm linear scaling. Existing ordering/split tests guard correctness.

findings.title: Scratch bump-allocator never reclaims within a call — N broker ops grow memory O(sum of all result sizes)
findings.problem: reserveScratch only ever advances; it never frees a region after the wasm side has consumed the previous result. Within a single invocation a handler that makes many broker calls (e.g. reads 1000 files, or one large readdir then many reads) forces linear-memory growth proportional to the SUM of every broker result it has ever received this call, even though each prior result is dead the moment the next op is issued. memory.grow() is irreversible, so peak memory is the cumulative total, not the max single result.
findings.fix: Reset the per-memory scratch offset to SCRATCH_BASE after each brokered op returns (the wasm side has already copied/consumed the (ptr,len) result before issuing the next import call, since imports are synchronous), or hand scratch out of the module's allocator (see u63-2) so its GC/free logic applies. Resetting to SCRATCH_BASE per op makes peak scratch = max single result instead of the sum.
findings.testStrategy: Drive buildWasmHostImports with many sequential broker_fs_stat calls returning large results and assert memory.buffer.byteLength stays bounded by ~one result size rather than growing per call.

findings.title: fileExists reads entire file content instead of stat/access
findings.problem: fileExists() does `await readFile(p)` purely to test existence, loading the whole file into memory and decoding nothing useful. For the plist/unit files this is tiny, but it is an unnecessary full read on a hot-ish path (called by getStatus/start/stop/uninstall on every service op) and is semantically wrong (a permission-denied-but-exists file reports false).
findings.fix: Use `fs.access(p, fs.constants.F_OK)` (or `stat`) and return true/false on resolve/reject — no content read.
findings.testStrategy: Existing service status tests still pass; add a case asserting existence is reported for a zero-byte and a large file without reading content.

findings.title: Read slurps + splits the entire file even when only a small line window is requested
findings.problem: readHandler reads the whole file (`fs.readFile` / brokered readFile), then `text.split('\n')` allocates an array of ALL lines, then `lines.slice(offset, offset+limit)`. For a multi-hundred-MB file with the default limit of 2000 lines, it still materializes the full content string and full line array just to keep 2000 lines. There is no file-size guard. Same memory-amplification class as grep but for a single file.
findings.fix: For the non-brokered path, stream with readline and stop after offset+limit lines (avoids splitting the whole file); and/or stat-guard very large files to a clear error suggesting offset/limit. Brokered path can keep current behavior or gain the same cap.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test reading offset/limit from a large fixture and asserting only the window is returned; (optional) assert a clear error for an oversized file.
findings.complexity: O(filesize) memory -> O(window) for large files

findings.title: Native wheel listener detached/reattached on every pan and zoom
findings.problem: The non-passive `wheel` useEffect lists `[applyZoom, setView, view]` in deps. `view` (the viewport) changes on every wheel/pan/zoom event, so the effect tears down and re-adds the DOM event listener on every single wheel tick. Functionally correct (it reads fresh `view`) but does needless removeEventListener/addEventListener churn on a hot input path.
findings.fix: Hold the latest viewport in a ref (viewRef.current = view each render) and register the wheel listener once with `[]` deps, reading viewRef inside onWheel. Same pattern applies to keep applyZoom/setView stable.
findings.testStrategy: Assert addEventListener('wheel') is called once across multiple simulated wheel events.

findings.title: Always-on cache-bust query on dynamic import leaks ESM module entries on repeated reload
findings.problem: For JS plugin entries the loader always appends `?v=<bust>` where bust defaults to `${Date.now()}-${Math.random()}` because no caller supplies cacheBust. Each `import(url)` with a unique URL creates a permanently-retained ESM registry entry. On every disable->enable toggle (reload -> discoverAndLoad re-imports the re-wanted plugin via loader.load) the same JS plugin is re-imported under a fresh URL, growing the registry across process lifetime.
findings.fix: Default to NO query string (stable URL -> registry dedupes); only supply a real cacheBust from the host on an actual reload of a changed module.
findings.testStrategy: Reload N times for an unchanged plugin and assert the dynamic-import URL is stable (spy on import).

findings.title: DiffView re-splits + re-styles the entire diff on every render with index keys
findings.problem: DiffView does `diff.split('\n')` and maps each line through lineStyle() on every render, keyed by array index. For a large git diff this rebuilds the whole <div> list each render; index keys defeat React reconciliation (any insertion reflows every subsequent node) and lineStyle re-evaluates startsWith checks for every line. It is rendered inside FileViewer, which re-renders on loading/error/body state transitions, so the diff is re-folded repeatedly.
findings.fix: Memoize the parsed+styled rows with useMemo keyed on `diff`, and prefer a stable key (e.g. `${i}:${line.slice(0,8)}` is still index-based but better to precompute the row objects once). Since the diff is static per selection, computing once is sufficient.
findings.testStrategy: Render DiffView, trigger an unrelated parent re-render, and assert (via a spy on lineStyle or React profiler) the styling work is not repeated.
findings.complexity: re-render: O(n) per render -> O(n) once per diff

findings.title: attach() allocates a fresh closure per registered command for every connection
findings.problem: For each accepted transport, `attach` iterates the full `methods` map and registers a new per-peer arrow closure for every command. With M registered commands and P connections over the server's life this is M*P closure allocations, and each closure re-captures `this.allowedCommands` and re-checks membership on every call. The allow-list membership check (`this.allowedCommands.has(channel)`) is constant-time per call, but the closure-per-command-per-connection setup is avoidable: the handler body is identical for all commands except the captured `channel`/`fn`.
findings.fix: Low priority given M and P are both small. If desired, register a single peer-level dispatcher keyed by method name that closes over `this` once, or have JsonRpcPeer support a fallback handler, so attach does O(1) wiring instead of O(M). Acceptable to leave as-is; flagged for the redesign's atomicity pass.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (behavioral parity covered by existing round-trip tests); micro-benchmark not warranted.
findings.complexity: O(M) per connection -> O(1) per connection

findings.title: Transcript messages array grows unbounded for the life of the surface session
findings.problem: Every inbound 'message' and 'file-diff' frame, and every sendPrompt, appends to the messages state array (setMessages(prev => [...prev, ...])) and it is never trimmed or capped. A long-lived web-surface session that the agent keeps re-rendering (the documented use case: iteratively refining the view over many turns) accumulates the entire transcript in memory and re-renders the whole list each frame. Combined with array-index React keys (chat.tsx) the whole list reconciles on every append.
findings.fix: Cap the transcript (e.g. keep last N=500 with a 'history trimmed' marker) when appending, and key messages by a stable per-message id (assign an incrementing id or use frame turnId+seq) instead of array index. Optionally virtualize the chat list.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test a pure appendCapped(messages, msg, max) reducer to assert it never exceeds max and preserves the newest entries; assert ordering stability.

findings.title: session.info fetched per-component (component-local state), not shared/deduped
findings.problem: The context window comes from a `session.info` IPC fetch stored in component-local useState, keyed on [workspaceId, model]. Every component instance that mounts useContextUsage fires its own round-trip, and a provider/model switch refetches in each. Other consumers (ContextMeter, AgentPicker, CommandPalette) independently invoke `session.info` too. The same descriptor is re-fetched N times instead of being read from a shared store like chatStore/connectionStore, while the runner already pushes `session.info.changed` (useSessionInfoBridge) which this hook ignores.
findings.fix: Hoist session.info into a shared module-level store keyed by workspace (mirroring connectionStore) primed once and invalidated on the SESSION_INFO_REFRESH_EVENT the bridge already emits; have useContextUsage/agent picker/command palette read from it via useSyncExternalStore.
findings.testStrategy: Mount two ContextMeter instances for the same workspace under a counting fake transport; assert session.info is invoked once.

findings.title: Abandoned open/verified transactions (each holding a full plugin snapshot) are never GC'd
findings.problem: gcTransactions only deletes terminal txns (committed/rolled_back/escalated) and runs only on self_update_apply. An 'open' txn created by self_update_begin recursively copies the entire target plugin dir into <txn>/before. If the model calls begin and then never verifies/applies/rolls back (a common abandonment), the snapshot directory is stranded forever — unbounded disk growth under ~/.moxxy/self-update/txns. Core txns have the analogous issue (listCoreTxns never prunes), compounded by the cloned repo + dist snapshots.
findings.fix: In gcTransactions also evict 'open'/'verified' txns older than a TTL (e.g. createdAt > N hours), or sweep stale open txns at finalizeStagedCoreUpdate/boot. Keep the most-recent active txn to allow resume.
findings.testStrategy: Test gcTransactions deletes an aged 'open' txn while preserving a recent one; assert the before/ snapshot dir is removed.

findings.title: AgentsPanel re-folds entire event log + rebuilds/sorts timeline on every render
findings.problem: collectAgents(events) walks the full session event log, and the flat-row build + rows.sort run unmemoized on EVERY render of the modal. The panel is mounted while live agents stream, so events grows and the modal re-renders on each event tick AND each scroll keypress (useScrollableList state change) — each of those re-walks O(events) and re-sorts O(rows log rows). Sister panels (ChatView) memoize the equivalent fold on the events reference; this one does not.
findings.fix: Wrap `collectAgents(events)` and the rows build+sort in useMemo keyed on [events]. The cursor/scroll state then no longer triggers a re-fold; only a genuinely new events array does.
findings.testStrategy: Render AgentsPanel with a fixed events array, spy/instrument collectAgents (or wrap with a call counter), fire several scroll inputs, assert collectAgents invoked once.
findings.complexity: per-keystroke O(n+r log r) -> O(1) when events unchanged

findings.title: System-prompt+skill-catalog string rebuilt on every loop iteration
findings.problem: buildMessages() is called each iteration and re-runs buildSystemPromptWithSkills(ctx.systemPrompt, ctx.skills.list()), which maps over the full skill list and concatenates the catalog string from scratch every time (mode-helpers.ts:54-77). The skill catalog and base system prompt are stable for the duration of a turn, so this is O(skills) string work repeated per provider round. For a long agentic turn (dozens of iterations) with a large skill set this is avoidable recomputation; it also feeds the cache-stable prefix, so any nondeterminism in skills.list() ordering would silently defeat the prompt cache (invariant 7).
findings.fix: Hoist `const systemPrompt = buildSystemPromptWithSkills(ctx.systemPrompt, ctx.skills.list())` to before the loop and pass it into a thinner per-iteration projectMessages call (projection over the log still must run each iteration; only the skill-prompt composition is loop-invariant). Confirm skills.list() returns a deterministic order. NOTE: a mid-turn reload_skills (SkillRegistry.replaceAll) would no longer be reflected until the next turn — acceptable but worth noting; load_skill does not change the catalog so is unaffected.
findings.testStrategy: Spy on buildSystemPromptWithSkills (or skills.list) and assert it is invoked once per turn rather than once per iteration in a multi-iteration tool-loop test.
findings.complexity: O(iterations*skills) -> O(skills) for the prompt composition

findings.title: Regexes recompiled on every check() call (inputMatches + glob name patterns)
findings.problem: check() runs on every tool call (hot path: session.ts wrapWithPolicy:467 invokes engine.check per PendingToolCall). matchRule recompiles `new RegExp(v)` for each inputMatches entry, and nameMatches recompiles the glob `new RegExp('^'+...+'$')` for every rule, on every call. Policies are typically small so the absolute cost is low, but this is avoidable repeated work — the rule set is immutable between mutations, so compiled regexes could be cached.
findings.fix: Compile rule name-globs and inputMatches regexes once when policy is set (constructor / load / each mutator) into a derived structure (e.g. CompiledRule[] for allow and deny) and have check() consult the precompiled forms. Invalid patterns get resolved at compile time, which also fixes u40-1's fail-open question.
findings.testStrategy: Behavioral tests unchanged (existing suite); add a test that a policy with a glob name + inputMatches still matches after addAllow to ensure recompilation on mutation.
findings.complexity: per-call O(rules*patterns) RegExp constructions -> O(rules*patterns) once per mutation

findings.title: scoreEntry counts token matches via haystack.split(t) — allocates a full array per token
findings.problem: For each query token, scoreEntry computes match count as `haystack.split(t).length - 1`, which builds an entire intermediate array of substrings just to count occurrences. Over Q tokens and a body of length L this is O(Q*L) work plus O(Q) large array allocations and GC churn. Called once per entry in rankByKeywords (O(N) entries), so a multi-word query over many entries does N*Q full string splits. Also fragile: empty-string or regex-special tokens behave oddly through split (here tokens come from tokenize so they're [a-z0-9_-], so correctness is fine — only the allocation cost matters).
findings.fix: Count occurrences with an indexOf loop (`let n=0,i=0; while((i=haystack.indexOf(t,i))!==-1){n++;i+=t.length;}`) — no array allocation, same result. Or precompute a token-frequency Map per entry once if rankByKeywords is called repeatedly with different queries (not the case today).
findings.testStrategy: store.test.ts:100 'recall ranks by token frequency' already pins the scoring outcome; refactor must keep those rankings identical.
findings.complexity: O(Q*L) with per-token array alloc -> O(Q*L) with zero alloc (constant-factor + GC win)

findings.title: views Map grows unbounded across distinct view names and is fully replayed to every new client
findings.problem: this.views is keyed by `frame.name ?? frame.viewId`. Re-renders of the same named view replace in place (good), but every distinct view name (or every un-named view, keyed by ever-incrementing viewId) accumulates forever. On each new browser connection onConnection replays the ENTIRE map (L433), so connect cost grows O(total distinct views) and memory grows unbounded in a long agent session that builds many different screens.
findings.fix: Bound the replay set: keep only the latest view (the surface is single-view; `replaces` already swaps in place client-side), or cap views to the last N and evict oldest. For the single-active-view model, storing just the most recent view frame suffices for replay.
findings.testStrategy: Channel test: emit many distinct named present_view results, then connect a new ws; assert it receives only the bounded/latest set rather than all historical views.

findings.title: resolveValue resolves object keys sequentially while arrays use Promise.all
findings.problem: Array elements are resolved concurrently via Promise.all, but object properties are resolved one-at-a-time in a `for…await` loop. Each leaf resolveString → vault.get() awaits open()+syncFromDisk() (a stat, sometimes a full read+parse), so a config object with many vault placeholders serializes all those awaits where the array branch parallelizes them. Inconsistent and slower; the asymmetry has no justification.
findings.fix: Mirror the array branch: build entries with Promise.all over Object.entries, e.g. `const pairs = await Promise.all(Object.entries(obj).map(async ([k, v]) => [k, await resolveValue(v, vault)] as const)); return Object.fromEntries(pairs);`. Keeps key order and parallelizes.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: object with 3 placeholders backed by a vault whose get() is instrumented with a counter and a small delay; assert wall-clock < sum of delays (i.e. overlapped) and values correct. placeholder.test.ts already exercises resolveValue and can be extended.
findings.complexity: sequential O(n) awaits -> O(1) await depth (parallel)

findings.title: readStoredCreds issues 10 sequential awaited vault.get calls instead of parallel
findings.problem: readStoredCreds reads access_token, refresh_token, expires_at, scope, token_type, id_token, client_id, client_secret, token_url, extras as ten serial awaits. Each is an independent vault read; on a per-request hot path (ensureFreshTokens runs before every upstream LLM call) this serializes ~10 round-trips. Only access_token gates an early return; the rest are independent and can run concurrently.
findings.fix: After the access_token check, Promise.all the remaining nine vault.get calls. (Vault reads are async I/O; concurrency is safe — they're distinct keys.)
findings.testStrategy: No behavior change; existing readStoredCreds tests cover correctness. Optionally assert with a counting fake vault that all keys are still read.

findings.title: ApprovalDialog re-splits + re-colorizes the full body on every render (incl. each scroll/keystroke)
findings.problem: request.body.split('\n'), isDiffBody (two regex scans of the whole body), and renderDiffLines (per-line regex classification of every line) run at component-body top level. The dialog re-renders on every scroll keypress and every text-entry keystroke, so the entire (potentially large diff) body is re-parsed and re-colorized each time even though body is immutable for the dialog's lifetime.
findings.fix: useMemo the `rendered` array on [request.body]. Only the offset slice (visible) depends on scroll state and should stay outside the memo.
findings.testStrategy: Memoize and assert via instrumented renderDiffLines that it runs once across multiple key events on a fixed request.

findings.title: Whole-buffer regex CRLF normalization re-runs on every chunk
findings.problem:
`buffer = buffer.replace(/
/g, '
')` runs over the entire accumulated `buffer` on every reader.read() iteration. After complete frames are sliced off the buffer is small (one partial frame), so this is bounded by max-frame-size rather than total-stream-size — but it is still redundant: each freshly-appended chunk is the only part that can contain new CRLFs, yet the whole residual is rescanned every loop. Cheap in the common case, wasteful for large tool-argument frames that span many chunks before the 

 separator appears.
findings.fix:
Normalize only the newly decoded chunk before appending (`buffer += decoder.decode(value,{stream:true}).replace(/
/g,'
')`), or split frames on /?
?
/ and lines on /?
/ and drop the global replace entirely. Both make it strictly per-chunk O(n) work.
findings.testStrategy: Add a fixture where a CRLF lands exactly on a chunk boundary (chunk ends with '\r', next starts '\n') and assert frames still parse correctly under the new normalization.
findings.complexity: O(residual-buffer) per chunk -> O(new-chunk) per chunk

findings.title: Intl.DateTimeFormat allocated per cursor step inside the minute-walk for explicit zones
findings.problem: `decomposeInZone` constructs a fresh `Intl.DateTimeFormat` on every loop iteration when a non-local `timeZone` is supplied. For a sparse cron in an explicit zone the walk can iterate many times (each a formatter allocation + formatToParts). The code even acknowledges this ('Slower (allocates a formatter)'). Since the timeZone is fixed for the whole `nextFireTime` call, the formatter can be built once and reused.
findings.fix: Hoist the formatter: build it once in nextFireTime when timeZone is an explicit zone and pass it (or a memoized closure) into decomposeInZone. Module-level Map<timeZone, Intl.DateTimeFormat> cache also works and is safe (formatters are stateless).
findings.testStrategy: Micro: no behavior change; covered by the new timeZone correctness tests. Optionally spy on Intl.DateTimeFormat constructor count.

findings.title: broadcast() re-serializes the identical payload once per peer
findings.problem: `broadcast` loops over peers and calls `peer.notify(channel, payload)`, and each `notify` -> `transport.send` does its own `JSON.stringify(frame)` of the SAME notification frame (channel + payload identical for all peers). For P peers this is P stringifies of one identical object. Bounded today by the default 8-connection cap, but the high-frequency event stream (frame pumps, streaming reasoning) fans out here per event, so it is O(P) redundant serialization on the hot path.
findings.fix: Stringify the notification frame once and send the prebuilt string to each open peer (e.g. expose a `sendRaw`/`notifyRaw` on the transport/peer, or have broadcast build the JSON string once and pass it down). The ws `send` accepts a string directly, so a single stringify can be reused. Keep behavior identical (one frame per peer).
findings.testStrategy: Spy on JSON.stringify (or a transport.sendRaw) and assert one serialization per broadcast regardless of peer count; assert each peer still receives one identical frame.
findings.complexity: O(P) stringify -> O(1) stringify per broadcast

findings.title: pcm16Peak constructs a DataView and does per-sample getInt16 instead of a direct Int16Array view
findings.problem: pcm16Peak iterates byte-by-byte (i += 2) through a DataView calling getInt16 per sample. For a multi-second 24kHz clip this is ~hundreds of thousands of bound-checked DataView calls. When the buffer is 2-byte aligned (it always is here — produced by audioToPcm16 with byteOffset from an Int16Array), a single `new Int16Array(bytes.buffer, bytes.byteOffset, bytes.byteLength/2)` loop is materially faster and simpler. The DataView is only needed for unaligned/odd-length safety.
findings.fix: Fast-path: if `bytes.byteOffset % 2 === 0`, iterate over `new Int16Array(bytes.buffer, bytes.byteOffset, bytes.byteLength >> 1)` with Math.abs; fall back to the DataView loop for the rare unaligned/odd case. Note this is LE-only — keep the DataView path if big-endian support is ever needed.
findings.testStrategy: Existing pcm16Peak tests already cover correctness; add an aligned vs odd-length-buffer case to lock both paths.

findings.title: process.stdout.columns read inside per-row render (ToolsPanel ToolRow) instead of once
findings.problem: ToolsPanel computes `const termWidth = process.stdout.columns` and `descWidth` INSIDE ToolRow (line 119-120), so it re-reads the tty width and recomputes for every visible row on every render (up to WINDOW rows). SkillsPanel/WorkflowsPanel correctly read it once at the panel level. Inconsistent with the sibling panels.
findings.fix: Hoist termWidth/descWidth to the ToolsPanel body (like SkillsPanel/WorkflowsPanel) and pass descWidth as a prop to ToolRow.
findings.testStrategy: Pure refactor; existing render snapshot at fixed COLUMNS env should be unchanged.

findings.title: `moxxy <channel>` boots/registers plugins up to three times (channels probe → run-channel probe → real boot)
findings.problem: Invoking `moxxy channels <name>` to actually run a channel performs: (1) a probeSession in runChannelsCommand (registers all plugins, then closes), (2) returns 'run-channel' → runChannelByName, which does ANOTHER probeSession (registers all plugins again, then closes), and (3) finally startRegisteredChannel boots the real session (third plugin registration). Plugin registration includes discovery I/O and dynamic imports; doing it three times for a single launch is wasteful. The double-probe is acknowledged in comments as 'cheap', but plugin discovery/import is not free on cold start.
findings.fix: Have runChannelsCommand pass its already-resolved channel def / setup decision down to runChannelByName rather than re-probing, or skip the channels.ts probe entirely for the run path and let runChannelByName own the single probe. At minimum, thread the registered registry from the first probe so plugins aren't rediscovered.
findings.testStrategy: Spy/count register-plugins invocations across a simulated `moxxy <channel>` launch; assert it runs once for the run path.

findings.title: Chat re-maps the entire event log on every keystroke (no memo)
findings.problem: The `lines` projection runs `events.map(renderLine).filter(...)` directly in the Chat render body. `draft` is component-local useState, so every character typed into the composer re-renders Chat and re-folds the whole event array O(n). As a session grows this becomes O(n) work per keystroke on the RN UI thread for data that has not changed.
findings.fix: Wrap the `lines` derivation in `useMemo(() => ..., [events])`, or split the composer (draft state) into its own child component so typing does not re-render the transcript projection.
findings.testStrategy: Render Chat with a large `events` fixture, type into the composer, assert renderLine is not re-invoked (spy) when only `draft` changes.
findings.complexity: O(n) per keystroke -> O(n) only when events change

findings.title: authorizeAttachments realpaths attachments sequentially in an await-in-loop
findings.problem: authorizeAttachments resolves each attachment's realpath one at a time inside a for-await loop, serialising independent fs.realpath calls. Attachment counts are small so impact is minor, but it is inconsistent with the Promise.all used a few lines above for the cwd roots.
findings.fix: Resolve all attachment realpaths via Promise.all, then partition into authorized/dropped synchronously.
findings.testStrategy: Existing attachment-authz.test.ts already covers correctness; add a many-file case to confirm behaviour unchanged.

findings.title: detectGoalTerminal copies the whole event log every productive iteration → O(n^2) per run
findings.problem: Goal mode runs up to GOAL_MAX_ITERATIONS=150 autonomous iterations. On every iteration that executes tools, goal-loop calls `detectGoalTerminal(ctx.log.slice(), toolUses)`. `ctx.log.slice()` copies the ENTIRE append-only event log (which only grows across the run) BEFORE the function early-returns null for batches with no goal tools. Across a long run the log can reach thousands of events; copying it ~150 times is quadratic work (O(n) copy × O(iterations)) for a check that, in the overwhelmingly common case (batch has no goal_complete/goal_abandon), needs no log at all, and otherwise needs only the tail.
findings.fix: Short-circuit before copying: in goal-loop, only call detectGoalTerminal when `toolUses.some(t => t.name === GOAL_COMPLETE_TOOL || t.name === GOAL_ABANDON_TOOL)`. Better, change `detectGoalTerminal` to accept `ReadonlyArray<MoxxyEvent>` and pass `ctx.log` directly (it never mutates, only reads), removing the defensive `.slice()` entirely; the reverse-tail walk already bounds the scan near the end.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test detectGoalTerminal with a large synthetic log + a batch with no goal tools asserting it returns null without depending on a copy; add a goal-loop test (FakeProvider scripting many tool-only iterations) and assert detectGoalTerminal is only invoked on goal-tool batches (spy) — guards against reintroducing the unconditional slice.
findings.complexity: O(n) log-copy per iteration → O(1) (skip when no goal tool in batch; pass log by reference)

findings.title: appContext() clones the entire process.env on every appended event
findings.problem: The constructor subscribes `(event) => this.dispatcher.dispatchEvent(event, this.appContext())` to the EventLog, and `appContext()` builds `env: { ...process.env }` — a full shallow clone of the process environment. This runs once per appended event (every user_prompt, assistant_message, tool_call, provider_response, reasoning delta, etc.), so a single turn that streams hundreds of events allocates hundreds of full env clones. The clone happens even when zero plugins register an `onEvent` hook (dispatchEvent then iterates an empty entries list), so the work is pure waste in the common case.
findings.fix: Build the AppContext lazily or memoize the env snapshot. Options: (a) short-circuit the subscription when `this.dispatcher` has no onEvent hooks registered (expose a `hasEventHooks()` on the dispatcher); (b) compute `appContext()` once per dispatch and reuse, or memoize the `env` clone for the session lifetime (env is effectively stable for a process) and only re-snapshot on demand. Cheapest correct fix: cache one frozen env snapshot at construction and reuse it in appContext() unless a fresh read is explicitly needed.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: register a session with no onEvent hooks, spy on a getter that counts env-clone work (or assert appContext is not invoked per event after the short-circuit). Benchmark: append 10k events and assert allocation/time bound; verify onEvent hooks still receive a correct env-bearing AppContext when present.
findings.complexity: O(events * |env|) -> O(events) (or O(1) when no onEvent hooks)

findings.title: appendEvents calls stat() twice and re-stats after append just to refresh mtimeMs
findings.problem: On the index-extend path appendEvents stats the file before the append (to verify size) and again after (to refresh mtimeMs), adding two extra syscalls per append on top of appendFile. The post-append stat is purely to keep the mtime guard happy; it could be folded into a single stat or dropped in favour of invalidating the index by size only.
findings.fix: Once a per-file mutex serialises appends (u56-1), the pre-append size check is the only guard needed; track size as the authoritative cursor and drop the mtime double-stat (or fstat the open handle once if a handle is already held).
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark/append-loop test asserting stat call count; behavioural test that loadSegment still returns the just-appended page.

findings.title: Discovery reads every package.json strictly sequentially across up to 8 node_modules roots
findings.problem: discoverPlugins awaits readPluginManifest (one fs.readFile + JSON.parse) one package at a time (L34-39), and listPackageDirs awaits each scoped @-dir readdir sequentially (L65-69). The reads are independent and could be parallelized.
findings.fix: Map pkgsDirs to readPluginManifest promises and await with Promise.all / bounded concurrency (~32 to avoid fd exhaustion); parallelize scoped-dir readdir; apply the `seen` dedupe after resolution.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark discovery on a synthetic node_modules with N packages; assert identical results and reduced wall-time.

findings.title: AgentsPanel/UsagePanel re-fold the entire events array on every event during a streaming turn
findings.problem: When the agents or usage overlay is open, OverlayOrNotice passes the full `events` array straight to AgentsPanel/UsagePanel, which derive their view by walking the whole log. SessionView re-renders on every appended non-chunk event (and chunk-driven delta flushes ~30Hz), so an open usage/agents overlay re-walks O(n) events per render → O(n^2) across a turn. The panels are not memoized on a stable events identity here and receive a fresh `stream.events` reference each append.
findings.fix: Memoize the derived agent/usage aggregates (e.g. via an incremental fold keyed on events.length, mirroring estimateContextTokens' incremental cache) inside the panels, or compute the aggregate in SessionView with a high-water-mark fold and pass the result rather than raw events.
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark AgentsPanel render with a 5k-event log open during streaming; assert per-append work is bounded.

findings.title: readLastRun reads the entire latest run-record into memory to show a one-line summary
findings.problem: readLastRun reads the full JSONL file with `fs.readFile` and `JSON.parse`s every line just to extract the `run` header and step statuses. Run records can be large (every step's full output is in the `output` line at engine.ts:65). For `inspect` this parses and allocates the entire record including the (potentially huge) final output, which is never shown. Minor, but it scales with workflow output size, not summary size.
findings.fix: Skip lines whose `kind` is `output` before JSON.parse (cheap string startsWith check), or stream line-by-line and stop after the run + step lines. Low priority.
findings.testStrategy: n/a (behavior-preserving micro-opt); cover via the engine/command round-trip test in u118-5.

findings.title: Keyword scoring builds a fresh array per (entry, query-token) via String.split
findings.problem: scoreEntry counts token occurrences with `haystack.split(t).length - 1`, allocating a full array split of the entire concatenated haystack for EACH query token of EACH entry — O(entries * tokens * bodyLen) with per-call array allocation. For larger memory bodies and multi-word queries this is wasteful versus a non-allocating count.
findings.fix: Replace split-counting with an indexOf scan loop (or precompute haystack once per entry, which it already does, and count via indexOf). Cheap, no allocation. Scale is small today but this is on the keyword-recall hot path.
findings.testStrategy: Existing 'recall ranks by token frequency' test (store.test.ts:100) guards correctness; add a multi-occurrence case to lock the count.
findings.complexity: allocates O(bodyLen) per token -> O(1) extra per token

findings.title: readyProviders probe resolves credentials sequentially for every registered provider on every boot
findings.problem: After activation, the function awaits resolveProviderCredentials for each registered provider serially to compute the readyProviders set. With the ~8 built-in providers (plus runtime-registered ones) this is a sequential chain of vault/keychain/disk lookups on the boot hot path; OAuth providers (codex/claude-code) hit disk token reads (and claude-code may even refresh near expiry). Parallelizable since each probe is independent and read-only.
findings.fix: Replace the for-await with Promise.all over session.providers.list() (skipping the already-added active one), each settling independently; build the Set from the fulfilled results. Keeps determinism (Set membership is name-based).
findings.testStrategy: Benchmark boot with N stub providers whose resolve sleeps; assert total time ~max not ~sum. Functional test: readyProviders set identical before/after.
findings.complexity: O(n) sequential -> O(n) parallel (wall-clock ~n× faster on cold credential stores)

findings.title: cwdForWorkspace re-reads+parses desks.json on every IPC, and double-loads when workspaceId misses
findings.problem: `cwdForWorkspace` calls `desks.list()` (which is `(await this.load()).desks` — a fresh `readFile` + `JSON.parse` of desks.json from disk) on every invocation, and when the find misses it then calls `desks.getActive()` which performs a SECOND full `load()` (read+parse) of the same file. This function is the cwd resolver for `workspace.listDir`, `workspace.readFile`, AND all three git handlers (`git.isRepo`, `git.status`, `git.diff`). Opening the Files pane fires git.isRepo + git.status back-to-back, so a single pane render does 2-3 synchronous-ish disk reads + JSON.parse of desks.json that almost never changes. DeskStore has no read cache; the parse work is repeated per IPC.
findings.fix: Resolve the active desk in a single `load()` inside cwdForWorkspace (compute both the id/session match and the activeId fallback from one DeskDoc), or add a short-TTL in-memory cache to DeskStore.load() invalidated on save(). Minimal local fix: change cwdForWorkspace to call desks.load() once and derive both arms from the returned doc instead of list()+getActive().
findings.testStrategy: Unit test cwdForWorkspace with a spy DeskStore counting load() calls: assert exactly one load() per call regardless of whether the id matches a desk, a session, or falls back to active.

findings.title: gatherSignals scans the ENTIRE event log to build callNames, then uses only the lookback window
findings.problem: log.ofType('tool_call_requested') filters the full event log (EventLog.ofType is `this.events.filter(...)`, O(n) over all history) to build a callId->name map, but only callIds referenced by the last `lookback` (40) events are ever read. For a long session this is a full-history rescan on every classify call. Low impact because self_update_classify is rare and read-only, but it is exactly the 're-fold the whole log per query' anti-pattern.
findings.fix: Build callNames lazily from within the recent window only, or take a single slice and partition tool_call_requested vs tool_result inside that window (callIds in a 40-event window will have their request in or near that window; if a correlation can span further, widen the slice once rather than scanning all of history).
findings.testStrategy: Same gatherSignals test as u106-2, asserting correlation still works within the window; micro-benchmark on a synthetic 10k-event log to confirm constant-ish time.

clusters.title: Long-tail: residual dead code beyond the proven-unused-export cluster
clusters.oneLine: Remaining dead branches / unused params / dormant plumbing flagged per-unit that were not in the global one-ref export cluster (re-verify each against invariant #11 before deletion). (21 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Mostly safe deletions but each needs a dynamic-dispatch re-check; do alongside the owning package work.

findings.title: Package exports (generateThemeCss/RootCss, CSS_VAR_MAP, tokens) have no runtime consumer anywhere
findings.problem: Nothing in the repo imports @moxxy/design-tokens or its ./css-vars subpath at runtime. apps/desktop/package.json declares the dep (workspace:*) but no .ts/.tsx in apps/desktop or any package imports `tokens`, `darkTokens`, `generateThemeCss`, `generateRootCss`, `CSS_VAR_MAP`, or `DARK_CSS_VAR_MAP`. styles.css remains the desktop's source of truth, so the generator is dormant. This is documented-on-purpose plumbing (the file header says 'shipped now but not yet consumed... the parity test guards the mapping so a later switch is safe'), so it is NOT to be deleted — flagging only so the dormancy is tracked and the desktop's declared-but-unused dependency does not look like accidental drift.
findings.fix: No code change needed now; keep as-is. When the desktop switch is made, replace the hand-maintained :root/[data-theme="dark"] blocks in apps/desktop/src/styles.css with generateThemeCss() output (build-time emit or a runtime <style> inject) so the parity test stops being the only thing exercising this code. Until then, leave a TECH_DEBT note pointing styles.css at this generator.
findings.testStrategy: Existing css-vars.test.ts already covers generateRootCss/generateThemeCss and styles.css parity; when wired, add a desktop-side test asserting the injected CSS equals generateThemeCss().

findings.title: claudeOauthProfile (whole profile.ts) is dead — never consumed by the framework or any caller
findings.problem: profile.ts exports `claudeOauthProfile: OAuthProviderProfile` and index.ts re-exports it, but nothing in the repo imports it. The OAuthProviderProfile abstraction exists so a provider can drive the framework's generic `runOauthLogin`/`ensureFreshTokens` orchestrators (as the codex provider does). Claude-code deliberately implements its own OOB JSON exchange/refresh in login.ts and never references the profile — its own module docstring says the framework's loopback/device orchestrators 'don't model' this flow. So the profile is pure dormant plumbing copied from the codex provider's shape.
findings.fix: Delete profile.ts and the `export { claudeOauthProfile } from './profile.js'` line in index.ts. If a future contributor wants framework-driven login they can reconstruct it from constants.ts; keeping a never-wired profile only invites the false impression that the generic orchestrator path is active here.
findings.testStrategy: After deletion run `pnpm -F @moxxy/plugin-provider-claude-code build` and the repo typecheck; no consumer references it so the build stays green. The existing provider.test.ts/login.test.ts cover the live surface.

findings.title: registerHookOptions is a dead empty no-op stub with zero callers
findings.problem: `Session.registerHookOptions(_hooks: LifecycleHooks)` has an empty body and is referenced nowhere in the repo. It is not part of any interface the class implements (`ClientSession`/`SessionRuntime`), so it is not required by structural typing. It is dormant 'just in case' plumbing that ships in the public d.ts and misleads readers into thinking there is a hook-attach path here.
findings.fix: Delete the method. If a one-off hook bundle is genuinely needed for tests, route it through `pluginHost.registerStatic(definePlugin({ hooks }))` (the existing, tested path) rather than a private no-op.
findings.testStrategy: Remove and run `pnpm --filter @moxxy/core build` + typecheck + test; confirm no breakage (there are no callers).

findings.title: focus-mic-pulse keyframe injected but never referenced by any element
findings.problem: The `@keyframes focus-mic-pulse` block (a ~20-line mic 'breathing ring' animation) is injected into the focus document's <style> tag on module load but no element ever sets `animation: focus-mic-pulse ...`. The recording state is now visualised by SpectroBackground (a canvas), so the pulse keyframe is dormant plumbing carried in every focus bundle.
findings.fix: Delete the `@keyframes focus-mic-pulse` block from the injected stylesheet (keep only `focus-thinking`, which Dot uses).
findings.testStrategy: Visual: confirm the mic/recording state still animates via SpectroBackground + Dot after removal; existing FocusWidget.test.tsx still passes.

findings.title: Unused `node:path` import in memory.ts kept alive only by `void path;`
findings.problem: `import * as path from 'node:path'` is no longer referenced anywhere in the file; the only thing that touches it is the trailing `void path;` statement, which exists solely to suppress the noUnusedLocals error. This is leftover plumbing from a removed code path that hides a genuinely dead import behind a no-op.
findings.fix: Delete the `import * as path from 'node:path'` line and the `void path;` line. (`os` is also imported — verify it is still used: it is not referenced either; `memory` uses `defaultMemoryDir()` from the plugin, so check and drop `os` too if unused.)
findings.testStrategy: Run `pnpm --filter @moxxy/cli typecheck` after removal; the noUnusedLocals check will confirm nothing else relied on the import.

findings.title: DEVICE_POLL_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS = 0 is a no-op constant
findings.problem: device-flow.ts defines `const DEVICE_POLL_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS = 0` and adds it to the poll interval at line 50 (`auth.intervalSec * 1000 + DEVICE_POLL_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS`). Adding 0 is a no-op; the constant is vestigial. The sibling orchestrator run-login.ts:23 uses the same name with value 3000, so a reader reasonably assumes this legacy path also adds a margin — it does not. This is a copy-paste divergence that silently changes timing behavior between the legacy runDeviceCodeFlow and the adapter-based runOauthLogin.
findings.fix: Either remove the constant and the `+ 0` (legacy flow keeps provider interval as-is), or unify on run-login.ts's 3000 margin and lift it to device-flow-shared.ts so both code paths share one value. Decide intent explicitly.
findings.testStrategy: n/a — mechanical; covered by the device-flow integration test asserting first-poll delay equals interval(+margin).

findings.title: wsBridgeTokenFile() export has no production caller (only its own test references it)
findings.problem: wsBridgeTokenFile() is documented 'for diagnostics' but is invoked nowhere in shipped code. Grep across apps/desktop/src, apps/desktop/electron, and packages/desktop-host/src finds it only in ws-bridge.test.ts (and the rotateWsBridgeToken doc comment in index.ts). It is dormant plumbing — either wire it into a diagnostics surface (boot-log / Settings -> Mobile) or drop it. (Checked: not referenced via any string-keyed registry, IPC contract, or dynamic import — the WS bridge has no manifest entry; it is a plain helper module.)
findings.fix: Either expose the token-file path through the mobile-gateway status/diagnostics IPC so the comment's stated purpose is real, or delete the export and inline the path constant where the token is resolved.
findings.testStrategy: If wired to diagnostics, assert the status payload includes the resolved absolute path; if deleted, the existing test that asserts file existence can target the resolveChannelToken dir directly.

findings.title: Dormant OAuth wrapper exports (exchangeCodeForTokens/buildAuthorizeUrl/generatePKCE/parseJwtClaims/DEFAULT_REDIRECT_URI/OAuthTokenResponse) with no live consumer
findings.problem: Since the codex login flow was migrated to `runOauthLogin(codexOauthProfile, ...)` (login.ts) / `ensureFreshTokens` (the generic plugin-oauth path), several codex-shaped wrappers exist only as thin re-shapers that nothing in the live path calls. They are kept 'for tests + downstream consumers' but no production caller exists. Repo-wide grep (excluding dist/) shows: `exchangeCodeForTokens` — only oauth.test.ts; `buildAuthorizeUrl` — only test; `generatePKCE` — only test; `parseJwtClaims` — only test + internal; `DEFAULT_REDIRECT_URI` — only the barrel re-export; `OAuthTokenResponse` type — zero importers anywhere. This is dormant public surface that must be maintained and tested without serving the runtime.
findings.fix: Either delete the unused wrappers + their tests and the index.ts re-exports (`buildAuthorizeUrl`, `generatePKCE`, `parseJwtClaims` if only test-internal, `exchangeCodeForTokens`, `DEFAULT_REDIRECT_URI`, `OAuthTokenResponse`), or — if the intent is a stable public test/interop surface — document them as such and drop the misleading 'downstream consumers use it directly' comments. Keep `refreshTokens`, `extractAccountId`, `CLIENT_ID`/`ISSUER`/`TOKEN_URL`/`CODEX_RESPONSES_URL`/`SCOPES`/`ORIGINATOR`, `ensureFreshCodexTokens`, `persistCodexTokens`, `readStoredTokens` (all have live consumers).
findings.testStrategy: After removal, `pnpm build` + `pnpm -F @moxxy/plugin-provider-openai-codex test` + repo typecheck; confirm plugin-stt-whisper-codex and cli/provider-credentials still resolve their imports.

findings.title: VoiceHandlerState/Deps carry 8 never-read fields passed in from channel.ts
findings.problem: handleVoiceMessage only ever reads state.busy, state.session, deps.pairing, deps.token, deps.logger, and cb. The remaining declared inputs — state.model, state.activeModelOverride, state.yolo, state.turnController, state.awaitingApprovalText, state.handle, deps.approvalResolver, deps.permissionResolver, deps.framePump — are all dead: channel.ts:202-227 constructs and passes them every voice event for nothing. This is copy-paste from the text handler's wider state and adds per-event allocation + misleading coupling.
findings.fix: Trim VoiceHandlerState to {session,busy} and VoiceHandlerDeps to {pairing,token,logger}; update channel.ts handleVoice to stop spreading the unused fields. (If u110-1 is fixed by capturing voice approval-text, keep awaitingApprovalText + approvalResolver and drop the rest.)
findings.testStrategy: Type-check after trimming; existing voice-handler.test.ts must still pass.

findings.title: fetchWasmBytes is a public export with no external consumer (only used internally)
findings.problem: fetchWasmBytes is exported from the package surface but, per a repo-wide grep, has no importer outside this package's own src (invoke() calls it internally; no test or other package imports it). It is exported 'just in case'. Unlike buildWasmHostImports/_resetScratch (which the unit tests use and are documented test-only), fetchWasmBytes has no caller justifying the export.
findings.fix: Drop the `export` keyword on fetchWasmBytes (make it module-private), or add a direct unit test that justifies keeping it public. Verified no dynamic/string-keyed dispatch references it (it's a plain named import, not registry-dispatched).
findings.testStrategy: Build + typecheck after un-exporting; confirm no consumer breaks.

findings.title: JitiLoaderOptions.cacheBust is dormant plumbing — no caller ever supplies it
findings.problem: The cacheBust option is part of the public JitiLoaderOptions surface but is never passed by any caller; its only effect is the always-on bust flagged in u41-3.
findings.fix: Remove the option and fix the bust default (u41-3), or wire cacheBust from reload() so it has a live consumer.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck + existing loader tests pass after removal; or assert reload passes a monotonic cacheBust after wiring.

findings.title: readAll's `_total` parameter is dead — never used
findings.problem: `readAll(res, _total, onChunk)` receives `_total` but never reads it (progress total is reported separately at the call site via res.headers content-length). The underscore-prefixed param is dormant plumbing that adds a misleading signature implying readAll uses total for progress.
findings.fix: Drop the `_total` parameter and the corresponding argument at the call site. Verified no other caller of readAll exists (module-local function, single call site at stager.ts:301).
findings.testStrategy: Existing stager.test.ts download tests cover readAll behaviorally; signature change is type-checked.

findings.title: Dead `Socket` import kept only to be `void`'d ('for parity with the desktop sweep helper')
findings.problem: `import { Socket } from 'node:net'` is imported solely so `killStaleRunnerAt` can do `void Socket;` with the comment 'kept for parity with the desktop sweep helper.' It is never instantiated or used. This is dormant 'just in case' plumbing that pulls node:net into the module graph for no behavior.
findings.fix: Delete the `import { Socket }` line and the `void Socket;` statement.
findings.testStrategy: Build + typecheck after removal; the no-unused-imports lint should pass.

findings.title: writePrefs exported but only used internally; not re-exported and no external importer
findings.problem: writePrefs is `export`ed but is only consumed by updatePrefs in the same module. It is NOT re-exported from index.ts (only readPrefs/updatePrefs are at index.ts:19) and grep finds no external importer. Exporting it advertises a mutex-bypassing whole-file write: a caller using writePrefs directly would skip writeMutex and could clobber a concurrent updatePrefs (the exact race the mutex comment warns about). Demoting it to a private function removes the foot-gun.
findings.fix: Drop the `export` keyword on writePrefs (make it a module-private function).
findings.testStrategy: Build + typecheck after removing the export; confirm no unresolved imports.

findings.title: type.ts builds an unused `do shell script cat` AppleScript then discards it via `void script`
findings.problem: The handler constructs a `script` string implementing a stdin/`cat` keystroke approach, then immediately abandons it with `void script;` and uses `directScript` instead. The dead variable plus its two explanatory comments are stale 'just in case' plumbing that misleads readers into thinking two code paths exist when only one runs.
findings.fix: Delete the unused `script` const (lines 26-29), the `void script;` line, and the two now-irrelevant comment blocks. Keep only the `toAppleScriptString` + `directScript` path.
findings.testStrategy: No behavior change; existing schema test still passes. Optionally add a unit test asserting toAppleScriptString output to lock the live path.

findings.title: mapNestedPluginEvent / `via` chaining is unreachable dormant plumbing in the current flatten design
findings.problem: `mapChildEvent` case 'plugin_event' -> `mapNestedPluginEvent` only fires when a `subagent_*` plugin_event is appended to a CHILD log. But nested children are spawned with the SAME root `parentSession`/`parentTurnId` (run-child.ts:101 spreads `rt`, overriding only `parentModel`), so grandchildren stream their `subagent_*` events to the ROOT log, never to the immediate child log. No other code appends a `subagent_*` plugin_event to a non-root log. So this branch and the `payload.via` it sets can never execute, and nothing reads `via` (grep: only the assignment site).
findings.fix: Either delete mapNestedPluginEvent + the 'plugin_event' case (and the `via` field), or — if nested-bubbling is the intended future — make nested spawners stream to their immediate parent log so the branch is actually exercised. Pick one; don't keep unreachable code without a test that drives it.
findings.testStrategy: If kept: add a test that spawns a grandchild and asserts a `via`-tagged subagent_* event reaches the immediate parent. If deleted: existing model-resolution + (new) mapping tests stay green.

findings.title: whisperFilenameFor exported but has zero consumers (internal or cross-package)
findings.problem: whisperFilenameFor is defined in audio.ts and re-exported from index.ts but never called anywhere. normalizeWhisperUpload (the live helper) indexes WHISPER_FILENAME_BY_MIME directly instead of calling whisperFilenameFor, and whisper.ts uses its own duplicate table. So the helper is dormant 'just in case' plumbing.
findings.fix: Either delete whisperFilenameFor (and its index re-export), or make normalizeWhisperUpload call it so the package has a single filename-inference codepath. Prefer the latter to keep one source of truth.
findings.testStrategy: If kept and wired into normalizeWhisperUpload, add a unit test covering mapped + unmapped MIME. If deleted, `pnpm build` + typecheck confirms no broken importer.

findings.title: ensureLoaded ENOENT branch is identical to its else branch (dead distinction)
findings.problem: The catch block distinguishes ENOENT from all other errors but both arms execute the exact same statement (`this.cache = webhookConfigSchema.parse({})`). The branch reads as if ENOENT (fresh start) and other errors (corruption/permission) are handled differently, but they are not — any read failure, including a permissions/IO error, silently resets config to defaults. Compare with store.ts:265-322 which deliberately refuses to operate on non-ENOENT read errors to avoid clobbering. config.ts here is the inconsistent/weaker sibling.
findings.fix: Collapse to a single assignment, OR (better, for consistency with the trigger store's safety model) on a non-ENOENT error re-throw / set a loadWarning instead of silently defaulting, so a transient permission error can't cause persist() to overwrite a real config with defaults.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: simulate an EACCES read error and assert get() either throws or preserves prior config rather than returning silent defaults; ENOENT returns defaults.

findings.title: Empty `else if` continuation branch in parseNumberedBlock is a pure no-op
findings.problem: The final `else if (inBlock && items.length > 0 && !/^[A-Z]/.test(line))` branch has an empty body (only a comment 'continuation indented under previous item — skip'). The whole branch evaluates a non-trivial predicate per line and then does nothing — it neither appends to the previous item nor breaks the loop, so it is unreachable-effect dead code. Worse, it advertises 'continuation handling' that does not exist: a multi-line query wraps are silently dropped, not joined onto the prior item.
findings.fix: Either delete the dead else-if entirely (queries are single-line by spec), or, if continuation joining is actually desired, implement it (append `line` to items[items.length-1]). Recommend deletion to remove the misleading comment.
findings.testStrategy: Add a parseQueries test with a wrapped multi-line query to pin the chosen behavior (drop vs join) so the intent is encoded, not just commented.

findings.title: MicIcon `big` prop is never passed (only <MicIcon /> with default size)
findings.problem: `MicIcon({ big = false })` branches its size between 28 and 16 px, but every call site renders `<MicIcon />` with no `big` prop, so the 28 px branch is unreachable. Dead parameter + dead branch.
findings.fix: Drop the `big` prop and the `size = big ? 28 : 16` branch; hardcode `size = 16` (or accept a generic `size` prop if a larger mic is anticipated — but YAGNI here).
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot/visual check the active-stage mic button is unchanged; FocusWidget.test.tsx 'record voice' button test still passes.

findings.title: MasterKeySource.persist() is dormant — no production caller invokes it
findings.problem: The MasterKeySource interface declares an optional `persist(key, salt)` hook and createCombinedKeySource implements it, but VaultStore (the only consumer) never calls keySource.persist — master-key persistence happens as a side effect inside obtain() (persistKey on the prompt path). So persist() is plumbing that is exercised only by keysource.test.ts. It is part of the public exported surface, so not strictly removable, but it is currently dead in the running system and risks confusing future readers into thinking it is wired.
findings.fix: Either (a) remove the unused interface method + implementation and rely on obtain()'s built-in persistence, or (b) document explicitly that persist() is a forced-rewrite hook for callers that already hold a key out-of-band, and add a real caller (e.g. a `moxxy doctor --reseed`). If kept, leave a comment noting it is intentionally not called by VaultStore.
findings.testStrategy: If removed: ensure keysource.test.ts persist() test is deleted and the suite stays green. If kept+documented: no behavior change, lint stays clean.

clusters.title: Long-tail: residual weak-typing nitpicks
clusters.oneLine: Remaining any / unchecked-cast / non-exhaustive-switch spots not in the focused type-safety clusters. (19 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Type-safety hardening, no behavior change; address when touching the file.

findings.title: createClient merges untrusted config LAST, can clobber host-wired vault/fetch
findings.problem: createClient spreads `...(config as Partial<CodexOAuthTranscriberOptions>)` after the host-supplied `vault`/`fetch`/`baseUrl`/`sessionIdProvider`. `config` is a `Record<string, unknown>` coming from the registry's `setActive(name, config)` path; an unchecked cast lets any caller-provided `vault`, `fetch`, etc. override the host's real vault binding. Today the only callers (runner `setActive(name)` with no config, CLI builtins, the in-process desktop host) pass `{}`, so it is not currently exploitable — but the cast erases the type check that would otherwise catch a config key shadowing a required dependency, and the spread order makes config authoritative over host wiring.
findings.fix: Whitelist the merge to known overridable keys (e.g. pick only `baseUrl`/`sessionIdProvider` from config) and keep `vault`/`fetch` host-authoritative, OR validate `config` with a zod schema before merge. At minimum spread host options last so they win, and drop the blanket cast.
findings.testStrategy: Add a test passing `createClient({ vault: someEvilVault })` and assert the transcriber still uses the host-wired vault (or that an unknown key is rejected).

findings.title: verbose_json response cast via `as unknown as` then trusted as { text: string } without validation
findings.problem: The OpenAI response is force-cast `response as unknown as { text: string; ... }` and r.text is assigned straight into result.text typed string. If the provider returns a body missing `text` (error envelope, schema drift, proxy), result.text is undefined at runtime while the type says string — every downstream caller does `result.text.trim()` (see use-voice-input.ts:91), which would throw a TypeError instead of a structured MoxxyError. The codex sibling explicitly validates `typeof payload.text === 'string'` and throws PROVIDER_UNKNOWN_RESPONSE otherwise; whisper.ts does not, so the two backends handle a malformed-success-body inconsistently.
findings.fix: After the cast, guard `typeof r.text !== 'string'` (both branches) and throw a MoxxyError({ code: 'PROVIDER_UNKNOWN_RESPONSE', ... }) to match the codex sibling, so a contract violation surfaces as a classified error rather than a downstream TypeError.
findings.testStrategy: Test: client returns `{}` (no text) -> transcribe rejects with MoxxyError code PROVIDER_UNKNOWN_RESPONSE rather than yielding `{ text: undefined }`.

findings.title: nodeToStep builds an untyped Record and casts `as unknown as WorkflowStep`, bypassing the SDK contract
findings.problem: nodeToStep assembles `base: Record<string, unknown>` then returns `base as unknown as WorkflowStep`. The double-cast erases all compile-time checking that the emitted shape matches the SDK WorkflowStep discriminated union, so a field rename in the SDK (or a missing required field) would not be caught here — the canvas could emit invalid YAML that only the server rejects. The autoLayout test also casts step fixtures `as never`, indicating the type seam is awkward.
findings.fix: Build per-kind typed objects (a small union-returning factory keyed by StepKind) so the assignment to WorkflowStep is checked, or define a typed Omit-based builder. At minimum narrow the cast to `base as WorkflowStep` after constructing a typed intermediate.
findings.testStrategy: A type-level test (.test-d.ts) asserting serialize(state).workflow.steps is WorkflowStep[] without `as unknown`.

findings.title: Entire chat.completions.create request body cast `as never`, erasing OpenAI SDK request typing
findings.problem: The request body is built as a plain object and forced through `as never` (plus `messages as never`, `tools as never`), so TypeScript validates nothing about the shape sent to the OpenAI SDK. A typo in a field name or a wrong value type (e.g. reasoning_effort, stream_options) would compile silently and only fail at runtime against the live API. The local OpenAIChatMessage type exists but isn't reconciled with the SDK's param types, so the cast papers over that mismatch instead of bridging it.
findings.fix: Type the request object as `OpenAI.Chat.Completions.ChatCompletionCreateParamsStreaming` (or a narrowed Partial) so field names/types are checked; keep a single, narrow cast only where the local message shape genuinely diverges from the SDK union (with a comment), rather than `as never` on the whole body.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck only — the existing create()-body-capture tests already lock behavior; the change is purely compile-time safety.

findings.title: guard<T>() return value redundantly re-cast at all 9 call sites, weakening the generic
findings.problem: guard<T>(value: T | symbol): T already narrows to T, yet every call site writes `guard(raw) as string` / `as boolean`. Because the clack helpers return `string | symbol` / `boolean | symbol`, T infers as the value type and the `as` is redundant. The redundant casts mean a future type change (e.g. a non-string select value) would be force-cast and silently masked rather than flagged.
findings.fix: Type guard to return the non-symbol component, e.g. `function guard<T>(value: T | symbol): Exclude<T, symbol>`, and drop the `as X` at every call site so the compiler enforces the real value type. (Current `: T` already strips symbol via inference; the point is removing the masking casts.)
findings.testStrategy: typecheck after removing the casts; ensure no new TS errors, confirming guard's return type already matches each consumer's expected type.

findings.title: defaultClientFactory leans on repeated `as unknown as` casts and `as never`
findings.problem: The @modelcontextprotocol/sdk Client is typed via a hand-rolled McpClientUntyped and then cast `(client as unknown as McpClientUntyped)` at every call site, plus the callTool result is forced with `as never` (L36). These double-casts defeat type checking: if the SDK's callTool signature or result shape changes, nothing flags it; a wrong `arguments` shape or missing field would surface only at runtime against a live MCP server.
findings.fix: Type `client` once as McpClientUntyped at construction (drop the per-call re-casts) and build the returned McpCallResult object with an explicit McpCallResult annotation instead of `as never`, narrowing content via a small mapper so the SDK->internal boundary is checked in one place.
findings.testStrategy: tsc passes; a typed mapper means a content-shape change becomes a compile error.

findings.title: Local `usage` accumulator typed without cache fields; relies on spread to dodge excess-prop check
findings.problem: usage is declared `{ inputTokens: number; outputTokens: number } | undefined`, yet the message_start assignment attaches cacheReadTokens/cacheCreationTokens. It only compiles because they are added via a conditional-object spread (excess-property check is bypassed for spreads). The declared type therefore lies about the value's shape; anyone editing the message_delta merge (line 408) could drop the cache fields with no type error, silently re-breaking cache-savings metering (the very thing the comment at 321-325 says must not happen).
findings.fix: Type the accumulator as `TokenUsage | undefined` (import the existing SDK type) so the cache fields are first-class and the merge at line 408 is type-checked to preserve them.
findings.testStrategy: Type-only: changing the annotation to TokenUsage should still compile; add the cache-token assertion from u94-2 to lock runtime behaviour.

findings.title: BlockView switch has no exhaustiveness guard — a new Block kind silently renders nothing
findings.problem: The `switch (block.kind)` covers the 6 current Block kinds but has no `default` with a `never`-assert. The function is typed `JSX.Element | null`; if @moxxy/chat-model adds a 7th Block kind, this falls off the end of the switch and returns `undefined` (the new kind renders blank) with no compile-time error forcing the renderer to handle it. The shared Block union is the contract; this is the one place that must stay exhaustive.
findings.fix: Add `default: { const _exhaustive: never = block; return null; }` so a new Block kind is a typecheck failure here.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: a TuiChannel.test-d-style expectError when a fabricated extra kind is passed; or rely on the `never` assignment failing tsc when the union grows.

findings.title: Unnecessary inline `(merged as { f?: T }).f = ...` casts in resolveSpec
findings.problem: resolveSpec builds a local SubagentSpec and assigns optional fields via repeated `(merged as { systemPrompt?: string }).systemPrompt = ...` casts to avoid writing undefined keys. tsconfig.base.json has exactOptionalPropertyTypes: false, so these casts are not needed to satisfy the optional-property rule — they are pure boilerplate that defeats type-checking on each assignment (a typo in the cast shape would compile). It is the same family of escape hatch invariant #10 discourages, applied here to a non-frozen local object (so not a violation, but a smell that erodes type safety and duplicates a conditional-assign pattern 5 times).
findings.fix: Build merged with a conditional object spread, e.g. `const merged: SubagentSpec = { prompt, label, agentType, ...(systemPrompt !== undefined && { systemPrompt }), ...(model !== undefined && { model }), ... }`, eliminating the readonly-bypass casts and the repeated pattern.
findings.testStrategy: Covered by the resolveSpec unit tests proposed in u109-1; behavior must be identical (no undefined keys emitted).

findings.title: Redundant inline re-typing of already-typed IPC subscribe payloads
findings.problem: `api().subscribe` is generic over the channel key (subscribe<K> in the desktop-ipc-contract), so the callback payload is inferred from IpcEvents. useAgentTask re-declares the payload shapes inline (`({ event: ev }: { workspaceId: string; event: MoxxyEvent })` and `({ turnId: id, error: err }: { workspaceId: string; turnId: string; error: string | null })`). This duplicates the contract types by hand; if IpcEvents['runner.event'] or ['runner.turn.complete'] changes shape, the inline annotation silently diverges instead of erroring. OAuthSignIn.tsx (lines 50-69) correctly relies on inference for the same channels, so this is an internal inconsistency too.
findings.fix: Drop the inline parameter type annotations and rely on the generic subscribe inference (matching OAuthSignIn), keeping the local destructure/rename.
findings.testStrategy: typecheck after removal; assert ev.type narrowing still resolves delta/content.

findings.title: runTurn passes opts via `as never`, disabling all RunTurnOptions field-checking to mask one branded-id mismatch
findings.problem: `this.session.runTurn(prompt, opts as never)` uses `as never` purely to bypass the TurnId brand mismatch (id is a plain randomUUID() string, RunTurnOptions.turnId is branded TurnId). `as never` is far broader than needed: it disables type-checking of EVERY field (model, signal, attachments, turnId) at the call boundary, so any future drift in RunTurnOptions (e.g. an attachment-shape change) compiles silently here. Violates invariant 10's spirit (no unchecked casts) and the audit's weak-typing lens.
findings.fix: Type the local id/turnId as the branded TurnId (mint via the sdk's id helper, e.g. asTurnId/newTurnId), type the opts variable as RunTurnOptions, and drop the cast entirely. If a brand cast is unavoidable, scope it to the single field (`turnId: id as TurnId`) rather than `opts as never`.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: a .test-d.ts that constructs the opts object and asserts it is assignable to RunTurnOptions without `as never`; a breaking change to RunTurnOptions should then fail typecheck.

findings.title: session.info result narrowed via unchecked cast to SessionInfoSlice
findings.problem: `const info = raw as SessionInfoSlice | null` blind-casts the IPC payload, and SessionInfoSlice (types.ts) is a hand-rolled local duplicate of the contract's commands slice rather than a type imported from @moxxy/desktop-ipc-contract. If the contract's CommandInfo shape drifts (e.g. adds required fields or renames), this local shape silently diverges with no compile error, and the cast hides it.
findings.fix: Import the command-info type from the shared contract (or @moxxy/client-core re-export) instead of redeclaring it, so a contract change forces a compile error here. If api().invoke is already generically typed by method name, drop the cast entirely.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: remove the cast and confirm tsc still passes once the shared type is imported; a test-d.ts asserting the imported type assignability.

findings.title: setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces accessed via double `as unknown as` cast (invariant #10 smell)
findings.problem: The Electron feature-probe for setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces is done with `(win as unknown as { setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces?: ... }).setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces` twice — an unchecked cast that the repo's invariant #10 bans the mutation form of. BrowserWindow actually types setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces, so the cast hides a real typed API and would mask a signature drift across Electron versions.
findings.fix: Call win.setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces(true, { visibleOnFullScreen: true }) directly (it is in @types/electron); if defensive version-guarding is still wanted, narrow via `typeof win.setVisibleOnAllWorkspaces === 'function'` without the unknown cast.
findings.testStrategy: typecheck only; no behavioural change.

findings.title: Repeated `as never` / `as VaultStore` / `as ... | undefined` casts erode type safety at the deps boundary
findings.problem: The plugin repeatedly casts untyped `deps`/`ctx.deps` fields: index.ts `logger: deps.logger as never` and `deps.options?.['token'] as string | undefined`; pair-flow.ts `ctx.deps.vault as VaultStore`, `ctx.deps.logger as never`; setup-wizard.ts `ctx.deps.vault as VaultStore`; index.ts subcommands `ctx.deps.vault as VaultStore | undefined`. `as never` in particular defeats all checking on the logger shape. These unchecked casts hide contract drift between the channel deps and the plugin.
findings.fix: Define a typed `TelegramDeps` interface (vault, logger, options) and narrow `deps` through a single typed accessor/validator once, instead of scattering `as never`/`as VaultStore` casts; route the logger type through @moxxy/sdk's channel-deps type so `as never` is unnecessary.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: a .test-d.ts asserting the deps accessor returns the typed shape; build/typecheck stays green.

findings.title: style record typed as Record<string,CSSProperties> erases key checking — typos compile
findings.problem: `export const style: Record<string, React.CSSProperties>` makes every `style.<key>` access type as `CSSProperties` regardless of whether the key exists. A typo (e.g. `style.activeRooot`) compiles to `undefined` and silently produces an unstyled element instead of a TS error. Consumers (Active/Inactive/MiniText/focus-primitives) all index this loosely-typed map.
findings.fix: Drop the explicit `Record<string,...>` annotation and use `satisfies Record<string, React.CSSProperties>` instead (`export const style = { ... } satisfies Record<string, React.CSSProperties>`). This validates each value as CSSProperties while preserving the literal key union so `style.<typo>` becomes a compile error.
findings.testStrategy: typecheck (tsc) — introduce a deliberate typo locally and confirm it now errors; revert.

findings.title: Unsafe literal cast `type as 'user_prompt'` in toContainEventOfType
findings.problem: received.ofType(type as 'user_prompt') casts a general MoxxyEventType down to one concrete literal purely to satisfy the generic signature `ofType<T extends MoxxyEventType>(type: T)`. This is an unnecessary unsafe cast that misrepresents the runtime type (the value is not necessarily 'user_prompt') and silently erases the return-type narrowing for every other event type.
findings.fix: Drop the cast: `const events = received.ofType(type);`. The matcher only needs `.length`, so no narrowing is required.
findings.testStrategy: Typecheck (tsc --noEmit) plus a matcher unit test (see u127-1) exercising multiple event types.

findings.title: ctx.session is `unknown` then cast to hand-written SessionShape/CompactSessionShape — bypasses type checking
findings.problem: Handlers receive `session` typed loosely and immediately do `session as SessionShape` / `session as CompactSessionShape` (lines 44, 95, 145). These locally-redeclared structural interfaces duplicate the real Session surface to avoid a core dependency, but the cast means any drift between the real Session and these shapes (e.g. a renamed method, or compact()'s new provider/model needs) goes undetected — directly enabling bugs u80-1 and u80-3. CompactSessionShape also omits sessionId/turnId which u80-3 needs.
findings.fix: Define these structural contracts once in @moxxy/sdk (the typed public surface) as the canonical narrow interface the host's Session must satisfy, and have the host assert conformance, so the cast is checked at the boundary rather than blindly here.
findings.testStrategy: Type-level: add a static assertion that the real Session type is assignable to SessionShape/CompactSessionShape in a host-side test.

findings.title: Double `as unknown as` / blind bigint cast on the wasm boundary masks real ABI shape mismatches
findings.problem: Two unchecked casts cross the wasm boundary: (1) line 153 `bytes as unknown as BufferSource` (documented workaround for SharedArrayBuffer typing, acceptable but a double-cast that defeats checking), and (2) line 175 `(handler as (a:number,b:number)=>bigint)(inputPtr, inputBytes.length)` — the handler is typed `WebAssembly.ExportValue` and is blindly cast to a bigint-returning fn. If a module exports a handler with the wrong signature (returns i32 not i64, or takes different arity), this cast hides it and `packed >> 32n` throws a cryptic 'Cannot mix BigInt' TypeError at runtime instead of a clear ABI-violation error.
findings.fix: After calling the handler, guard `if (typeof packed !== 'bigint') throw new Error('[security:wasm] handler must return i64 (got '+typeof packed+'); check the (i32,i32)->i64 calling convention')` before the bigint shifts, turning a confusing TypeError into an actionable ABI error.
findings.testStrategy: Hand-encode a fixture whose handler returns i32 and assert run() rejects with the explicit calling-convention error.

findings.title: webkitAudioContext cast `(window as unknown as {...})` duplicated; should be one typed helper
findings.problem: The same `window.AudioContext ?? (window as unknown as { webkitAudioContext?: typeof AudioContext }).webkitAudioContext` resolution is copy-pasted in audio-capture.ts (L72-74) and pcm16.ts (L17-19). It uses the banned-ish `as unknown as` shape to reach a vendor-prefixed global, and the duplication means a future fix (e.g. handling a suspended context) must be made twice.
findings.fix: Extract a single `function getAudioContextCtor(): typeof AudioContext | undefined` in pcm16.ts (or a small internal `web-audio.ts`), with the cast centralized and documented; import it in both call sites.
findings.testStrategy: No behavior change; covered by typecheck. A small unit test stubbing window.webkitAudioContext asserts the helper returns it when AudioContext is absent.

clusters.title: Long-tail: smaller god-component / coupling splits
clusters.oneLine: Smaller single-file god-component / over-coupling findings (App.tsx gating+hotkey+heartbeat, oauth refresh dup, etc.) beneath the headline god-file cluster. (13 deduped findings.)
