clusters.rationale: Structural; sequence after the headline god-file decomposition and dead-code shrink.

findings.title: session-like.ts is a god-file mixing the SessionLike contract with 6 unrelated admin-view DTOs
findings.problem: session-like.ts (354 LOC) defines the core SessionLike/SessionInfo seam AND inlines six independent feature-admin view contracts — McpAdminView, ProviderAdminView/ProviderConfigurePatch, WorkflowsView (+5 view DTOs), PluginsAdminView (+2 DTOs), CredentialResolver. These are optional capabilities each owned by a different plugin (plugin-mcp, plugin-provider-admin, plugin-workflows, plugin-plugins-admin). Bundling them here over-couples the central session interface to every optional plugin's UI contract: touching the workflows builder DTO churns the file every channel and RemoteSession imports, and a reader auditing the session seam wades through workflow/mcp/plugins DTOs unrelated to it.
findings.fix: Split the optional admin-view contracts into co-located modules (e.g. session-admin-views.ts or per-capability files) re-exported from the SDK index, leaving session-like.ts to the SessionLike/SessionInfo/SessionLogReader core seam. No behavior change; reduces churn radius.
findings.testStrategy: Type-only refactor; `pnpm typecheck` across all importers (channels, runner RemoteSession) is the regression gate.

findings.title: runTurn drain + final-assistant extraction copy-pasted across all three turn handlers
findings.problem: The `const events=[]; for await(...runTurn...) events.push(event)` loop and the `events.findLast(...assistant_message...)` extraction are duplicated verbatim in handleTurn and handleTurnAudio, and the runTurn drive loop again in handleTurnStream. Any fix to abort wiring (u70-1) or event shaping must be applied in three places, which is exactly how the missing-signal divergence arose.
findings.fix: Extract `async function driveTurn(session, prompt, opts, signal): Promise<{events, assistant}>` and `async function* streamTurn(...)`, used by all three handlers. Centralizes signal handling and event collection.
findings.testStrategy: Existing integration.test.ts continues to pass; add a unit test on the extracted driveTurn helper.

findings.title: SessionView is a god-component mixing 8+ concerns (state, slash routing wiring, reset, init effects, render)
findings.problem: SessionView wires permission queue, turn runner, voice, images, mcp status, hotkeys, mode cycling, picker handlers, slash submit, session-reset side effects, two one-shot mount effects, AND the full render tree. The reset routine (performSessionAction) reaches across four other hooks' internals (stream buffers, permission arrays, turn queue refs) by hand, coupling SessionView to each hook's private shape. This is the root cause of u78-1 (reset forgets one hook's slot).
findings.fix: Extract a single useSessionReset() (or a reset() method on each hook handle, e.g. turn.reset(), stream.reset(), permissions.reset()) so the reset becomes `for (const h of resettables) h.reset()` and no hook's internal slot can be forgotten.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test each hook's reset() in isolation; assert performSessionAction calls all of them.

findings.title: Rich-component expansion (results→cards) is hardcoded, not registry-driven like other moxxy strategies
findings.problem: expand()/expandResults() hardcode the single `results`→card-stack component transform with a literal `if (node.tag === 'results')`. ViewTagSpec already carries a `component: true` flag (view-renderer.ts) signaling 'rich component the renderer expands into primitives', but the expansion logic is not wired to it — a second component would require editing this file rather than registering an expander. Per the repo's swappable-blocks preference, component expanders should be a keyed map (tag → expander) so new components are additive. Low severity since `results` is currently the only component.
findings.fix: Introduce a `Record<string, (node)=>ViewNode>` expander table keyed by tag (or drive it off the `component` flag), so expand() dispatches via the table. Makes adding a future rich component a registration rather than an edit to the recursion.
findings.testStrategy: Existing results-expansion tests should pass unchanged; add a test registering a stub expander for a second component tag to prove the table is honored.

findings.title: append() and ingest() duplicate listener-snapshot + per-listener try/catch fanout
findings.problem: append() (66-81) and ingest() (97-121) each independently: push the event, snapshot `[...this.listeners]`, iterate, and swallow per-listener failures. The two diverge only in await-vs-fire-and-forget semantics. The shared 'snapshot then guarded fanout' logic is copy-pasted, so a future change to fanout (e.g. logging swallowed errors, or filtering) must be made in two places and can drift.
findings.fix: Extract a private `#fanout(event, { await: boolean })` (or two thin helpers `#fanoutAwait`/`#fanoutFireForget`) sharing the snapshot+guard, called by both. Keeps the distinct await semantics explicit while de-duplicating the snapshot/guard plumbing.
findings.testStrategy: Existing 'survives listener throws' and 'ingest survives a rejecting async listener' tests already cover both paths; refactor must keep them green.

findings.title: scopedSessionView hand-re-implements the entire RunTurnSession shape; brittle to runtime-protocol drift
findings.problem: scopedSessionView manually mirrors the SessionRuntime getters one by one. Any new field runTurn reads from the session that the view forgets to mirror will be silently `undefined` on a webhook fire with a non-empty allowedTools. Because the omitted field is OPTIONAL on SessionRuntime, the compiler does NOT flag the gap — the structural return type still satisfies `Parameters<typeof runTurn>[0]`.
findings.fix: Replace the hand-rolled proxy with `Object.create(session)` + only the overrides (tools, resolver) and the write-through lastResolvedModel, or a Proxy whose get-trap delegates to session except for the overridden keys. That keeps the view in lockstep with the real session automatically (and would have carried `reasoning` for free).
findings.testStrategy: Add a test that runs a real turn through scopedSessionView (allowedTools non-empty) on a session with `reasoning` set, asserting the ModeContext/provider call still receives the reasoning preference; or assert Reflect.ownKeys/getter parity against a Session instance.

findings.title: App.tsx is a god-component: gating + global hotkey + boot-heartbeat IPC + CLI self-heal + inline banners
findings.problem: The top-level shell mixes at least five separable concerns in one ~290-line component: (1) the 3-layer render gate, (2) a window-level Cmd/Ctrl+B hotkey effect, (3) a boot-probe heartbeat retry loop that drives app.appBooted/app.bootHeartbeatFailed IPC with backoff, (4) the CLI protocol-self-heal orchestration (onUpdateCli -> api().invoke('app.updateCli') + retry()), and (5) three inline-styled presentational sub-components (GlobalAskFallback, ReconnectBanner, describePhase). Items (3) and (4) are transport/host-orchestration logic, not view logic, and the same boot-heartbeat pattern would be needed by any client surface — it belongs behind a client-core hook (mirrors useSessionInfoBridge/useAppUpdate already living there), not inlined in the desktop shell. This makes the gate hard to test and couples the renderer shell to IPC mechanics.
findings.fix: Extract the boot-heartbeat retry loop into a client-core hook (e.g. useBootHeartbeat) and the CLI self-heal IPC into useConnection/useCliRecovery; move ReconnectBanner/GlobalAskFallback/describePhase into shell/ presentational files. Leave App.tsx as the gate orchestrator only.
findings.testStrategy: Once the heartbeat loop is a hook, unit-test it with a fake api() that fails N times then succeeds, asserting exactly one app.appBooted lands and bootHeartbeatFailed fires only when all attempts fail.

findings.title: Avatar-tile + collapsible-header scaffold copy-pasted across three block components
findings.problem: ToolBlock, SubagentView and SubagentGroupView each hand-inline the identical structural shell: a 34x34 rounded tile span (width/height/flexShrink/borderRadius/inline-flex centering), a flex-1 min-width-0 column, a full-width left-aligned toggle button, and a chevron span with the same rotate(open?90deg)/120ms transition. The styles are duplicated verbatim (e.g. the chevron block is byte-identical in all three). This is ~40 lines x3 of structural boilerplate that drifts independently — a styling tweak must be applied in three places, and block-shared.ts already exists as the home for exactly this kind of shared block primitive.
findings.fix: Extract a <BlockShell tileIcon tileBg tileFg open onToggle header>...</BlockShell> (or a CollapsibleBlockHeader + AvatarTile pair) into block-shared.tsx and have all three call it; keeps single-responsibility and one place to restyle.
findings.testStrategy: Snapshot/render test that each of the three blocks still renders its data-testid and toggles aria-expanded after extraction; visual diff unchanged.

findings.title: runProcess and runProcessBinary are near-duplicate copy-paste (abort/timer/error/close plumbing)
findings.problem: The two helpers share ~90% identical plumbing: spawn, onAbort closure, timeout timer, error handler, close handler, listener cleanup. They differ only in stdio config ('pipe' vs 'ignore' for stdin), stdout accumulation (string vs Buffer), and the stdin write. This copy-paste is exactly where the u81-1 divergence (one helper got the O(n) accumulation fix, the other didn't) crept in, and the u81-2 timeout gap must now be fixed in two places.
findings.fix: Extract a single private `spawnCapture(cmd, args, { input, signal, timeoutMs, binary })` core that always accumulates a Buffer[] and concats once; have runProcess return `.toString('utf8')` and runProcessBinary return the Buffer. Eliminates the divergence class entirely.
findings.testStrategy: The shell.test.ts suite from u81-3 covers the unified core directly.

findings.title: Subagent status (running/error/done) color+label logic duplicated across scope/group views
findings.problem: The running = `completedAtMs == null && error == null`, dotColor = `error ? danger : running ? busy : DotColors.subagent`, and the status-text triad are copy-pasted in SubagentScopeView (L8,L17-22) and in SubagentGroupView's SubagentRow (L77,L80-85). Divergence risk: SubagentScopeView uses verbs 'failed'/'running'/'done' while SubagentRow uses 'running'/'Done' (inconsistent casing/wording for the same states).
findings.fix: Extract a shared helper in @moxxy/chat-model, e.g. `subagentStatus(agent): { running: boolean; color: keyof DotColors; label: string }`, and consume it from both views so wording and color stay in lockstep.
findings.testStrategy: Unit-test the extracted helper for the three states; snapshot both views to confirm unchanged rendering.

findings.title: Upward-walk + MAX_CONFIG_SEARCH_DEPTH duplicated between loader.ts and plugin.ts
findings.problem: plugin.ts redeclares MAX_CONFIG_SEARCH_DEPTH (=12) and reimplements the bounded upward directory walk (findScopePath, lines 44-60) that loader.ts already implements (findUpward/findFile, lines 128-151). Two copies of the same traversal invariant drift independently: loader searches 6 CONFIG_NAMES, plugin searches only ['moxxy.config.yaml','moxxy.config.yml'], so the resolved 'project config path' differs between load time and edit time (a project using moxxy.config.ts is loaded by loader but config_path/config_set won't find it and will create a competing .yaml).
findings.fix: Export a shared `findProjectConfig(cwd, names?)` from loader.ts (or a util module) and call it from plugin.ts; the divergent name list should be a deliberate parameter, not an accidental copy. At minimum share the constant.
findings.testStrategy: Test that config_path on a project rooted by a moxxy.config.ts resolves to that file (or at least documents the yaml-only intent) rather than null.

findings.title: Tunnel providers wrapped as defineTunnelProvider but never registered — registry abstraction half-wired
findings.problem: webhookTunnelProviders is built via the SDK's registry-backed defineTunnelProvider contract (the same abstraction core's TunnelProviderRegistry consumes, with localhostTunnel registered in session.ts:201), yet these provider objects are never registered into any TunnelProviderRegistry. They are used only internally to back isTunnelCliAvailable() and openCliTunnel(), so the plugin re-implements its own tunnel orchestration (startTunnel + tunnelHandle ref + webhook_tunnel_start/stop tools) instead of going through the registry. The defineTunnelProvider wrapping is therefore decorative — it neither participates in discovery nor erases the bespoke orchestration. Per invariant 11 this is NOT dead (isAvailable/open are called internally), but it is an over-built abstraction that misleads readers into thinking tunnels are registry-managed.
findings.fix: Either register these providers into the session's TunnelProviderRegistry and drive webhook_tunnel_start through the registry (so they participate in the shared abstraction), or drop defineTunnelProvider and keep a plain SPAWN_CONFIG-backed helper — don't keep a registry def that is never registered.
findings.testStrategy: If registered: integration test that the registry resolves 'cloudflared'/'ngrok' and webhook_tunnel_start uses it. If simplified: ensure tunnel.test.ts still asserts isAvailable/open shape.

findings.title: RunnerSupervisor is a god-class mixing CLI resolution, socket probing, process lifecycle, protocol-mismatch recovery, log-ring redaction, and phase machine
findings.problem: A single 668-line class owns: CLI resolution, unix-socket probe/cleanup/dir-creation, child spawn + graceful terminate, protocol-mismatch recovery state, the connection phase state-machine, and a redacted log ring. teardown logic (close session → terminateChild → forceRetry) is copy-pasted across setCwd, resetSession, restart, and attempt's disconnect tail (4 near-identical blocks). This is hard to test in isolation and the duplicated teardown is a maintenance hazard — a fix to one path silently misses the others.
findings.fix: Extract a private `teardownChildAndSession()` used by all four call sites, and split socket-lifecycle helpers into a small collaborator object. Keep the phase machine as the class's single responsibility.
findings.testStrategy: After extraction, unit-test teardownChildAndSession once (it already has integration coverage via runner-supervisor.test.ts).

clusters.title: Long-tail: half-built / dormant features
clusters.oneLine: Skeleton/dormant features and unreachable UI (wasm authoring path, reasoning-effort dead UI, paused-run mishandling) that need a product decision: finish or remove. (8 deduped findings.)
clusters.rationale: Each needs a finish-or-remove call; not a pure refactor. Propose-only.

findings.title: dimensions override silently accepted for ada-002, which the API does not support
findings.problem: text-embedding-ada-002 does not support the `dimensions` truncation parameter; OpenAI rejects it. The constructor accepts `dimensions` for any model and both forwards it in embed() (line 70) and returns it from the `dim` getter (line 59). With ada-002 + dimensions set, `dim` reports a truncated size that the API will never produce, and the embed() call 400s at runtime. The doc on line 21 even scopes dimensions to 'text-embedding-3-*' but nothing enforces it.
findings.fix: Reject (or ignore + warn) an explicit dimensions value when model === 'text-embedding-ada-002'. Simplest: in the constructor, if model is ada-002 and opts.dimensions is set, throw or drop it and warn so dim/embed stay consistent with what the API will return.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: OpenAIEmbedder({ model:'text-embedding-ada-002', dimensions:512 }) either throws or leaves dim===1536 and omits dimensions from the create() call args.

findings.title: zodToJsonSchema silently drops value schema for ZodRecord/ZodTuple/ZodAny (unhandled types)
findings.problem: zodToJsonSchema has no branch for ZodRecord, ZodTuple, ZodAny, ZodUnknown, ZodMap, ZodSet — they all fall through to the permissive `{ type: 'object', properties: {}, additionalProperties: true }` fallback, discarding the value/element schema. A tool whose inputSchema is `z.record(z.string(), z.unknown())` (e.g. the MCP lazy-tool wrapper, plugin-mcp/wrap.ts:100) would advertise an empty object schema to the provider. Currently dormant because the MCP path always supplies `inputJsonSchema` (so zodToJsonSchema is bypassed), and the fallback is at least valid (strict validators accept it). Worth closing so a future record/tuple-typed tool schema isn't silently lossy.
findings.fix: Add explicit ZodRecord (`{type:'object', additionalProperties: zodToJsonSchema(valueType)}`) and ZodTuple/ZodAny/ZodUnknown branches; the comment already concedes 'best-effort', so at minimum document the unsupported set.
findings.testStrategy: Extend provider-utils.test.ts with z.record(z.string(), z.number()) asserting additionalProperties carries {type:'number'}, and z.any() asserting a permissive {} not a false object schema.

findings.title: --max-iterations silently drops non-numeric input instead of validating
findings.problem: `flags.maxIterations = Number(argv[++i] ?? '0')` turns `--max-iterations abc` into NaN. Downstream `collectTurn` is called with `...(flags.maxIterations ? { maxIterations } : {})` (line 99) where NaN is falsy, so the cap is silently ignored — the recorder runs unbounded against the real paid Anthropic API with no error or warning. A malformed `--max-iterations 0` is likewise dropped (0 falsy), which may be intended but is undocumented.
findings.fix: Parse with validation: `const n = Number(value); if (!Number.isInteger(n) || n < 1) throw new Error('--max-iterations must be a positive integer')`. Then no truthiness guard is needed at the call site.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test: `--max-iterations abc` throws; `--max-iterations 3` yields maxIterations:3 in the parsed Flags.

findings.title: --output-format value is cast, not validated; unknown values silently fall through to JSON
findings.problem: `outputFormat` is read and cast to the union type without validation: `(stringFlag(argv, 'output-format') ?? 'text') as 'text' | 'json' | 'stream-json'`. The dispatch is `if text … else if stream-json … else (json)`, so any unrecognized value (typo like `--output-format=jsonl`) silently produces full JSON output rather than erroring. This is a weak-typing cast masking missing input validation on a user-facing flag.
findings.fix: Validate against the allowed set before running: if the value is not one of `text|json|stream-json`, `printError` and return 2. Use a const tuple + `includes` check instead of an unchecked `as` cast.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test runPromptCommand (or extract the format-resolver) asserting an unknown `--output-format` returns exit code 2 and writes an error.

findings.title: TranscribeOptions.language/prompt silently dropped; result omits language/duration/segments
findings.problem: `transcribe()` accepts `TranscribeOptions` (which declares `language`, `prompt`, plus the result type carries optional `language`/`durationSec`/`segments`) but ignores `opts.language` and `opts.prompt` entirely and always returns only `{ text }`. Callers that set a language hint or a vocabulary prompt get no effect, and richer downstream consumers (diarization/segment-aware UIs) get nothing even when the Codex backend may report it. This is a quiet contract under-fulfilment rather than a bug.
findings.fix: If the Codex transcribe endpoint supports a language/prompt field, forward `opts.language`/`opts.prompt` in the form body; and surface `payload.language`/`payload.duration`/`payload.segments` into the result when present. If the backend does not support them, document the no-op explicitly so the omission is intentional.
findings.testStrategy: Mock a 200 response that includes `language`/`segments` and assert they propagate to TranscriptionResult; assert language/prompt are sent in the multipart body when provided.

findings.title: Reasoning-effort UI is unreachable + localStorage round-trip is dormant plumbing
findings.problem: `providerSupportsReasoning(p)` reads `(p as {supportsReasoning?}).supportsReasoning`, but `ProviderEntry` (the contract type the desktop receives) has no such field — only the server-side `ModelDescriptor` does (verified repo-wide grep: supportsReasoning is set only in provider model catalogs and sdk ModelDescriptor, never on ProviderEntry / settings.providers IPC). So the predicate is always false and the entire `<select> Reasoning effort` block never renders. The companion `reasoningEffortFor`/`setReasoningEffortFor` localStorage helpers and the `reasoning` state are therefore dead: even if the block rendered, the TODO at lines 26-32 confirms the runner never reads `moxxy.reasoning.effort`, so the choice has no effect. This is half-built feature plumbing shipped in production UI.
findings.fix: Either (a) plumb `supportsReasoning` onto `ProviderEntry` AND wire a `DesktopPrefs.reasoning` config-apply step so the control is real, or (b) remove the reasoning select, the two localStorage helpers, the `reasoning` state, and `providerSupportsReasoning` until the contract/runner support lands — keeping dormant cast-based plumbing out of the shipped panel.
findings.testStrategy: If removed: a render test asserting no `provider-reasoning-select` testid appears. If wired: assert the effort persists through DesktopPrefs and the runner config-apply receives it.

findings.title: Desktop per-provider reasoning-effort control is a dead UI: persists to localStorage, runner never reads it
findings.problem: The Providers settings tab renders a reasoning-effort selector (off/low/medium/high) per provider, but the chosen value is written only to a localStorage key (moxxy.reasoning.effort) via setReasoningEffortFor(). Nothing on the backend consumes it: there is no `reasoning` field in @moxxy/desktop-ipc-contract, and neither packages/desktop-host nor packages/runner reference reasoning at all. So the user picks an effort level and the runner keeps generating at its default — a control that silently does nothing. The functional path exists for the CLI (config.context.reasoning) but was never plumbed to the desktop session.
findings.fix: Either (a) complete the wiring per the in-code TODO: add a typed `DesktopPrefs.reasoning` field (+ `ProviderEntry.supportsReasoning`) to @moxxy/desktop-ipc-contract, an IPC command to apply it, and a runner config-apply step that maps it onto config.context.reasoning (mirror the proven CLI path); or (b) if reasoning-on-desktop is out of scope, remove the selector + REASONING_PREF_KEY/getter/setter so the UI doesn't present a non-functional control. Option (a) is the higher-value completion.
findings.testStrategy: After (a): integration test that setting effort via the IPC command flows into the runner's effective config.context.reasoning and changes the provider request (assert the provider stream receives the effort param). After (b): assert the control is gone and no localStorage key is written.

findings.title: No batching/bound on embed() input — whole corpus sent to onnx in one call
findings.problem: embed() forwards the entire `texts` array to the extractor in a single call (line 82), unlike the OpenAI sibling which chunks by batchSize (plugin-embeddings-openai/src/embedder.ts:62-76). For an on-device ONNX model a large memory-consolidation batch can spike RAM or stall the event loop with no upper bound. There is also no per-input length guard before sending to the model.
findings.fix: Add an optional batchSize to TransformersEmbedderOptions and loop in fixed chunks (mirroring the OpenAI embedder), accumulating results in input order. Pick a conservative on-device default.
findings.testStrategy: Unit test with a small batchSize and an input array larger than it; assert the extractor is called ceil(n/batch) times and output order/length is preserved.
